### [1] [SIDeR: Semantic Identity Decoupling for Unrestricted Face Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04994)
*Zhuosen Bao,Xia Du,Zheng Lin,Jizhe Zhou,Zihan Fang,Jiening Wu,Yuxin Zhang,Zhe Chen,Chi-man Pun,Wei Ni,Jun Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SIDeR introduces a semantic decoupling framework that separates machine-recognizable identity features from the visible semantic appearance in faces, using diffusion-model latent-space recomposition to produce visually anonymous yet machine-consistent faces, with momentum-based unrestricted perturbations and a semantic-visual balancing factor, enabling reversible restoration with a password and achieving high black-box attack success and superior restoration PSNR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protect privacy of face data during storage and transmission by decoupling identity information from visual appearance while preserving machine recognition; enable reversible privacy for authorized restoration.

Method: Decompose a facial image into an identity feature vector and a visually perceptible semantic appearance component. Use semantic-guided recomposition in the diffusion model's latent space to generate visually anonymous faces while preserving machine-level identity. Introduce momentum-driven unrestricted perturbation optimization and a semantic-visual balancing factor to synthesize multiple visually diverse, natural adversarial samples. Provide a restoration mechanism via password for authorized access.

Result: Experiments on CelebA-HQ and FFHQ show 99% attack success in black-box scenarios and a 41.28% improvement over baselines in PSNR-based restoration quality; the method maintains machine-level identity consistency and produces high-quality, natural-looking anonymized faces.

Conclusion: SIDeR offers a practical, reversible privacy-protection framework for face data by semantically decoupling identity from appearance and leveraging diffusion-based recomposition to generate diverse anonymized samples with high restoration quality for authorized users.

Abstract: With the deep integration of facial recognition into online banking, identity verification, and other networked services, achieving effective decoupling of identity information from visual representations during image storage and transmission has become a critical challenge for privacy protection. To address this issue, we propose SIDeR, a Semantic decoupling-driven framework for unrestricted face privacy protection. SIDeR decomposes a facial image into a machine-recognizable identity feature vector and a visually perceptible semantic appearance component. By leveraging semantic-guided recomposition in the latent space of a diffusion model, it generates visually anonymous adversarial faces while maintaining machine-level identity consistency. The framework incorporates momentum-driven unrestricted perturbation optimization and a semantic-visual balancing factor to synthesize multiple visually diverse, highly natural adversarial samples. Furthermore, for authorized access, the protected image can be restored to its original form when the correct password is provided. Extensive experiments on the CelebA-HQ and FFHQ datasets demonstrate that SIDeR achieves a 99% attack success rate in black-box scenarios and outperforms baseline methods by 41.28% in PSNR-based restoration quality.

</details>


### [2] [Food Portion Estimation: From Pixels to Calories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05078)
*Gautham Vinod,Fengqing Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A survey of strategies for estimating food portion size from 2D images, reviewing depth maps, multi-view inputs, model-based/template matching, and deep learning with monocular or auxiliary data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Image-based dietary assessment aims to estimate accurate 3D portion sizes from 2D images to support health monitoring and disease prevention; this is hindered by the loss of depth information.

Method: Literature survey and qualitative comparison of strategies for portion estimation, including depth-based, multi-view, model-based/template matching, and deep learning approaches that use monocular images or combine auxiliary inputs.

Result: No single method dominates; each approach has trade-offs. Deep learning can improve estimates when combined with depth or auxiliary data; multi-view/depth approaches yield higher accuracy but require more data and setup.

Conclusion: Progress is promising but requires integrated, practical systems, standardized benchmarks, and data-efficient fusion of depth/multi-view with learning-based models for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Reliance on images for dietary assessment is an important strategy to accurately and conveniently monitor an individual's health, making it a vital mechanism in the prevention and care of chronic diseases and obesity. However, image-based dietary assessment suffers from estimating the three dimensional size of food from 2D image inputs. Many strategies have been devised to overcome this critical limitation such as the use of auxiliary inputs like depth maps, multi-view inputs, or model-based approaches such as template matching. Deep learning also helps bridge the gap by either using monocular images or combinations of the image and the auxillary inputs to precisely predict the output portion from the image input. In this paper, we explore the different strategies employed for accurate portion estimation.

</details>


### [3] [Visual concept ranking uncovers medical shortcuts used by large multimodal models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05096)
*Joseph D. Janizek,Sonnet Xu,Junayd Lateef,Roxana Daneshjou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Visual Concept Ranking (VCR) to audit large multimodal models (LMMs) on medical tasks by identifying influential visual concepts; reveals demographic-subgroup performance gaps; validates hypotheses via manual interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensure reliability and safety of ML in medicine by diagnosing model shortcomings and understanding concept-level dependencies in multimodal prompts.

Method: Compute and rank the influence of visual concepts within LMM prompts; apply to malignant skin lesion classification (plus chest X-ray and natural images); generate testable hypotheses about visual-feature dependencies; validate through targeted prompt/intervention experiments.

Result: Uncovered unexpected gaps in performance across demographic subgroups when demonstrations are used; VCR yields hypotheses about visual-feature dependencies; some hypotheses validated via manual interventions, demonstrating explainability and auditing utility.

Conclusion: VCR provides a practical auditing framework for LMMs in medical settings, enabling diagnose-and-intervene workflows to improve reliability and fairness.

Abstract: Ensuring the reliability of machine learning models in safety-critical domains such as healthcare requires auditing methods that can uncover model shortcomings. We introduce a method for identifying important visual concepts within large multimodal models (LMMs) and use it to investigate the behaviors these models exhibit when prompted with medical tasks. We primarily focus on the task of classifying malignant skin lesions from clinical dermatology images, with supplemental experiments including both chest radiographs and natural images. After showing how LMMs display unexpected gaps in performance between different demographic subgroups when prompted with demonstrating examples, we apply our method, Visual Concept Ranking (VCR), to these models and prompts. VCR generates hypotheses related to different visual feature dependencies, which we are then able to validate with manual interventions.

</details>


### [4] [CLEAR-HPV: Interpretable Concept Discovery for HPV-Associated Morphology in Whole-Slide Histology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05126)
*Weiyi Qin,Yingci Liu-Swetz,Shiwei Tan,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLEAR-HPV introduces concept-level explainable attention-guided representation for HPV in histopathology; discovers keratinizing, basaloid, and stromal concepts; reduces MIL embeddings from 1536-d to a 10-d concept-fraction vector; backbone-agnostic and generalizes across TCGA-HNSCC, TCGA-CESC, CPTAC-HNSCC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limited interpretability of attention-based MIL in whole-slide histopathology for HPV-related prognosis and treatment response; enable concept discovery without requiring labeled concepts during training.

Method: Restructure the MIL latent space with attention to unsupervisedly discover morphologic concepts; generate spatial concept maps; represent each slide with a compact concept-fraction vector; backbone-agnostic MIL framework.

Result: Concept-fraction vectors preserve predictive information of the original MIL embeddings while reducing dimensionality; consistent generalization across TCGA-HNSCC, TCGA-CESC, and CPTAC-HNSCC.

Conclusion: Provides compact, concept-level interpretability and a general backbone-agnostic framework for attention-based MIL in whole-slide histopathology of HPV-related cancers.

Abstract: Human papillomavirus (HPV) status is a critical determinant of prognosis and treatment response in head and neck and cervical cancers. Although attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) achieves strong slide-level prediction for HPV-related whole-slide histopathology, it provides limited morphologic interpretability. To address this limitation, we introduce Concept-Level Explainable Attention-guided Representation for HPV (CLEAR-HPV), a framework that restructures the MIL latent space using attention to enable concept discovery without requiring concept labels during training. Operating in an attention-weighted latent space, CLEAR-HPV automatically discovers keratinizing, basaloid, and stromal morphologic concepts, generates spatial concept maps, and represents each slide using a compact concept-fraction vector. CLEAR-HPV's concept-fraction vectors preserve the predictive information of the original MIL embeddings while reducing the high-dimensional feature space (e.g., 1536 dimensions) to only 10 interpretable concepts. CLEAR-HPV generalizes consistently across TCGA-HNSCC, TCGA-CESC, and CPTAC-HNSCC, providing compact, concept-level interpretability through a general, backbone-agnostic framework for attention-based MIL models of whole-slide histopathology.

</details>


### [5] [ARGaze: Autoregressive Transformers for Online Egocentric Gaze Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05132)
*Jia Li,Wenjie Zhao,Shijian Deng,Bolin Lai,Yuheng Wu,RUijia Chen,Jon E. Froehlich,Yuhang Zhao,Yapeng Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This work reframes online egocentric gaze estimation as autoregressive sequential prediction, introducing ARGaze which uses a transformer decoder conditioned on current visual features and a bounded history of recent gaze targets to predict the current gaze in a causally-consistent, streaming-friendly way, achieving state-of-the-art results on online benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gaze estimation from first-person video lacks explicit head/eye signals and must infer attention from indirect cues; leveraging temporal continuity of gaze and enabling online, resource-bounded inference can improve accuracy and practicality for AR/assistive applications.

Method: ARGaze uses a vision-conditioned transformer decoder. At each timestep, it predicts the current gaze by conditioning on (i) current visual features and (ii) a fixed-length Gaze Context Window containing recent gaze target estimates. This enforces causality and supports bounded-resource streaming inference, enabling online evaluation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple egocentric benchmarks under online evaluation. Ablation studies indicate autoregressive modeling with bounded gaze history is critical for robustness.

Conclusion: The authors plan to release source code and pre-trained models, highlighting potential impact on real-time AR and assistive technologies, with emphasis on the importance of gaze history autoregression and streaming-friendly design.

Abstract: Online egocentric gaze estimation predicts where a camera wearer is looking from first-person video using only past and current frames, a task essential for augmented reality and assistive technologies. Unlike third-person gaze estimation, this setting lacks explicit head or eye signals, requiring models to infer current visual attention from sparse, indirect cues such as hand-object interactions and salient scene content. We observe that gaze exhibits strong temporal continuity during goal-directed activities: knowing where a person looked recently provides a powerful prior for predicting where they look next. Inspired by vision-conditioned autoregressive decoding in vision-language models, we propose ARGaze, which reformulates gaze estimation as sequential prediction: at each timestep, a transformer decoder predicts current gaze by conditioning on (i) current visual features and (ii) a fixed-length Gaze Context Window of recent gaze target estimates. This design enforces causality and enables bounded-resource streaming inference. We achieve state-of-the-art performance across multiple egocentric benchmarks under online evaluation, with extensive ablations validating that autoregressive modeling with bounded gaze history is critical for robust prediction. We will release our source code and pre-trained models.

</details>


### [6] [AirGlove: Exploring Egocentric 3D Hand Tracking and Appearance Generalization for Sensing Gloves](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05159)
*Wenhui Cui,Ziyi Kou,Chuan Qin,Ergys Ristani,Li Guan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AirGlove improves vision-based hand tracking on sensing gloves by learning glove-aware representations that generalize to new glove designs with limited data, addressing performance degradation of bare-hand models on gloves.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-based hand tracking models trained on bare hands perform poorly on gloved hands due to large appearance gap; practical teleoperation requires robust, glove-agnostic tracking across varied glove designs with limited data.

Method: Introduce AirGlove, a glove-aware representation approach that leverages existing glove data to generalize to unseen glove designs with limited additional data, enabling rapid adaptation of glove-based hand pose models across multiple sensing gloves.

Result: AirGlove generalizes to unseen glove designs and yields significant performance gains over baseline schemes across experiments with multiple sensing gloves.

Conclusion: Glove-aware representation learning can bridge the appearance gap between bare hands and gloves, enabling robust, data-efficient adaptation of vision-based hand tracking to diverse glove designs.

Abstract: Sensing gloves have become important tools for teleoperation and robotic policy learning as they are able to provide rich signals like speed, acceleration and tactile feedback. A common approach to track gloved hands is to directly use the sensor signals (e.g., angular velocity, gravity orientation) to estimate 3D hand poses. However, sensor-based tracking can be restrictive in practice as the accuracy is often impacted by sensor signal and calibration quality. Recent advances in vision-based approaches have achieved strong performance on human hands via large-scale pre-training, but their performance on gloved hands with distinct visual appearances remains underexplored. In this work, we present the first systematic evaluation of vision-based hand tracking models on gloved hands under both zero-shot and fine-tuning setups. Our analysis shows that existing bare-hand models suffer from substantial performance degradation on sensing gloves due to large appearance gap between bare-hand and glove designs. We therefore propose AirGlove, which leverages existing gloves to generalize the learned glove representations towards new gloves with limited data. Experiments with multiple sensing gloves show that AirGlove effectively generalizes the hand pose models to new glove designs and achieves a significant performance boost over the compared schemes.

</details>


### [7] [SHaSaM: Submodular Hard Sample Mining for Fair Facial Attribute Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05162)
*Anay Majee,Rishabh Iyer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage submodular hard-sample mining framework (SHaSaM) for fair representation learning, combining hard-sample mining (SHaSaM-MINE) and a Submodular Conditional Mutual Information-based loss (SHaSaM-LEARN) to improve fairness (Equalized Odds) and accuracy on CelebA/UTKFace with faster convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate data imbalance across sensitive attribute groups and avoid model over-reliance on sensitive features during training; improve fairness without sacrificing performance.

Method: Stage 1 SHaSaM-MINE applies submodular subset selection to identify hard positives/negatives. Stage 2 SHaSaM-LEARN uses a family of combinatorial losses based on Submodular Conditional Mutual Information to maximize target-class decision boundary while minimizing sensitivity to protected attributes.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art fairness and accuracy on CelebA and UTKFace; up to 2.7-point gain in Equalized Odds and 3.5% accuracy improvement; faster convergence in fewer epochs than baseline methods.

Conclusion: A unified combinatorial objective that curbs learning of sensitive attributes while preserving or improving performance, demonstrating effectiveness across facial attribute datasets.

Abstract: Deep neural networks often inherit social and demographic biases from annotated data during model training, leading to unfair predictions, especially in the presence of sensitive attributes like race, age, gender etc. Existing methods fall prey to the inherent data imbalance between attribute groups and inadvertently emphasize on sensitive attributes, worsening unfairness and performance. To surmount these challenges, we propose SHaSaM (Submodular Hard Sample Mining), a novel combinatorial approach that models fairness-driven representation learning as a submodular hard-sample mining problem. Our two-stage approach comprises of SHaSaM-MINE, which introduces a submodular subset selection strategy to mine hard positives and negatives - effectively mitigating data imbalance, and SHaSaM-LEARN, which introduces a family of combinatorial loss functions based on Submodular Conditional Mutual Information to maximize the decision boundary between target classes while minimizing the influence of sensitive attributes. This unified formulation restricts the model from learning features tied to sensitive attributes, significantly enhancing fairness without sacrificing performance. Experiments on CelebA and UTKFace demonstrate that SHaSaM achieves state-of-the-art results, with up to 2.7 points improvement in model fairness (Equalized Odds) and a 3.5% gain in Accuracy, within fewer epochs as compared to existing methods.

</details>


### [8] [LOBSTgER-enhance: an underwater image enhancement pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05163)
*Andreas Mentzelopoulos,Keith Ellenbogen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based image-to-image restoration for underwater images using a synthetic degradation pipeline, trained on ~2.5k high-quality images, producing 512×768 outputs with ~11M parameters and strong perceptual generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater imagery suffers from reduced contrast, spatial blur, and wavelength-dependent color distortions, which require heavy post-processing; aim to learn to reverse degradations with limited data.

Method: Introduce a synthetic corruption pipeline that mimics underwater degradations and train a diffusion-based image-to-image model to revert these corruptions; training from scratch on ~2.5k high-quality awareness photography images; outputs at 512×768 with a model of ~11M parameters.

Result: The approach achieves high perceptual consistency and strong generalization in restoring underwater imagery on the target resolution and dataset size.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of diffusion-based restoration for underwater photography with limited data and a compact model, enabling practical restoration with modest computational resources.

Abstract: Underwater photography presents significant inherent challenges including reduced contrast, spatial blur, and wavelength-dependent color distortions. These effects can obscure the vibrancy of marine life and awareness photographers in particular are often challenged with heavy post-processing pipelines to correct for these distortions.
  We develop an image-to-image pipeline that learns to reverse underwater degradations by introducing a synthetic corruption pipeline and learning to reverse its effects with diffusion-based generation. Training and evaluation are performed on a small high-quality dataset of awareness photography images by Keith Ellenbogen. The proposed methodology achieves high perceptual consistency and strong generalization in synthesizing 512x768 images using a model of ~11M parameters after training from scratch on ~2.5k images.

</details>


### [9] [ShapePuri: Shape Guided and Appearance Generalized Adversarial Purification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05175)
*Zhe Li,Bernhard Kainz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ShapePuri combines a Shape Encoding Module (SEM) using Signed Distance Functions with a Global Appearance Debiasing (GAD) module to create a robust defense against adversarial attacks. It achieves strong accuracy on clean and adversarial data (84.06% clean, 81.64% robust under AutoAttack) and claims no extra inference cost, surpassing 80% robustness for the first time on this benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial vulnerability of deep networks and limitations of diffusion-based purification (high computational cost and information loss). The work seeks a scalable, efficient defense that preserves prediction stability without heavy inference overhead.

Method: Two components: SEM provides dense geometric guidance via Signed Distance Functions to align representations with stable structural invariants; GAD applies stochastic transformations to mitigate appearance bias. The framework is presented as not requiring auxiliary modules or additional computational cost at inference.

Result: Empirical results show 84.06% clean accuracy and 81.64% robust accuracy under AutoAttack, surpassing the 80% robustness threshold on this benchmark. Claims to be scalable and efficient, maintaining stability during inference.

Conclusion: ShapePuri offers a novel, geometry-guided purification approach that improves robustness while maintaining accuracy and efficiency, suggesting promising directions for scalable defenses against adversarial attacks. Further evaluation details would clarify generalization and computational implications.

Abstract: Deep neural networks demonstrate impressive performance in visual recognition, but they remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks that is imperceptible to the human. Although existing defense strategies such as adversarial training and purification have achieved progress, diffusion-based purification often involves high computational costs and information loss. To address these challenges, we introduce Shape Guided Purification (ShapePuri), a novel defense framework enhances robustness by aligning model representations with stable structural invariants. ShapePuri integrates two components: a Shape Encoding Module (SEM) that provides dense geometric guidance through Signed Distance Functions (SDF), and a Global Appearance Debiasing (GAD) module that mitigates appearance bias via stochastic transformations. In our experiments, ShapePuri achieves $84.06\%$ clean accuracy and $81.64\%$ robust accuracy under the AutoAttack protocol, representing the first defense framework to surpass the $80\%$ threshold on this benchmark. Our approach provides a scalable and efficient adversarial defense that preserves prediction stability during inference without requiring auxiliary modules or additional computational cost.

</details>


### [10] [PoseGaussian: Pose-Driven Novel View Synthesis for Robust 3D Human Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05190)
*Ju Shen,Chen Chen,Tam V. Nguyen,Vijayan K. Asari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PoseGaussian is a pose-guided Gaussian Splatting framework for high-fidelity, real-time dynamic human novel view synthesis, embedding pose into both geometry and temporal modules to improve robustness and temporal consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address articulated motion and severe self-occlusion in dynamic human scenes by leveraging pose signals as both a structural prior for depth estimation and a temporal cue for consistency, within a fully differentiable end-to-end pipeline.

Method: Integrates pose signals into two pathways within a Gaussian Splatting framework: (1) a structural prior fused with a color encoder to refine depth estimation; (2) a dedicated pose encoder to enforce temporal consistency. The pose is embedded into both geometric and temporal stages, unlike prior works that use pose only as conditioning or for warping. The system is end-to-end trainable and real-time.

Result: Real-time rendering at 100 FPS with state-of-the-art perceptual quality and structural accuracy (PSNR 30.86, SSIM 0.979, LPIPS 0.028) validated on ZJU-MoCap, THuman2.0, and in-house datasets.

Conclusion: PoseGaussian effectively leverages pose signals in both geometry and temporal pathways to enhance robustness, generalization, and efficiency in dynamic human novel view synthesis, achieving high fidelity at real-time speeds across multiple datasets.

Abstract: We propose PoseGaussian, a pose-guided Gaussian Splatting framework for high-fidelity human novel view synthesis. Human body pose serves a dual purpose in our design: as a structural prior, it is fused with a color encoder to refine depth estimation; as a temporal cue, it is processed by a dedicated pose encoder to enhance temporal consistency across frames. These components are integrated into a fully differentiable, end-to-end trainable pipeline. Unlike prior works that use pose only as a condition or for warping, PoseGaussian embeds pose signals into both geometric and temporal stages to improve robustness and generalization. It is specifically designed to address challenges inherent in dynamic human scenes, such as articulated motion and severe self-occlusion. Notably, our framework achieves real-time rendering at 100 FPS, maintaining the efficiency of standard Gaussian Splatting pipelines. We validate our approach on ZJU-MoCap, THuman2.0, and in-house datasets, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in perceptual quality and structural accuracy (PSNR 30.86, SSIM 0.979, LPIPS 0.028).

</details>


### [11] [GT-SVJ: Generative-Transformer-Based Self-Supervised Video Judge For Efficient Video Reward Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05202)
*Shivanshu Shekhar,Uttaran Bhattacharya,Raghavendra Addanki,Mehrab Tanjim,Somdeb Sarkhel,Tong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Repurposes state-of-the-art video generative models as temporally-aware energy-based reward evaluators, introducing a Generative-Transformer-based Self-Supervised Video Judge. Trains with contrastive objectives using synthetic latent-space perturbations to yield robust temporal quality assessment. Achieves SOTA with substantially fewer human annotations (30k) on GenAI-Bench and MonteBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLM-based reward models struggle to capture temporal dynamics essential for video quality alignment. Generative video models intrinsically model temporal structure, offering a rich basis for temporally-aware evaluation. Reformulating generative models as EBMs allows low energy for high-quality videos and high energy for degraded ones, potentially improving discrimination beyond static VLM cues.

Method: Reformulate video generative models as energy-based models (EBMs) that assign low energy to high-quality videos. Train via contrastive objectives to distinguish real/high-quality videos from negatives. Generate challenging synthetic negatives by latent-space perturbations: temporal slicing, feature swapping, and frame shuffling to simulate realistic temporal degradations. Use only 30k human annotations.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on GenAI-Bench and MonteBench, with 6× to 65× fewer annotations than existing VLM-based approaches.

Conclusion: Temporally-aware reward modeling can be effectively achieved by repurposing generative video models as EBMs and training with targeted, temporally-corrupt negatives. This yields high-quality alignment with human preferences while markedly reducing supervision needs.

Abstract: Aligning video generative models with human preferences remains challenging: current approaches rely on Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for reward modeling, but these models struggle to capture subtle temporal dynamics. We propose a fundamentally different approach: repurposing video generative models, which are inherently designed to model temporal structure, as reward models. We present the Generative-Transformer-based Self-Supervised Video Judge (\modelname), a novel evaluation model that transforms state-of-the-art video generation models into powerful temporally-aware reward models. Our key insight is that generative models can be reformulated as energy-based models (EBMs) that assign low energy to high-quality videos and high energy to degraded ones, enabling them to discriminate video quality with remarkable precision when trained via contrastive objectives. To prevent the model from exploiting superficial differences between real and generated videos, we design challenging synthetic negative videos through controlled latent-space perturbations: temporal slicing, feature swapping, and frame shuffling, which simulate realistic but subtle visual degradations. This forces the model to learn meaningful spatiotemporal features rather than trivial artifacts. \modelname achieves state-of-the-art performance on GenAI-Bench and MonteBench using only 30K human-annotations: $6\times$ to $65\times$ fewer than existing VLM-based approaches.

</details>


### [12] [Dual-Representation Image Compression at Ultra-Low Bitrates via Explicit Semantics and Implicit Textures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05213)
*Chuqin Zhou,Xiaoyue Ling,Yunuo Chen,Jincheng Dai,Guo Lu,Wenjun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free unified framework that blends explicit high-level semantics with implicit diffusion-based details for ultra-low bitrate compression; uses reverse-channel coding and a plug-in encoder to control distortion-perception; achieves state-of-the-art rate-perception gains, outperforming DiffC on Kodak, DIV2K, and CLIC2020.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ultra-low bitrate neural codecs suffer from a trade-off between semantic fidelity and perceptual realism. Explicit representations preserve structure but lack texture; implicit methods add texture at risk of semantic drift. A unified, training-free approach is needed to bridge this gap.

Method: A diffusion model conditioned on explicit high-level semantics is used, with reverse-channel coding to implicitly convey fine-grained details. A plug-in encoder enables flexible control of the distortion-perception trade-off by modulating implicit information; the approach is training-free.

Result: State-of-the-art rate-perception performance on standard benchmarks, with DISTS BD-Rate improvements of 29.92% (Kodak), 19.33% (DIV2K), and 20.89% (CLIC2020) over DiffC.

Conclusion: The framework coherently integrates explicit and implicit representations in a training-free manner, mitigating the semantic-perception trade-off and delivering strong rate-perception performance across datasets.

Abstract: While recent neural codecs achieve strong performance at low bitrates when optimized for perceptual quality, their effectiveness deteriorates significantly under ultra-low bitrate conditions. To mitigate this, generative compression methods leveraging semantic priors from pretrained models have emerged as a promising paradigm. However, existing approaches are fundamentally constrained by a tradeoff between semantic faithfulness and perceptual realism. Methods based on explicit representations preserve content structure but often lack fine-grained textures, whereas implicit methods can synthesize visually plausible details at the cost of semantic drift. In this work, we propose a unified framework that bridges this gap by coherently integrating explicit and implicit representations in a training-free manner. Specifically, We condition a diffusion model on explicit high-level semantics while employing reverse-channel coding to implicitly convey fine-grained details. Moreover, we introduce a plug-in encoder that enables flexible control of the distortion-perception tradeoff by modulating the implicit information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves state-of-the-art rate-perception performance, outperforming existing methods and surpassing DiffC by 29.92%, 19.33%, and 20.89% in DISTS BD-Rate on the Kodak, DIV2K, and CLIC2020 datasets, respectively.

</details>


### [13] [E.M.Ground: A Temporal Grounding Vid-LLM with Holistic Event Perception and Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05215)
*Jiahao Nie,Wenbin An,Gongjie Zhang,Yicheng Xu,Yap-Peng Tan,Alex C. Kot,Shijian Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: E.M.Ground is a Vid-LLM for Temporal Video Grounding that uses a dedicated <event> token to capture semantic continuity, applies Savitzky-Golay smoothing to frame-token similarities, and employs multi-grained frame feature aggregation to improve temporal localization and robustness to compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current TVG methods rely on separate start/end tokens and frame-by-frame matching, which isolates events and misses semantic continuity, causing ambiguous localization. A holistic, noise-robust event-centric approach is needed.

Method: (i) Introduces a special <event> token that aggregates information from all frames of a query event to preserve semantic continuity; (ii) applies Savitzky-Golay smoothing to the token-to-frame similarity sequence to reduce noise; (iii) uses multi-grained frame feature aggregation to enhance matching reliability and counter information loss from compression.

Result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show that E.M.Ground consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Vid-LLMs by significant margins.

Conclusion: The proposed event-centric, smoothing-aware, multi-grained approach improves semantic continuity and temporal understanding in TVG, yielding robust and accurate localization across datasets.

Abstract: Despite recent advances in Video Large Language Models (Vid-LLMs), Temporal Video Grounding (TVG), which aims to precisely localize time segments corresponding to query events, remains a significant challenge. Existing methods often match start and end frames by comparing frame features with two separate tokens, relying heavily on exact timestamps. However, this approach fails to capture the event's semantic continuity and integrity, leading to ambiguities. To address this, we propose E.M.Ground, a novel Vid-LLM for TVG that focuses on holistic and coherent event perception. E.M.Ground introduces three key innovations: (i) a special <event> token that aggregates information from all frames of a query event, preserving semantic continuity for accurate event matching; (ii) Savitzky-Golay smoothing to reduce noise in token-to-frame similarities across timestamps, improving prediction accuracy; (iii) multi-grained frame feature aggregation to enhance matching reliability and temporal understanding, compensating for compression-induced information loss. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets show that E.M.Ground consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Vid-LLMs by significant margins.

</details>


### [14] [Cross-Domain Few-Shot Segmentation via Multi-view Progressive Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05217)
*Jiahao Nie,Guanqiao Fu,Wenbin An,Yap-Peng Tan,Alex C. Kot,Shijian Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Multi-view Progressive Adaptation (MPA) for cross-domain few-shot segmentation, progressively adapting few-shot capability to target domains via data-centric augmentation and strategy-centric multi-view prediction, achieving large gains over state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited target-domain data and large domain gaps that hinder adaptation when transferring few-shot segmentation from a source domain. Target weak initial few-shot capability and domain shift degrade performance.

Method: Data perspective: Hybrid Progressive Augmentation—gradually generates more diverse and complex views through cumulative strong augmentations to create harder learning scenarios. Strategy perspective: Dual-chain Multi-view Prediction—utilizes sequential and parallel learning paths to exploit progressively complex views under extensive supervision, enforcing prediction consistency across views.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows that MPA effectively adapts few-shot capability to target domains and outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a large margin (≈+7.0%).

Conclusion: Joint data- and strategy-driven progressive adaptation yields robust and accurate cross-domain few-shot segmentation by leveraging diverse and progressively complex views and enforcing cross-view consistency.

Abstract: Cross-Domain Few-Shot Segmentation aims to segment categories in data-scarce domains conditioned on a few exemplars. Typical methods first establish few-shot capability in a large-scale source domain and then adapt it to target domains. However, due to the limited quantity and diversity of target samples, existing methods still exhibit constrained performance. Moreover, the source-trained model's initially weak few-shot capability in target domains, coupled with substantial domain gaps, severely hinders the effective utilization of target samples and further impedes adaptation. To this end, we propose Multi-view Progressive Adaptation, which progressively adapts few-shot capability to target domains from both data and strategy perspectives. (i) From the data perspective, we introduce Hybrid Progressive Augmentation, which progressively generates more diverse and complex views through cumulative strong augmentations, thereby creating increasingly challenging learning scenarios. (ii) From the strategy perspective, we design Dual-chain Multi-view Prediction, which fully leverages these progressively complex views through sequential and parallel learning paths under extensive supervision. By jointly enforcing prediction consistency across diverse and complex views, MPA achieves both robust and accurate adaptation to target domains. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MPA effectively adapts few-shot capability to target domains, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by a large margin (+7.0%).

</details>


### [15] [Boosting SAM for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Segmentation via Conditional Point Sparsification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05218)
*Jiahao Nie,Yun Xing,Wenbin An,Qingsong Zhao,Jiawei Shao,Yap-Peng Tan,Alex C. Kot,Shijian Lu,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Conditional Point Sparsification (CPS), a training-free method that adaptively sparsifies SAM's dense point prompts for cross-domain few-shot segmentation using reference exemplars and ground-truth masks, improving accuracy under domain shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense point prompts from SAM underperform in Cross-Domain FSS due to domain shifts between source/reference and target images; point density critically affects prompt effectiveness in CD-FSS.

Method: Introduce CPS: a training-free approach that uses ground-truth masks in reference images to adaptively sparsify matched dense points guiding SAM interactions conditioned on reference exemplars.

Result: Extensive experiments show CPS surpasses existing training-free SAM-based methods on diverse CD-FSS datasets.

Conclusion: Adaptive, reference-guided point sparsification effectively mitigates domain shift in SAM-based CD-FSS without training, enabling more reliable cross-domain segmentation.

Abstract: Motivated by the success of the Segment Anything Model (SAM) in promptable segmentation, recent studies leverage SAM to develop training-free solutions for few-shot segmentation, which aims to predict object masks in the target image based on a few reference exemplars. These SAM-based methods typically rely on point matching between reference and target images and use the matched dense points as prompts for mask prediction. However, we observe that dense points perform poorly in Cross-Domain Few-Shot Segmentation (CD-FSS), where target images are from medical or satellite domains. We attribute this issue to large domain shifts that disrupt the point-image interactions learned by SAM, and find that point density plays a crucial role under such conditions. To address this challenge, we propose Conditional Point Sparsification (CPS), a training-free approach that adaptively guides SAM interactions for cross-domain images based on reference exemplars. Leveraging ground-truth masks, the reference images provide reliable guidance for adaptively sparsifying dense matched points, enabling more accurate segmentation results. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CPS outperforms existing training-free SAM-based methods across diverse CD-FSS datasets.

</details>


### [16] [PatchFlow: Leveraging a Flow-Based Model with Patch Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05238)
*Boxiang Zhang,Baijian Yang,Xiaoming Wang,Corey Vian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel unsupervised/one-class anomaly-detection method for die-casting surface defects that fuses local patch features with a normalizing flow, enhanced by an adapter bridging pretrained features to industrial images; achieves state-of-the-art AUROC on MVTec AD and VisA and strong proprietary-dataset performance without anomalous training samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Surface defects in die casting hinder quality control; existing pretrained features do not align well with industrial product images; need an efficient, accurate, and data-efficient anomaly detection approach that can operate with normal (non-anomalous) training data.

Method: Patch-based, local neighbor-aware feature extraction combined with a normalizing flow model. An adapter module aligns generic pretrained features with industrial die-casting images to improve efficiency and accuracy, enabling effective anomaly detection with limited anomalous data.

Result: On MVTec AD, image-level AUROC = 99.28% with a 20% reduction in error rate over state-of-the-art. On VisA, image-level AUROC = 96.48% (28.2% error reduction). Proprietary die-casting dataset achieves 95.77% accuracy for anomaly detection without anomalous training samples.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates strong potential to advance die-casting inspection through effective integration of local patch representations, normalizing-flow-based density estimation, and adapter-powered bridging of pretrained features to industrial imagery, delivering high accuracy with efficient training data requirements.

Abstract: Die casting plays a crucial role across various industries due to its ability to craft intricate shapes with high precision and smooth surfaces. However, surface defects remain a major issue that impedes die casting quality control. Recently, computer vision techniques have been explored to automate and improve defect detection. In this work, we combine local neighbor-aware patch features with a normalizing flow model and bridge the gap between the generic pretrained feature extractor and industrial product images by introducing an adapter module to increase the efficiency and accuracy of automated anomaly detection. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our approach reduces the error rate by 20\% on the MVTec AD dataset, achieving an image-level AUROC of 99.28\%. Our approach has also enhanced performance on the VisA dataset , achieving an image-level AUROC of 96.48\%. Compared to the state-of-the-art models, this represents a 28.2\% reduction in error. Additionally, experiments on a proprietary die casting dataset yield an accuracy of 95.77\% for anomaly detection, without requiring any anomalous samples for training. Our method illustrates the potential of leveraging computer vision and deep learning techniques to advance inspection capabilities for the die casting industry

</details>


### [17] [Active Label Cleaning for Reliable Detection of Electron Dense Deposits in Transmission Electron Microscopy Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05250)
*Jieyun Tan,Shuo Liu,Guibin Zhang,Ziqi Li,Jian Geng,Lei Zhang,Lei Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Active label cleaning using active learning to denoise crowdsourced labels for electron dense deposit detection in glomerular disease, achieving near-expert performance with substantial cost savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scarcity of high-quality labeled data for EDD detection; crowdsourcing reduces annotation costs but introduces label noise. Efficient denoising is needed to enable reliable medical AI with limited expert resources.

Method: An active label cleaning pipeline with a Label Selection Module that (1) selects the most informative noisy samples for expert re-annotation via active learning, and (2) grades instance-level noise by comparing crowdsourced labels with model predictions to guide cleaning. This yields a high-accuracy cleaning model.

Result: On a private dataset, the method achieves 67.18% AP50, an 18.83 percentage point improvement over training on noisy labels, reaching 95.79% of the performance achieved with full expert annotation while reducing annotation cost by 73.30%.

Conclusion: The approach offers a practical, cost-effective solution for building reliable medical AI with limited expert resources by efficiently denoising crowdsourced annotations through active learning and discrepancy-based noise grading.

Abstract: Automated detection of electron dense deposits (EDD) in glomerular disease is hindered by the scarcity of high-quality labeled data. While crowdsourcing reduces annotation cost, it introduces label noise. We propose an active label cleaning method to efficiently denoise crowdsourced datasets. Our approach uses active learning to select the most valuable noisy samples for expert re-annotation, building high-accuracy cleaning models. A Label Selection Module leverages discrepancies between crowdsourced labels and model predictions for both sample selection and instance-level noise grading. Experiments show our method achieves 67.18% AP\textsubscript{50} on a private dataset, an 18.83% improvement over training on noisy labels. This performance reaches 95.79% of that with full expert annotation while reducing annotation cost by 73.30%. The method provides a practical, cost-effective solution for developing reliable medical AI with limited expert resources.

</details>


### [18] [RFM-Pose:Reinforcement-Guided Flow Matching for Fast Category-Level 6D Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05257)
*Diya He,Qingchen Liu,Cong Zhang,Jiahu Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Efficient category-level 6D pose generation via flow-matching along an optimal transport path, with PPO-based refinement to jointly generate and score pose hypotheses; achieves lower computation with competitive accuracy on REAL275 and supports pose tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce high sampling cost and rotational ambiguity in score-based diffusion for category-level pose estimation; enable active hypothesis evaluation.

Method: RFM-Pose uses a flow-matching generative model to generate pose candidates along an optimal transport path from a simple prior to the pose distribution; treats sampling as an MDP and optimizes with PPO; models the flow field as a learnable policy and uses a value network mapping from an estimator; integrates generation and scoring in an RL framework.

Result: On REAL275, achieves favorable performance with significantly reduced computational cost; extends to pose tracking with competitive results.

Conclusion: RFM-Pose offers an efficient, adaptable framework for category-level pose estimation and tracking by unifying flow-based sampling and reinforcement learning to reduce cost while maintaining accuracy; suggests broader applicability in embodied perception tasks.

Abstract: Object pose estimation is a fundamental problem in computer vision and plays a critical role in virtual reality and embodied intelligence, where agents must understand and interact with objects in 3D space. Recently, score based generative models have to some extent solved the rotational symmetry ambiguity problem in category level pose estimation, but their efficiency remains limited by the high sampling cost of score-based diffusion. In this work, we propose a new framework, RFM-Pose, that accelerates category-level 6D object pose generation while actively evaluating sampled hypotheses. To improve sampling efficiency, we adopt a flow-matching generative model and generate pose candidates along an optimal transport path from a simple prior to the pose distribution. To further refine these candidates, we cast the flow-matching sampling process as a Markov decision process and apply proximal policy optimization to fine-tune the sampling policy. In particular, we interpret the flow field as a learnable policy and map an estimator to a value network, enabling joint optimization of pose generation and hypothesis scoring within a reinforcement learning framework. Experiments on the REAL275 benchmark demonstrate that RFM-Pose achieves favorable performance while significantly reducing computational cost. Moreover, similar to prior work, our approach can be readily adapted to object pose tracking and attains competitive results in this setting.

</details>


### [19] [ReGLA: Efficient Receptive-Field Modeling with Gated Linear Attention Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05262)
*Junzhou Li,Manqi Zhao,Yilin Gao,Zhiheng Yu,Yin Li,Dongsheng Jiang,Li Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReGLA is a lightweight hybrid vision model that combines efficient convolutions for local features with ReLU-based gated linear attention for global modeling, featuring the ELRF and RGMA modules and a multi-teacher distillation strategy. It achieves strong high-resolution performance with low latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to balance high accuracy and low latency on high-resolution images, where Transformer-based architectures often incur prohibitive latency; current lightweight models struggle to capture global context efficiently.

Method: Introduce ELRF (efficient large receptive field) to maintain a large receptive field with convolutional efficiency; introduce RGMA (ReLU Gated Modulated Attention) to retain linear complexity while enhancing global modeling; adopt a multi-teacher distillation strategy to boost downstream performance; combine these components into the ReGLA family, with ReGLA-M achieving a representative trade-off.

Result: ReGLA-M attains 80.85% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K at 224px with 4.98 ms latency at 512px; shows +3.1% AP on COCO object detection and +3.6% mIoU on ADE20K semantic segmentation over comparable baselines, outperforming similarly scaled iFormer models.

Conclusion: ReGLA provides a state-of-the-art solution for high-resolution visual tasks, achieving high accuracy with low latency by combining efficient convolutions and a fast, gated attention mechanism, aided by distillation to improve downstream performance.

Abstract: Balancing accuracy and latency on high-resolution images is a critical challenge for lightweight models, particularly for Transformer-based architectures that often suffer from excessive latency. To address this issue, we introduce \textbf{ReGLA}, a series of lightweight hybrid networks, which integrates efficient convolutions for local feature extraction with ReLU-based gated linear attention for global modeling. The design incorporates three key innovations: the Efficient Large Receptive Field (ELRF) module for enhancing convolutional efficiency while preserving a large receptive field; the ReLU Gated Modulated Attention (RGMA) module for maintaining linear complexity while enhancing local feature representation; and a multi-teacher distillation strategy to boost performance on downstream tasks. Extensive experiments validate the superiority of ReGLA; particularly the ReGLA-M achieves \textbf{80.85\%} Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K at $224px$, with only \textbf{4.98 ms} latency at $512px$. Furthermore, ReGLA outperforms similarly scaled iFormer models in downstream tasks, achieving gains of \textbf{3.1\%} AP on COCO object detection and \textbf{3.6\%} mIoU on ADE20K semantic segmentation, establishing it as a state-of-the-art solution for high-resolution visual applications.

</details>


### [20] [Unlocking Prototype Potential: An Efficient Tuning Framework for Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05271)
*Shengqin Jiang,Xiaoran Feng,Yuankai Qi,Haokui Zhang,Renlong Hang,Qingshan Liu,Lina Yao,Quan Z. Sheng,Ming-Hsuan Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Freezing the feature extractor and fine-tuning prototypes with dual-calibration offsets converts static class prototypes into dynamic, learnable entities, improving FSCIL with minimal parameter updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In FSCIL, reliance on frozen backbones and static prototypes causes representation bias and poor adaptation to new classes under extreme data scarcity; optimizing decision regions in the existing high-quality feature space offers a more efficient path to incremental learning.

Method: Propose a prototype fine-tuning framework that keeps the encoder fixed and evolves centroids into dynamic components. Introduce a dual-calibration scheme: class-specific offsets and task-aware offsets to calibrate decision boundaries and adapt to incremental tasks with limited data.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows superior performance on multiple FSCIL benchmarks with a minimal number of learnable parameters.

Conclusion: Reframing FSCIL around prototype optimization in a fixed feature space is effective; dual-calibration offsets enable dynamic decision regions that better support ongoing incremental classes.

Abstract: Few-shot class-incremental learning (FSCIL) seeks to continuously learn new classes from very limited samples while preserving previously acquired knowledge. Traditional methods often utilize a frozen pre-trained feature extractor to generate static class prototypes, which suffer from the inherent representation bias of the backbone. While recent prompt-based tuning methods attempt to adapt the backbone via minimal parameter updates, given the constraint of extreme data scarcity, the model's capacity to assimilate novel information and substantively enhance its global discriminative power is inherently limited. In this paper, we propose a novel shift in perspective: freezing the feature extractor while fine-tuning the prototypes. We argue that the primary challenge in FSCIL is not feature acquisition, but rather the optimization of decision regions within a static, high-quality feature space. To this end, we introduce an efficient prototype fine-tuning framework that evolves static centroids into dynamic, learnable components. The framework employs a dual-calibration method consisting of class-specific and task-aware offsets. These components function synergistically to improve the discriminative capacity of prototypes for ongoing incremental classes. Extensive results demonstrate that our method attains superior performance across multiple benchmarks while requiring minimal learnable parameters.

</details>


### [21] [Magic-MM-Embedding: Towards Visual-Token-Efficient Universal Multimodal Embedding with MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05275)
*Qi Li,Yanzhe Zhao,Yongxin Zhou,Yameng Wang,Yandong Yang,Yuanjia Zhou,Jue Wang,Zuojian Wang,Jinxiang Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Magic-MM-Embedding introduces an efficient multimodal embedding framework combining visual token compression with a multi-stage progressive training regime to achieve state-of-the-art performance and lower inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs show promise for universal multimodal retrieval but are hampered by high computational cost due to large visual token counts; an efficient architecture plus a principled training strategy is needed for practical deployment.

Method: An efficient MLLM architecture with visual token compression, coupled with a multi-stage progressive training pipeline: extensive continue pretraining, large-scale contrastive pretraining with hard negative mining, and task-aware fine-tuning guided by an MLLM-as-a-Judge for data curation.

Result: The model outperforms existing methods by a large margin while being more inference-efficient (lower latency and memory usage).

Conclusion: Magic-MM-Embedding achieves high retrieval accuracy with substantially reduced inference cost, advancing practical universal multimodal embedding for MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown immense promise in universal multimodal retrieval, which aims to find relevant items of various modalities for a given query. But their practical application is often hindered by the substantial computational cost incurred from processing a large number of tokens from visual inputs. In this paper, we propose Magic-MM-Embedding, a series of novel models that achieve both high efficiency and state-of-the-art performance in universal multimodal embedding. Our approach is built on two synergistic pillars: (1) a highly efficient MLLM architecture incorporating visual token compression to drastically reduce inference latency and memory footprint, and (2) a multi-stage progressive training strategy designed to not only recover but significantly boost performance. This coarse-to-fine training paradigm begins with extensive continue pretraining to restore multimodal understanding and generation capabilities, progresses to large-scale contrastive pretraining and hard negative mining to enhance discriminative power, and culminates in a task-aware fine-tuning stage guided by an MLLM-as-a-Judge for precise data curation. Comprehensive experiments show that our model outperforms existing methods by a large margin while being more inference-efficient.

</details>


### [22] [Fast-SAM3D: 3Dfy Anything in Images but Faster](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05293)
*Weilun Feng,Mingqiang Wu,Zhiliang Chen,Chuanguang Yang,Haotong Qin,Yuqi Li,Xiaokun Liu,Guoxin Fan,Zhulin An,Libo Huang,Yulun Zhang,Michele Magno,Yongjun Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Fast-SAM3D is a training-free framework that accelerates SAM3D inference by aligning computation with generation complexity through heterogeneity-aware mechanisms, achieving up to 2.67x end-to-end speedup with minimal fidelity loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM3D enables open-world 3D reconstruction but suffers from prohibitive inference latency. Generic acceleration strategies are brittle due to multi-level heterogeneity in the pipeline: (i) kinematic separation between shape and layout, (ii) sparse texture refinement, and (iii) spectral variance across geometries.

Method: Propose Fast-SAM3D, a training-free framework with three heterogeneity-aware mechanisms: (1) Modality-Aware Step Caching to decouple structural updates from layout updates; (2) Joint Spatiotemporal Token Carving to focus refinement on high-entropy regions; (3) Spectral-Aware Token Aggregation to adapt decoding resolution. The method dynamically matches computation to instantaneous generation complexity.

Result: Extensive experiments show up to 2.67× end-to-end speedup with negligible fidelity loss, establishing a new Pareto frontier for efficient single-view 3D generation; code is released at https://github.com/wlfeng0509/Fast-SAM3D.

Conclusion: Accounting for pipeline heterogeneity enables effective, training-free acceleration of SAM3D inference. The proposed heterogeneity-aware mechanisms offer a practical path to faster, scalable single-view 3D generation without significant quality trade-offs.

Abstract: SAM3D enables scalable, open-world 3D reconstruction from complex scenes, yet its deployment is hindered by prohibitive inference latency. In this work, we conduct the \textbf{first systematic investigation} into its inference dynamics, revealing that generic acceleration strategies are brittle in this context. We demonstrate that these failures stem from neglecting the pipeline's inherent multi-level \textbf{heterogeneity}: the kinematic distinctiveness between shape and layout, the intrinsic sparsity of texture refinement, and the spectral variance across geometries. To address this, we present \textbf{Fast-SAM3D}, a training-free framework that dynamically aligns computation with instantaneous generation complexity. Our approach integrates three heterogeneity-aware mechanisms: (1) \textit{Modality-Aware Step Caching} to decouple structural evolution from sensitive layout updates; (2) \textit{Joint Spatiotemporal Token Carving} to concentrate refinement on high-entropy regions; and (3) \textit{Spectral-Aware Token Aggregation} to adapt decoding resolution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Fast-SAM3D delivers up to \textbf{2.67$\times$} end-to-end speedup with negligible fidelity loss, establishing a new Pareto frontier for efficient single-view 3D generation. Our code is released in https://github.com/wlfeng0509/Fast-SAM3D.

</details>


### [23] [FlashBlock: Attention Caching for Efficient Long-Context Block Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05305)
*Zhuokun Chen,Jianfei Cai,Bohan Zhuang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlashBlock caches cross-step block-external attention in diffusion models to reduce redundant attention computations, achieving up to 1.44x token throughput and up to 1.6x faster attention with negligible quality loss, and is compatible with sparse attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context diffusion models incur high attention costs and expanding KV caches; cross-step redundancy within a block is underexplored and may enable reusing stable attention outputs to accelerate inference without changing the diffusion dynamics.

Method: 1) Empirically observe that attention from tokens outside the current block remains stable across diffusion steps; 2) Propose FlashBlock, a cached block-external attention mechanism that reuses stable outputs; 3) Integrate as a residual reuse strategy that does not modify the diffusion process; 4) Demonstrate orthogonality to sparse attention and combine with sparsification to improve accuracy under aggressive sparsification.

Result: Demonstrates significant efficiency gains: up to 1.44× token throughput and up to 1.6× reduction in attention time, with negligible impact on generation quality; validated on diffusion language models and video generation; compatible with sparse attention methods.

Conclusion: FlashBlock offers a simple, orthogonal caching strategy that leverages cross-block attention stability to reduce computation in long-context diffusion without altering the diffusion process, and can complement sparse attention for additional gains.

Abstract: Generating long-form content, such as minute-long videos and extended texts, is increasingly important for modern generative models. Block diffusion improves inference efficiency via KV caching and block-wise causal inference and has been widely adopted in diffusion language models and video generation. However, in long-context settings, block diffusion still incurs substantial overhead from repeatedly computing attention over a growing KV cache. We identify an underexplored property of block diffusion: cross-step redundancy of attention within a block. Our analysis shows that attention outputs from tokens outside the current block remain largely stable across diffusion steps, while block-internal attention varies significantly. Based on this observation, we propose FlashBlock, a cached block-external attention mechanism that reuses stable attention output, reducing attention computation and KV cache access without modifying the diffusion process. Moreover, FlashBlock is orthogonal to sparse attention and can be combined as a complementary residual reuse strategy, substantially improving model accuracy under aggressive sparsification. Experiments on diffusion language models and video generation demonstrate up to 1.44$\times$ higher token throughput and up to 1.6$\times$ reduction in attention time, with negligible impact on generation quality. Project page: https://caesarhhh.github.io/FlashBlock/.

</details>


### [24] [Wid3R: Wide Field-of-View 3D Reconstruction via Camera Model Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05321)
*Dongki Jung,Jaehoon Choi,Adil Qureshi,Somi Jeong,Dinesh Manocha,Suyong Yeon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Wid3R is a generalizable multi-view 3D reconstruction model for wide FOV cameras (fisheye/panoramic) using a ray representation, spherical harmonics, and a learned camera-model token; claims strong zero-shot robustness and substantial improvements on Stanford2D3D (+77.33).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-view 3D methods largely assume rectified images or pinhole cameras, requiring distortion handling/undistortion and limiting real-world applicability to wide FOV cameras.

Method: Introduce Wid3R with a ray-based representation and spherical harmonics, augmented by a learned camera-model token within a feed-forward network to capture distortion; capable of reconstructing 3D from 360 imagery in a zero-shot, multi-view setting.

Result: Reported state-of-the-art performance with strong zero-shot generalization; up to +77.33 improvement on Stanford2D3D over prior methods.

Conclusion: Wid3R extends multi-view 3D reconstruction to wide FOV and 360 imagery, providing distortion-aware, feed-forward 3D reconstruction and broad applicability across camera models; validation on Stanford2D3D indicates substantial gains.

Abstract: We present Wid3R, a feed-forward neural network for visual geometry reconstruction that supports wide field-of-view camera models. Prior methods typically assume that input images are rectified or captured with pinhole cameras, since both their architectures and training datasets are tailored to perspective images only. These assumptions limit their applicability in real-world scenarios that use fisheye or panoramic cameras and often require careful calibration and undistortion. In contrast, Wid3R is a generalizable multi-view 3D estimation method that can model wide field-of-view camera types. Our approach leverages a ray representation with spherical harmonics and a novel camera model token within the network, enabling distortion-aware 3D reconstruction. Furthermore, Wid3R is the first multi-view foundation model to support feed-forward 3D reconstruction directly from 360 imagery. It demonstrates strong zero-shot robustness and consistently outperforms prior methods, achieving improvements of up to +77.33 on Stanford2D3D.

</details>


### [25] [VISTA: Enhancing Visual Conditioning via Track-Following Preference Optimization in Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05049)
*Yiye Chen,Yanan Jian,Xiaoyi Dong,Shuxin Cao,Jing Wu,Patricio Vela,Benjamin E. Lundell,Dongdong Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training framework to strengthen visual conditioning in Vision-Language-Action models by aligning actions to visual input via track-following preference optimization and latent-space distillation, improving performance on discrete and continuous VLA tasks without new data or architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Action predictions in VLM-based VLA models can be weakly dependent on the current visual state, causing vision-action misalignment and unreliable outputs; empirical observation that successful rollouts show stronger visual dependence.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) preference optimization on a track-following surrogate to align actions with visual input; (2) latent-space distillation during supervised finetuning to transfer the alignment to instruction-following tasks; no architectural changes or extra data.

Result: Improves visual conditioning and task performance for discrete OpenVLA, and yields gains for continuous OpenVLA-OFT.

Conclusion: Explicit visual conditioning improves robustness of VLA models and generalizes across discrete and continuous settings without additional data or architectural modifications.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of robotic manipulation tasks. Despite the success, extending large pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to the action space can induce vision-action misalignment, where action predictions exhibit weak dependence on the current visual state, leading to unreliable action outputs. In this work, we study VLA models through the lens of visual conditioning and empirically show that successful rollouts consistently exhibit stronger visual dependence than failed ones. Motivated by this observation, we propose a training framework that explicitly strengthens visual conditioning in VLA models. Our approach first aligns action prediction with visual input via preference optimization on a track-following surrogate task, and then transfers the enhanced alignment to instruction-following task through latent-space distillation during supervised finetuning. Without introducing architectural modifications or additional data collection, our method improves both visual conditioning and task performance for discrete OpenVLA, and further yields consistent gains when extended to the continuous OpenVLA-OFT setting. Project website: https://vista-vla.github.io/ .

</details>


### [26] [Multimodal Latent Reasoning via Hierarchical Visual Cues Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05359)
*Yiming Zhang,Qiangyu Yan,Borui Jiang,Kai Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HIVE enables grounded, multi-step reasoning in the latent space of multimodal LLMs by injectively incorporating hierarchical visual cues, avoiding reliance on text-based chain-of-thought and using iterative latent refinement via extended transformer blocks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional multimodal LLMs rely on end-to-end generation or textual chain-of-thought, which are inefficient and prone to hallucination. There is a need for robust, multimodal reasoning that operates inside the model's latent space, grounded in rich visual information from global to regional cues.

Method: Introduce HIVE (HIerarchical Visual cuEs injection) that recursively extends transformer blocks to form an internal loop for iterative latent reasoning. It injects hierarchical visual cues—from global scene context to fine-grained regions—directly into latent representations, enabling grounded, multi-step inference entirely in the latent space.

Result: Extensive evaluations show that test-time scaling benefits from incorporating vision knowledge, and that hierarchical information integration significantly enhances understanding of complex scenes in multimodal reasoning.

Conclusion: Latent-space, hierarchically grounded reasoning is feasible and improves multimodal understanding in LLMs, reducing dependence on superficial textual rationales and enabling robust, multi-step inference.

Abstract: The advancement of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) has enabled impressive perception capabilities. However, their reasoning process often remains a "fast thinking" paradigm, reliant on end-to-end generation or explicit, language-centric chains of thought (CoT), which can be inefficient, verbose, and prone to hallucination. This work posits that robust reasoning should evolve within a latent space, integrating multimodal signals seamlessly. We propose multimodal latent reasoning via HIerarchical Visual cuEs injection (\emph{HIVE}), a novel framework that instills deliberate, "slow thinking" without depending on superficial textual rationales. Our method recursively extends transformer blocks, creating an internal loop for iterative reasoning refinement. Crucially, it injectively grounds this process with hierarchical visual cues from global scene context to fine-grained regional details directly into the model's latent representations. This enables the model to perform grounded, multi-step inference entirely in the aligned latent space. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that test-time scaling is effective when incorporating vision knowledge, and that integrating hierarchical information significantly enhances the model's understanding of complex scenes.

</details>


### [27] [Breaking Semantic Hegemony: Decoupling Principal and Residual Subspaces for Generalized OOD Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05360)
*Ningkang Peng,Xiaoqian Peng,Yuhao Zhang,Qianfeng Yu,Feng Xing,Peirong Ma,Xichen Yang,Yi Chen,Tingyu Lu,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Identifies a Simplicity Paradox in SOTA OOD detectors: models are highly sensitive to semantically subtle OOD but exhibit geometric blindness to structurally distinct or noisy (high-frequency) OOD. Proposes D-KNN, a training-free geometric decoupling method that orthogonally separates semantic and structural residuals and calibrates two latent spaces. Achieves SOTA on CIFAR and ImageNet, notably reducing FPR95 (31.3% to 2.3%) and increasing AUROC (79.7% to 94.9%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent feature-based post-hoc OOD detectors excel at detecting subtle semantic shifts but struggle with structurally distinct or high-frequency perturbations. This Simplicity Paradox stems from Semantic Hegemony in the deep feature space, where neural collapse and high-variance principal subspaces mask residual structure signals, hindering detection of distribution shifts that are not semantically aligned with learned features.

Method: D-KNN is a training-free, plug-and-play framework that performs orthogonal decomposition in feature space to separate semantic components from structural residuals. It employs dual-space calibration—adjusting both semantic and residual spaces—to re-sensitize the detector to weak residual signals. It requires no retraining and can be deployed as a modular add-on to existing feature-based OOD detectors.

Result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR and ImageNet show that D-KNN breaks Semantic Hegemony and achieves state-of-the-art OOD detection performance. Quantitatively, FPR95 drops from 31.3% to 2.3% and AUROC improves from 79.7% to 94.9% under sensor-noise scenarios such as Gaussian noise.

Conclusion: The work resolves the Simplicity Paradox by introducing a geometric decoupling approach that makes detectors sensitive to both semantic and weak structural signals, challenging the dominance of semantic hegemonies in neural representations and establishing a practical, training-free path to improved OOD detection.

Abstract: While feature-based post-hoc methods have made significant strides in Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection, we uncover a counter-intuitive Simplicity Paradox in existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) models: these models exhibit keen sensitivity in distinguishing semantically subtle OOD samples but suffer from severe Geometric Blindness when confronting structurally distinct yet semantically simple samples or high-frequency sensor noise. We attribute this phenomenon to Semantic Hegemony within the deep feature space and reveal its mathematical essence through the lens of Neural Collapse. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the spectral concentration bias, induced by the high variance of the principal subspace, numerically masks the structural distribution shift signals that should be significant in the residual subspace. To address this issue, we propose D-KNN, a training-free, plug-and-play geometric decoupling framework. This method utilizes orthogonal decomposition to explicitly separate semantic components from structural residuals and introduces a dual-space calibration mechanism to reactivate the model's sensitivity to weak residual signals. Extensive experiments demonstrate that D-KNN effectively breaks Semantic Hegemony, establishing new SOTA performance on both CIFAR and ImageNet benchmarks. Notably, in resolving the Simplicity Paradox, it reduces the FPR95 from 31.3% to 2.3%; when addressing sensor failures such as Gaussian noise, it boosts the detection performance (AUROC) from a baseline of 79.7% to 94.9%.

</details>


### [28] [SAIL: Self-Amplified Iterative Learning for Diffusion Model Alignment with Minimal Human Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05380)
*Xiaoxuan He,Siming Fu,Wanli Li,Zhiyuan Li,Dacheng Yin,Kang Rong,Fengyun Rao,Bo Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAIL enables diffusion models to self-improve with minimal human feedback by iteratively self-annotating preferences and re-training, achieving strong alignment with only 6% of the usual preference data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the difficulty of aligning diffusion models without reward models and with limited human preference data; exploit latent capabilities of diffusion models to self-teach.

Method: Seed with a small set of human-annotated preferences; iteratively generate diverse samples, self-annotate preferences, and fine-tune the model in a closed-loop. Introduce a ranked preference mixup strategy to balance exploration with adherence to initial priors and prevent catastrophic forgetting.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows SAIL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art across benchmarks while using only 6% of the data typically required, demonstrating that diffusion models can self-improve and substitute for large-scale human annotation and external reward models.

Conclusion: Diffusion models can be effectively aligned with minimal human input using self-generated signals when paired with careful training strategies like ranked mixup; highlights data efficiency and the potential for broader self-supervised alignment approaches; future work may explore failure modes and domain generalization.
