To bridge this critical gap, we propose LocateEdit-Bench, a large-scale dataset comprising $231$K edited images, designed specifically to benchmark localization methods against instruction-driven image editing. Our dataset incorporates four cutting-edge editing models and covers three common edit types. We conduct a detailed analysis of the dataset and develop two multi-metric evaluation protocols to assess existing localization methods. Our work establishes a foundation to keep pace with the evolving landscape of image editing, thereby facilitating the development of effective methods for future forgery localization. Dataset will be open-sourced upon acceptance.

</details>


### [55] [LoGoSeg: Integrating Local and Global Features for Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05578)
*Junyang Chen,Xiangbo Lv,Zhiqiang Kou,Xingdong Sheng,Ning Xu,Yiguo Qiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LoGoSeg is a single-stage open-vocabulary semantic segmentation framework that uses an object existence prior, region-aware alignment, and dual-stream fusion to achieve accurate, proposal-free segmentations without extra backbones or data, showing strong generalization across six benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-vocabulary segmentation suffers from spatial misalignment due to image-level pretraining and lacks strong object priors and region-level constraints, causing hallucinations or missed detections in cluttered scenes.

Method: (i) object existence prior that reweights categories via global image-text similarity; (ii) region-aware alignment for precise region-level visual-text correspondence; (iii) dual-stream fusion integrating local structure with global semantic context; no external mask proposals, backbones, or datasets required.

Result: Extensive experiments on six benchmarks (A-847, PC-459, A-150, PC-59, PAS-20, PAS-20b) show competitive performance and strong generalization in open-vocabulary settings, with improved robustness to ambiguous scenes and clutter.

Conclusion: LoGoSeg provides an efficient, robust solution for OVSS by incorporating object priors, precise region-level alignment, and a dual-stream fusion mechanism in a single-stage pipeline, reducing reliance on external proposals and datasets.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) extends traditional closed-set segmentation by enabling pixel-wise annotation for both seen and unseen categories using arbitrary textual descriptions. While existing methods leverage vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP, their reliance on image-level pretraining often results in imprecise spatial alignment, leading to mismatched segmentations in ambiguous or cluttered scenes. However, most existing approaches lack strong object priors and region-level constraints, which can lead to object hallucination or missed detections, further degrading performance. To address these challenges, we propose LoGoSeg, an efficient single-stage framework that integrates three key innovations: (i) an object existence prior that dynamically weights relevant categories through global image-text similarity, effectively reducing hallucinations; (ii) a region-aware alignment module that establishes precise region-level visual-textual correspondences; and (iii) a dual-stream fusion mechanism that optimally combines local structural information with global semantic context. Unlike prior works, LoGoSeg eliminates the need for external mask proposals, additional backbones, or extra datasets, ensuring efficiency. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks (A-847, PC-459, A-150, PC-59, PAS-20, and PAS-20b) demonstrate its competitive performance and strong generalization in open-vocabulary settings.

</details>


### [56] [Geometric Observability Index: An Operator-Theoretic Framework for Per-Feature Sensitivity, Weak Observability, and Dynamic Effects in SE(3) Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05582)
*Joe-Mei Feng,Sheng-Wei Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An operator-theoretic, geometrically grounded framework (Geometric Observability Index, GOI) for per-feature sensitivity in SE(3) pose estimation, unifying conditioning, Fisher information, influence functions, and dynamic observability in SLAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain how individual image features influence a pose estimate on SE(3) and to address limitations of classical sensitivity tools in dynamic or inconsistent observation settings.

Method: Extend influence function theory to matrix Lie groups; define intrinsic perturbation operator for left-trivialized M-estimators on SE(3); derive the Geometric Observability Index (GOI) via the curvature operator and the Lie algebraic observable subspace; perform spectral decomposition along observable curvature directions; show correspondence with Fisher information geometry in the population limit; explain degeneracies and feature amplification through curvature; provide lightweight, training-free diagnostics integrated into Gauss-Newton SLAM pipelines.

Result: GOI quantifies the per-measurement contribution through the curvature spectrum; its eigen-directions reveal observability strength and sensitivity amplification; in the large-sample limit GOI coincides with the Fisher information geometry on SE(3) and yields a single-measurement analogue of the Cramer-Rao bound; explains pure rotation and vanishing parallax as degeneracies and dynamic-feature amplification via weak curvature directions; enables diagnostic signals for dynamic features and weak observability without altering SLAM architectures.

Conclusion: GOI unifies conditioning analysis, Fisher information geometry, influence function theory, and dynamic scene detectability within a geometrically consistent framework; it provides interpretable spectral quantities that diagnose feature influence and weak observability directly in Gauss-Newton pipelines, facilitating lightweight, training-free monitoring and feature management in SLAM/structure-from-motion.

Abstract: We present a unified operator-theoretic framework for analyzing per-feature sensitivity in camera pose estimation on the Lie group SE(3). Classical sensitivity tools - conditioning analyses, Euclidean perturbation arguments, and Fisher information bounds - do not explain how individual image features influence the pose estimate, nor why dynamic or inconsistent observations can disproportionately distort modern SLAM and structure-from-motion systems. To address this gap, we extend influence function theory to matrix Lie groups and derive an intrinsic perturbation operator for left-trivialized M-estimators on SE(3).
  The resulting Geometric Observability Index (GOI) quantifies the contribution of a single measurement through the curvature operator and the Lie algebraic structure of the observable subspace. GOI admits a spectral decomposition along the principal directions of the observable curvature, revealing a direct correspondence between weak observability and amplified sensitivity. In the population regime, GOI coincides with the Fisher information geometry on SE(3), yielding a single-measurement analogue of the Cramer-Rao bound.
  The same spectral mechanism explains classical degeneracies such as pure rotation and vanishing parallax, as well as dynamic feature amplification along weak curvature directions. Overall, GOI provides a geometrically consistent description of measurement influence that unifies conditioning analysis, Fisher information geometry, influence function theory, and dynamic scene detectability through the spectral geometry of the curvature operator. Because these quantities arise directly within Gauss-Newton pipelines, the curvature spectrum and GOI also yield lightweight, training-free diagnostic signals for identifying dynamic features and detecting weak observability configurations without modifying existing SLAM architectures.

</details>


### [57] [A Mixed Reality System for Robust Manikin Localization in Childbirth Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05588)
*Haojie Cheng,Chang Liu,Abhiram Kanneganti,Mahesh Arjandas Choolani,Arundhati Tushar Gosavi,Eng Tat Khoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A mixed-reality childbirth training system merges virtual guidance with tactile manikin interaction, enabling autonomous practice with real-time virtual overlays; it localizes the manikin and neonatal head using fiducials and RGB-D sensors on a standalone headset. In a large study (n=83), MR training outperformed VR in objective performance and was preferred by trainees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical training for vaginal births is constrained by shortened rotations, patient reluctance, and unpredictable labor. The study aims to reduce instructional burden, preserve authentic haptic feedback, and improve learning efficiency through MR-guided training that does not require constant on-site supervision or external compute.

Method: A mixed-reality system extends HMD passthrough via spatially calibrated external RGB-D camera to fuse real and virtual content. A coarse-to-fine localization pipeline first aligns a maternal manikin to fiducials to define a delivery region, then registers a pre-scanned neonatal head within that region. This enables spatially accurate overlay of virtual guiding hands with haptic feedback. Localization runs on a standalone headset. A large user study with 83 fourth-year medical students compared MR-based to VR-based childbirth training, with four senior obstetricians rating performance using standardized criteria.

Result: MR training yielded significantly higher scores in delivery, post-delivery, and overall task performance compared to VR. Trainees also consistently preferred MR over VR. The system achieved accurate and stable manikin localization on a standalone headset, supporting practical deployment.

Conclusion: MR-based childbirth training can provide superior performance and user preference over VR, with scalable deployment on standalone headsets and no need for continuous external supervision. The approach convincingly demonstrates the fusion of realistic haptics and visual guidance for obstetric skill acquisition.

Abstract: Opportunities for medical students to gain practical experience in vaginal births are increasingly constrained by shortened clinical rotations, patient reluctance, and the unpredictable nature of labour. To alleviate clinicians' instructional burden and enhance trainees' learning efficiency, we introduce a mixed reality (MR) system for childbirth training that combines virtual guidance with tactile manikin interaction, thereby preserving authentic haptic feedback while enabling independent practice without continuous on-site expert supervision. The system extends the passthrough capability of commercial head-mounted displays (HMDs) by spatially calibrating an external RGB-D camera, allowing real-time visual integration of physical training objects. Building on this capability, we implement a coarse-to-fine localization pipeline that first aligns the maternal manikin with fiducial markers to define a delivery region and then registers the pre-scanned neonatal head within this area. This process enables spatially accurate overlay of virtual guiding hands near the manikin, allowing trainees to follow expert trajectories reinforced by haptic interaction. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the system achieves accurate and stable manikin localization on a standalone headset, ensuring practical deployment without external computing resources. A large-scale user study involving 83 fourth-year medical students was subsequently conducted to compare MR-based and virtual reality (VR)-based childbirth training. Four senior obstetricians independently assessed performance using standardized criteria. Results showed that MR training achieved significantly higher scores in delivery, post-delivery, and overall task performance, and was consistently preferred by trainees over VR training.

</details>


### [58] [EgoPoseVR: Spatiotemporal Multi-Modal Reasoning for Egocentric Full-Body Pose in Virtual Reality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05590)
*Haojie Cheng,Shaun Jing Heng Ong,Shaoyu Cai,Aiden Tat Yang Koh,Fuxi Ouyang,Eng Tat Khoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EgoPoseVR integrates headset motion with egocentric RGB-D via a spatiotemporal encoder, cross-attention fusion, and a kinematic optimizer to achieve accurate, temporally coherent full-body pose estimation for VR, validated on a large synthetic dataset and by user studies, outperforming baselines without extra body-worn sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for accurate, temporally stable full-body pose tracking in VR using only headset-captured data and egocentric RGB-D; existing HMD-based methods struggle with temporal instability and lower-body accuracy, and real-time performance is often lacking.

Method: End-to-end framework with dual-modality fusion: a spatiotemporal encoder extracts frame- and joint-level representations; cross-attention fuses HMD motion cues with RGB-D features; a kinematic optimization module imposes constraints from HMD signals; training on a large synthetic dataset (≈1.8M temporally aligned HMD and RGB-D frames) and evaluation including real-world user studies.

Result: EgoPoseVR outperforms state-of-the-art egocentric pose estimation models. Real-world user study reports significantly higher subjective ratings for accuracy, stability, embodiment, and intention for future use compared to baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed dual-modality, end-to-end approach provides robust, practical full-body pose tracking for VR without additional body-worn sensors or room-scale tracking, supported by large-scale synthetic data and user validation; it advances VR embodiment realism and applicability.

Abstract: Immersive virtual reality (VR) applications demand accurate, temporally coherent full-body pose tracking. Recent head-mounted camera-based approaches show promise in egocentric pose estimation, but encounter challenges when applied to VR head-mounted displays (HMDs), including temporal instability, inaccurate lower-body estimation, and the lack of real-time performance. To address these limitations, we present EgoPoseVR, an end-to-end framework for accurate egocentric full-body pose estimation in VR that integrates headset motion cues with egocentric RGB-D observations through a dual-modality fusion pipeline. A spatiotemporal encoder extracts frame- and joint-level representations, which are fused via cross-attention to fully exploit complementary motion cues across modalities. A kinematic optimization module then imposes constraints from HMD signals, enhancing the accuracy and stability of pose estimation. To facilitate training and evaluation, we introduce a large-scale synthetic dataset of over 1.8 million temporally aligned HMD and RGB-D frames across diverse VR scenarios. Experimental results show that EgoPoseVR outperforms state-of-the-art egocentric pose estimation models. A user study in real-world scenes further shows that EgoPoseVR achieved significantly higher subjective ratings in accuracy, stability, embodiment, and intention for future use compared to baseline methods. These results show that EgoPoseVR enables robust full-body pose tracking, offering a practical solution for accurate VR embodiment without requiring additional body-worn sensors or room-scale tracking systems.

</details>


### [59] [CAViT -- Channel-Aware Vision Transformer for Dynamic Feature Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05598)
*Aon Safdar,Mohamed Saadeldin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAViT replaces static MLP-based channel mixing in ViTs with a dynamic, attention-based mechanism (dual-attention) that performs spatial and channel-wise self-attention within each block, yielding better accuracy with fewer parameters/FLOPs on natural and medical datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ViTs rely on fixed, content-agnostic MLPs for channel mixing, limiting representational flexibility. A dynamic, global-context-aware channel interaction could enhance feature recalibration without adding depth or complexity.

Method: In each Transformer block, apply spatial self-attention to capture long-range dependencies, followed by channel-wise self-attention to adapt feature interactions. This dual-attention design replaces static MLPs, enabling content-aware token mixing. The architecture maintains depth and computational budget while enabling dynamic cross-channel recalibration.

Result: On five diverse benchmarks (natural and medical), CAViT surpasses ViT baselines by up to 3.6 percentage points in accuracy and reduces parameters and FLOPs by over 30%. Qualitative attention maps indicate sharper, more semantically meaningful activations.

Conclusion: Dynamic, attention-based channel mixing coupled with spatial attention enhances ViT expressiveness and efficiency. The unified dual-attention token mixing improves performance across domains without added depth, validating the approach.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated strong performance across a range of computer vision tasks by modeling long-range spatial interactions via self-attention. However, channel-wise mixing in ViTs remains static, relying on fixed multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) that lack adaptability to input content. We introduce 'CAViT', a dual-attention architecture that replaces the static MLP with a dynamic, attention-based mechanism for feature interaction. Each Transformer block in CAViT performs spatial self-attention followed by channel-wise self-attention, allowing the model to dynamically recalibrate feature representations based on global image context. This unified and content-aware token mixing strategy enhances representational expressiveness without increasing depth or complexity. We validate CAViT across five benchmark datasets spanning both natural and medical domains, where it outperforms the standard ViT baseline by up to +3.6% in accuracy, while reducing parameter count and FLOPs by over 30%. Qualitative attention maps reveal sharper and semantically meaningful activation patterns, validating the effectiveness of our attention-driven token mixing.

</details>


### [60] [Multi-instance robust fitting for non-classical geometric models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05602)
*Zongliang Zhang,Shuxiang Li,Xingwang Huang,Zongyue Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a multi-instance fitting framework for non-classical models using a model-to-data error estimator and a meta-heuristic optimizer to handle outliers without a fixed threshold.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust fitting has predominantly addressed classical models (lines, circles, planes); there is a gap for non-classical models (spiral curves, procedural character models, free-form surfaces) and for recovering multiple instances from noisy data.

Method: Formulates as an optimization problem with an estimator based on model-to-data error (threshold-free) and a non-differentiable objective that is optimized using a meta-heuristic algorithm to seek the global optimum, enabling multi-instance fitting.

Result: Experimental results on various non-classical models demonstrate effectiveness; code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: The approach provides a viable framework for robust multi-instance fitting of non-classical models, combining a threshold-free model-to-data estimator with meta-heuristic optimization.

Abstract: Most existing robust fitting methods are designed for classical models, such as lines, circles, and planes. In contrast, fewer methods have been developed to robustly handle non-classical models, such as spiral curves, procedural character models, and free-form surfaces. Furthermore, existing methods primarily focus on reconstructing a single instance of a non-classical model. This paper aims to reconstruct multiple instances of non-classical models from noisy data. We formulate this multi-instance fitting task as an optimization problem, which comprises an estimator and an optimizer. Specifically, we propose a novel estimator based on the model-to-data error, capable of handling outliers without a predefined error threshold. Since the proposed estimator is non-differentiable with respect to the model parameters, we employ a meta-heuristic algorithm as the optimizer to seek the global optimum. The effectiveness of our method are demonstrated through experimental results on various non-classical models. The code is available at https://github.com/zhangzongliang/fitting.

</details>


### [61] [Unified Sensor Simulation for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05617)
*Nikolay Patakin,Arsenii Shirokov,Anton Konushin,Dmitry Senushkin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes XSIM, a generalized sensor simulation framework for autonomous driving that extends 3DGUT splatting with phase-aware rolling-shutter modeling and a dual-opacity 3D Gaussian representation, addressing spherical camera edge cases and achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for realistic, unified sensor rendering in autonomous driving that can handle rolling shutter distortions, spherical camera edge cases, and geometry-color consistency in dynamic scenes.

Method: Introduce XSIM; extend 3DGUT splatting with a generalized rolling-shutter model; implement a phase modeling mechanism to handle temporal and shape discontinuities of Gaussians at azimuth borders; propose an extended 3D Gaussian with two opacity parameters; provide a unified appearance/geometry formulation and render complex sensor distortions in dynamic environments; evaluate on Waymo Open Dataset, Argoverse 2, PandaSet; release source code at GitHub.

Result: Empirically, XSIM consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves state-of-the-art results across all evaluated datasets, with improved geometric consistency and photorealistic appearance.

Conclusion: XSIM offers a robust, unified sensor simulation framework for autonomous driving that addresses azimuth boundary discontinuities and geometry-color mismatches, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and providing open-source code.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce \textbf{XSIM}, a sensor simulation framework for autonomous driving. XSIM extends 3DGUT splatting with a generalized rolling-shutter modeling tailored for autonomous driving applications. Our framework provides a unified and flexible formulation for appearance and geometric sensor modeling, enabling rendering of complex sensor distortions in dynamic environments. We identify spherical cameras, such as LiDARs, as a critical edge case for existing 3DGUT splatting due to cyclic projection and time discontinuities at azimuth boundaries leading to incorrect particle projection. To address this issue, we propose a phase modeling mechanism that explicitly accounts temporal and shape discontinuities of Gaussians projected by the Unscented Transform at azimuth borders. In addition, we introduce an extended 3D Gaussian representation that incorporates two distinct opacity parameters to resolve mismatches between geometry and color distributions. As a result, our framework provides enhanced scene representations with improved geometric consistency and photorealistic appearance. We evaluate our framework extensively on multiple autonomous driving datasets, including Waymo Open Dataset, Argoverse 2, and PandaSet. Our framework consistently outperforms strong recent baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance across all datasets. The source code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/whesense/XSIM}{https://github.com/whesense/XSIM}.

</details>


### [62] [ROMAN: Reward-Orchestrated Multi-Head Attention Network for Autonomous Driving System Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05629)
*Jianlei Chi,Yuzhen Wu,Jiaxuan Hou,Xiaodong Zhang,Ming Fan,Suhui Sun,Weijun Dai,Bo Li,Jianguo Sun,Jun Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ROMAN: a high-risk scenario generator for ADS testing using multi-head attention and traffic-law weighting with an LLM-based risk module. Demonstrates higher violation generation and diversity than ABLE/LawBreaker in CARLA/Baidu Apollo tests; claims full clause coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in generating complex, high-risk law-breaking scenarios and capturing interactions among multiple agents and signals to thoroughly test ADS safety and compliance.

Method: A multi-head attention network models interactions among vehicles, signals, and factors; a traffic-law weighting workflow uses an LLM-based risk weighting module to evaluate violations by severity and occurrence, guiding scenario generation.

Result: ROMAN achieved 7.91% higher average violation count than ABLE and 55.96% higher than LawBreaker; improved scenario diversity; reportedly generated violations for every clause of input traffic laws in experiments with Baidu Apollo in CARLA.

Conclusion: ROMAN is a promising approach for targeted ADS testing that enhances violation coverage and scenario diversity; further validation across more ADS platforms and real-world scenarios is warranted.

Abstract: Automated Driving System (ADS) acts as the brain of autonomous vehicles, responsible for their safety and efficiency. Safe deployment requires thorough testing in diverse real-world scenarios and compliance with traffic laws like speed limits, signal obedience, and right-of-way rules. Violations like running red lights or speeding pose severe safety risks. However, current testing approaches face significant challenges: limited ability to generate complex and high-risk law-breaking scenarios, and failing to account for complex interactions involving multiple vehicles and critical situations. To address these challenges, we propose ROMAN, a novel scenario generation approach for ADS testing that combines a multi-head attention network with a traffic law weighting mechanism. ROMAN is designed to generate high-risk violation scenarios to enable more thorough and targeted ADS evaluation. The multi-head attention mechanism models interactions among vehicles, traffic signals, and other factors. The traffic law weighting mechanism implements a workflow that leverages an LLM-based risk weighting module to evaluate violations based on the two dimensions of severity and occurrence. We have evaluated ROMAN by testing the Baidu Apollo ADS within the CARLA simulation platform and conducting extensive experiments to measure its performance. Experimental results demonstrate that ROMAN surpassed state-of-the-art tools ABLE and LawBreaker by achieving 7.91% higher average violation count than ABLE and 55.96% higher than LawBreaker, while also maintaining greater scenario diversity. In addition, only ROMAN successfully generated violation scenarios for every clause of the input traffic laws, enabling it to identify more high-risk violations than existing approaches.

</details>


### [63] [UniSurg: A Video-Native Foundation Model for Universal Understanding of Surgical Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05638)
*Jinlin Wu,Felix Holm,Chuxi Chen,An Wang,Yaxin Hu,Xiaofan Ye,Zelin Zang,Miao Xu,Lihua Zhou,Huai Liao,Danny T. M. Chan,Ming Feng,Wai S. Poon,Hongliang Ren,Dong Yi,Nassir Navab,Gaofeng Meng,Jiebo Luo,Hongbin Liu,Zhen Lei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniSurg is a video-native foundation model for surgical video understanding that shifts from pixel-level reconstruction to latent motion prediction, achieving strong performance on multiple benchmarks with a large-scale pretraining dataset (UniSurg-15M).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-level reconstruction objectives allocate model capacity to low-level visual details in surgical videos, which are often semantically less informative; there is a need for semantically meaningful, motion-centric representations to improve generalization across surgical tasks.

Method: Built on Video Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA) with three innovations: (1) motion-guided latent prediction to emphasize semantically meaningful regions, (2) spatiotemporal affinity self-distillation to enforce relational consistency, and (3) feature diversity regularization to prevent representation collapse in texture-sparse surgical scenes. Trained on UniSurg-15M (3,658 hours, 50 sources, 13 anatomical regions). Evaluated across 17 benchmarks.

Result: Significant gains over state-of-the-art: +14.6% F1 on EgoSurgery, +10.3% on PitVis; 39.54% mAP-IVT on CholecT50 for action triplet recognition; positive impacts on skill assessment, polyp segmentation, and depth estimation.

Conclusion: UniSurg sets a new standard for universal, motion-oriented surgical video understanding by leveraging latent motion prediction and the proposed innovations, demonstrating strong cross-task transfer and scalable pretraining on a large, diverse surgical video corpus.

Abstract: While foundation models have advanced surgical video analysis, current approaches rely predominantly on pixel-level reconstruction objectives that waste model capacity on low-level visual details - such as smoke, specular reflections, and fluid motion - rather than semantic structures essential for surgical understanding. We present UniSurg, a video-native foundation model that shifts the learning paradigm from pixel-level reconstruction to latent motion prediction. Built on the Video Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (V-JEPA), UniSurg introduces three key technical innovations tailored to surgical videos: 1) motion-guided latent prediction to prioritize semantically meaningful regions, 2) spatiotemporal affinity self-distillation to enforce relational consistency, and 3) feature diversity regularization to prevent representation collapse in texture-sparse surgical scenes. To enable large-scale pretraining, we curate UniSurg-15M, the largest surgical video dataset to date, comprising 3,658 hours of video from 50 sources across 13 anatomical regions. Extensive experiments across 17 benchmarks demonstrate that UniSurg significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on surgical workflow recognition (+14.6% F1 on EgoSurgery, +10.3% on PitVis), action triplet recognition (39.54% mAP-IVT on CholecT50), skill assessment, polyp segmentation, and depth estimation. These results establish UniSurg as a new standard for universal, motion-oriented surgical video understanding.

</details>


### [64] [Enhancing Personality Recognition by Comparing the Predictive Power of Traits, Facets, and Nuances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05650)
*Amir Ansari,Jana Subirana,Bruna Silva,Sergio Escalera,David Gallardo-Pujol,Cristina Palmero*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Nuance-level (facets/sub-facets) representations improve audiovisual personality prediction over trait-level ground truth, with up to 74% MSE reduction on UDIVA v0.5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truths based on broad Big-Five traits can mask context-dependent behaviors; richer, hierarchical representations (facets/nuances) may enhance generalization in personality recognition.

Method: Transformer-based model using audiovisual data with cross-modal attention and dyad-aware cross-subject interactions; compares nuance-level, facet-level, and trait-level models on UDIVA v0.5.

Result: Nuance-level models consistently outperform facet- and trait-level models, achieving up to 74% reduction in mean squared error across interaction scenarios.

Conclusion: Hierarchical, nuance-focused representations yield better predictive performance in audiovisual personality recognition, suggesting benefits of modeling personality as a multi-level construct.

Abstract: Personality is a complex, hierarchical construct typically assessed through item-level questionnaires aggregated into broad trait scores. Personality recognition models aim to infer personality traits from different sources of behavioral data. However, reliance on broad trait scores as ground truth, combined with limited training data, poses challenges for generalization, as similar trait scores can manifest through diverse, context dependent behaviors. In this work, we explore the predictive impact of the more granular hierarchical levels of the Big-Five Personality Model, facets and nuances, to enhance personality recognition from audiovisual interaction data. Using the UDIVA v0.5 dataset, we trained a transformer-based model including cross-modal (audiovisual) and cross-subject (dyad-aware) attention mechanisms. Results show that nuance-level models consistently outperform facet and trait-level models, reducing mean squared error by up to 74% across interaction scenarios.

</details>


### [65] [ShapeUP: Scalable Image-Conditioned 3D Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05676)
*Inbar Gat,Dana Cohen-Bar,Guy Levy,Elad Richardson,Daniel Cohen-Or*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ShapeUP is a scalable, image-conditioned 3D editing framework that translates latent representations inside a native 3D model via a 3D Diffusion Transformer, trained on triplets of (source shape, edited image, edited shape) to achieve fine-grained, identity-preserving edits with strong geometric consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 3D editing methods trade off controllability, geometry, and scalability. Optimization-based methods are slow, 2D propagation can drift visually, and training-free priors can't leverage scaling. A scalable, image-aware approach that can utilize a pretrained 3D foundation model is needed.

Method: Formulate editing as supervised latent-to-latent translation within a native 3D representation. Use a 3D Diffusion Transformer (DiT) trained on triplets (source 3D shape, edited 2D image, edited 3D shape). Treat the input image as a prompt to guide local/global edits, achieving implicit localization without masks while preserving structural consistency.

Result: Empirically, ShapeUP outperforms both trained and training-free baselines in identity preservation and edit fidelity across evaluations, demonstrating robustness and scalability for native 3D content creation.

Conclusion: ShapeUP provides a scalable, image-conditioned editing paradigm that leverages a pretrained 3D foundation model through supervised latent-to-latent translation, enabling precise, consistent 3D edits and improved fidelity.

Abstract: Recent advancements in 3D foundation models have enabled the generation of high-fidelity assets, yet precise 3D manipulation remains a significant challenge. Existing 3D editing frameworks often face a difficult trade-off between visual controllability, geometric consistency, and scalability. Specifically, optimization-based methods are prohibitively slow, multi-view 2D propagation techniques suffer from visual drift, and training-free latent manipulation methods are inherently bound by frozen priors and cannot directly benefit from scaling. In this work, we present ShapeUP, a scalable, image-conditioned 3D editing framework that formulates editing as a supervised latent-to-latent translation within a native 3D representation. This formulation allows ShapeUP to build on a pretrained 3D foundation model, leveraging its strong generative prior while adapting it to editing through supervised training. In practice, ShapeUP is trained on triplets consisting of a source 3D shape, an edited 2D image, and the corresponding edited 3D shape, and learns a direct mapping using a 3D Diffusion Transformer (DiT). This image-as-prompt approach enables fine-grained visual control over both local and global edits and achieves implicit, mask-free localization, while maintaining strict structural consistency with the original asset. Our extensive evaluations demonstrate that ShapeUP consistently outperforms current trained and training-free baselines in both identity preservation and edit fidelity, offering a robust and scalable paradigm for native 3D content creation.

</details>


### [66] [Poster: Camera Tampering Detection for Outdoor IoT Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05706)
*Shadi Attarha,Kanaga Shanmugi,Anna Förster*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This work compares rule-based and deep-learning approaches for detecting tampering in still images from outdoor smart cameras, evaluating accuracy, computational requirements, and data needs; deep learning yields higher accuracy, while rule-based methods are better for resource-constrained settings; public tampering datasets (normal, blurred, rotated) are released to support evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outdoor smart cameras face tampering (vandalism, environmental effects) which can degrade surveillance. Detecting tampering in still images is particularly challenging due to the absence of temporal context, creating a need for effective methods and benchmarks.

Method: Two approaches are evaluated: (1) a rule-based tampering detection method using predefined image cues, and (2) a deep-learning-based classifier trained to detect tampered vs. untampered images. The study assesses performance on real-world scenarios in terms of accuracy, computational cost, and data requirements, and provides publicly available datasets comprising normal, blurred, and rotated images.

Result: Deep-learning model achieves higher accuracy in tamper detection. The rule-based method is more suitable for resource-limited environments and scenarios requiring less calibration time. The study also releases public datasets (normal, blurred, rotated) to aid future method development and evaluation.

Conclusion: There is a trade-off between accuracy and resource usage: deep learning offers superior tamper detection performance at the cost of higher computation and data needs, while rule-based methods provide a lightweight alternative suitable for constrained settings with longer calibration intervals. Public datasets help standardize evaluation and foster method development for camera tampering detection.

Abstract: Recently, the use of smart cameras in outdoor settings has grown to improve surveillance and security. Nonetheless, these systems are susceptible to tampering, whether from deliberate vandalism or harsh environmental conditions, which can undermine their monitoring effectiveness. In this context, detecting camera tampering is more challenging when a camera is capturing still images rather than video as there is no sequence of continuous frames over time. In this study, we propose two approaches for detecting tampered images: a rule-based method and a deep-learning-based method. The aim is to evaluate how each method performs in terms of accuracy, computational demands, and the data required for training when applied to real-world scenarios. Our results show that the deep-learning model provides higher accuracy, while the rule-based method is more appropriate for scenarios where resources are limited and a prolonged calibration phase is impractical. We also offer publicly available datasets with normal, blurred, and rotated images to support the development and evaluation of camera tampering detection methods, addressing the need for such resources.

</details>


### [67] [Exploring the Temporal Consistency for Point-Level Weakly-Supervised Temporal Action Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05718)
*Yunchuan Ma,Laiyun Qing,Guorong Li,Yuqing Liu,Yuankai Qi,Qingming Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-task self-supervised framework for point-supervised temporal action localization (PTAL) leveraging temporal consistency via three tasks—Action Completion, Action Order Understanding, and Action Regularity Understanding—to improve localization on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal relations among frames are essential to define and localize actions but are not explicitly modeled in existing point-supervised approaches; exploiting temporal consistency can enhance PTAL performance.

Method: Introduce three self-supervised tasks integrated into a PTAL architecture: (i) Action Completion (predicting missing parts of action sequence), (ii) Action Order Understanding (ordering frames temporally), (iii) Action Regularity Understanding (assessing temporal regularities).

Result: Achieves improvements over state-of-the-art on four benchmark datasets, validating the effectiveness of the proposed temporal-consistency-driven multi-task framework.

Conclusion: First to explicitly explore temporal consistency for point-supervision action localization; the proposed multi-task framework enhances temporal understanding and localization accuracy.

Abstract: Point-supervised Temporal Action Localization (PTAL) adopts a lightly frame-annotated paradigm (\textit{i.e.}, labeling only a single frame per action instance) to train a model to effectively locate action instances within untrimmed videos. Most existing approaches design the task head of models with only a point-supervised snippet-level classification, without explicit modeling of understanding temporal relationships among frames of an action. However, understanding the temporal relationships of frames is crucial because it can help a model understand how an action is defined and therefore benefits localizing the full frames of an action. To this end, in this paper, we design a multi-task learning framework that fully utilizes point supervision to boost the model's temporal understanding capability for action localization. Specifically, we design three self-supervised temporal understanding tasks: (i) Action Completion, (ii) Action Order Understanding, and (iii) Action Regularity Understanding. These tasks help a model understand the temporal consistency of actions across videos. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to explicitly explore temporal consistency for point supervision action localization. Extensive experimental results on four benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared to several state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [68] [Adaptive Global and Fine-Grained Perceptual Fusion for MLLM Embeddings Compatible with Hard Negative Amplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05729)
*Lexiang Hu,Youze Xue,Dian Li,Gang Liu,Zhouchen Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AGFF-Embed: adaptive fusion of multiple MLLM-generated embeddings to capture both global and fine-grained semantics, with EGA-enhanced hard negatives; achieves state-of-the-art on MMEB and MMVP-VLM benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal embeddings (CLIP-based or MLLM-based) mainly capture global semantics. Real-world tasks require a hybrid of global and fine-grained information; a compatible fusion mechanism is needed.

Method: Prompt the MLLM to produce multiple embeddings that emphasize different semantic dimensions; adaptively and smoothly fuse these embeddings. Extend with Explicit Gradient Amplification (EGA) to boost in-batch hard negatives without dataset editing.

Result: On MMEB and MMVP-VLM benchmarks, AGFF-Embed achieves state-of-the-art performance for both general and fine-grained understanding compared to existing multimodal embedding models.

Conclusion: AGFF-Embed provides a unified fusion framework that leverages multi-embedding prompts and adaptive aggregation, with EGA further enhancing hard negative mining, delivering comprehensive improvements across tasks.

Abstract: Multimodal embeddings serve as a bridge for aligning vision and language, with the two primary implementations -- CLIP-based and MLLM-based embedding models -- both limited to capturing only global semantic information. Although numerous studies have focused on fine-grained understanding, we observe that complex scenarios currently targeted by MLLM embeddings often involve a hybrid perceptual pattern of both global and fine-grained elements, thus necessitating a compatible fusion mechanism. In this paper, we propose Adaptive Global and Fine-grained perceptual Fusion for MLLM Embeddings (AGFF-Embed), a method that prompts the MLLM to generate multiple embeddings focusing on different dimensions of semantic information, which are then adaptively and smoothly aggregated. Furthermore, we adapt AGFF-Embed with the Explicit Gradient Amplification (EGA) technique to achieve in-batch hard negatives enhancement without requiring fine-grained editing of the dataset. Evaluation on the MMEB and MMVP-VLM benchmarks shows that AGFF-Embed comprehensively achieves state-of-the-art performance in both general and fine-grained understanding compared to other multimodal embedding models.

</details>


### [69] [Depth as Prior Knowledge for Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05730)
*Moussa Kassem Sbeyti,Nadja Klein*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DepthPrior treats depth as prior information rather than a fused feature to boost small-object detection, avoiding architecture changes. It uses depth-based loss weighting and loss stratification during training, plus depth-aware confidence thresholding at inference. It yields notable gains on small objects across diverse datasets and detectors with minimal overhead (depth estimation).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small and distant objects suffer from scale variation, low resolution, and clutter. Depth information can help but prior approaches require complex, model-specific changes. The authors provide a theoretical analysis of depth-detection relationships and propose a depth-prior framework to leverage depth without altering detectors.

Method: Introduce DepthPrior framework comprising: (1) Depth-Based Loss Weighting (DLW) - weight losses by depth; (2) Depth-Based Loss Stratification (DLS) - stratify training losses by depth regimes; (3) Depth-Aware Confidence Thresholding (DCT) - adjust inference thresholds based on depth; training uses depth estimates as priors rather than features; no architectural modifications.

Result: Empirical results on KITTI, MS COCO, VisDrone, SUN RGB-D with YOLOv11 and EfficientDet show up to +9% mAP_S and +7% mAR_S for small objects. Inference recovery rates reach up to 95:1 (true:false detections). DepthPrior requires depth estimation only at preprocessing, with no sensor/architectural changes or extra runtime costs beyond the depth estimate.

Conclusion: DepthPrior demonstrates that treating depth as a prior can yield substantial gains in small-object detection across multiple benchmarks without modifying detector architectures, offering a practical, sensor-free enhancement route at training and inference time.

Abstract: Detecting small and distant objects remains challenging for object detectors due to scale variation, low resolution, and background clutter. Safety-critical applications require reliable detection of these objects for safe planning. Depth information can improve detection, but existing approaches require complex, model-specific architectural modifications. We provide a theoretical analysis followed by an empirical investigation of the depth-detection relationship. Together, they explain how depth causes systematic performance degradation and why depth-informed supervision mitigates it. We introduce DepthPrior, a framework that uses depth as prior knowledge rather than as a fused feature, providing comparable benefits without modifying detector architectures. DepthPrior consists of Depth-Based Loss Weighting (DLW) and Depth-Based Loss Stratification (DLS) during training, and Depth-Aware Confidence Thresholding (DCT) during inference. The only overhead is the initial cost of depth estimation. Experiments across four benchmarks (KITTI, MS COCO, VisDrone, SUN RGB-D) and two detectors (YOLOv11, EfficientDet) demonstrate the effectiveness of DepthPrior, achieving up to +9% mAP$_S$ and +7% mAR$_S$ for small objects, with inference recovery rates as high as 95:1 (true vs. false detections). DepthPrior offers these benefits without additional sensors, architectural changes, or performance costs. Code is available at https://github.com/mos-ks/DepthPrior.

</details>


### [70] [Neuro-Inspired Visual Pattern Recognition via Biological Reservoir Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05737)
*Luca Ciampi,Ludovico Iannello,Fabrizio Tonelli,Gabriele Lagani,Angelo Di Garbo,Federico Cremisi,Giuseppe Amato*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Demonstrates a biosynthetic reservoir computing system using in vitro cortical networks as the physical reservoir, with high-density multi-electrode stimulation and readout, applying a linear readout to classify static visual patterns (including MNIST) despite biological variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore whether living neural circuits can serve as computational substrates for reservoir computing and visually grounded pattern recognition, bridging neurobiology and neuromorphic ML.

Method: In vitro cultured cortical neuron cultures form a biological reservoir controlled by a high-density multi-electrode array (HD-MEA). Input patterns are delivered via selected electrodes; hundreds of channels record the high-dimensional reservoir states. A linear readout (single-layer perceptron) is trained to classify these states. Tasks progress from simple pointwise stimuli to oriented bars, clock-digit-like shapes, and MNIST digits.

Result: Despite variability from noise, spontaneous activity, and inter-session differences, the living neural reservoir consistently produces high-dimensional representations that enable accurate static visual pattern recognition, validating the feasibility of BRC for vision tasks within a computer-vision framework.

Conclusion: Biological neural networks can function as effective reservoirs for static visual pattern recognition, supporting neuro-inspired approaches to neuromorphic computing and suggesting that living substrates can inform and enhance ML models based on biological principles.

Abstract: In this paper, we present a neuro-inspired approach to reservoir computing (RC) in which a network of in vitro cultured cortical neurons serves as the physical reservoir. Rather than relying on artificial recurrent models to approximate neural dynamics, our biological reservoir computing (BRC) system leverages the spontaneous and stimulus-evoked activity of living neural circuits as its computational substrate. A high-density multi-electrode array (HD-MEA) provides simultaneous stimulation and readout across hundreds of channels: input patterns are delivered through selected electrodes, while the remaining ones capture the resulting high-dimensional neural responses, yielding a biologically grounded feature representation. A linear readout layer (single-layer perceptron) is then trained to classify these reservoir states, enabling the living neural network to perform static visual pattern-recognition tasks within a computer-vision framework. We evaluate the system across a sequence of tasks of increasing difficulty, ranging from pointwise stimuli to oriented bars, clock-digit-like shapes, and handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset. Despite the inherent variability of biological neural responses-arising from noise, spontaneous activity, and inter-session differences-the system consistently generates high-dimensional representations that support accurate classification. These results demonstrate that in vitro cortical networks can function as effective reservoirs for static visual pattern recognition, opening new avenues for integrating living neural substrates into neuromorphic computing frameworks. More broadly, this work contributes to the effort to incorporate biological principles into machine learning and supports the goals of neuro-inspired vision by illustrating how living neural systems can inform the design of efficient and biologically grounded computational models.

</details>


### [71] [FMPose3D: monocular 3D pose estimation via flow matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05755)
*Ti Wang,Xiaohang Yu,Mackenzie Weygandt Mathis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FMPose3D uses Flow Matching with an ODE-based velocity field to efficiently generate multiple 3D pose hypotheses conditioned on 2D inputs, enabling fast inference with few steps and diverse predictions; it integrates a Reprojection-based Posterior Expectation Aggregation to obtain a single accurate estimate, achieving state-of-the-art on human and animal 3D pose benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular 3D pose estimation is ill-posed due to depth ambiguity and occlusions, and diffusion-based methods, while accurate, are computationally expensive due to lengthy denoising chains. A faster generative framework is desired that can produce multiple plausible poses with fewer steps.

Method: Formulates 3D pose estimation as a conditional distribution transport using a velocity field defined by an ODE trained via Flow Matching. Samples are drawn from a standard Gaussian prior and transported to the conditional 3D pose distribution. Diverse hypotheses arise from different noise seeds; a Reprojection-based Posterior Expectation Aggregation (RPEA) module approximates the Bayesian posterior mean to produce a single prediction. Evaluated on Human3.6M, MPI-INF-3DHP, Animal3D, and CtrlAni3D; provides code online.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP in 3D human pose estimation, and achieves state-of-the-art results on Animal3D and CtrlAni3D, indicating strong cross-domain performance and efficiency due to reduced integration steps.

Conclusion: The FM-based transport framework (FMPose3D) delivers accurate, diverse 3D pose hypotheses with significantly fewer integration steps than diffusion models, while the RPEA module yields a robust single estimate; the approach generalizes across both human and animal pose domains and is released with open-source code.

Abstract: Monocular 3D pose estimation is fundamentally ill-posed due to depth ambiguity and occlusions, thereby motivating probabilistic methods that generate multiple plausible 3D pose hypotheses. In particular, diffusion-based models have recently demonstrated strong performance, but their iterative denoising process typically requires many timesteps for each prediction, making inference computationally expensive. In contrast, we leverage Flow Matching (FM) to learn a velocity field defined by an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE), enabling efficient generation of 3D pose samples with only a few integration steps. We propose a novel generative pose estimation framework, FMPose3D, that formulates 3D pose estimation as a conditional distribution transport problem. It continuously transports samples from a standard Gaussian prior to the distribution of plausible 3D poses conditioned only on 2D inputs. Although ODE trajectories are deterministic, FMPose3D naturally generates various pose hypotheses by sampling different noise seeds. To obtain a single accurate prediction from those hypotheses, we further introduce a Reprojection-based Posterior Expectation Aggregation (RPEA) module, which approximates the Bayesian posterior expectation over 3D hypotheses. FMPose3D surpasses existing methods on the widely used human pose estimation benchmarks Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP, and further achieves state-of-the-art performance on the 3D animal pose datasets Animal3D and CtrlAni3D, demonstrating strong performance across both 3D pose domains. The code is available at https://github.com/AdaptiveMotorControlLab/FMPose3D.

</details>


### [72] [ReText: Text Boosts Generalization in Image-Based Person Re-identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05785)
*Timur Mamedov,Karina Kvanchiani,Anton Konushin,Vadim Konushin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Multimodal Re-ID (ReText) trained on mixed multi-camera and single-camera data with text cues, achieving strong cross-domain generalization and surpassing SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalizable image-based person Re-ID suffers from domain gaps; stylistically diverse single-camera data augmented with textual descriptions enriches semantic cues, potentially improving cross-domain generalization. A mixture with multi-camera data leverages cross-view variation.

Method: Jointly train on three tasks: (1) Re-ID on multi-camera data, (2) image-text matching for cross-modal alignment, (3) text-guided image reconstruction on single-camera data with accompanying textual descriptions.

Result: Empirical results show strong generalization to unseen domains and performance surpassing state-of-the-art on cross-domain Re-ID benchmarks.

Conclusion: Multimodal joint learning on a mixture of multi-camera and single-camera data with textual supervision effectively enhances cross-domain Re-ID performance; first work to explore this combination.

Abstract: Generalizable image-based person re-identification (Re-ID) aims to recognize individuals across cameras in unseen domains without retraining. While multiple existing approaches address the domain gap through complex architectures, recent findings indicate that better generalization can be achieved by stylistically diverse single-camera data. Although this data is easy to collect, it lacks complexity due to minimal cross-view variation. We propose ReText, a novel method trained on a mixture of multi-camera Re-ID data and single-camera data, where the latter is complemented by textual descriptions to enrich semantic cues. During training, ReText jointly optimizes three tasks: (1) Re-ID on multi-camera data, (2) image-text matching, and (3) image reconstruction guided by text on single-camera data. Experiments demonstrate that ReText achieves strong generalization and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on cross-domain Re-ID benchmarks. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to explore multimodal joint learning on a mixture of multi-camera and single-camera data in image-based person Re-ID.

</details>


### [73] [Allocentric Perceiver: Disentangling Allocentric Reasoning from Egocentric Visual Priors via Frame Instantiation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05789)
*Hengyi Wang,Ruiqiang Zhang,Chang Liu,Guanjie Wang,Zehua Ma,Han Fang,Weiming Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-freeAllocentric Perceiver recovers metric 3D geometry from images via off-the-shelf geometry engines, builds a query-conditioned allocentric frame, and prompts the VLM with geometry-grounded representations to improve allocentric spatial reasoning while preserving egocentric skills.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle with allocentric spatial queries requiring perspective shifts. An explicit, target-centric frame and geometry grounding can reduce implicit mental rotation and improve robustness on allocentric tasks.

Method: Recover metric 3D states from one or more images using off-the-shelf geometric experts; instantiate a query-conditioned allocentric reference frame aligned with the instruction's semantics; transform recovered geometry into the target frame; prompt the backbone VLM with structured, geometry-grounded representations.

Result: Consistent and substantial gains (~10%) on allocentric tasks across multiple backbone families, while maintaining strong egocentric performance; outperforms spatial-perception-finetuned models and both open-source and proprietary SOTA models.

Conclusion: Allocentric Perciver effectively offloads mental rotation to explicit geometry computation, enabling training-free enhancement of allocentric spatial reasoning in VLMs with broad compatibility and without sacrificing egocentric accuracy.

Abstract: With the rising need for spatially grounded tasks such as Vision-Language Navigation/Action, allocentric perception capabilities in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are receiving growing focus. However, VLMs remain brittle on allocentric spatial queries that require explicit perspective shifts, where the answer depends on reasoning in a target-centric frame rather than the observed camera view. Thus, we introduce Allocentric Perceiver, a training-free strategy that recovers metric 3D states from one or more images with off-the-shelf geometric experts, and then instantiates a query-conditioned allocentric reference frame aligned with the instruction's semantic intent. By deterministically transforming reconstructed geometry into the target frame and prompting the backbone VLM with structured, geometry-grounded representations, Allocentric Perceriver offloads mental rotation from implicit reasoning to explicit computation. We evaluate Allocentric Perciver across multiple backbone families on spatial reasoning benchmarks, observing consistent and substantial gains ($\sim$10%) on allocentric tasks while maintaining strong egocentric performance, and surpassing both spatial-perception-finetuned models and state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary models.

</details>


### [74] [Focus-Scan-Refine: From Human Visual Perception to Efficient Visual Token Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05809)
*Enwei Tong,Yuanchao Bai,Yao Zhu,Junjun Jiang,Xianming Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FSR is a training-free, plug-and-play token-pruning framework for vision-language models that improves accuracy-efficiency under aggressive compression by mimicking focus-scan-refine behavior to preserve useful evidence while capturing global context without increasing token budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language models deploy massive visual tokens, leading to high latency/memory. Existing pruning struggles to balance local evidence and global context, especially under aggressive compression; need training-free methods that avoid bias toward visually salient but query-irrelevant regions.

Method: Three-stage process: (1) Focus: merge visual importance with instruction relevance to select key evidence and reduce bias toward irrelevant regions; (2) Scan: conditioned on focused tokens, select complementary tokens that differ from focused evidence to broaden context; (3) Refine: merge nearby informative tokens into scan anchors using similarity-based assignment and score-weighted merging to improve context without increasing token budget.

Result: Empirical results across multiple VLM backbones and vision-language benchmarks show FSR consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off versus existing state-of-the-art pruning methods.

Conclusion: FSR provides a practical, generalizable, training-free pruning framework that enhances performance under aggressive token pruning; its human-inspired focus-scan-refine paradigm yields better local/global evidence balance and can be readily adopted across VLMs.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) often generate massive visual tokens that greatly increase inference latency and memory footprint; while training-free token pruning offers a practical remedy, existing methods still struggle to balance local evidence and global context under aggressive compression. We propose Focus-Scan-Refine (FSR), a human-inspired, plug-and-play pruning framework that mimics how humans answer visual questions: focus on key evidence, then scan globally if needed, and refine the scanned context by aggregating relevant details. FSR first focuses on key evidence by combining visual importance with instruction relevance, avoiding the bias toward visually salient but query-irrelevant regions. It then scans for complementary context conditioned on the focused set, selecting tokens that are most different from the focused evidence. Finally, FSR refines the scanned context by aggregating nearby informative tokens into the scan anchors via similarity-based assignment and score-weighted merging, without increasing the token budget. Extensive experiments across multiple VLM backbones and vision-language benchmarks show that FSR consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off over existing state-of-the-art pruning methods. The source codes can be found at https://github.com/ILOT-code/FSR

</details>


### [75] [NVS-HO: A Benchmark for Novel View Synthesis of Handheld Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05822)
*Musawar Ali,Manuel Carranza-García,Nicola Fioraio,Samuele Salti,Luigi Di Stefano*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new RGB-only benchmark NVS-HO for novel view synthesis of handheld objects, with two RGB sequences (handheld in front of static camera; board with ChArUco for ground-truth poses). Evaluates NeRF and Gaussian Splatting-based NVS models, using SfM and VGGT as pose estimators; reveals large gaps in current methods under handheld conditions; aims to drive progress.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of real-world, RGB-only benchmarks for robust NVS of handheld objects; demonstrate that unconstrained handheld capture remains challenging.

Method: Construct NVS-HO dataset: per object two sequences; use board sequence to obtain camera poses via marker detection; train NVS models (NeRF, Gaussian Splatting) on handheld sequence; baseline pose estimation via classical SfM and VGGT; evaluate against ground-truth frames from board sequence.

Result: Experiments show significant performance gaps in current methods under unconstrained handheld conditions, indicating robustness issues; the benchmark reveals this gap and provides a challenging testbed.

Conclusion: NVS-HO provides a real-world RGB-based NVS benchmark for handheld objects to push progress toward robust methods; baselines defined; encourages development of methods that handle motion, lighting, and perspective variations in real-world settings.

Abstract: We propose NVS-HO, the first benchmark designed for novel view synthesis of handheld objects in real-world environments using only RGB inputs. Each object is recorded in two complementary RGB sequences: (1) a handheld sequence, where the object is manipulated in front of a static camera, and (2) a board sequence, where the object is fixed on a ChArUco board to provide accurate camera poses via marker detection. The goal of NVS-HO is to learn a NVS model that captures the full appearance of an object from (1), whereas (2) provides the ground-truth images used for evaluation. To establish baselines, we consider both a classical SfM pipeline and a state-of-the-art pre-trained feed-forward neural network (VGGT) as pose estimators, and train NVS models based on NeRF and Gaussian Splatting. Our experiments reveal significant performance gaps in current methods under unconstrained handheld conditions, highlighting the need for more robust approaches. NVS-HO thus offers a challenging real-world benchmark to drive progress in RGB-based novel view synthesis of handheld objects.

</details>


### [76] [Sparse Video Generation Propels Real-World Beyond-the-View Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05827)
*Hai Zhang,Siqi Liang,Li Chen,Yuxian Li,Yukuan Xu,Yichao Zhong,Fu Zhang,Hongyang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SparseVideoNav uses a video-generation model to perform sub-second Beyond-the-View Navigation (BVN) by predicting a sparse 20s future to guide navigation, achieving 2.5x higher success than SOTA LLM baselines and 27x speed-up over unoptimized approaches, including night scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address BVN where agents must reach distant, unseen targets using only simple, high-level intents. Dense, long-horizon instructions improve decision quality but conflict with real-world autonomy and destabilize LLM training if horizon is extended. Video-generation models, with long-horizon supervision, align well with language directions and may offer a viable planning signal for BVN.

Method: Introduce a video-generation model into BVN. Generate a sparse future trajectory spanning 20 seconds to guide real-time navigation, achieving sub-second inference. This sparse forecast reduces latency (27x speed-up) while maintaining alignment with language instructions.

Result: Extensive real-world zero-shot experiments show SparseVideoNav attains 2.5× higher success rate than state-of-the-art LLM baselines on BVN tasks and demonstrates the first ability to handle challenging night scenes in BVN.

Conclusion: Incorporating long-horizon video generation into BVN enables rapid, robust navigation guided by high-level intents. Sparse future forecasting provides real-time feasibility while preserving alignment with language, marking a new direction for BVN and night-time navigation capabilities.

Abstract: Why must vision-language navigation be bound to detailed and verbose language instructions? While such details ease decision-making, they fundamentally contradict the goal for navigation in the real-world. Ideally, agents should possess the autonomy to navigate in unknown environments guided solely by simple and high-level intents. Realizing this ambition introduces a formidable challenge: Beyond-the-View Navigation (BVN), where agents must locate distant, unseen targets without dense and step-by-step guidance. Existing large language model (LLM)-based methods, though adept at following dense instructions, often suffer from short-sighted behaviors due to their reliance on short-horimzon supervision. Simply extending the supervision horizon, however, destabilizes LLM training. In this work, we identify that video generation models inherently benefit from long-horizon supervision to align with language instructions, rendering them uniquely suitable for BVN tasks. Capitalizing on this insight, we propose introducing the video generation model into this field for the first time. Yet, the prohibitive latency for generating videos spanning tens of seconds makes real-world deployment impractical. To bridge this gap, we propose SparseVideoNav, achieving sub-second trajectory inference guided by a generated sparse future spanning a 20-second horizon. This yields a remarkable 27x speed-up compared to the unoptimized counterpart. Extensive real-world zero-shot experiments demonstrate that SparseVideoNav achieves 2.5x the success rate of state-of-the-art LLM baselines on BVN tasks and marks the first realization of such capability in challenging night scenes.

</details>


### [77] [Weaver: End-to-End Agentic System Training for Video Interleaved Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05829)
*Yudi Shi,Shangzhe Di,Qirui Chen,Qinian Wang,Jiayin Cai,Xiaolong Jiang,Yao Hu,Weidi Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Weaver is an end-to-end multimodal reasoning agent that dynamically invokes tools with reinforcement learning to improve video reasoning, especially for long videos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video reasoning is a demanding multimodal task; text-only Chain-of-Thought approaches suffer from representational mismatch and limited perceptual acuity.

Method: Propose Weaver, an end-to-end trainable multimodal reasoning system whose policy can dynamically invoke diverse tools during reasoning; enables progressive visual cue acquisition and authentic multimodal trajectories; integrates reinforcement learning to explore tool usage with trajectory-free data.

Result: Weaver achieves enhanced performance on several complex video reasoning benchmarks, particularly those involving long videos.

Conclusion: Tool-augmented multimodal reasoning with RL exploration strengthens video reasoning capabilities and yields more robust, authentic reasoning trajectories.

Abstract: Video reasoning constitutes a comprehensive assessment of a model's capabilities, as it demands robust perceptual and interpretive skills, thereby serving as a means to explore the boundaries of model performance. While recent research has leveraged text-centric Chain-of-Thought reasoning to augment these capabilities, such approaches frequently suffer from representational mismatch and restricted by limited perceptual acuity. To address these limitations, we propose Weaver, a novel, end-to-end trainable multimodal reasoning agentic system. Weaver empowers its policy model to dynamically invoke diverse tools throughout the reasoning process, enabling progressive acquisition of crucial visual cues and construction of authentic multimodal reasoning trajectories. Furthermore, we integrate a reinforcement learning algorithm to allow the system to freely explore strategies for employing and combining these tools with trajectory-free data. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our system, Weaver, enhances performance on several complex video reasoning benchmarks, particularly those involving long videos.

</details>


### [78] [UI-Mem: Self-Evolving Experience Memory for Online Reinforcement Learning in Mobile GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05832)
*Han Xiao,Guozhi Wang,Hao Wang,Shilong Liu,Yuxiang Chai,Yue Pan,Yufeng Zhou,Xiaoxin Chen,Yafei Wen,Hongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A reinforcement learning framework for GUI agents that uses a hierarchical experience memory (UI-Mem) to store structured, transferable knowledge (workflows, subskills, failure patterns) and guides online RL via Stratified Group Sampling, with a Self-Evolving Loop to keep memory aligned with policy, yielding improved performance and generalization on GUI benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon GUI tasks suffer from credit assignment difficulty and experience non-transferability across tasks, leading to repetitive errors. A memory that captures high-level, reusable knowledge could improve sample efficiency and generalization.

Method: UI-Mem stores parameterized templates of high-level workflows, subtask skills, and failure patterns in a hierarchical memory. Stratified Group Sampling injects varying guidance within rollout groups to balance exploration and guided behavior. A Self-Evolving Loop continually abstracts new strategies and errors to update the memory as the policy evolves.

Result: Experiments on online GUI benchmarks show significant improvements over traditional RL baselines and static reuse strategies, with strong generalization to unseen applications.

Conclusion: Hierarchical, transferable memory with guided sampling and an adaptive update loop improves online GUI RL performance and generalization, enabling cross-task transfer of structured knowledge.

Abstract: Online Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers a promising paradigm for enhancing GUI agents through direct environment interaction. However, its effectiveness is severely hindered by inefficient credit assignment in long-horizon tasks and repetitive errors across tasks due to the lack of experience transfer. To address these challenges, we propose UI-Mem, a novel framework that enhances GUI online RL with a Hierarchical Experience Memory. Unlike traditional replay buffers, our memory accumulates structured knowledge, including high-level workflows, subtask skills, and failure patterns. These experiences are stored as parameterized templates that enable cross-task and cross-application transfer. To effectively integrate memory guidance into online RL, we introduce Stratified Group Sampling, which injects varying levels of guidance across trajectories within each rollout group to maintain outcome diversity, driving the unguided policy toward internalizing guided behaviors. Furthermore, a Self-Evolving Loop continuously abstracts novel strategies and errors to keep the memory aligned with the agent's evolving policy. Experiments on online GUI benchmarks demonstrate that UI-Mem significantly outperforms traditional RL baselines and static reuse strategies, with strong generalization to unseen applications. Project page: https://ui-mem.github.io

</details>


### [79] [Self-Supervised Learning with a Multi-Task Latent Space Objective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05845)
*Pierre-François De Plaen,Abhishek Jha,Luc Van Gool,Tinne Tuytelaars,Marc Proesmans*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Separating predictors per view for SSL with multi-crop stabilizes training; extends to multi-task alignment using global/local/masked views; applicable across backbones and improves ImageNet performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fix instability in predictor-based Siamese SSL when using multi-crop, which treats different crops with a shared predictor and causes divergence.

Method: Assign a distinct predictor to each view type, treat each spatial transformation as a separate alignment task, and include cutout (masked) views; formulate a multi-task asymmetric Siamese SSL integrating global, local, and masked views.

Result: The approach stabilizes training and yields consistent performance gains for both ResNet and ViT backbones on ImageNet.

Conclusion: A simple, stable, and broadly applicable SSL framework that combines multiple view types via per-view predictors and multi-task objectives, improving robustness and performance.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) methods based on Siamese networks learn visual representations by aligning different views of the same image. The multi-crop strategy, which incorporates small local crops to global ones, enhances many SSL frameworks but causes instability in predictor-based architectures such as BYOL, SimSiam, and MoCo v3. We trace this failure to the shared predictor used across all views and demonstrate that assigning a separate predictor to each view type stabilizes multi-crop training, resulting in significant performance gains. Extending this idea, we treat each spatial transformation as a distinct alignment task and add cutout views, where part of the image is masked before encoding. This yields a simple multi-task formulation of asymmetric Siamese SSL that combines global, local, and masked views into a single framework. The approach is stable, generally applicable across backbones, and consistently improves the performance of ResNet and ViT models on ImageNet.

</details>


### [80] [Pathwise Test-Time Correction for Autoregressive Long Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05871)
*Xunzhi Xiang,Zixuan Duan,Guiyu Zhang,Haiyu Zhang,Zhe Gao,Junta Wu,Shaofeng Zhang,Tengfei Wang,Qi Fan,Chunchao Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free Test-Time Correction (TTC) reduces drift in long-sequence generation for distilled autoregressive diffusion models by anchoring intermediate states to the initial frame; it integrates with various distilled models, offering longer, high-quality video synthesis with negligible overhead, comparable to training-based methods on 30-second benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distilled autoregressive diffusion models enable real-time short video synthesis but accumulate errors across long sequences. Test-Time Optimization (TTO) helps for images/short clips but falters on long sequences due to unstable reward landscapes and hypersensitive distilled parameters.

Method: TTC uses the initial frame as a stable reference anchor to calibrate intermediate stochastic states along the sampling trajectory without additional training. It performs corrections during sampling to mitigate drift.

Result: Extensive experiments show TTC integrates with various distilled models, extends generation length with negligible overhead, and achieves quality on par with resource-intensive training-based methods on 30-second benchmarks.

Conclusion: TTC offers a training-free alternative to TTO, enabling longer, higher-quality video synthesis with minimal overhead.

Abstract: Distilled autoregressive diffusion models facilitate real-time short video synthesis but suffer from severe error accumulation during long-sequence generation.
