While existing Test-Time Optimization (TTO) methods prove effective for images or short clips, we identify that they fail to mitigate drift in extended sequences due to unstable reward landscapes and the hypersensitivity of distilled parameters. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Test-Time Correction (TTC), a training-free alternative. Specifically, TTC utilizes the initial frame as a stable reference anchor to calibrate intermediate stochastic states along the sampling trajectory. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method seamlessly integrates with various distilled models, extending generation lengths with negligible overhead while matching the quality of resource-intensive training-based methods on 30-second benchmarks.

</details>


### [81] [Contour Refinement using Discrete Diffusion in Low Data Regime](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05880)
*Fei Yu Guan,Ian Keefe,Sophie Wilkinson,Daniel D. B. Perrakis,Steven Waslander*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight discrete diffusion-based contour refinement method for boundary detection in low-data regimes, using CNNs with self-attention conditioned on a segmentation mask to iteratively denoise a sparse contour into a dense contour, with minimal post-processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Boundary detection for irregular, translucent objects in domains with scarce labeled data and limited compute; boundary detection is underexplored in low-data regimes, necessitating efficient, data-efficient methods.

Method: A lightweight discrete diffusion contour refinement pipeline. A CNN with self-attention is conditioned on a segmentation mask and iteratively denoises a sparse contour representation. Simplified diffusion process, a customized architecture, and minimal post-processing produce a dense, isolated contour. Works with datasets <500 images.

Result: Outperforms several SOTA baselines on the KVASIR medical imaging dataset, competitive on HAM10K and Smoke (wildfire dataset), and achieves a 3.5x improvement in inference framerate.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that low-data, efficient diffusion-based contour refinement is a viable approach for robust boundary detection across medical and environmental datasets, with strong inference efficiency gains and competitive accuracy, though broader generalization and limitations warrant further study.

Abstract: Boundary detection of irregular and translucent objects is an important problem with applications in medical imaging, environmental monitoring and manufacturing, where many of these applications are plagued with scarce labeled data and low in situ computational resources. While recent image segmentation studies focus on segmentation mask alignment with ground-truth, the task of boundary detection remains understudied, especially in the low data regime. In this work, we present a lightweight discrete diffusion contour refinement pipeline for robust boundary detection in the low data regime. We use a Convolutional Neural Network(CNN) architecture with self-attention layers as the core of our pipeline, and condition on a segmentation mask, iteratively denoising a sparse contour representation. We introduce multiple novel adaptations for improved low-data efficacy and inference efficiency, including using a simplified diffusion process, a customized model architecture, and minimal post processing to produce a dense, isolated contour given a dataset of size <500 training images. Our method outperforms several SOTA baselines on the medical imaging dataset KVASIR, is competitive on HAM10K and our custom wildfire dataset, Smoke, while improving inference framerate by 3.5X.

</details>


### [82] [EoCD: Encoder only Remote Sensing Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05882)
*Mubashir Noman,Mustansar Fiaz,Hiyam Debary,Abdul Hannan,Shah Nawaz,Fahad Shahbaz Khan,Salman Khan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Encoder-only change detection (EoCD) uses early fusion of temporal data and a parameter-free multiscale feature fusion module to replace the decoder, achieving strong speed–accuracy trade-offs with reduced model complexity across multiple encoders and four datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational cost and network complexity inherent to Siamese encoders and decoder-heavy designs, while overcoming the inferior performance of early-fusion methods and the costs of late-fusion approaches.

Method: Early temporal data fusion (no Siamese encoder) and a parameter-free multiscale feature fusion module in place of the decoder; evaluate across diverse encoders on four datasets.

Result: EoCD achieves favorable speed–accuracy balance; performance largely determined by the encoder; decoder acts as an additional component; validated on four challenging datasets.

Conclusion: Encoder-only change detection provides a simple, effective alternative that lowers complexity while maintaining performance; the decoder is not essential for competitive results.

Abstract: Being a cornerstone of temporal analysis, change detection has been playing a pivotal role in modern earth observation. Existing change detection methods rely on the Siamese encoder to individually extract temporal features followed by temporal fusion. Subsequently, these methods design sophisticated decoders to improve the change detection performance without taking into consideration the complexity of the model. These aforementioned issues intensify the overall computational cost as well as the network's complexity which is undesirable. Alternatively, few methods utilize the early fusion scheme to combine the temporal images. These methods prevent the extra overhead of Siamese encoder, however, they also rely on sophisticated decoders for better performance. In addition, these methods demonstrate inferior performance as compared to late fusion based methods. To bridge these gaps, we introduce encoder only change detection (EoCD) that is a simple and effective method for the change detection task. The proposed method performs the early fusion of the temporal data and replaces the decoder with a parameter-free multiscale feature fusion module thereby significantly reducing the overall complexity of the model. EoCD demonstrate the optimal balance between the change detection performance and the prediction speed across a variety of encoder architectures. Additionally, EoCD demonstrate that the performance of the model is predominantly dependent on the encoder network, making the decoder an additional component. Extensive experimentation on four challenging change detection datasets reveals the effectiveness of the proposed method.

</details>


### [83] [Neural Implicit 3D Cardiac Shape Reconstruction from Sparse CT Angiography Slices Mimicking 2D Transthoracic Echocardiography Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05884)
*Gino E. Jansen,Carolina Brás,R. Nils Planken,Mark J. Schuuring,Berto J. Bouma,Ivana Išgum*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Neural implicit 3D reconstruction of cardiac structures from sparse 2D planes offers improved 3D quantification over 2D methods, using CTA-trained priors to reconstruct from simulated 2D TTE views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate 3D cardiac shapes enable quantitative anatomy and function analysis. 2D TTE views are limited; reconstructing 3D shapes from sparse 2D planes can reduce errors and improve chamber quantification.

Method: Train a neural implicit function (MLP) to learn 3D shape priors from CTA segmentations of cardiac chambers and LV myocardium. At test time, jointly optimize a latent code and rigid transforms to map observed sparse planes (TTE-mimicking CTA planes) into 3D space, reconstructing multi-class volumes. Simulate four apical views and compare to reference CTA volumes. Evaluate with Dice and volume error against Simpson's biplane rule.

Result: Average Dice coefficient 0.86 ± 0.04 across structures on held-out CTA segmentations. LV volume error 4.88 ± 4.26 mL vs 8.14 ± 6.04 mL (Simpson's rule); LA volume error 6.40 ± 7.37 mL vs 37.76 ± 22.96 mL (Simpson's rule).

Conclusion: Neural implicit 3D reconstruction from sparse 2D views is a viable route to more accurate 3D cardiac quantification in 2D TTE, potentially outperforming conventional 2D-based volume estimation.

Abstract: Accurate 3D representations of cardiac structures allow quantitative analysis of anatomy and function. In this work, we propose a method for reconstructing complete 3D cardiac shapes from segmentations of sparse planes in CT angiography (CTA) for application in 2D transthoracic echocardiography (TTE). Our method uses a neural implicit function to reconstruct the 3D shape of the cardiac chambers and left-ventricle myocardium from sparse CTA planes. To investigate the feasibility of achieving 3D reconstruction from 2D TTE, we select planes that mimic the standard apical 2D TTE views. During training, a multi-layer perceptron learns shape priors from 3D segmentations of the target structures in CTA. At test time, the network reconstructs 3D cardiac shapes from segmentations of TTE-mimicking CTA planes by jointly optimizing the latent code and the rigid transforms that map the observed planes into 3D space. For each heart, we simulate four realistic apical views, and we compare reconstructed multi-class volumes with the reference CTA volumes. On a held-out set of CTA segmentations, our approach achieves an average Dice coefficient of 0.86 $\pm$ 0.04 across all structures. Our method also achieves markedly lower volume errors than the clinical standard, Simpson's biplane rule: 4.88 $\pm$ 4.26 mL vs. 8.14 $\pm$ 6.04 mL, respectively, for the left ventricle; and 6.40 $\pm$ 7.37 mL vs. 37.76 $\pm$ 22.96 mL, respectively, for the left atrium. This suggests that our approach offers a viable route to more accurate 3D chamber quantification in 2D transthoracic echocardiography.

</details>


### [84] [CLIP-Map: Structured Matrix Mapping for Parameter-Efficient CLIP Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05909)
*Kangjie Zhang,Wenxuan Huang,Xin Zhou,Boxiang Zhou,Dejia Song,Yuan Xie,Baochang Zhang,Lizhuang Ma,Nemo Chen,Xu Tang,Yao Hu,Shaohui Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mapping-based CLIP compression (CLIP-Map) preserves information by learnable mappings (Full-Mapping with Kronecker Factorization) of pretrained weights, with Diagonal Inheritance Initialization to stabilize optimization; outperforms weight-subset methods, especially at high compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP's strong performance is offset by high memory/computation costs; existing select-based compression degrades feature representations, particularly under extreme compression; a method that preserves information more effectively is needed.

Method: Proposes CLIP-Map: uses learnable matrices to map and combine pretrained weights via Full-Mapping with Kronecker Factorization; introduces Diagonal Inheritance Initialization to reduce distribution shift and enable efficient learning of the mapping.

Result: Experimental results show CLIP-Map outperforms select-based compression across various compression ratios, with significant gains at high compression.

Conclusion: Mapping-based CLIP compression is effective for preserving information from pretrained weights and enabling efficient high-compression deployment; CLIP-Map outperforms traditional subset-based approaches.

Abstract: Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) has achieved widely applications in various computer vision tasks, e.g., text-to-image generation, Image-Text retrieval and Image captioning. However, CLIP suffers from high memory and computation cost, which prohibits its usage to the resource-limited application scenarios. Existing CLIP compression methods typically reduce the size of pre-trained CLIP weights by selecting their subset as weight inheritance for further retraining via mask optimization or important weight measurement. However, these select-based weight inheritance often compromises the feature presentation ability, especially on the extreme compression. In this paper, we propose a novel mapping-based CLIP compression framework, CLIP-Map. It leverages learnable matrices to map and combine pretrained weights by Full-Mapping with Kronecker Factorization, aiming to preserve as much information from the original weights as possible. To mitigate the optimization challenges introduced by the learnable mapping, we propose Diagonal Inheritance Initialization to reduce the distribution shifting problem for efficient and effective mapping learning. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed CLIP-Map outperforms select-based frameworks across various compression ratios, with particularly significant gains observed under high compression settings.

</details>


### [85] [Multi-Scale Global-Instance Prompt Tuning for Continual Test-time Adaptation in Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05937)
*Lingrui Li,Yanfeng Zhou,Nan Pu,Xin Chen,Zhun Zhong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MGIPT: Multi-scale Global-Instance Prompt Tuning for continual test-time adaptation in medical image segmentation, integrating Adaptive-scale Instance Prompts (AIP) and Multi-scale Global Prompts (MGP) to achieve dual-level adaptation, improved scale diversity, and anti-forgetting, surpassing SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distribution shift across clinical centers hinders deployment of pre-trained segmentation models. CTTA seeks to adapt targets over time without catastrophic forgetting or error accumulation. Prior prompt-tuning approaches lack multi-scale prompt diversity, underutilize instance-level knowledge, and pose privacy risks. MGIPT aims to address these gaps by learning instance- and scale-diverse prompts while preserving privacy via parameter-efficient updates.

Method: MGIPT comprises Adaptive-scale Instance Prompt (AIP) and Multi-scale Global-level Prompt (MGP). AIP learns lightweight, instance-specific prompts with an adaptive-scale selection mechanism to mitigate error accumulation. MGP captures domain-level knowledge across multiple scales to ensure robust adaptation and anti-forgetting. A weighted ensemble combines global and local prompts to enable dual-level adaptation without updating the base model.

Result: Extensive experiments on medical image segmentation benchmarks show MGIPT outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving robust adaptation across continually changing target domains.

Conclusion: MGIPT provides a dual-level adaptation framework that enhances scale diversity and instance/global knowledge capture, yielding robust CTTA performance and reduced forgetting in medical image segmentation.

Abstract: Distribution shift is a common challenge in medical images obtained from different clinical centers, significantly hindering the deployment of pre-trained semantic segmentation models in real-world applications across multiple domains. Continual Test-Time Adaptation(CTTA) has emerged as a promising approach to address cross-domain shifts during continually evolving target domains. Most existing CTTA methods rely on incrementally updating model parameters, which inevitably suffer from error accumulation and catastrophic forgetting, especially in long-term adaptation. Recent prompt-tuning-based works have shown potential to mitigate the two issues above by updating only visual prompts. While these approaches have demonstrated promising performance, several limitations remain:1)lacking multi-scale prompt diversity, 2)inadequate incorporation of instance-specific knowledge, and 3)risk of privacy leakage. To overcome these limitations, we propose Multi-scale Global-Instance Prompt Tuning(MGIPT), to enhance scale diversity of prompts and capture both global- and instance-level knowledge for robust CTTA. Specifically, MGIPT consists of an Adaptive-scale Instance Prompt(AIP) and a Multi-scale Global-level Prompt(MGP). AIP dynamically learns lightweight and instance-specific prompts to mitigate error accumulation with adaptive optimal-scale selection mechanism. MGP captures domain-level knowledge across different scales to ensure robust adaptation with anti-forgetting capabilities. These complementary components are combined through a weighted ensemble approach, enabling effective dual-level adaptation that integrates both global and local information. Extensive experiments on medical image segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that our MGIPT outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving robust adaptation across continually changing target domains.

</details>


### [86] [Better Source, Better Flow: Learning Condition-Dependent Source Distribution for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05951)
*Junwan Kim,Jiho Park,Seonghu Jeon,Seungryong Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Learning a conditioned source distribution for flow matching in text-to-image generation yields better exploitation of conditioning signals, stabilizes training, and accelerates convergence, with robust gains across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow matching typically uses a standard Gaussian source inherited from diffusion models and rarely optimizes the source distribution itself. There is potential to improve conditional generation by designing a principled, condition-aware source distribution.

Method: Introduce a condition-dependent source distribution within the flow-matching objective. Address failure modes like distributional collapse and instability using variance regularization and directional alignment between source and target. Analyze how the target representation space affects flow matching with structured sources. Validate via extensive experiments on multiple text-to-image benchmarks.

Result: Demonstrates consistent improvements across benchmarks, including up to a 3x faster convergence in FID. Highlights practical benefits of optimizing the source distribution for conditional flow matching.

Conclusion: Optimizing the source distribution is beneficial for conditional flow matching in text-to-image generation. Proper variance regularization and alignment are critical, and the choice of target representation space influences effectiveness. This approach yields more robust and faster-converging conditional generative models.

Abstract: Flow matching has recently emerged as a promising alternative to diffusion-based generative models, particularly for text-to-image generation. Despite its flexibility in allowing arbitrary source distributions, most existing approaches rely on a standard Gaussian distribution, a choice inherited from diffusion models, and rarely consider the source distribution itself as an optimization target in such settings. In this work, we show that principled design of the source distribution is not only feasible but also beneficial at the scale of modern text-to-image systems. Specifically, we propose learning a condition-dependent source distribution under flow matching objective that better exploit rich conditioning signals. We identify key failure modes that arise when directly incorporating conditioning into the source, including distributional collapse and instability, and show that appropriate variance regularization and directional alignment between source and target are critical for stable and effective learning. We further analyze how the choice of target representation space impacts flow matching with structured sources, revealing regimes in which such designs are most effective. Extensive experiments across multiple text-to-image benchmarks demonstrate consistent and robust improvements, including up to a 3x faster convergence in FID, highlighting the practical benefits of a principled source distribution design for conditional flow matching.

</details>


### [87] [LSA: Localized Semantic Alignment for Enhancing Temporal Consistency in Traffic Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05966)
*Mirlan Karimov,Teodora Spasojevic,Markus Braun,Julian Wiederer,Vasileios Belagiannis,Marc Pollefeys*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Localized Semantic Alignment (LSA) fine-tunes pre-trained video generation models to improve temporal consistency without inference-time control signals by aligning semantic features around dynamic objects using a ground-truth vs generated clip comparison; achieves better metrics on nuScenes and KITTI with a single epoch of training and no extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current controllable video generation methods require inference-time control signals and struggle with temporal consistency, limiting scalability as data engines.

Method: LSA aligns semantic features between ground-truth and generated clips localized around dynamic objects using an off-the-shelf feature extractor; combines semantic feature consistency loss with standard diffusion loss; fine-tune base model for one epoch; no extra inference overhead.

Result: Outperforms baselines on common video generation metrics; demonstrated temporal consistency improvements; adapts mAP and mIoU metrics from object detection to evaluate temporal consistency; experiments on nuScenes and KITTI show effectiveness; no external control signals required at inference and no computational overheads.

Conclusion: LSA provides a simple yet effective fine-tuning strategy to improve temporal consistency in video generation and can serve as a scalable data engine without inference-time controls; potential applicability to other domains with dynamic objects.

Abstract: Controllable video generation has emerged as a versatile tool for autonomous driving, enabling realistic synthesis of traffic scenarios. However, existing methods depend on control signals at inference time to guide the generative model towards temporally consistent generation of dynamic objects, limiting their utility as scalable and generalizable data engines. In this work, we propose Localized Semantic Alignment (LSA), a simple yet effective framework for fine-tuning pre-trained video generation models. LSA enhances temporal consistency by aligning semantic features between ground-truth and generated video clips. Specifically, we compare the output of an off-the-shelf feature extraction model between the ground-truth and generated video clips localized around dynamic objects inducing a semantic feature consistency loss. We fine-tune the base model by combining this loss with the standard diffusion loss. The model fine-tuned for a single epoch with our novel loss outperforms the baselines in common video generation evaluation metrics. To further test the temporal consistency in generated videos we adapt two additional metrics from object detection task, namely mAP and mIoU. Extensive experiments on nuScenes and KITTI datasets show the effectiveness of our approach in enhancing temporal consistency in video generation without the need for external control signals during inference and any computational overheads.

</details>


### [88] [RISE-Video: Can Video Generators Decode Implicit World Rules?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05986)
*Mingxin Liu,Shuran Ma,Shibei Meng,Xiangyu Zhao,Zicheng Zhang,Shaofeng Zhang,Zhihang Zhong,Peixian Chen,Haoyu Cao,Xing Sun,Haodong Duan,Xue Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RISE-Video is a new TI2V benchmark with 467 human-annotated samples across eight categories, four evaluation metrics (Reasoning Alignment, Temporal Consistency, Physical Rationality, Visual Quality), plus an automated LMM-based evaluation pipeline. Experiments on 11 TI2V models show substantial gaps in reasoning under implicit constraints, highlighting areas for improvement in world-simulation video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where high-fidelity generative video models struggle with internalized world rules and reasoning, by providing a dataset and evaluation framework focused on cognitive reasoning rather than surface-level visuals.

Method: Constructs the RISE-Video dataset (467 samples, eight categories), defines a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol with four metrics, and proposes an automated pipeline using Large Multimodal Models to simulate human assessment for scalable evaluation.

Result: Across 11 state-of-the-art TI2V models, the study finds pervasive deficiencies in reasoning about implicit constraints, demonstrating the benchmark's effectiveness at exposing current limitations and enabling scalable assessment via LMM-based evaluation.

Conclusion: RISE-Video establishes a structured, scalable framework for evaluating and guiding the development of world-simulating TI2V models, emphasizing deep cognitive reasoning and consistency over mere visual fidelity.

Abstract: While generative video models have achieved remarkable visual fidelity, their capacity to internalize and reason over implicit world rules remains a critical yet under-explored frontier. To bridge this gap, we present RISE-Video, a pioneering reasoning-oriented benchmark for Text-Image-to-Video (TI2V) synthesis that shifts the evaluative focus from surface-level aesthetics to deep cognitive reasoning. RISE-Video comprises 467 meticulously human-annotated samples spanning eight rigorous categories, providing a structured testbed for probing model intelligence across diverse dimensions, ranging from commonsense and spatial dynamics to specialized subject domains. Our framework introduces a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol consisting of four metrics: \textit{Reasoning Alignment}, \textit{Temporal Consistency}, \textit{Physical Rationality}, and \textit{Visual Quality}. To further support scalable evaluation, we propose an automated pipeline leveraging Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) to emulate human-centric assessment. Extensive experiments on 11 state-of-the-art TI2V models reveal pervasive deficiencies in simulating complex scenarios under implicit constraints, offering critical insights for the advancement of future world-simulating generative models.

</details>


### [89] [VisRefiner: Learning from Visual Differences for Screenshot-to-Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05998)
*Jie Deng,Kaichun Yao,Libo Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VisRefiner enables screenshot-to-code models to learn from visual differences between rendered predictions and reference designs, using difference-aligned supervision and an RL-based self-refinement stage to improve code fidelity and layout accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal LLMs map screenshots directly to code without observing the actual visual outcomes of their generated code. Humans iteratively render, compare, and learn how design differences relate to code changes, motivating a training paradigm that leverages visual discrepancies.

Method: 1) Difference-aligned supervision pairs visual discrepancies with corresponding code edits to teach the model how appearance changes arise from specific implementation changes. 2) A reinforcement learning stage for self-refinement where the model iteratively renders output, compares it to the target design, identifies visual differences, and updates the code accordingly.

Result: VisRefiner yields substantial improvements in single-step generation quality and layout fidelity, and equips models with strong self-refinement capabilities, demonstrating the effectiveness of learning from visual differences for screenshot-to-code generation.

Conclusion: Learning from visual differences is an effective strategy to advance screenshot-to-code generation, and VisRefiner provides a practical framework combining difference-aligned supervision with self-refinement to improve generated frontend code.

Abstract: Screenshot-to-code generation aims to translate user interface screenshots into executable frontend code that faithfully reproduces the target layout and style. Existing multimodal large language models perform this mapping directly from screenshots but are trained without observing the visual outcomes of their generated code. In contrast, human developers iteratively render their implementation, compare it with the design, and learn how visual differences relate to code changes. Inspired by this process, we propose VisRefiner, a training framework that enables models to learn from visual differences between rendered predictions and reference designs. We construct difference-aligned supervision that associates visual discrepancies with corresponding code edits, allowing the model to understand how appearance variations arise from implementation changes. Building on this, we introduce a reinforcement learning stage for self-refinement, where the model improves its generated code by observing both the rendered output and the target design, identifying their visual differences, and updating the code accordingly. Experiments show that VisRefiner substantially improves single-step generation quality and layout fidelity, while also endowing models with strong self-refinement ability. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of learning from visual differences for advancing screenshot-to-code generation.

</details>


### [90] [GenArena: How Can We Achieve Human-Aligned Evaluation for Visual Generation Tasks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06013)
*Ruihang Li,Leigang Qu,Jingxu Zhang,Dongnan Gui,Mengde Xu,Xiaosong Zhang,Han Hu,Wenjie Wang,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GenArena introduces a pairwise comparison framework for evaluating visual generation, addressing limitations of pointwise scoring and aligning with human judgment; it enables open-source models to surpass proprietary ones, improves evaluation accuracy (~+20%) and achieves a Spearman correlation of 0.86 with LMArena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pointwise absolute scoring in visual generation evaluation is noisy, inconsistent due to stochastic generation, and poorly aligned with human perception; a stable, human-aligned, scalable evaluation is needed.

Method: Propose GenArena, a unified evaluation framework that uses pairwise comparisons instead of pointwise scores. Leverages off-the-shelf open-source models, automates evaluation across diverse tasks, and benchmarks state-of-the-art visual generation models.

Result: Pairwise protocol yields over 20% gain in evaluation accuracy and a Spearman correlation of 0.86 with the LMArena leader-board, far surpassing the 0.36 of pointwise methods; open-source models outperform top-tier proprietary models under this framework.

Conclusion: GenArena provides a rigorous, automated evaluation standard for visual generation, enabling robust benchmarking across tasks and improving alignment with human judgment and reliability of model comparisons.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of visual generation models has outpaced traditional evaluation approaches, necessitating the adoption of Vision-Language Models as surrogate judges. In this work, we systematically investigate the reliability of the prevailing absolute pointwise scoring standard, across a wide spectrum of visual generation tasks. Our analysis reveals that this paradigm is limited due to stochastic inconsistency and poor alignment with human perception. To resolve these limitations, we introduce GenArena, a unified evaluation framework that leverages a pairwise comparison paradigm to ensure stable and human-aligned evaluation. Crucially, our experiments uncover a transformative finding that simply adopting this pairwise protocol enables off-the-shelf open-source models to outperform top-tier proprietary models. Notably, our method boosts evaluation accuracy by over 20% and achieves a Spearman correlation of 0.86 with the authoritative LMArena leaderboard, drastically surpassing the 0.36 correlation of pointwise methods. Based on GenArena, we benchmark state-of-the-art visual generation models across diverse tasks, providing the community with a rigorous and automated evaluation standard for visual generation.

</details>


### [91] [Context Forcing: Consistent Autoregressive Video Generation with Long Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06028)
*Shuo Chen,Cong Wei,Sun Sun,Ping Nie,Kai Zhou,Ge Zhang,Ming-Hsuan Yang,Wenhu Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Context Forcing, a framework to train long-context video generators using a long-context teacher and a Slow-Fast Memory context management, achieving longer context and better long-duration consistency than prior work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the student–teacher mismatch in streaming tuning where teachers see only short windows, hindering learning of global temporal dependencies and long-term coherence in video generation.

Method: Utilizes a long-context teacher with full generation history (eliminating supervision mismatch) and a Slow-Fast Memory system to manage growing context, enabling efficient training of long-horizon video synthesis; trains a long-context student under this guidance.

Result: Empirical results show context lengths exceeding 20 seconds, offering 2–10x longer context than state-of-the-art baselines like LongLive and Infinite-RoPE, with improved long-duration consistency across metrics.

Conclusion: Context Forcing effectively enables robust long-term video generation by aligning teacher-student contexts and reducing redundancy, demonstrating scalable long-context capabilities and surpassing prior baselines.

Abstract: Recent approaches to real-time long video generation typically employ streaming tuning strategies, attempting to train a long-context student using a short-context (memoryless) teacher. In these frameworks, the student performs long rollouts but receives supervision from a teacher limited to short 5-second windows. This structural discrepancy creates a critical \textbf{student-teacher mismatch}: the teacher's inability to access long-term history prevents it from guiding the student on global temporal dependencies, effectively capping the student's context length. To resolve this, we propose \textbf{Context Forcing}, a novel framework that trains a long-context student via a long-context teacher. By ensuring the teacher is aware of the full generation history, we eliminate the supervision mismatch, enabling the robust training of models capable of long-term consistency. To make this computationally feasible for extreme durations (e.g., 2 minutes), we introduce a context management system that transforms the linearly growing context into a \textbf{Slow-Fast Memory} architecture, significantly reducing visual redundancy. Extensive results demonstrate that our method enables effective context lengths exceeding 20 seconds -- 2 to 10 times longer than state-of-the-art methods like LongLive and Infinite-RoPE. By leveraging this extended context, Context Forcing preserves superior consistency across long durations, surpassing state-of-the-art baselines on various long video evaluation metrics.

</details>


### [92] [Splat and Distill: Augmenting Teachers with Feed-Forward 3D Reconstruction For 3D-Aware Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06032)
*David Shavin,Sagie Benaim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Splat and Distill introduces a feed-forward 3D reconstruction pipeline to instill 3D awareness into 2D Vision Foundation Models by lifting 2D features into 3D Gaussians and splatting them to novel viewpoints for distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D awareness gap in 2D VFMs; prior approaches rely on slow per-scene optimization and can incur feature-averaging artifacts; the goal is fast, geometry-grounded supervision to robustly inject 3D understanding and enrich semantic richness.

Method: From teacher's 2D features, lift to an explicit 3D Gaussian representation in a feed-forward manner; splat these 3D features onto novel viewpoints to create 2D feature maps; use these maps to supervise and distill into a student model; replaces slow optimization with a fast lifting pipeline, enabling a dynamic learning process where teacher and student co-improve.

Result: Significant improvements over prior work across downstream tasks—monocular depth estimation, surface normal estimation, multi-view correspondence, and semantic segmentation—demonstrating enhanced 3D awareness and richer semantic structure in 2D features.

Conclusion: Offers a scalable, geometry-grounded distillation framework that elevates 3D awareness in 2D Vision Foundation Models while avoiding feature-averaging artifacts; includes a project page for implementation details.

Abstract: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) have achieved remarkable success when applied to various downstream 2D tasks. Despite their effectiveness, they often exhibit a critical lack of 3D awareness. To this end, we introduce Splat and Distill, a framework that instills robust 3D awareness into 2D VFMs by augmenting the teacher model with a fast, feed-forward 3D reconstruction pipeline. Given 2D features produced by a teacher model, our method first lifts these features into an explicit 3D Gaussian representation, in a feedforward manner. These 3D features are then ``splatted" onto novel viewpoints, producing a set of novel 2D feature maps used to supervise the student model, ``distilling" geometrically grounded knowledge. By replacing slow per-scene optimization of prior work with our feed-forward lifting approach, our framework avoids feature-averaging artifacts, creating a dynamic learning process where the teacher's consistency improves alongside that of the student. We conduct a comprehensive evaluation on a suite of downstream tasks, including monocular depth estimation, surface normal estimation, multi-view correspondence, and semantic segmentation. Our method significantly outperforms prior works, not only achieving substantial gains in 3D awareness but also enhancing the underlying semantic richness of 2D features. Project page is available at https://davidshavin4.github.io/Splat-and-Distill/

</details>


### [93] [V-Retrver: Evidence-Driven Agentic Reasoning for Universal Multimodal Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06034)
*Dongyang Chen,Chaoyang Wang,Dezhao SU,Xi Xiao,Zeyu Zhang,Jing Xiong,Qing Li,Yuzhang Shang,Shichao Ka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An evidence-driven multimodal retrieval framework (V-Retrver) that uses agentic visual inspection to selectively acquire evidence via external tools, yielding improved retrieval accuracy and reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs tend to be language-driven with static visual encodings, often making speculative judgments in visually ambiguous cases. There is a need for active visual verification and evidence-grounded reasoning.

Method: V-Retrver enables selective visual evidence acquisition through external visual tools, forming a multimodal interleaved reasoning process that alternates hypothesis generation with targeted visual verification. Training uses curriculum-based learning: supervised reasoning activation, rejection-based refinement, and reinforcement learning with an evidence-aligned objective.

Result: Experiments on multiple multimodal retrieval benchmarks show consistent improvements, including an average 23.0% increase in retrieval accuracy, enhanced perception-driven reasoning reliability, and better generalization.

Conclusion: Evidence-driven visual verification within multimodal retrieval is effective, improving accuracy and reliability and suggesting broader applicability for agentic, evidence-grounded reasoning in MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently been applied to universal multimodal retrieval, where Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning improves candidate reranking. However, existing approaches remain largely language-driven, relying on static visual encodings and lacking the ability to actively verify fine-grained visual evidence, which often leads to speculative reasoning in visually ambiguous cases. We propose V-Retrver, an evidence-driven retrieval framework that reformulates multimodal retrieval as an agentic reasoning process grounded in visual inspection. V-Retrver enables an MLLM to selectively acquire visual evidence during reasoning via external visual tools, performing a multimodal interleaved reasoning process that alternates between hypothesis generation and targeted visual verification.To train such an evidence-gathering retrieval agent, we adopt a curriculum-based learning strategy combining supervised reasoning activation, rejection-based refinement, and reinforcement learning with an evidence-aligned objective. Experiments across multiple multimodal retrieval benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in retrieval accuracy (with 23.0% improvements on average), perception-driven reasoning reliability, and generalization.

</details>


### [94] [Thinking with Geometry: Active Geometry Integration for Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06037)
*Haoyuan Li,Qihang Cao,Tao Tang,Kun Xiang,Zihan Guo,Jianhua Han,Hang Xu,Xiaodan Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoThinker enables active, selective retrieval of geometric evidence in multimodal LLMs via Spatial-Grounded Fusion and Importance Gating, outperforming prior passive fusion on spatial reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Passive geometric priors in existing MLLMs are exposed as a global, indiscriminate stream, causing semantic-geometry misalignment and redundancy; there is a need for active perception that selectively grounds geometry according to reasoning demands.

Method: Introduces Spatial-Grounded Fusion applied at carefully selected VLM layers, using frame-strict cross-attention to query and integrate task-relevant geometry. Enhanced by Importance Gating that biases per-frame attention towards structures relevant to the task.

Result: Achieves a new state-of-the-art VSI-Bench score (72.6). Demonstrates robust generalization and improved spatial perception in complex downstream tasks (embodied referring, autonomous driving). Code available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Active integration of spatial structures is essential for next-generation spatial intelligence, as evidenced by GeoThinker's performance and generalization capabilities.

Abstract: Recent progress in spatial reasoning with Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) increasingly leverages geometric priors from 3D encoders. However, most existing integration strategies remain passive: geometry is exposed as a global stream and fused in an indiscriminate manner, which often induces semantic-geometry misalignment and redundant signals. We propose GeoThinker, a framework that shifts the paradigm from passive fusion to active perception. Instead of feature mixing, GeoThinker enables the model to selectively retrieve geometric evidence conditioned on its internal reasoning demands. GeoThinker achieves this through Spatial-Grounded Fusion applied at carefully selected VLM layers, where semantic visual priors selectively query and integrate task-relevant geometry via frame-strict cross-attention, further calibrated by Importance Gating that biases per-frame attention toward task-relevant structures. Comprehensive evaluation results show that GeoThinker sets a new state-of-the-art in spatial intelligence, achieving a peak score of 72.6 on the VSI-Bench. Furthermore, GeoThinker demonstrates robust generalization and significantly improved spatial perception across complex downstream scenarios, including embodied referring and autonomous driving. Our results indicate that the ability to actively integrate spatial structures is essential for next-generation spatial intelligence. Code can be found at https://github.com/Li-Hao-yuan/GeoThinker.

</details>


### [95] [SwimBird: Eliciting Switchable Reasoning Mode in Hybrid Autoregressive MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06040)
*Jintao Tong,Shilin Yan,Hongwei Xue,Xiaojun Tang,Kunyu Shi,Guannan Zhang,Ruixuan Li,Yixiong Zou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SwimBird is a multimodal LLM that adaptively switches among text-only, vision-only, and interleaved reasoning modes, using a hybrid autoregressive framework and a curated SwimBird-SFT-92K dataset, achieving state-of-the-art results on text and vision-heavy tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs rely on fixed reasoning patterns (text-only CoT or fixed visual thoughts), which hurts performance on vision-dense tasks and/or textual logic. There is a need for adaptive, mode-switchable reasoning conditioned on the input.

Method: A hybrid autoregressive model unifies next-token prediction for textual thoughts and next-embedding prediction for visual thoughts, with three switching modes: text-only, vision-only, and interleaved. A reasoning-mode curation strategy constructs SwimBird-SFT-92K, a diverse supervised fine-tuning dataset covering all three reasoning patterns.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on textual reasoning and challenging vision-dense tasks, with robust gains over prior fixed-pattern multimodal reasoning methods.

Conclusion: Adaptive, query-conditioned reasoning switching preserves textual logical rigor while enhancing performance on vision-intensive tasks, demonstrating the value of flexible thinking modalities in MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have made remarkable progress in multimodal perception and reasoning by bridging vision and language. However, most existing MLLMs perform reasoning primarily with textual CoT, which limits their effectiveness on vision-intensive tasks. Recent approaches inject a fixed number of continuous hidden states as "visual thoughts" into the reasoning process and improve visual performance, but often at the cost of degraded text-based logical reasoning. We argue that the core limitation lies in a rigid, pre-defined reasoning pattern that cannot adaptively choose the most suitable thinking modality for different user queries. We introduce SwimBird, a reasoning-switchable MLLM that dynamically switches among three reasoning modes conditioned on the input: (1) text-only reasoning, (2) vision-only reasoning (continuous hidden states as visual thoughts), and (3) interleaved vision-text reasoning. To enable this capability, we adopt a hybrid autoregressive formulation that unifies next-token prediction for textual thoughts with next-embedding prediction for visual thoughts, and design a systematic reasoning-mode curation strategy to construct SwimBird-SFT-92K, a diverse supervised fine-tuning dataset covering all three reasoning patterns. By enabling flexible, query-adaptive mode selection, SwimBird preserves strong textual logic while substantially improving performance on vision-dense tasks. Experiments across diverse benchmarks covering textual reasoning and challenging visual understanding demonstrate that SwimBird achieves state-of-the-art results and robust gains over prior fixed-pattern multimodal reasoning methods.

</details>


### [96] [Predicting Camera Pose from Perspective Descriptions for Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06041)
*Xuejun Zhang,Aditi Tiwari,Zhenhailong Wang,Heng Ji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CAMCUE is a pose-aware multi-image framework for cross-view reasoning that injects per-view pose into visual tokens, grounds language to a target camera pose, and synthesizes a pose-conditioned imagined target view; achieves faster, accurate multi-view reasoning on CAMCUE-DATA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackling multi-image spatial reasoning and perspective taking in multimodal LLMs requires explicit geometric grounding to build a coherent 3D understanding across views and efficient target-view reasoning.

Method: Propose CAMCUE: injects per-view pose into visual tokens; grounds natural-language viewpoint descriptions to a target camera pose; synthesizes a pose-conditioned imagined target view to support answering. Curates CAMCUE-DATA with 27,668 training and 508 test instances pairing multi-view images and poses with diverse target-viewpoint descriptions and perspective-shift questions; includes human-annotated viewpoint descriptions in test set.

Result: CAMCUE improves overall accuracy by 9.06% and achieves target-pose estimation >90% rotation accuracy within 20° and translation accuracy within 0.5 threshold. Reduces inference time from 256.6s to 1.45s per example, enabling fast, interactive use.

Conclusion: Pose-aware cross-view fusion enables accurate, fast multi-image reasoning for perspective-taking tasks, avoiding expensive search-and-match and supporting practical novel-view reasoning in real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Multi-image spatial reasoning remains challenging for current multimodal large language models (MLLMs). While single-view perception is inherently 2D, reasoning over multiple views requires building a coherent scene understanding across viewpoints. In particular, we study perspective taking, where a model must build a coherent 3D understanding from multi-view observations and use it to reason from a new, language-specified viewpoint. We introduce CAMCUE, a pose-aware multi-image framework that uses camera pose as an explicit geometric anchor for cross-view fusion and novel-view reasoning. CAMCUE injects per-view pose into visual tokens, grounds natural-language viewpoint descriptions to a target camera pose, and synthesizes a pose-conditioned imagined target view to support answering. To support this setting, we curate CAMCUE-DATA with 27,668 training and 508 test instances pairing multi-view images and poses with diverse target-viewpoint descriptions and perspective-shift questions. We also include human-annotated viewpoint descriptions in the test split to evaluate generalization to human language. CAMCUE improves overall accuracy by 9.06% and predicts target poses from natural-language viewpoint descriptions with over 90% rotation accuracy within 20° and translation accuracy within a 0.5 error threshold. This direct grounding avoids expensive test-time search-and-match, reducing inference time from 256.6s to 1.45s per example and enabling fast, interactive use in real-world scenarios.

</details>


### [97] [Consistency-Preserving Concept Erasure via Unsafe-Safe Pairing and Directional Fisher-weighted Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05339)
*Yongwoo Kim,Sungmin Cha,Hyunsoo Kim,Jaewon Lee,Donghyun Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PAIR reframes concept erasure as consistency-preserving semantic realignment using unsafe-safe pairs, introducing paired semantic realignment and Fisher-weighted initialization for DoRA to enable targeted erasure while preserving structure/semantics; outperforms baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing concept erasure methods remove unsafe concepts but fail to guide safe alternatives, often breaking structural/semantic fidelity between original and erased generations.

Method: Generate safe counterparts from unsafe inputs to form paired unsafe-safe multimodal data. Introduce Paired Semantic Realignment objective to map target concepts to semantically aligned safe anchors. Use Fisher-weighted Initialization for DoRA to initialize low-rank adaptation matrices, steering generation toward safe alternatives while suppressing unsafe concepts.

Result: Extensive experiments report significant improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in erasure effectiveness while maintaining structural integrity, semantic coherence, and generation quality.

Conclusion: PAIR enables fine-grained, consistency-preserving erasure by leveraging unsafe-safe pairs for semantic realignment and Fisher-informed DoRA initialization, yielding better-controlled and safer diffusion-model generations.

Abstract: With the increasing versatility of text-to-image diffusion models, the ability to selectively erase undesirable concepts (e.g., harmful content) has become indispensable. However, existing concept erasure approaches primarily focus on removing unsafe concepts without providing guidance toward corresponding safe alternatives, which often leads to failure in preserving the structural and semantic consistency between the original and erased generations. In this paper, we propose a novel framework, PAIRed Erasing (PAIR), which reframes concept erasure from simple removal to consistency-preserving semantic realignment using unsafe-safe pairs. We first generate safe counterparts from unsafe inputs while preserving structural and semantic fidelity, forming paired unsafe-safe multimodal data. Leveraging these pairs, we introduce two key components: (1) Paired Semantic Realignment, a guided objective that uses unsafe-safe pairs to explicitly map target concepts to semantically aligned safe anchors; and (2) Fisher-weighted Initialization for DoRA, which initializes parameter-efficient low-rank adaptation matrices using unsafe-safe pairs, encouraging the generation of safe alternatives while selectively suppressing unsafe concepts. Together, these components enable fine-grained erasure that removes only the targeted concepts while maintaining overall semantic consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving effective concept erasure while preserving structural integrity, semantic coherence, and generation quality.

</details>


### [98] [VRIQ: Benchmarking and Analyzing Visual-Reasoning IQ of VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05382)
*Tina Khezresmaeilzadeh,Jike Zhong,Konstantinos Psounis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VRIQ benchmarks visual reasoning in VLMs, revealing weaknesses in abstract puzzle reasoning (~28% accuracy) and natural-image reasoning (~45%), with only modest gains from tool-augmented reasoning. A diagnostic split shows most failures stem from perception (56%), with 43% from both perception and reasoning, and 1% from pure reasoning. Fine-grained perception probes reveal category-specific weaknesses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematically evaluate and diagnose nonverbal visual reasoning in VLMs, separating perceptual vs. inferential bottlenecks to guide improvements in multimodal systems.

Method: Introduce VRIQ benchmark with two task streams (abstract puzzle-style and natural-image reasoning). Evaluate VLMs with and without visual reasoning tools. Develop diagnostic probes targeting perception vs. reasoning. Classify failures into perception-only, perception+reasoning, and reasoning-only. Further analyze by perception categories (shape, count, position, depth, etc.).

Result: Abstract puzzles yield ~28% accuracy (near random); natural tasks yield ~45% accuracy (still weak). Tool-augmented reasoning offers only modest gains. Diagnostic breakdown: ~56% failures due to perception, ~43% due to both perception and reasoning, ~1% due to reasoning alone. Perception categories show varying difficulty, with some (e.g., shape, count, position, 3D/depth) driving more failures.

Conclusion: Current VLMs with or without tooling remain unreliable abstract reasoners, chiefly hindered by perceptual limitations. The proposed diagnostic framework provides principled insights for targeted improvements in perception and subsequent reasoning in multimodal models.

Abstract: Recent progress in Vision Language Models (VLMs) has raised the question of whether they can reliably perform nonverbal reasoning. To this end, we introduce VRIQ (Visual Reasoning IQ), a novel benchmark designed to assess and analyze the visual reasoning ability of VLMs. We evaluate models on two sets of tasks: abstract puzzle-style and natural-image reasoning tasks. We find that on abstract puzzles, performance remains near random with an average accuracy of around 28%, while natural tasks yield better but still weak results with 45% accuracy. We also find that tool-augmented reasoning demonstrates only modest improvements. To uncover the source of this weakness, we introduce diagnostic probes targeting perception and reasoning. Our analysis demonstrates that around 56% of failures arise from perception alone, 43% from both perception and reasoning, and only a mere 1% from reasoning alone. This motivates us to design fine-grained diagnostic probe questions targeting specific perception categories (e.g., shape, count, position, 3D/depth), revealing that certain categories cause more failures than others. Our benchmark and analysis establish that current VLMs, even with visual reasoning tools, remain unreliable abstract reasoners, mostly due to perception limitations, and offer a principled basis for improving visual reasoning in multimodal systems.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [99] [A Causal Perspective for Enhancing Jailbreak Attack and Defense](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04893)
*Licheng Pan,Yunsheng Lu,Jiexi Liu,Jialing Tao,Haozhe Feng,Hui Xue,Zhixuan Chu,Kui Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Causal Analyst to uncover direct causal drivers of jailbreaking in LLMs via joint LLM encoding and GNN-based causal graphs, using a 35k-attack dataset across 7 models; demonstrates attack and defense utilities and provides code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap where jailbreak mechanisms are not causally understood; current work probes latent representations but lacks causal links between interpretable prompt features and jailbreaks, hindering robust safety improvements.

Method: Construct a dataset of 35k jailbreak attempts from 100 attack templates and 50 harmful queries across seven LLMs; annotate 37 human-readable prompt features; jointly train an LLM-based prompt encoder with a GNN for causal graph learning to map prompt features to jailbreak outcomes and causal pathways.

Result: Identifies direct causal drivers (e.g., 'Positive Character', 'Number of Task Steps'); validates causal structure and robustness vs non-causal methods; demonstrates (1) Jailbreaking Enhancer to boost attack success on benchmarks and (2) Guardrail Advisor to extract true malicious intent from obfuscated queries; code available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: Causal analysis of jailbreak features offers an interpretable and effective approach for improving LLM reliability, with practical defense and attack utilities and a path toward better safety mechanisms.

Abstract: Uncovering the mechanisms behind "jailbreaks" in large language models (LLMs) is crucial for enhancing their safety and reliability, yet these mechanisms remain poorly understood. Existing studies predominantly analyze jailbreak prompts by probing latent representations, often overlooking the causal relationships between interpretable prompt features and jailbreak occurrences. In this work, we propose Causal Analyst, a framework that integrates LLMs into data-driven causal discovery to identify the direct causes of jailbreaks and leverage them for both attack and defense. We introduce a comprehensive dataset comprising 35k jailbreak attempts across seven LLMs, systematically constructed from 100 attack templates and 50 harmful queries, annotated with 37 meticulously designed human-readable prompt features. By jointly training LLM-based prompt encoding and GNN-based causal graph learning, we reconstruct causal pathways linking prompt features to jailbreak responses. Our analysis reveals that specific features, such as "Positive Character" and "Number of Task Steps", act as direct causal drivers of jailbreaks. We demonstrate the practical utility of these insights through two applications: (1) a Jailbreaking Enhancer that targets identified causal features to significantly boost attack success rates on public benchmarks, and (2) a Guardrail Advisor that utilizes the learned causal graph to extract true malicious intent from obfuscated queries. Extensive experiments, including baseline comparisons and causal structure validation, confirm the robustness of our causal analysis and its superiority over non-causal approaches. Our results suggest that analyzing jailbreak features from a causal perspective is an effective and interpretable approach for improving LLM reliability. Our code is available at https://github.com/Master-PLC/Causal-Analyst.

</details>


### [100] [Mind the Performance Gap: Capability-Behavior Trade-offs in Feature Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04903)
*Eitan Sprejer,Oscar Agustín Stanchi,María Victoria Carro,Denise Alejandra Mester,Iván Arcuschin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feature steering can actively modify targeted behaviors but at a significant cost to task performance and coherence; prompting-based baselines often achieve a better balance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the practical effectiveness of feature steering for LLMs, quantify the trade-offs between behavioral control and output quality, and assess deployment viability.

Method: Empirical comparison of Goodfire's Auto Steer versus prompt-based baselines across 14 steering queries (innocuous and safety-relevant) on 171 MMLU questions using Llama-8B and Llama-70B, measuring accuracy, coherence, and behavioral control.

Result: Auto Steer modifies targeted behaviors (control scores: 3.33 vs 2.98 on Llama-8B; 3.57 vs 3.10 on Llama-70B) but causes large performance drops (accuracy: 66%→46% on 8B; 87%→73% on 70B; coherence: 4.62→2.24 on 8B; 4.94→3.89 on 70B). Simple prompting yields the best overall balance.

Conclusion: Mechanistic (feature) control methods incur capability-behavior trade-offs; empirical characterization is necessary before practical deployment; prompt-based approaches currently offer a more favorable balance between control and task performance.

Abstract: Feature steering has emerged as a promising approach for controlling LLM behavior through direct manipulation of internal representations, offering advantages over prompt engineering. However, its practical effectiveness in real-world applications remains poorly understood, particularly regarding potential trade-offs with output quality. We show that feature steering methods substantially degrade model performance even when successfully controlling target behaviors, a critical trade-off. Specifically, we evaluate Goodfire's Auto Steer against prompt engineering baselines across 14 steering queries (covering innocuous and safety-relevant behaviors) on 171 Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) questions using Llama-8B and Llama-70B, measuring accuracy, coherence, and behavioral control. Our findings show that Auto Steer successfully modifies target behaviors (achieving scores of 3.33 vs. 2.98 for prompting on Llama-8B and 3.57 vs. 3.10 on Llama-70B), but causes dramatic performance degradation: accuracy on the MMLU questions drops from 66% to 46% on Llama-8B and 87% to 73% on Llama-70B, with coherence falling from 4.62 to 2.24 and 4.94 to 3.89 respectively. Simple prompting achieves the best overall balance. These findings highlight limitations of current feature steering methods for practical deployment where task performance cannot be sacrificed. More broadly, our work demonstrates that mechanistic control methods face fundamental capability-behavior trade-offs that must be empirically characterized before deployment.

</details>


### [101] [DCER: Dual-Stage Compression and Energy-Based Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04904)
*Yiwen Wang,Jiahao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DCER is a unified multimodal fusion framework that tackles noise and missing modalities via dual-stage compression and energy-based reconstruction, achieving state-of-the-art results on MOSI, MOSEI, and CH-SIMS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust multimodal fusion faces degradation from noisy inputs and failures when modalities are missing; a unified approach is needed.

Method: Dual-stage compression: (1) within-modality frequency transforms (audio wavelet, video DCT) to denoise while preserving task-relevant patterns; (2) cross-modality bottleneck tokens to enforce genuine integration rather than modality-specific shortcuts. For missing modalities: energy-based reconstruction via gradient descent on a learned energy function; the final energy provides intrinsic uncertainty quantification (rho > 0.72 correlation with prediction error).

Result: State-of-the-art on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, CH-SIMS; a U-shaped robustness pattern indicating fusion is most beneficial under complete or highly missing conditions.

Conclusion: DCER provides a unified framework addressing noise and missing modalities in multimodal fusion, with demonstrated strong performance and planned code release on GitHub.

Abstract: Multimodal fusion faces two robustness challenges: noisy inputs degrade representation quality, and missing modalities cause prediction failures. We propose DCER, a
  unified framework addressing both challenges through dual-stage compression and energy-based reconstruction. The compression stage operates at two levels:
  within-modality frequency transforms (wavelet for audio, DCT for video) remove noise while preserving task-relevant patterns, and cross-modality bottleneck tokens
  force genuine integration rather than modality-specific shortcuts. For missing modalities, energy-based reconstruction recovers representations via gradient descent
  on a learned energy function, with the final energy providing intrinsic uncertainty quantification (\r{ho} > 0.72 correlation with prediction error). Experiments on
  CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and CH-SIMS demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks, with a U-shaped robustness pattern favoring multimodal fusion at
  both complete and high-missing conditions. The code will be available on Github.

</details>


### [102] [LISA: Laplacian In-context Spectral Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04906)
*Julio Candanedo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LISA adapts Laplacian-based time-series models at inference using an observed prefix, combining delay-coordinate embeddings with Laplacian spectral learning to form diffusion-coordinate states and a frozen decoder, augmented by lightweight latent-space adapters (GP or Markov-like attention) to improve forecasting, especially under changing dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable inference-time adaptation for dynamical systems without retraining, leveraging nonparametric spectral methods to handle regime shifts and evolving dynamics.

Method: Use delay-coordinate embeddings and Laplacian spectral learning to construct diffusion-coordinate representations; apply a frozen nonlinear decoder for one-step prediction; introduce lightweight latent-space adapters based on Gaussian-process regression or an attention-like Markov operator over context windows to adapt predictions at inference.

Result: In forecasting and autoregressive rollout experiments, LISA outperforms the frozen baseline, with gains often largest when dynamics change, demonstrating effective in-context adaptation.

Conclusion: Links in-context adaptation with nonparametric spectral methods for dynamical systems, providing a practical framework for inference-time adaptation of Laplacian-based time-series models.

Abstract: We propose Laplacian In-context Spectral Analysis (LISA), a method for inference-time adaptation of Laplacian-based time-series models using only an observed prefix. LISA combines delay-coordinate embeddings and Laplacian spectral learning to produce diffusion-coordinate state representations, together with a frozen nonlinear decoder for one-step prediction. We introduce lightweight latent-space residual adapters based on either Gaussian-process regression or an attention-like Markov operator over context windows. Across forecasting and autoregressive rollout experiments, LISA improves over the frozen baseline and is often most beneficial under changing dynamics. This work links in-context adaptation to nonparametric spectral methods for dynamical systems.

</details>


### [103] [Physics as the Inductive Bias for Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04907)
*Jianhong Chen,Naichen Shi,Xubo Yue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Integrates partial physical knowledge into causal discovery for dynamical systems by modeling dynamics as an SDE with known drift (ODE dynamics) and learned diffusion (unknown causal effects), using a sparsity-regularized MLE to recover the causal graph with theoretical guarantees and improved robustness over purely data-driven baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal discovery in real dynamical systems is confounded by feedback, cycles, non-stationarity, and the limitations of acyclicity or equilibrium assumptions. Incorporating physics as inductive bias can improve identifiability, stability, and robustness.

Method: Model system evolution as a stochastic differential equation where the drift encodes known ODE dynamics and the diffusion captures unknown causal couplings. Develop a scalable sparsity-inducing maximum likelihood estimation algorithm that exploits causal graph structure for efficient parameter estimation and graph recovery.

Result: Empirical results on dynamical systems with diverse causal structures show improved causal graph recovery and more stable, physically consistent estimates compared to purely data-driven state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Partial physical knowledge acts as a valuable inductive bias for dynamical-system causal discovery, enabling better identifiability and robustness; the proposed framework provides theoretical guarantees for graph recovery alongside practical performance gains.

Abstract: Causal discovery is often a data-driven paradigm to analyze complex real-world systems. In parallel, physics-based models such as ordinary differential equations (ODEs) provide mechanistic structure for many dynamical processes. Integrating these paradigms potentially allows physical knowledge to act as an inductive bias, improving identifiability, stability, and robustness of causal discovery in dynamical systems. However, such integration remains challenging: real dynamical systems often exhibit feedback, cyclic interactions, and non-stationary data trend, while many widely used causal discovery methods are formulated under acyclicity or equilibrium-based assumptions. In this work, we propose an integrative causal discovery framework for dynamical systems that leverages partial physical knowledge as an inductive bias. Specifically, we model system evolution as a stochastic differential equation (SDE), where the drift term encodes known ODE dynamics and the diffusion term corresponds to unknown causal couplings beyond the prescribed physics. We develop a scalable sparsity-inducing MLE algorithm that exploits causal graph structure for efficient parameter estimation. Under mild conditions, we establish guarantees to recover the causal graph. Experiments on dynamical systems with diverse causal structures show that our approach improves causal graph recovery and produces more stable, physically consistent estimates than purely data-driven state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [104] [Temporal Pair Consistency for Variance-Reduced Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04908)
*Chika Maduabuchi,Jindong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC) is a lightweight variance-reduction technique for continuous-time generative models that couples velocity predictions at paired timesteps along the same probability path, reducing gradient variance without altering the model, path, or solver; it improves sample quality and efficiency in flow matching and extends to state-of-the-art pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Treating timesteps independently in diffusion/flow models yields high estimator variance and inefficient sampling. Prior approaches use trajectory regularization, smoothness penalties, or modified probability paths and solvers to mitigate this issue.

Method: Introduce Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC) that couples velocity predictions at paired times along the same trajectory, delivering a quadratic trajectory-coupled regularization. The approach operates at the estimator level without changing the model architecture, probability path, or solver. A theoretical analysis shows variance reduction while preserving the original flow-matching objective.

Result: In flow matching, TPC improves sample quality and efficiency on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet across resolutions, achieving lower FID at equal or lower computational cost than prior methods, and extends to modern pipelines with noise-augmented training, score-based denoising, and rectified flow.

Conclusion: TPC provides a simple, theoretically grounded variance-reduction mechanism for continuous-time generative models that can be adopted without modifying the model, path, or solver, and generalizes to contemporary SOTA-style pipelines.

Abstract: Continuous-time generative models, such as diffusion models, flow matching, and rectified flow, learn time-dependent vector fields but are typically trained with objectives that treat timesteps independently, leading to high estimator variance and inefficient sampling. Prior approaches mitigate this via explicit smoothness penalties, trajectory regularization, or modified probability paths and solvers. We introduce Temporal Pair Consistency (TPC), a lightweight variance-reduction principle that couples velocity predictions at paired timesteps along the same probability path, operating entirely at the estimator level without modifying the model architecture, probability path, or solver. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that TPC induces a quadratic, trajectory-coupled regularization that provably reduces gradient variance while preserving the underlying flow-matching objective. Instantiated within flow matching, TPC improves sample quality and efficiency across CIFAR-10 and ImageNet at multiple resolutions, achieving lower FID at identical or lower computational cost than prior methods, and extends seamlessly to modern SOTA-style pipelines with noise-augmented training, score-based denoising, and rectified flow.

</details>


### [105] [Learning Where It Matters: Geometric Anchoring for Robust Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04909)
*Youngjae Cho,Jongsuk Kim,Ji-Hoon Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GAPO introduces a dynamic, geometry-aware anchor for preference optimization in LLM alignment, replacing a fixed reference with an adversarial local perturbation of the current policy; it uses a gap-based weighting to improve robustness to noisy supervision while maintaining or improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed, static references in DPO can become miscalibrated as policies drift, causing distributional mismatch and amplifying spurious signals under noise. Reference-free methods avoid drift but lack constraints on reward updates; there is a need for adaptive, robust anchoring.

Method: Propose GAPO: replace the static reference with an adaptive anchor—an adversarial local perturbation of the current policy within a small radius. Introduce an adaptive reweighting of each preference pair based on local sensitivity. Define Anchor Gap as the reward discrepancy between the policy and its anchor; show under smoothness conditions that it approximates worst-case local margin degradation. Optimize a logistic objective weighted by the Anchor Gap to downweight brittle instances and emphasize robust signals.

Result: Empirically, GAPO yields improved robustness across diverse noise settings and either matches or surpasses standard LLM alignment and reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: GAPO provides a robust, adaptive preference optimization framework by using a geometry-aware anchor to mitigate miscalibration and brittle signals, with the Anchor Gap linking local perturbations to worst-case margin behavior.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and related methods align large language models from pairwise preferences by regularizing updates against a fixed reference policy. As the policy drifts, a static reference, however, can become increasingly miscalibrated, leading to distributional mismatch and amplifying spurious preference signals under noisy supervision. Conversely, reference-free variants avoid mismatch but often suffer from unconstrained reward drift. We propose Geometric Anchor Preference Optimization (GAPO), which replaces the fixed reference with a dynamic, geometry-aware anchor: an adversarial local perturbation of the current policy within a small radius that serves as a pessimistic baseline. This anchor enables an adaptive reweighting mechanism, modulating the importance of each preference pair based on its local sensitivity. We further introduce the Anchor Gap, the reward discrepancy between the policy and its anchor, and show under smoothness conditions that it approximates worst-case local margin degradation. Optimizing a logistic objective weighted by this gap downweights geometrically brittle instances while emphasizing robust preference signals. Across diverse noise settings, GAPO consistently improves robustness while matching or improving performance on standard LLM alignment and reasoning benchmarks.

</details>


### [106] [A logical re-conception of neural networks: Hamiltonian bitwise part-whole architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04911)
*E Bowen,R Granger,A Rodriguez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A graph-Hamiltonian framework that directly encodes relations in a low-precision, scalable architecture, blending symbolic and neural representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the difficulty of representing structured relations in standard ANNs and enable abductive, logic-like inference while maintaining computational efficiency.

Method: Encode arbitrary data as graphs with edges representing primitive pairwise relations. Introduce a graph-Hamiltonian operator that assigns energies to encodings; ground states satisfy all relation constraints among vertices. Computation uses very low-precision arithmetic and scales linearly with the number of edges. The system can process standard ANN tasks and yield symbolic-like relational representations; derive an equivalent subset of ANN operations.

Result: Ground states reflect consistent relation constraints; method identifies relational structures (e.g., part-of, next-to) and builds hierarchical representations enabling position-based encodings and abductive inferences. Demonstrates an equivalent set of ANN operations in a special case, suggesting a link to higher-level semantic representations.

Conclusion: The approach offers a hybrid symbolic–neural framework with low computational cost, capable of revealing structured relational encodings beyond conventional neural networks. It invites further tool integration, refinement, and expansion of relational primitives and learning capabilities.

Abstract: We introduce a simple initial working system in which relations (such as part-whole) are directly represented via an architecture with operating and learning rules fundamentally distinct from standard artificial neural network methods. Arbitrary data are straightforwardly encoded as graphs whose edges correspond to codes from a small fixed primitive set of elemental pairwise relations, such that simple relational encoding is not an add-on, but occurs intrinsically within the most basic components of the system. A novel graph-Hamiltonian operator calculates energies among these encodings, with ground states denoting simultaneous satisfaction of all relation constraints among graph vertices.
