The method solely uses radically low-precision arithmetic; computational cost is correspondingly low, and scales linearly with the number of edges in the data. The resulting unconventional architecture can process standard ANN examples, but also produces representations that exhibit characteristics of symbolic computation. Specifically, the method identifies simple logical relational structures in these data (part-of; next-to), building hierarchical representations that enable abductive inferential steps generating relational position-based encodings, rather than solely statistical representations. Notably, an equivalent set of ANN operations are derived, identifying a special case of embedded vector encodings that may constitute a useful approach to current work in higher-level semantic representation. The very simple current state of the implemented system invites additional tools and improvements.

</details>


### [107] [A$^2$-LLM: An End-to-end Conversational Audio Avatar Large Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04913)
*Xiaolin Hu,Hang Yuan,Xinzhu Sang,Binbin Yan,Zhou Yu,Cong Huang,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: End-to-end multimodal LLM (A^2-LLM) for conversational audio avatars that jointly reasons about language, prosody, and 3D facial motion, trained with FLAME-QA to align semantics with expressive dynamics; achieves expressive, real-time avatars (500 ms latency, 0.7 RTF).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome cascading AI pipelines' limitations (error propagation, latency, poor real-time performance) and to enrich emotional expression by integrating language, audio prosody, and facial motion in a single model.

Method: Develop an end-to-end framework (A^2-LLM) that jointly models language, audio prosody, and 3D facial motion; introduce FLAME-QA, a high-quality multimodal QA-based dataset that aligns semantic intent with expressive facial dynamics for training.

Result: Experimental results show superior emotional expressiveness of the avatar while maintaining real-time performance (500 ms latency, 0.7x real-time factor, 0.7 RTF).

Conclusion: End-to-end multimodal reasoning in A^2-LLM enables emotionally rich, responsive conversational avatars with efficient real-time performance, facilitated by the FLAME-QA dataset for alignment of intent and facial dynamics.

Abstract: Developing expressive and responsive conversational digital humans is a cornerstone of next-generation human-computer interaction. While large language models (LLMs) have significantly enhanced dialogue capabilities, most current systems still rely on cascaded architectures that connect independent modules. These pipelines are often plagued by accumulated errors, high latency, and poor real-time performance. Lacking access to the underlying conversational context, these pipelines inherently prioritize rigid lip-sync over emotional depth. To address these challenges, we propose A$^2$-LLM, an end-to-end conversational audio avatar large language model that jointly reasons about language, audio prosody, and 3D facial motion within a unified framework. To facilitate training, we introduce FLAME-QA, a high-quality multimodal dataset designed to align semantic intent with expressive facial dynamics within a QA format. By leveraging deep semantic understanding, A$^2$-LLM generates emotionally rich facial movements beyond simple lip-synchronization. Experimental results demonstrate that our system achieves superior emotional expressiveness while maintaining real-time efficiency (500 ms latency, 0.7 RTF).

</details>


### [108] [SLAY: Geometry-Aware Spherical Linearized Attention with Yat-Kernel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04915)
*Jose Miguel Luna,Taha Bouhsine,Krzysztof Choromanski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces SLAY, a linear-time attention variant using spherical Yat-kernel; achieves near-softmax performance with O(L) complexity and positive semidefinite random-feature approximation, outperforming Performers and Cosformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the quadratic cost of softmax attention while maintaining accuracy by enforcing a geometry-aware, positive, linear-time mechanism on the unit sphere.

Method: Constrain queries/keys to the unit sphere so attention depends on angular alignment; express the spherical Yat-kernel as a nonnegative mixture of polynomial-exponential product kernels via Bernstein's theorem; derive a strictly positive random-feature approximation enabling O(L) attention; prove positive definiteness and boundedness on the sphere; ensure nonnegative attention scores.

Result: Empirically, SLAY nearly matches standard softmax attention in performance while maintaining linear time/memory; consistently outperforms prior linear-time methods (Performers, Cosformers); claimed as the closest linear-time approximation to softmax to date.

Conclusion: SLAY enables scalable Transformers with minimal performance trade-offs by combining geometry-aware kernel design, positive random-feature approximation, and rigorous theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: We propose a new class of linear-time attention mechanisms based on a relaxed and computationally efficient formulation of the recently introduced E-Product, often referred to as the Yat-kernel (Bouhsine, 2025). The resulting interactions are geometry-aware and inspired by inverse-square interactions in physics. Our method, Spherical Linearized Attention with Yat Kernels (SLAY), constrains queries and keys to the unit sphere so that attention depends only on angular alignment. Using Bernstein's theorem, we express the spherical Yat-kernel as a nonnegative mixture of polynomial-exponential product kernels and derive a strictly positive random-feature approximation enabling linear-time O(L) attention. We establish positive definiteness and boundedness on the sphere and show that the estimator yields well-defined, nonnegative attention scores. Empirically, SLAY achieves performance that is nearly indistinguishable from standard softmax attention while retaining linear time and memory scaling, and consistently outperforms prior linear-time attention mechanisms such as Performers and Cosformers. To the best of our knowledge, SLAY represents the closest linear-time approximation to softmax attention reported to date, enabling scalable Transformers without the typical performance trade-offs of attention linearization.

</details>


### [109] [Multi-Aspect Mining and Anomaly Detection for Heterogeneous Tensor Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04917)
*Soshi Kakio,Yasuko Matsubara,Ren Fujiwara,Yasushi Sakurai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HeteroComp introduces a Gaussian-process-based framework to summarize heterogeneous timestamped tensor streams into latent components and their temporal dynamics, enabling accurate group anomaly detection while avoiding discretization and handling both continuous and categorical attributes jointly; achieves group anomaly detection with computational efficiency independent of stream length.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional tensor methods struggle with heterogeneous streams that mix categorical and continuous attributes and with detecting group-level (temporal) anomalies. Discretizing continuous attributes or treating categories as numbers distorts distributions, and time-discretization loses temporal dynamics essential for identifying collective anomalies such as DoS attacks.

Method: Model continuous attributes and their temporal dynamics with Gaussian process priors to directly estimate probability densities from data. Represent each attribute via latent components corresponding to latent groups and track their temporal evolution to detect group anomalies. Provide a compact summary through extracted components.

Result: Extensive experiments on real datasets show HeteroComp outperforms state-of-the-art group anomaly detection methods in accuracy, with computational time independent of the data stream length.

Conclusion: HeteroComp offers a principled, scalable approach for heterogeneous tensor streams, jointly modeling continuous distributions and temporal dynamics to enable effective group anomaly detection without heavy discretization or incorrect distributional assumptions.

Abstract: Analysis and anomaly detection in event tensor streams consisting of timestamps and multiple attributes - such as communication logs(time, IP address, packet length)- are essential tasks in data mining. While existing tensor decomposition and anomaly detection methods provide useful insights, they face the following two limitations. (i) They cannot handle heterogeneous tensor streams, which comprises both categorical attributes(e.g., IP address) and continuous attributes(e.g., packet length). They typically require either discretizing continuous attributes or treating categorical attributes as continuous, both of which distort the underlying statistical properties of the data.Furthermore, incorrect assumptions about the distribution family of continuous attributes often degrade the model's performance. (ii) They discretize timestamps, failing to track the temporal dynamics of streams(e.g., trends, abnormal events), which makes them ineffective for detecting anomalies at the group level, referred to as 'group anomalies' (e.g, DoS attacks). To address these challenges, we propose HeteroComp, a method for continuously summarizing heterogeneous tensor streams into 'components' representing latent groups in each attribute and their temporal dynamics, and detecting group anomalies. Our method employs Gaussian process priors to model unknown distributions of continuous attributes, and temporal dynamics, which directly estimate probability densities from data. Extracted components give concise but effective summarization, enabling accurate group anomaly detection. Extensive experiments on real datasets demonstrate that HeteroComp outperforms the state-of-the-art algorithms for group anomaly detection accuracy, and its computational time does not depend on the data stream length.

</details>


### [110] [Simulated Adoption: Decoupling Magnitude and Direction in LLM In-Context Conflict Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04918)
*Long Zhang,Fangwei Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit in-context compliance through a geometric mechanism called Orthogonal Interference, not global magnitude dilution, across multiple models; residual updates induced by counterfactual prompts rotate representations via a steering vector quasi-orthogonal to the ground-truth direction, challenging scalar hallucination metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the mechanistic basis of sycophancy in LLMs—whether commensurate compliance arises from reduced signal magnitude (dilation) or geometric reorientation in the residual stream during conflicting in-context information, and how this manifests across different architectures.

Method: Layer-wise geometric analysis on Qwen-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, and GLM-4-9B. Decompose residual-stream updates caused by counterfactual contexts into radial (norm-based) and angular (cosine-based) components, compare norms and directional shifts, and test the universality of proposed dilution versus interference hypotheses.

Result: Manifold Dilution is not universal: two of three models maintain stable residual norms despite performance degradation on factual queries. Compliance is consistently characterized by Orthogonal Interference: the counterfactual context injects a steering vector nearly orthogonal to the ground-truth direction, rotating the hidden state rather than reducing magnitude. Models neither unlearn nor dampen the true information; instead they geometrically displace representations to simulate adoption while preserving magnitude.

Conclusion: Scalar confidence metrics for hallucination detection are insufficient; effective detection requires vectorial, directional monitoring of the residual stream to distinguish genuine knowledge integration from superficial in-context mimicry.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently prioritize conflicting in-context information over pre-existing parametric memory, a phenomenon often termed sycophancy or compliance. However, the mechanistic realization of this behavior remains obscure, specifically how the model resolves these knowledge conflicts through compliance, and whether this suppression arises from signal magnitude dilution or directional geometric alteration within the residual stream. To resolve this, we conducted a layer-wise geometric analysis across Qwen-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, and GLM-4-9B, decomposing the residual stream updates induced by counter-factual contexts into radial (norm-based) and angular (cosine-based) components. Our empirical results reject the universality of the "Manifold Dilution" hypothesis, as two of the three architectures maintained stable residual norms despite exhibiting significant performance degradation on factual queries. Instead, we observed that compliance is consistently characterized by "Orthogonal Interference," where the conflicting context injects a steering vector that is quasi-orthogonal to the ground-truth direction, effectively rotating the hidden state representation. This suggests that models do not "unlearn" or suppress the magnitude of internal truths but rather employ a mechanism of geometric displacement to bypass the correct unembedding vector, effectively simulating adoption while preserving the original structural magnitude. These findings challenge scalar confidence metrics for detecting hallucinations and underscore the necessity of vectorial monitoring to distinguish between genuine knowledge integration and superficial in-context mimicry.

</details>


### [111] [Gradually Compacting Large Language Models for Reasoning Like a Boiling Frog](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04919)
*Yiran Zhao,Shengyang Zhou,Zijian Wu,Tongyan Hu,Yuhui Xu,Rengan Dou,Kenji Kawaguchi,Shafiq Joty,Junnan Li,Michael Qizhe Shieh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Prune-Tune Loop (PTL), an iterative, fine-grained pruning framework that gradually compresses LLMs while recovering performance through staged fine-tuning. It achieves ~50% size reduction with lightweight post-training and maintains reasoning performance, showing flexibility across pruning strategies (neuron, layer) and post-training methods (continual pre-training, RL) and applicability beyond math reasoning (e.g., code generation).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational resources and memory footprint of large language models without sacrificing reasoning capability. Conventional pruning causes abrupt performance drops and long post-training, motivating a gradual, iterative approach that allows progressive adaptation.

Method: Decompose compression into multiple small iterations. At each stage, apply a Prune-Tune Loop (PTL): prune a portion of parameters, then fine-tune to recover lost performance; supports multiple pruning strategies (neuron, layer) and various post-training methods (continual pre-training, reinforcement learning). The process is designed to be incremental to avoid sharp performance degradation (boiling frog effect).

Result: Empirical evaluation indicates near-half-size compression with lightweight post-training while preserving reasoning performance comparable to the original model on several tasks. PTL also demonstrates effectiveness on tasks beyond mathematics, such as code generation, indicating broad applicability and robustness across strategies and post-training approaches.

Conclusion: PTL provides a flexible, incremental compression paradigm that mitigates performance loss and reduces training resource demands. Its compatibility with multiple pruning strategies and post-training methods suggests wide applicability for deploying compact, capable LLMs across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning capabilities, but their substantial size often demands significant computational resources. To reduce resource consumption and accelerate inference, it is essential to eliminate redundant parameters without compromising performance. However, conventional pruning methods that directly remove such parameters often lead to a dramatic drop in model performance in reasoning tasks, and require extensive post-training to recover the lost capabilities. In this work, we propose a gradual compacting method that divides the compression process into multiple fine-grained iterations, applying a Prune-Tune Loop (PTL) at each stage to incrementally reduce model size while restoring performance with finetuning. This iterative approach-reminiscent of the "boiling frog" effect-enables the model to be progressively compressed without abrupt performance loss. Experimental results show that PTL can compress LLMs to nearly half their original size with only lightweight post-training, while maintaining performance comparable to the original model on reasoning tasks. Moreover, PTL is flexible and can be applied to various pruning strategies, such as neuron pruning and layer pruning, as well as different post-training methods, including continual pre-training and reinforcement learning. Additionally, experimental results confirm the effectiveness of PTL on a variety of tasks beyond mathematical reasoning, such as code generation, demonstrating its broad applicability.

</details>


### [112] [CyIN: Cyclic Informative Latent Space for Bridging Complete and Incomplete Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04920)
*Ronghao Lin,Qiaolin He,Sijie Mai,Ying Zeng,Aolin Xiong,Li Huang,Yap-Peng Tan,Haifeng Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A cyclic informative learning framework (CyIN) for complete and incomplete multimodal learning using token- and label-level Information Bottleneck (IB) and cross-modal cyclic translation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real-world settings, multimodal inputs are often incomplete or missing entirely. Existing models trained on perfectly paired data suffer significant performance drops under such conditions; a unified approach is needed to handle both complete and incomplete multimodal data robustly.

Method: CyIN constructs an informative latent space by applying token- and label-level Information Bottleneck (IB) cyclically across modalities, using variational approximation to extract task-relevant features and purify latent representations. It also performs cross-modal cyclic translation to reconstruct missing modalities from the remaining ones via forward and reverse propagation. The model jointly optimizes complete and incomplete multimodal learning within a single framework.

Result: Experiments on four multimodal datasets show that CyIN achieves superior performance in both fully paired and various incomplete modality scenarios.

Conclusion: CyIN effectively bridges complete and incomplete multimodal learning by leveraging IB-driven informative latents and bidirectional cross-modal reconstruction, enabling robust, unified multimodal modeling.

Abstract: Multimodal machine learning, mimicking the human brain's ability to integrate various modalities has seen rapid growth. Most previous multimodal models are trained on perfectly paired multimodal input to reach optimal performance. In real-world deployments, however, the presence of modality is highly variable and unpredictable, causing the pre-trained models in suffering significant performance drops and fail to remain robust with dynamic missing modalities circumstances. In this paper, we present a novel Cyclic INformative Learning framework (CyIN) to bridge the gap between complete and incomplete multimodal learning. Specifically, we firstly build an informative latent space by adopting token- and label-level Information Bottleneck (IB) cyclically among various modalities. Capturing task-related features with variational approximation, the informative bottleneck latents are purified for more efficient cross-modal interaction and multimodal fusion. Moreover, to supplement the missing information caused by incomplete multimodal input, we propose cross-modal cyclic translation by reconstruct the missing modalities with the remained ones through forward and reverse propagation process. With the help of the extracted and reconstructed informative latents, CyIN succeeds in jointly optimizing complete and incomplete multimodal learning in one unified model. Extensive experiments on 4 multimodal datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our method in both complete and diverse incomplete scenarios.

</details>


### [113] [Imposing Boundary Conditions on Neural Operators via Learned Function Extensions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04923)
*Sepehr Mousavi,Siddhartha Mishra,Laura De Lorenzis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A general framework to condition neural operators on complex non-homogeneous boundary conditions by mapping boundary data to latent pseudo-extensions over the entire domain, enabling standard operator architectures to learn boundary-influenced solutions. Demonstrated on 18 challenging PDE datasets across Poisson, linear elasticity, and hyperelasticity with diverse BCs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators struggle with highly variable boundary conditions and sensitivity to boundary forcings; there is a need for a general, plug-in approach to incorporate complex BCs into operator learning.

Method: Introduce boundary-to-domain extensions: transform boundary data into latent extensions defined over the full domain, then feed these alongside input domain functions into an arbitrary domain-to-domain neural operator; this enables learning rich BC dependencies without changing existing architectures. Benchmark on 18 datasets with mixed-type, component-wise, multi-segment BCs across diverse geometries.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, with large performance gains over baselines; robust across geometry and BC complexity; requires no hyperparameter tuning across datasets.

Conclusion: Learning boundary-to-domain extensions is an effective, practical strategy to impose complex BCs within neural operator frameworks, broadening applicability to PDE problems.

Abstract: Neural operators have emerged as powerful surrogates for the solution of partial differential equations (PDEs), yet their ability to handle general, highly variable boundary conditions (BCs) remains limited. Existing approaches often fail when the solution operator exhibits strong sensitivity to boundary forcings. We propose a general framework for conditioning neural operators on complex non-homogeneous BCs through function extensions. Our key idea is to map boundary data to latent pseudo-extensions defined over the entire spatial domain, enabling any standard operator learning architecture to consume boundary information. The resulting operator, coupled with an arbitrary domain-to-domain neural operator, can learn rich dependencies on complex BCs and input domain functions at the same time. To benchmark this setting, we construct 18 challenging datasets spanning Poisson, linear elasticity, and hyperelasticity problems, with highly variable, mixed-type, component-wise, and multi-segment BCs on diverse geometries. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, outperforming baselines by large margins, while requiring no hyperparameter tuning across datasets. Overall, our results demonstrate that learning boundary-to-domain extensions is an effective and practical strategy for imposing complex BCs in existing neural operator frameworks, enabling accurate and robust scientific machine learning models for a broader range of PDE-governed problems.

</details>


### [114] [Knowing When to Answer: Adaptive Confidence Refinement for Reliable Audio-Visual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04924)
*Dinh Phu Tran,Jihoon Jeong,Saad Wazir,Seongah Kim,Thao Do,Cem Subakan,Daeyoung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ACR enhances Reliable Audio-Visual Question Answering by post-hoc refinement of MSP confidence via two learned heads (Residual Risk Head and Confidence Gating Head) to enable input-adaptive abstention, yielding improved reliability across distributions and biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AVQA models are accurate but lack reliable uncertainty estimates to know when they are likely wrong; abstention (rejecting to answer) is desired but underexplored, especially under distribution shifts and biases; MSP is not Bayes-optimal in typical neural networks due to poor calibration, motivating more robust confidence mechanisms.

Method: Introduce Adaptive Confidence Refinement (ACR) that preserves MSP as the primary confidence signal and applies input-adaptive residual corrections. ACR adds two learned heads: (i) Residual Risk Head predicting low-magnitude correctness residuals not captured by MSP, and (ii) Confidence Gating Head assessing MSP trustworthiness. The approach is evaluated on three AVQA architectures under in/distribution, out-of-distribution, and data bias settings, with theoretical analysis supporting the method.

Result: ACR consistently outperforms existing methods in reliability and abstention across in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and biased data settings on three AVQA architectures, with supportive theoretical analysis.

Conclusion: ACR provides a solid, lightweight foundation for Reliable AVQA, enhancing MSP with input-adaptive corrections and gating to improve abstention decisions across diverse conditions; code will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: We present a formal problem formulation for \textit{Reliable} Audio-Visual Question Answering ($\mathcal{R}$-AVQA), where we prefer abstention over answering incorrectly. While recent AVQA models have high accuracy, their ability to identify when they are likely wrong and their consequent abstention from answering remain underexplored areas of research. To fill this gap, we explore several approaches and then propose Adaptive Confidence Refinement (ACR), a lightweight method to further enhance the performance of $\mathcal{R}$-AVQA. Our key insight is that the Maximum Softmax Probability (MSP) is Bayes-optimal only under strong calibration, a condition usually not met in deep neural networks, particularly in multimodal models. Instead of replacing MSP, our ACR maintains it as a primary confidence signal and applies input-adaptive residual corrections when MSP is deemed unreliable. ACR introduces two learned heads: i) a Residual Risk Head that predicts low-magnitude correctness residuals that MSP does not capture, and ii) a Confidence Gating Head to determine MSP trustworthiness. Our experiments and theoretical analysis show that ACR consistently outperforms existing methods on in- and out-of-disrtibution, and data bias settings across three different AVQA architectures, establishing a solid foundation for $\mathcal{R}$-AVQA task. The code and checkpoints will be available upon acceptance \href{https://github.com/PhuTran1005/R-AVQA}{at here}

</details>


### [115] [Internalizing LLM Reasoning via Discovery and Replay of Latent Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04925)
*Zhenning Shi,Yijia Zhu,Junhan Shi,Xun Zhang,Lei Wang,Congcong Miao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STIR introduces dynamic latent trajectory control to internalize reasoning within model states, using a three-stage pipeline to learn steering primitives, build a compact tool library, and modulate trajectories for context-specific gains, yielding accuracy improvements and resource savings over vanilla decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation-steering methods rely on static control vectors, which fail to adapt to non-stationary reasoning tasks. There is a need to internalize reasoning processes into latent states and dynamically guide them to improve correctness and efficiency without explicit chain-of-thought generation.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) differential intrinsic action induction to harvest latent reasoning successes and crystallize steering primitives; (2) sparse control basis construction to curate a compact, geometrically diverse library of tools; (3) value-modulated trajectory intervention that injects context-specific impulses via anchor-based gating, enabling dynamic internal reasoning control during decoding.

Result: Empirical evaluation on six arithmetic and logical benchmarks across four models shows STIR improves average accuracy by 1.9% to 7.5% and reduces average token consumption by up to 35% compared with vanilla decoding.

Conclusion: Dynamic latent trajectory control can realize the benefits of explicit chain-of-thought by internalizing the reasoning process, bypassing explicit generation while achieving higher fidelity and efficiency. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: The internalization of chain-of-thought processes into hidden states has emerged as a highly efficient paradigm for scaling test-time compute. However, existing activation steering methods rely on static control vectors that fail to adapt to the non-stationary evolution of complex reasoning tasks. To address this limitation, we propose STIR (Self-Distilled Tools for Internal Reasoning), a framework that reformulates reasoning enhancement as a dynamic latent trajectory control problem. STIR introduces a synergistic three-stage pipeline: (1) differential intrinsic action induction harvests latent reasoning successes to crystallize steering primitives; (2) sparse control basis construction curates a compact, geometrically diverse tool library; and (3) value-modulated trajectory intervention dynamically injects context-specific impulses via anchor-based gating. Extensive experiments on six arithmetic and logical benchmarks across four representative models demonstrate that STIR improves average accuracy by 1.9% to 7.5% while reducing average token consumption by up to 35% compared to vanilla decoding. These findings demonstrate that the benefits of explicit chain-of-thought can be realized through dynamic latent trajectory control, internalizing the reasoning process to bypass the explicit generation while achieving superior fidelity. Our code is available at https://github.com/sznnzs/LLM-Latent-Action.

</details>


### [116] [Euphonium: Steering Video Flow Matching via Process Reward Gradient Guided Stochastic Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04928)
*Ruizhe Zhong,Jiesong Lian,Xiaoyue Mi,Zixiang Zhou,Yuan Zhou,Qinglin Lu,Junchi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Euphonium integrates reward-gradient guided dynamics into a stochastic differential equation to steer flow-model sampling, improving alignment and training efficiency, with a distillation objective to embed guidance into the model; demonstrated on text-to-video with 1.66x faster convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current online RL for flow-matching models relies on undirected stochasticity and sparse rewards, leading to inefficient exploration and slow optimization. A principled gradient-guided sampling framework is proposed to efficiently locate high-reward regions.

Method: Formulate sampling as a stochastic differential equation whose drift term includes the gradient of a Process Reward Model. Derive a distillation objective to absorb the guidance into the flow network, removing inference-time reward-model dependence. Instantiate with Dual-Reward Group Relative Policy Optimization (DR-GRPO), combining latent process rewards for credit assignment with pixel-level outcome rewards for fidelity.

Result: Empirical evaluation on text-to-video generation shows Euphonium achieves better alignment with existing methods and accelerates training convergence by 1.66x.

Conclusion: The framework generalizes and subsumes existing sampling methods (Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO) as special cases, offering a principled approach with practical training-time benefits; code is available at the provided repository.

Abstract: While online Reinforcement Learning has emerged as a crucial technique for aligning flow matching models with human preferences, current approaches are hindered by inefficient exploration during training rollouts. Relying on undirected stochasticity and sparse outcome rewards, these methods struggle to discover high-reward samples, resulting in data-inefficient and slow optimization. To address these limitations, we propose Euphonium, a novel framework that steers generation via process reward gradient guided dynamics. Our key insight is to formulate the sampling process as a theoretically principled Stochastic Differential Equation that explicitly incorporates the gradient of a Process Reward Model into the flow drift. This design enables dense, step-by-step steering toward high-reward regions, advancing beyond the unguided exploration in prior works, and theoretically encompasses existing sampling methods (e.g., Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO) as special cases. We further derive a distillation objective that internalizes the guidance signal into the flow network, eliminating inference-time dependency on the reward model. We instantiate this framework with a Dual-Reward Group Relative Policy Optimization algorithm, combining latent process rewards for efficient credit assignment with pixel-level outcome rewards for final visual fidelity. Experiments on text-to-video generation show that Euphonium achieves better alignment compared to existing methods while accelerating training convergence by 1.66x. Our code is available at https://github.com/zerzerzerz/Euphonium

</details>


### [117] [TurboBoA: Faster and Exact Attention-aware Quantization without Backpropagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04929)
*Junhan Kim,Yeo Jeong Park,Seungwoo Son,Chungman Lee,Ho-young Kim,Joonyoung Kim,Yongkweon Jeon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TurboBoA is a backpropagation-free PTQ method that jointly quantizes multiple out-channels with a closed-form error compensation rule, adds an error-propagation correction mechanism, and uses adaptive grid computation with coordinate descent refinement. It delivers over 3x speedup vs BoA while improving accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art results (with outlier suppression) for weight-only and weight-activation PTQ.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training quantization (PTQ) for large language models is needed to reduce memory and compute. GPTQ’s layer-wise independence hurts accuracy in low-bit regimes, and BoA’s inter-layer dependencies improve accuracy but require sequential quantization, hindering efficiency. TurboBoA aims to retain BoA’s accuracy gains while removing sequential bottlenecks to accelerate the process.

Method: Backpropagation-free PTQ. (i) Joint quantization of multiple out-channels with a closed-form error compensation rule to reduce sequential bottlenecks; (ii) a correction mechanism for errors propagated from preceding quantized layers; (iii) adaptive grid computation with coordinate descent refinement to maintain alignment during iterative updates.

Result: Extensive experiments show substantial acceleration over BoA and improved accuracy. When combined with outlier suppression, TurboBoA achieves state-of-the-art results in both weight-only and weight-activation quantization.

Conclusion: TurboBoA provides a fast, accurate PTQ framework for large LLMs by preserving BoA’s accuracy benefits while removing sequential quantization bottlenecks, enabling practical quantization of billion-scale models; code will be released.

Abstract: The rapid growth of large language models (LLMs) has heightened the importance of post-training quantization (PTQ) for reducing memory and computation costs. Among PTQ methods, GPTQ has gained significant attention for its efficiency, enabling billion-scale LLMs to be quantized within a few GPU hours. However, GPTQ's assumption of layer-wise independence leads to severe accuracy drops in low-bit regimes. Recently, BoA improved upon GPTQ by incorporating inter-layer dependencies within attention modules, but its reliance on sequential quantization across all out-channels makes it substantially less efficient. In this paper, we propose TurboBoA, a new backpropagation-free PTQ algorithm that preserves the accuracy benefits of BoA while significantly accelerating the process. The proposed TurboBoA introduces three key innovations: (i) joint quantization of multiple out-channels with a closed-form error compensation rule, which reduces sequential bottlenecks and yields more than a three-fold speedup; (ii) a correction mechanism for errors propagated from preceding quantized layers; and (iii) adaptive grid computation with coordinate descent refinement to maintain alignment during iterative updates. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TurboBoA delivers substantial acceleration over BoA while consistently improving accuracy. When combined with outlier suppression techniques, it achieves state-of-the-art results in both weight-only and weight-activation quantization. The code will be available at https://github.com/SamsungLabs/TurboBoA.

</details>


### [118] [Depth-Wise Emergence of Prediction-Centric Geometry in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04931)
*Shahar Haim,Daniel C McNamee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decoder-only LLMs show a depth-wise shift from context-processing to prediction-formation, driven by a structured late-layer geometric code where angular organization governs prediction similarity and norms encode context without determining the exact token, enabling targeted causal control over predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the mechanistic and geometric principles by which contextual information is transformed into token predictions in LLMs, and to identify how geometry constrains causal interventions on predictions.

Method: A unified framework combining geometric analysis with mechanistic interventions across layers; analysis of representation geometry (angles and norms) and causal perturbations to reveal how late-layer codes influence prediction distributions.

Result: Late-layer representations implement a structured geometric code enabling selective causal control over token prediction; angular geometry parametrizes prediction distributional similarity; representation norms encode context-specific information that does not determine predictions.

Conclusion: The work provides a mechanistic-geometric account of context-to-prediction transformation in decoder-only LLMs, linking depth, geometry, and causality in the predictive process.

Abstract: We show that decoder-only large language models exhibit a depth-wise transition from context-processing to prediction-forming phases of computation accompanied by a reorganization of representational geometry. Using a unified framework combining geometric analysis with mechanistic intervention, we demonstrate that late-layer representations implement a structured geometric code that enables selective causal control over token prediction. Specifically, angular organization of the representation geometry parametrizes prediction distributional similarity, while representation norms encode context-specific information that does not determine prediction. Together, these results provide a mechanistic-geometric account of the dynamics of transforming context into predictions in LLMs.

</details>


### [119] [Comparing Euclidean and Hyperbolic K-Means for Generalized Category Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04932)
*Mohamad Dalal,Thomas B. Moeslund,Joakim Bruslund Haurum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: End-to-end hyperbolic clustering for generalized category discovery (GCD) via Lorentz hyperboloid embeddings and hyperbolic K-Means; achieves competitive accuracy with state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD, with Euclidean clustering lagging behind, and shows dataset-dependent effects of norm clipping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address suboptimality when clustering is performed in Euclidean space after hyperbolic representation learning; hypothesis: keeping clustering in hyperbolic space improves accuracy and stability, especially for unseen classes in GCD.

Method: Learn embeddings in the Lorentz hyperboloid model and perform clustering directly in hyperbolic space using a hyperbolic K-Means algorithm. Evaluate on Semantic Shift Benchmark datasets and compare against prior hyperbolic GCD methods and Euclidean clustering baselines. Conduct ablations on norm clipping of Euclidean embeddings and on clustering granularity.

Result: HC-GCD matches the performance of the previous state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD method. Hyperbolic K-Means yields higher clustering accuracy than Euclidean K-Means. Ablations show clipping the Euclidean embedding norms hurts clustering unseen classes (but may help seen classes); overall accuracy is dataset-dependent. Hyperbolic K-Means also produces more consistent clusters across varying label granularity.

Conclusion: End-to-end hyperbolic clustering in the Lorentz model can offer advantages over transforming to Euclidean space for clustering in GCD, improving accuracy and cluster stability with respect to label granularity; results motivate broader use of hyperbolic clustering in open-world recognition tasks and warrant further exploration across datasets and model variants.

Abstract: Hyperbolic representation learning has been widely used to extract implicit hierarchies within data, and recently it has found its way to the open-world classification task of Generalized Category Discovery (GCD). However, prior hyperbolic GCD methods only use hyperbolic geometry for representation learning and transform back to Euclidean geometry when clustering. We hypothesize this is suboptimal. Therefore, we present Hyperbolic Clustered GCD (HC-GCD), which learns embeddings in the Lorentz Hyperboloid model of hyperbolic geometry, and clusters these embeddings directly in hyperbolic space using a hyperbolic K-Means algorithm. We test our model on the Semantic Shift Benchmark datasets, and demonstrate that HC-GCD is on par with the previous state-of-the-art hyperbolic GCD method. Furthermore, we show that using hyperbolic K-Means leads to better accuracy than Euclidean K-Means. We carry out ablation studies showing that clipping the norm of the Euclidean embeddings leads to decreased accuracy in clustering unseen classes, and increased accuracy for seen classes, while the overall accuracy is dataset dependent. We also show that using hyperbolic K-Means leads to more consistent clusters when varying the label granularity.

</details>


### [120] [Privileged Information Distillation for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04942)
*Emiliano Penaloza,Dheeraj Vattikonda,Nicolas Gontier,Alexandre Lacoste,Laurent Charlin,Massimo Caccia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies distilling frontier models using training-time privileged information (PI) to action-only policies; it introduces π-Distill, a joint PI-conditioned teacher and unconditioned student training objective, and OPSD, an RL method with a reverse KL penalty to the PI-conditioned teacher. Both methods aim to transfer PI-based capabilities to inference without PI; they outperform standard supervised finetuning + RL that relies on Chain-of-Thought supervision, across multiple benchmarks. The work also analyzes factors that enable PI learning and when OPSD is competitive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transfer of training-time privileged information (PI) to inference-time policies where PI is hidden (e.g., frontier models with observable action trajectories but inaccessible internal reasoning). In multi-turn, agentic environments, standard distillation fails when the reasoning process is not observable; need methods that exploit PI without direct CoT supervision.

Method: Introduce π-Distill: a joint objective that trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student simultaneously using the same model, using action trajectories as supervision. Introduce OPSD (On-Policy Self-Distillation): RL-based approach with a reverse KL penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher, encouraging the student to imitate the teacher's policy under PI.

Result: Empirically, both π-Distill and, in some cases, OPSD effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI, outperforming standard practice—supervised finetuning followed by RL—that assumes access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision, across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and PI forms.

Conclusion: Training-time PI can be successfully distilled into PI-free policies via specialized objectives (π-Distill) or RL with PI-aware regularization (OPSD). The work provides extensive analysis of the factors that enable PI learning and clarifies the regimes where OPSD is competitive.

Abstract: Training-time privileged information (PI) can enable language models to succeed on tasks they would otherwise fail, making it a powerful tool for reinforcement learning in hard, long-horizon settings. However, transferring capabilities learned with PI to policies that must act without it at inference time remains a fundamental challenge. We study this problem in the context of distilling frontier models for multi-turn agentic environments, where closed-source systems typically hide their internal reasoning and expose only action trajectories. This breaks standard distillation pipelines, since successful behavior is observable but the reasoning process is not. For this, we introduce π-Distill, a joint teacher-student objective that trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student simultaneously using the same model. Additionally, we also introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), an alternative approach that trains using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a reverse KL-penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher. We show that both of these algorithms effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI. Specifically we find that π-Distill and in some cases OPSD, outperform industry standard practices (Supervised finetuning followed by RL) that assume access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and forms of PI. We complement our results with extensive analysis that characterizes the factors enabling effective learning with PI, focusing primarily on π-Distill and characterizing when OPSD is competitive.

</details>


### [121] [Near-Optimal Dynamic Matching via Coarsening with Application to Heart Transplantation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04989)
*Itai Zilberstein,Ioannis Anagnostides,Zachary W. Sollie,Arman Kilic,Tuomas Sandholm*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Coarsening-based online matching with capacitated clustering yields near-optimal guarantees; applied to heart transplant allocation with strong empirical performance (competitive ratio 0.91 vs 0.51 baseline).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between practical online matching algorithms and theoretical guarantees; leverage coarsening to reduce granularity while preserving performance; extend to organ allocation using historical data structure.

Method: Propose online matching algorithms built on aggregating offline nodes into capacitated clusters; provide theoretical analysis showing near-optimal guarantees; apply to heart transplant allocation using historical data properties; validate with simulations on real data.

Result: The coarsening approach yields near-optimal guarantees; policy closely tracks omniscient benchmark with competitive ratio 0.91; greatly surpasses US status quo policy at 0.51 in simulations.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that coarsening can maintain granularity and deliver strong guarantees; bridges data-driven heuristics with worst-case bounds; shows practical value in organ allocation and online matching in general.

Abstract: Online matching has been a mainstay in domains such as Internet advertising and organ allocation, but practical algorithms often lack strong theoretical guarantees. We take an important step toward addressing this by developing new online matching algorithms based on a coarsening approach. Although coarsening typically implies a loss of granularity, we show that, to the contrary, aggregating offline nodes into capacitated clusters can yield near-optimal theoretical guarantees. We apply our methodology to heart transplant allocation to develop theoretically grounded policies based on structural properties of historical data. Furthermore, in simulations based on real data, our policy closely matches the performance of the omniscient benchmark, achieving competitive ratio 0.91, drastically higher than the US status quo policy's 0.51. Our work bridges the gap between data-driven heuristics and pessimistic theoretical lower bounds.

</details>


### [122] [EntRGi: Entropy Aware Reward Guidance for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05000)
*Atula Tejaswi,Litu Rout,Constantine Caramanis,Sanjay Shakkottai,Sujay Sanghavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces EntRGi, an entropy-aware reward guidance mechanism for discrete diffusion language models that modulates gradients from a reward model based on the model's confidence, addressing issues with continuous relaxations and straight-through estimators; demonstrated improvements on a 7B model across multiple rewards and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward-guided optimization is effective for continuous diffusion models, but for discrete language modeling the non-differentiability of outputs hinders gradient-based reward signals. Prior approaches either convert tokens to continuous relaxations (which mismatches reward-model training) or rely on straight-through estimators (leading to biased updates). A need exists for a gradient mechanism that preserves reward feedback while respecting discreteness.

Method: Propose EntRGi (Entropy aware Reward Guidance): dynamically regulate gradients from the reward model by modulating the continuous relaxation using the model’s confidence (entropy) in its predictions. This mitigates poor gradient feedback from relaxations and prevents incorrect updates from undesired token discreteness. Implemented in a 7B-parameter diffusion LM and evaluated with three distinct reward models across three multi-skill benchmarks, comparing against state-of-the-art discrete reward-guidance baselines.

Result: Empirical results show consistent improvements over strong baselines across all tested settings (3 reward models, 3 benchmarks) with a 7B-parameter diffusion LM, demonstrating robust gains from entropy-aware modulation of reward gradients.

Conclusion: Entropy-aware reward guidance (EntRGi) effectively bridges the gap between discrete token outputs and differentiable training signals, yielding more reliable and informative reward gradients for discrete diffusion LMs and achieving consistent performance gains over existing methods.

Abstract: Reward guidance has been applied to great success in the test-time adaptation of continuous diffusion models; it updates each denoising step using the gradients from a downstream reward model. We study reward guidance for discrete diffusion language models, where one cannot differentiate through the natural outputs of the model because they are discrete tokens. Existing approaches either replace these discrete tokens with continuous relaxations, or employ techniques like the straight-through estimator. In this work, we show the downsides of both these methods. The former degrades gradient feedback because the reward model has never been trained with continuous inputs. The latter involves incorrect optimization because the gradient evaluated at discrete tokens is used to update continuous logits. Our key innovation is to go beyond this tradeoff by introducing a novel mechanism called EntRGi: Entropy aware Reward Guidance that dynamically regulates the gradients from the reward model. By modulating the continuous relaxation using the model's confidence, our approach substantially improves reward guidance while providing reliable inputs to the reward model. We empirically validate our approach on a 7B-parameter diffusion language model across 3 diverse reward models and 3 multi-skill benchmarks, showing consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [123] [Formal Synthesis of Certifiably Robust Neural Lyapunov-Barrier Certificates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05311)
*Chengxiao Wang,Haoze Wu,Gagandeep Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes robust neural Lyapunov and barrier certificates to certify safety and stability of RL controllers under bounded dynamical perturbations; uses adversarial training, Lipschitz bounds, and global Lipschitz regularization; validated on Inverted Pendulum and 2D Docking; reports up to 4.6x improvement in certified robustness and up to 2.4x improvement in empirical success under perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neural certificates for Lyapunov/barrier-based safety guarantees assume fixed ideal, unperturbed dynamics, which is unrealistic for real-world deployments where dynamics may deviate due to uncertainty. There is a need for robustness guarantees of these certificates themselves under perturbations.

Method: Define a robust Lyapunov-barrier function and derive sufficient conditions based on Lipschitz continuity to ensure robustness against bounded dynamical perturbations. Propose practical training objectives implementing these conditions via adversarial training, a Lipschitz neighborhood bound, and global Lipschitz regularization to enforce the robustness constraints during learning.

Result: Empirical validation on Inverted Pendulum and 2D Docking shows substantial gains: certified robustness bounds improve up to 4.6× and empirical success rates under strong perturbations improve up to 2.4× compared to a baseline.

Conclusion: Training robust neural certificates can effectively guarantee safety and stability of RL controllers under perturbed dynamics, improving reliability for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Neural Lyapunov and barrier certificates have recently been used as powerful tools for verifying the safety and stability properties of deep reinforcement learning (RL) controllers. However, existing methods offer guarantees only under fixed ideal unperturbed dynamics, limiting their reliability in real-world applications where dynamics may deviate due to uncertainties. In this work, we study the problem of synthesizing \emph{robust neural Lyapunov barrier certificates} that maintain their guarantees under perturbations in system dynamics. We formally define a robust Lyapunov barrier function and specify sufficient conditions based on Lipschitz continuity that ensure robustness against bounded perturbations. We propose practical training objectives that enforce these conditions via adversarial training, Lipschitz neighborhood bound, and global Lipschitz regularization. We validate our approach in two practically relevant environments, Inverted Pendulum and 2D Docking. The former is a widely studied benchmark, while the latter is a safety-critical task in autonomous systems. We show that our methods significantly improve both certified robustness bounds (up to $4.6$ times) and empirical success rates under strong perturbations (up to $2.4$ times) compared to the baseline. Our results demonstrate effectiveness of training robust neural certificates for safe RL under perturbations in dynamics.

</details>


### [124] [Private PoEtry: Private In-Context Learning via Product of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05012)
*Rob Romijnders,Mohammad Mahdi Derakhshani,Jonathan Petit,Max Welling,Christos Louizos,Yuki M. Asano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled Product-of-Experts (PoE) formulation for private in-context learning (DP-ICL) that is scalable and empirically gains substantial accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy-sensitive in-context examples in ICL can leak information; existing DP-ICL methods are costly or heuristic. A grounded, scalable approach is needed.

Method: Reformulates private ICL as a Product-of-Experts model, enabling a theoretically grounded framework and trivially parallelizable algorithm. The method combines multiple experts to produce a privacy-preserving ICL output.

Result: Evaluated on five datasets spanning text classification, math, and vision-language; average accuracy improvement of over 30 percentage points over prior DP-ICL methods, while maintaining strong privacy guarantees.

Conclusion: The PoE-based approach provides a principled, scalable, and effective solution for private ICL, achieving large accuracy gains without compromising privacy.

Abstract: In-context learning (ICL) enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to adapt to new tasks with only a small set of examples at inference time, thereby avoiding task-specific fine-tuning. However, in-context examples may contain privacy-sensitive information that should not be revealed through model outputs. Existing differential privacy (DP) approaches to ICL are either computationally expensive or rely on heuristics with limited effectiveness, including context oversampling, synthetic data generation, or unnecessary thresholding. We reformulate private ICL through the lens of a Product-of-Experts model. This gives a theoretically grounded framework, and the algorithm can be trivially parallelized. We evaluate our method across five datasets in text classification, math, and vision-language. We find that our method improves accuracy by more than 30 percentage points on average compared to prior DP-ICL methods, while maintaining strong privacy guarantees.

</details>


### [125] [A Simple Reduction Scheme for Constrained Contextual Bandits with Adversarial Contexts via Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05019)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Abhishek Sinha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular reduction of constrained contextual bandits with adversarial contexts to an unconstrained contextual bandit problem using surrogate rewards, enabling online regression oracles and improving guarantees in the adversarial setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To handle budgeted decision-making under adversarially chosen contexts, by converting the constrained problem into a standard online regression/CB task; aims to simultaneously bound regret and constraint violation in the continuing setting.

Method: Extends the SquareCB framework by defining adaptive surrogate rewards via online regression oracles; constructs a reduction from constrained CB to unconstrained CB, applied in adversarial-context settings; operates in the continuing setting where the horizon continues after budget exhaustion.

Result: Provides improved theoretical guarantees for regret and cumulative constraint violation in adversarial-context CCB, with a compact, modular analysis and a simple algorithmic scheme.

Conclusion: The proposed reduction offers a transparent, modular approach to CCB under adversarial contexts, leveraging online regression oracles to obtain strong performance guarantees without heavy analysis complexity.

Abstract: We study constrained contextual bandits (CCB) with adversarially chosen contexts, where each action yields a random reward and incurs a random cost. We adopt the standard realizability assumption: conditioned on the observed context, rewards and costs are drawn independently from fixed distributions whose expectations belong to known function classes. We consider the continuing setting, in which the algorithm operates over the entire horizon even after the budget is exhausted. In this setting, the objective is to simultaneously control regret and cumulative constraint violation. Building on the seminal SquareCB framework of Foster et al. (2018), we propose a simple and modular algorithmic scheme that leverages online regression oracles to reduce the constrained problem to a standard unconstrained contextual bandit problem with adaptively defined surrogate reward functions. In contrast to most prior work on CCB, which focuses on stochastic contexts, our reduction yields improved guarantees for the more general adversarial context setting, together with a compact and transparent analysis.

</details>


### [126] [Laplacian Representations for Decision-Time Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05031)
*Dikshant Shehmar,Matthew Schlegel,Matthew E. Taylor,Marlos C. Machado*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Laplacian latent spaces enable effective planning; introduces ALPS hierarchical planner; yields superior performance on offline GOAL-conditioned RL tasks from OGBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of planning with learned models in model-based RL; requires state representations that support local cost computation while preserving long-horizon structure and mitigating error compounding.

Method: Use Laplacian representation to encode state-space distances at multiple time scales; develop ALPS, a hierarchical planning algorithm; evaluate on offline GOAL-conditioned tasks from OGBench.

Result: ALPS outperforms common baselines on selected offline GOAL-conditioned RL tasks from OGBench; demonstrates improved planning efficiency and reduced error accumulation.

Conclusion: Laplacian-based latent space is effective for planning in model-based RL; hierarchical ALPS leverages multi-scale distances and subgoals to improve performance.

Abstract: Planning with a learned model remains a key challenge in model-based reinforcement learning (RL). In decision-time planning, state representations are critical as they must support local cost computation while preserving long-horizon structure. In this paper, we show that the Laplacian representation provides an effective latent space for planning by capturing state-space distances at multiple time scales. This representation preserves meaningful distances and naturally decomposes long-horizon problems into subgoals, also mitigating the compounding errors that arise over long prediction horizons. Building on these properties, we introduce ALPS, a hierarchical planning algorithm, and demonstrate that it outperforms commonly used baselines on a selection of offline goal-conditioned RL tasks from OGBench, a benchmark previously dominated by model-free methods.

</details>


### [127] [Quantile-Physics Hybrid Framework for Safe-Speed Recommendation under Diverse Weather Conditions Leveraging Connected Vehicle and Road Weather Information Systems Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05053)
*Wen Zhang,Adel W. Sadek,Chunming Qiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid real-time speed interval predictor for freeway safety under weather, combining Quantile Regression Forests with physics-based limits and road/weather data; strong accuracy and generalization across weather and road segments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inclement weather degrades driver visibility and tire-road friction, increasing crash risk. The paper targets safe speed recommendations rather than a single point estimate, leveraging real-time, high-resolution CV/RWIS data to provide speed intervals.

Method: Dataset: ~6.6 million records over 73 days (Buffalo, NY, 2022–2023). 10-minute windows with 26 features capturing meteorology, pavement, and temporal factors. Core model: Quantile Regression Forests to estimate speed distributions. A physics-based upper speed limit is computed per interval from real-time road grip and sight distance. Final interval combines QRF-predicted quantiles with posted speed limits and the physics-derived bound.

Result: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 1.55 mph. 96.43% of median speed predictions within 5 mph. PICP (50%) = 48.55%. Demonstrates robust generalization across weather types and road segments.

Conclusion: The framework shows promise for real-world deployment, offering safe-speed interval recommendations that adapt to changing weather and road conditions, with potential to reduce weather-related crashes and improve traffic safety.

Abstract: Inclement weather conditions can significantly impact driver visibility and tire-road surface friction, requiring adjusted safe driving speeds to reduce crash risk. This study proposes a hybrid predictive framework that recommends real-time safe speed intervals for freeway travel under diverse weather conditions. Leveraging high-resolution Connected Vehicle (CV) data and Road Weather Information System (RWIS) data collected in Buffalo, NY, from 2022 to 2023, we construct a spatiotemporally aligned dataset containing over 6.6 million records across 73 days. The core model employs Quantile Regression Forests (QRF) to estimate vehicle speed distributions in 10-minute windows, using 26 input features that capture meteorological, pavement, and temporal conditions. To enforce safety constraints, a physics-based upper speed limit is computed for each interval based on real-time road grip and visibility, ensuring that vehicles can safely stop within their sight distance. The final recommended interval fuses QRF-predicted quantiles with both posted speed limits and the physics-derived upper bound. Experimental results demonstrate strong predictive performance: the QRF model achieves a mean absolute error of 1.55 mph, with 96.43% of median speed predictions within 5 mph, a PICP (50%) of 48.55%, and robust generalization across weather types. The model's ability to respond to changing weather conditions and generalize across road segments shows promise for real-world deployment, thereby improving traffic safety and reducing weather-related crashes.

</details>


### [128] [E-Globe: Scalable $ε$-Global Verification of Neural Networks via Tight Upper Bounds and Pattern-Aware Branching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05068)
*Wenting Li,Saif R. Kazi,Russell Bent,Duo Zhou,Huan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid branch-and-bound verifier using NLP-CC upper bounds preserves ReLU structure, enabling tight bounds and faster verification than prior MIP/PGD baselines on MNIST/CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robustness verification for neural networks remains challenging due to scalability and completeness; need scalable verifiers that provide guarantees.

Method: A BaB framework with exact NLP-CC upper bounding that preserves the ReLU input-output graph, enabling valid counterexamples; warm-started NLP solves; pattern-aligned strong branching; conditions for tightness; GPU batching.

Result: Experiments show markedly tighter upper bounds than PGD over large perturbation radii; fast per-node solves; substantial end-to-end speedups over MIP-based verification; improvements amplified by warm-starting and batching.

Conclusion: The proposed NLP-CC BaB verifier offers tighter and faster robustness verification than PGD and MIP-based methods, advancing scalability and practical applicability for safety-critical NN verification.

Abstract: Neural networks achieve strong empirical performance, but robustness concerns still hinder deployment in safety-critical applications. Formal verification provides robustness guarantees, but current methods face a scalability-completeness trade-off. We propose a hybrid verifier in a branch-and-bound (BaB) framework that efficiently tightens both upper and lower bounds until an $ε-$global optimum is reached or early stop is triggered. The key is an exact nonlinear program with complementarity constraints (NLP-CC) for upper bounding that preserves the ReLU input-output graph, so any feasible solution yields a valid counterexample and enables rapid pruning of unsafe subproblems. We further accelerate verification with (i) warm-started NLP solves requiring minimal constraint-matrix updates and (ii) pattern-aligned strong branching that prioritizes splits most effective at tightening relaxations. We also provide conditions under which NLP-CC upper bounds are tight. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 show markedly tighter upper bounds than PGD across perturbation radii spanning up to three orders of magnitude, fast per-node solves in practice, and substantial end-to-end speedups over MIP-based verification, amplified by warm-starting, GPU batching, and pattern-aligned branching.

</details>


### [129] [Reliable Explanations or Random Noise? A Reliability Metric for XAI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05082)
*Poushali Sengupta,Sabita Maharjan,Frank Eliassen,Shashi Raj Pandey,Yan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Explanation Reliability Index (ERI) to quantify stability of model explanations under four axioms, with ERI-T and ERI-Bench; finds instability in SHAP/IG under realistic conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability methods are used in high-stakes domains but their reliability under realistic changes is under-measured; there is a need for formal guarantees and benchmarks for explanation reliability.

Method: Introduce ERI family of metrics to quantify explanation stability across four reliability axioms (robustness to small input perturbations, consistency under feature redundancy, smoothness across model evolution, resilience to mild distributional shifts); provide Lipschitz-type guarantees and temporal stability results; develop ERI-T for sequential models; create ERI-Bench benchmark; conduct experiments showing instability of popular explainers.

Result: Demonstrates widespread reliability failures of popular explanation methods; explanations are unstable under realistic deployment conditions; ERI metrics reveal instability and provide a framework for evaluation; ERI-Bench enables systematic stress-testing across synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: ERI enables principled assessment of explanation reliability and supports more trustworthy explainable AI (XAI) systems; provides a benchmark and temporal measure to compare explainers and guide improvements.

Abstract: In recent years, explaining decisions made by complex machine learning models has become essential in high-stakes domains such as energy systems, healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems. However, the reliability of these explanations, namely, whether they remain stable and consistent under realistic, non-adversarial changes, remains largely unmeasured. Widely used methods such as SHAP and Integrated Gradients (IG) are well-motivated by axiomatic notions of attribution, yet their explanations can vary substantially even under system-level conditions, including small input perturbations, correlated representations, and minor model updates. Such variability undermines explanation reliability, as reliable explanations should remain consistent across equivalent input representations and small, performance-preserving model changes. We introduce the Explanation Reliability Index (ERI), a family of metrics that quantifies explanation stability under four reliability axioms: robustness to small input perturbations, consistency under feature redundancy, smoothness across model evolution, and resilience to mild distributional shifts. For each axiom, we derive formal guarantees, including Lipschitz-type bounds and temporal stability results. We further propose ERI-T, a dedicated measure of temporal reliability for sequential models, and introduce ERI-Bench, a benchmark designed to systematically stress-test explanation reliability across synthetic and real-world datasets. Experimental results reveal widespread reliability failures in popular explanation methods, showing that explanations can be unstable under realistic deployment conditions. By exposing and quantifying these instabilities, ERI enables principled assessment of explanation reliability and supports more trustworthy explainable AI (XAI) systems.

</details>


### [130] [Individual Fairness In Strategic Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05084)
*Zhiqun Zuo,Mohammad Mahdi Khalili*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deterministic thresholds fail individual fairness in strategic settings; a randomized classifier under appropriate conditions can achieve individual fairness and be optimized via a linear program, with extensions to group fairness; empirical results show improved fairness-accuracy trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strategic classification enables individuals to adapt features to influence decisions, raising fairness concerns. Individual fairness in this context is underexplored compared to group fairness; the work aims to guarantee individual fairness while maintaining predictive performance.

Method: 1) Analyze threshold-based, deterministic classifiers and prove violation of individual fairness under strategic manipulation. 2) Introduce a randomized classifier and derive sufficient conditions that guarantee individual fairness. 3) Formulate an optimization problem (linear program) to find an optimal, individually fair randomized classifier. 4) Extend the framework to accommodate group fairness notions. 5) Validate via experiments on real-world datasets.

Result: Derives conditions under which a randomized classifier ensures individual fairness; provides an LP-based method to obtain the optimal, individually fair randomized classifier; demonstrates that the approach can extend to group fairness; empirical results indicate improved fairness-accuracy trade-offs and reduced unfairness in real data.

Conclusion: Randomized classifiers, when designed with appropriate conditions, can achieve individual fairness in strategic settings and can be extended to group fairness. The approach is practically viable as shown by empirical results.

Abstract: Strategic classification, where individuals modify their features to influence machine learning (ML) decisions, presents critical fairness challenges. While group fairness in this setting has been widely studied, individual fairness remains underexplored. We analyze threshold-based classifiers and prove that deterministic thresholds violate individual fairness. Then, we investigate the possibility of using a randomized classifier to achieve individual fairness. We introduce conditions under which a randomized classifier ensures individual fairness and leverage these conditions to find an optimal and individually fair randomized classifier through a linear programming problem. Additionally, we demonstrate that our approach can be extended to group fairness notions. Experiments on real-world datasets confirm that our method effectively mitigates unfairness and improves the fairness-accuracy trade-off.

</details>


### [131] [Autodiscover: A reinforcement learning recommendation system for the cold-start imbalance challenge in active learning, powered by graph-aware thompson sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05087)
*Parsa Vares*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoDiscover reframes active learning for systematic literature reviews as an online decision-making problem using a heterogeneous graph attention network and a Discounted Thompson Sampling agent to dynamically manage a portfolio of query strategies, improving screening efficiency and addressing cold-start with minimal labels; includes a visual analytics dashboard (TS-Insight).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SLRs require screening large corpora where relevant studies are rare and expert labels are costly. Traditional static query strategies underperform due to non-stationary dynamics and ignoring the relational structure of scientific literature networks.

Method: Model literature as a heterogeneous graph (documents, authors, metadata) and learn node representations with a Heterogeneous Graph Attention Network (HAN). A Discounted Thompson Sampling (DTS) agent maintains and adapts a portfolio of query strategies, balancing exploration and exploitation under non-stationary dynamics with real-time human-in-the-loop labels. Evaluation on 26-dataset SYNERGY benchmark; bootstrapping from minimal initial labels. TS-Insight provides interpretability/diagnostics for agent decisions.

Result: AutoDiscover achieves higher screening efficiency than static AL baselines on the SYNERGY benchmark and mitigates cold-start by leveraging minimal initial labels; demonstrates effective adaptation to changing strategy utilities over time.

Conclusion: The framework combines graph-based representation learning with online decision-making to enhance SLR screening efficiency under scarce expert labels, with TS-Insight enabling interpretation and diagnosis of the agent’s strategy selections.

Abstract: Systematic literature reviews (SLRs) are fundamental to evidence-based research, but manual screening is an increasing bottleneck as scientific output grows. Screening features low prevalence of relevant studies and scarce, costly expert decisions. Traditional active learning (AL) systems help, yet typically rely on fixed query strategies for selecting the next unlabeled documents. These static strategies do not adapt over time and ignore the relational structure of scientific literature networks. This thesis introduces AutoDiscover, a framework that reframes AL as an online decision-making problem driven by an adaptive agent. Literature is modeled as a heterogeneous graph capturing relationships among documents, authors, and metadata. A Heterogeneous Graph Attention Network (HAN) learns node representations, which a Discounted Thompson Sampling (DTS) agent uses to dynamically manage a portfolio of query strategies. With real-time human-in-the-loop labels, the agent balances exploration and exploitation under non-stationary review dynamics, where strategy utility changes over time. On the 26-dataset SYNERGY benchmark, AutoDiscover achieves higher screening efficiency than static AL baselines. Crucially, the agent mitigates cold start by bootstrapping discovery from minimal initial labels where static approaches fail. We also introduce TS-Insight, an open-source visual analytics dashboard to interpret, verify, and diagnose the agent's decisions. Together, these contributions accelerate SLR screening under scarce expert labels and low prevalence of relevant studies.

</details>


### [132] [Unbiased Single-Queried Gradient for Combinatorial Objective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05119)
*Thanawat Sornwanee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces an unbiased, one-query stochastic gradient estimator for hypercube-based probabilistic reformulations, unifying REINFORCE (via importance sampling) and a broader class of gradient estimators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient gradient estimation in combinatorial optimization where binary variables are modeled by Bernoulli parameters; exact gradient requires multiple queries to the discrete oracle, so reducing query count is crucial for scalability.

Method: Derive an unbiased gradient estimator of the expected objective with respect to Bernoulli parameters that requires only a single evaluation of the combinatorial function; recovers REINFORCE as a special case via importance sampling; extends to a broader class of stochastic gradient estimators.

Result: Proposes an unbiased estimator that includes REINFORCE as a special case and introduces a framework for additional stochastic gradient families with potentially different variance properties; establishes theoretical properties of unbiasedness and applicability to hypercube-based problems.
