However, standard policy optimization methods, such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), often converge to low-entropy policies, leading to severe mode collapse and limited output diversity. We analyze this issue from the perspective of sampling probability dynamics, identifying that the standard objective disproportionately reinforces the highest-likelihood paths, thereby suppressing valid alternative reasoning chains. To address this, we propose a novel Advantage Re-weighting Mechanism (ARM) designed to equilibrate the confidence levels across all correct responses. By incorporating Prompt Perplexity and Answer Confidence into the advantage estimation, our method dynamically reshapes the reward signal to attenuate the gradient updates of over-confident reasoning paths, while redistributing probability mass toward under-explored correct solutions. Empirical results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances generative diversity and response entropy while maintaining competitive accuracy, effectively achieving a superior trade-off between exploration and exploitation in reasoning tasks. Empirical results on Qwen2.5 and DeepSeek models across mathematical and coding benchmarks show that ProGRPO significantly mitigates entropy collapse. Specifically, on Qwen2.5-7B, our method outperforms GRPO by 5.7% in Pass@1 and, notably, by 13.9% in Pass@32, highlighting its superior capability in generating diverse correct reasoning paths.

</details>


### [159] [Robust Inference-Time Steering of Protein Diffusion Models via Embedding Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05285)
*Minhuan Li,Jiequn Han,Pilar Cossio,Luhuan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EmbedOpt is an inference-time method that steers diffusion models in the conditional embedding space to optimize experimental likelihoods, improving map-fitting performance, matching distance-constraint results, and offering robustness and efficiency gains over coordinate-based posterior sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biophysical inverse problems require conformations that are both physically plausible and consistent with experimental data. Posterior sampling with data-driven diffusion priors can be brittle when targets lie in low-density regions, necessitating aggressive likelihood weighting. A more robust, efficient inference strategy is needed.

Method: Introduce EmbedOpt, an inference-time approach that optimizes within the conditional embedding space (which encodes sequence and coevolutionary signals) to steer diffusion priors toward experimental constraints. This avoids brittle coordinate-based likelihood weighting and leverages rich embedding information.

Result: EmbedOpt outperforms coordinate-based posterior sampling in cryo-EM map-fitting benchmarks, matches performance on distance-constraint tasks, demonstrates superior robustness across hyperparameters over two orders of magnitude, and enables fewer diffusion steps for inference, improving efficiency.

Conclusion: Optimizing in embedding space provides a robust and efficient alternative to coordinate-based posterior sampling for diffusion-model-based biophysical structure inference, leveraging rich sequence/co-evolutionary signals to align with experimental constraints.

Abstract: In many biophysical inverse problems, the goal is to generate biomolecular conformations that are both physically plausible and consistent with experimental measurements. As recent sequence-to-structure diffusion models provide powerful data-driven priors, posterior sampling has emerged as a popular framework by guiding atomic coordinates to target conformations using experimental likelihoods. However, when the target lies in a low-density region of the prior, posterior sampling requires aggressive and brittle weighting of the likelihood guidance. Motivated by this limitation, we propose EmbedOpt, an alternative inference-time approach for steering diffusion models to optimize experimental likelihoods in the conditional embedding space. As this space encodes rich sequence and coevolutionary signals, optimizing over it effectively shifts the diffusion prior to align with experimental constraints. We validate EmbedOpt on two benchmarks simulating cryo-electron microscopy map fitting and experimental distance constraints. We show that EmbedOpt outperforms the coordinate-based posterior sampling method in map fitting tasks, matches performance on distance constraint tasks, and exhibits superior engineering robustness across hyperparameters spanning two orders of magnitude. Moreover, its smooth optimization behavior enables a significant reduction in the number of diffusion steps required for inference, leading to better efficiency.

</details>


### [160] [HealthMamba: An Uncertainty-aware Spatiotemporal Graph State Space Model for Effective and Reliable Healthcare Facility Visit Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05286)
*Dahai Yu,Lin Jiang,Rongchao Xu,Guang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HealthMamba introduces an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal framework for predicting healthcare facility visits, addressing spatial dependencies and abnormal-event robustness. It combines a Unified Spatiotemporal Context Encoder, a Graph State Space Model (GraphMamba) for hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling, and a three-mechanism uncertainty quantification module. Evaluated on four large real-world datasets (CA, NY, TX, FL), it yields ~6% accuracy gains and ~3.5% uncertainty-quantification gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many existing works treat healthcare visit prediction as pure time-series forecasting, neglecting intrinsic spatial dependencies across facility types and failing to produce reliable forecasts in abnormal scenarios (e.g., public emergencies). There is a need for (i) integrating heterogeneous static/dynamic context, (ii) modeling hierarchical spatiotemporal interactions via graph-based state-space methods, and (iii) robust uncertainty estimation to guide decision-making.

Method: 1) Unified Spatiotemporal Context Encoder that fuses heterogeneous static and dynamic information (e.g., facility types, demographics, mobility, time features). 2) GraphMamba: a Graph State Space Model for hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling, capturing multi-scale spatial dependencies and temporal dynamics. 3) Uncertainty quantification module with three mechanisms to provide reliable predictions under normal and abnormal conditions. 4) Evaluations on four large-scale real-world datasets from CA, NY, TX, and FL, comparing to state-of-the-art baselines.

Result: HealthMamba achieved around 6.0% improvement in prediction accuracy and 3.5% improvement in uncertainty quantification over state-of-the-art baselines across four datasets (California, New York, Texas, Florida).

Conclusion: The proposed HealthMamba framework delivers accurate and reliable healthcare facility visit predictions by integrating rich spatiotemporal context, hierarchical graph-based modeling, and multi-faceted uncertainty quantification. It demonstrates consistent gains across diverse states, indicating practical utility for resource optimization and policy support.

Abstract: Healthcare facility visit prediction is essential for optimizing healthcare resource allocation and informing public health policy. Despite advanced machine learning methods being employed for better prediction performance, existing works usually formulate this task as a time-series forecasting problem without considering the intrinsic spatial dependencies of different types of healthcare facilities, and they also fail to provide reliable predictions under abnormal situations such as public emergencies. To advance existing research, we propose HealthMamba, an uncertainty-aware spatiotemporal framework for accurate and reliable healthcare facility visit prediction. HealthMamba comprises three key components: (i) a Unified Spatiotemporal Context Encoder that fuses heterogeneous static and dynamic information, (ii) a novel Graph State Space Model called GraphMamba for hierarchical spatiotemporal modeling, and (iii) a comprehensive uncertainty quantification module integrating three uncertainty quantification mechanisms for reliable prediction. We evaluate HealthMamba on four large-scale real-world datasets from California, New York, Texas, and Florida. Results show HealthMamba achieves around 6.0% improvement in prediction accuracy and 3.5% improvement in uncertainty quantification over state-of-the-art baselines.

</details>


### [161] [A Short and Unified Convergence Analysis of the SAG, SAGA, and IAG Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05304)
*Feng Zhu,Robert W. Heath,Aritra Mitra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified finite-sum convergence analysis for SAG, SAGA, and IAG that yields high-probability bounds via a delay-bound and a novel Lyapunov function; extends to non-convex objectives and Markov sampling; improves IAG rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the fragmented and intricate analyses across SAG,SAGA,IAG; SAG's original proof is complex and not easily generalizable; create a single modular framework with high-probability guarantees that can extend beyond convexity and standard sampling.

Method: (i) bound delays from stochastic sub-sampling using concentration tools; (ii) construct a Lyapunov function that incorporates these delays to enable contractive analysis; (iii) apply to SAG, SAGA, IAG within finite-sum, smooth, strongly convex setting; extendable to non-convex and Markov sampling.

Result: First high-probability bounds for SAG and SAGA; modular proof; best-known rates for IAG; framework extends to non-convex objectives and Markov sampling.

Conclusion: A concise, unified, and modular convergence analysis for SAG/SAGA/IAG with potential broad applicability and easier extension to more general settings; benefits include simpler proofs and stronger guarantees across variants.

Abstract: Stochastic variance-reduced algorithms such as Stochastic Average Gradient (SAG) and SAGA, and their deterministic counterparts like the Incremental Aggregated Gradient (IAG) method, have been extensively studied in large-scale machine learning. Despite their popularity, existing analyses for these algorithms are disparate, relying on different proof techniques tailored to each method. Furthermore, the original proof of SAG is known to be notoriously involved, requiring computer-aided analysis. Focusing on finite-sum optimization with smooth and strongly convex objective functions, our main contribution is to develop a single unified convergence analysis that applies to all three algorithms: SAG, SAGA, and IAG. Our analysis features two key steps: (i) establishing a bound on delays due to stochastic sub-sampling using simple concentration tools, and (ii) carefully designing a novel Lyapunov function that accounts for such delays. The resulting proof is short and modular, providing the first high-probability bounds for SAG and SAGA that can be seamlessly extended to non-convex objectives and Markov sampling. As an immediate byproduct of our new analysis technique, we obtain the best known rates for the IAG algorithm, significantly improving upon prior bounds.

</details>


### [162] [Accelerated Sequential Flow Matching: A Bayesian Filtering Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05319)
*Yinan Huang,Hans Hao-Hsun Hsu,Junran Wang,Bo Dai,Pan Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sequential Flow Matching frames sequential inference as Bayesian filtering, learning a probability flow to transport the predictive distribution across time steps, enabling fast real-time sampling with few steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Streaming forecasting and state estimation require low-latency, accurate inference; naïve diffusion-based sampling with non-informative initial conditions incurs high latency and backlog risk.

Method: Model the update from time t to t+1 as a probability flow (learned mapping) that transports the predictive distribution; initialize generation from the previous posterior (warm start) to accelerate sampling; formal justification and theoretical backing for this warm start; empirical validation across forecasting, decision-making, and state estimation tasks.

Result: Achieves competitive performance with full-step diffusion while requiring only one or a few sampling steps, enabling faster sampling and real-time deployment across diverse tasks.

Conclusion: Bayesian filtering provides a principled lens for sequential inference in flow-based models, offering efficient, real-time deployment potential and a new perspective on sequential diffusion/flow-matching frameworks.

Abstract: Sequential prediction from streaming observations is a fundamental problem in stochastic dynamical systems, where inherent uncertainty often leads to multiple plausible futures. While diffusion and flow-matching models are capable of modeling complex, multi-modal trajectories, their deployment in real-time streaming environments typically relies on repeated sampling from a non-informative initial distribution, incurring substantial inference latency and potential system backlogs. In this work, we introduce Sequential Flow Matching, a principled framework grounded in Bayesian filtering. By treating streaming inference as learning a probability flow that transports the predictive distribution from one time step to the next, our approach naturally aligns with the recursive structure of Bayesian belief updates. We provide theoretical justification that initializing generation from the previous posterior offers a principled warm start that can accelerate sampling compared to naïve re-sampling. Across a wide range of forecasting, decision-making and state estimation tasks, our method achieves performance competitive with full-step diffusion while requiring only one or very few sampling steps, therefore with faster sampling. It suggests that framing sequential inference via Bayesian filtering provides a new and principled perspective towards efficient real-time deployment of flow-based models.

</details>


### [163] [GAS: Enhancing Reward-Cost Balance of Generative Model-assisted Offline Safe RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05323)
*Zifan Liu,Xinran Li,Shibo Chen,Jun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel offline safe RL algorithm, GAS, uses transition-level augmentation/relabeling and expectile-regression learned goals to stitch suboptimal trajectories and flexibly balance reward vs. costs, achieving superior tradeoffs and stable training in OSRL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline Safe RL relies on fixed datasets to maximize performance under safety constraints. GM-based methods struggle to stitch optimal transitions from suboptimal trajectories and to balance conflicting reward and cost targets. GAS addresses these gaps by improving data utilization and learning goal-guided policies.

Method: GAS augments and relabels the dataset at the transition level to enable high-quality trajectory construction. It introduces goal functions that estimate the optimal achievable reward and cost from the data, trained via expectile regression on the relabeled/augmented dataset. These goals broaden the target reward-cost pairs and improve the reward–cost tradeoff. The dataset is reshaped to produce a more uniform reward-cost distribution, and the estimated goals guide policy training to robustly satisfy constraints.

Result: Empirical results show GAS achieves superior balance between reward maximization and constraint satisfaction compared with existing GM-assisted OSRL methods.

Conclusion: GAS improves OSRL by enhancing transition stitching, learning flexible goal targets from data, and stabilizing training through data reshaping, yielding better reward–cost tradeoffs and robust constrained performance.

Abstract: Offline Safe Reinforcement Learning (OSRL) aims to learn a policy to achieve high performance in sequential decision-making while satisfying constraints, using only pre-collected datasets. Recent works, inspired by the strong capabilities of Generative Models (GMs), reformulate decision-making in OSRL as a conditional generative process, where GMs generate desirable actions conditioned on predefined reward and cost values. However, GM-assisted methods face two major challenges in OSRL: (1) lacking the ability to "stitch" optimal transitions from suboptimal trajectories within the dataset, and (2) struggling to balance reward targets with cost targets, particularly when they are conflict. To address these issues, we propose Goal-Assisted Stitching (GAS), a novel algorithm designed to enhance stitching capabilities while effectively balancing reward maximization and constraint satisfaction. To enhance the stitching ability, GAS first augments and relabels the dataset at the transition level, enabling the construction of high-quality trajectories from suboptimal ones. GAS also introduces novel goal functions, which estimate the optimal achievable reward and cost goals from the dataset. These goal functions, trained using expectile regression on the relabeled and augmented dataset, allow GAS to accommodate a broader range of reward-cost return pairs and achieve a better tradeoff between reward maximization and constraint satisfaction compared to human-specified values. The estimated goals then guide policy training, ensuring robust performance under constrained settings. Furthermore, to improve training stability and efficiency, we reshape the dataset to achieve a more uniform reward-cost return distribution. Empirical results validate the effectiveness of GAS, demonstrating superior performance in balancing reward maximization and constraint satisfaction compared to existing methods.

</details>


### [164] [Linear Model Merging Unlocks Simple and Scalable Multimodal Data Mixture Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04937)
*Davide Berasi,Matteo Farina,Massimiliano Mancini,Elisa Ricci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Merging-based proxy models predict data-mix performance, enabling efficient Data Mixture Optimization (DMO) without retraining; shows high rank correlation with data-mixed models across 14 benchmarks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DMO is expensive due to combinatorial search and costly training runs; need scalable, accurate proxies to guide data mixture weights.

Method: Train domain-specific multimodal experts and evaluate weighted parameter-space interpolations (merged proxies); assess their performance proxy against models trained on actual data mixtures across multiple datasets.

Result: Merged proxy models exhibit high rank correlation with performance of corresponding data-mixture-trained models across 14 multimodal benchmarks, enabling decoupled and scalable DMO.

Conclusion: Model merging can serve as an efficient proxy for DMO, reducing training cost by guiding mixture weight search; code available for replication.

Abstract: Selecting the best data mixture is critical for successful Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) of Multimodal Large Language Models. However, determining the optimal mixture weights across multiple domain-specific datasets remains a significant bottleneck due to the combinatorial search space and the high cost associated with even a single training run. This is the so-called Data Mixture Optimization (DMO) problem. On the other hand, model merging unifies domain-specific experts through parameter interpolation. This strategy is efficient, as it only requires a single training run per domain, yet oftentimes leads to suboptimal models. In this work, we take the best of both worlds, studying model merging as an efficient strategy for estimating the performance of different data mixtures. We train domain-specific multimodal experts and evaluate their weighted parameter-space combinations to estimate the efficacy of corresponding data mixtures. We conduct extensive experiments on 14 multimodal benchmarks, and empirically demonstrate that the merged proxy models exhibit a high rank correlation with models trained on actual data mixtures. This decouples the search for optimal mixtures from the resource-intensive training process, thereby providing a scalable and efficient strategy for navigating the complex landscape of mixture weights. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/BerasiDavide/mLLMs_merging_4_DMO.

</details>


### [165] [Pool-based Active Learning as Noisy Lossy Compression: Characterizing Label Complexity via Finite Blocklength Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05333)
*Kosuke Sugiyama,Masato Uchida*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces an information-theoretic framework for pool-based active learning by recasting data selection as compression and learning as decoding in a noisy lossy compression setting, and derives finite-blocklength lower bounds on label complexity and generalization error that reveal overfitting and inductive-bias misalignment effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide theoretical limits for pool-based active learning and unify data selection with learning under information-theoretic analysis. Address the gap where pool-based AL lacks established information-theoretic bounds and connect AL behavior to stability theory.

Method: Reformulate pool-based AL as a noisy lossy compression problem: pool observations -> noisy symbols, data selection -> compression, learning -> decoding. Apply finite blocklength analysis from noisy lossy compression to derive lower bounds on label complexity and generalization error under optimal data selection.

Result: Derivation of information-theoretic lower bounds on label complexity and generalization error for a given learning algorithm with its associated optimal data selection strategy; bounds include terms capturing overfitting and inductive-bias–task mismatch; links to existing information-theoretic bounds and stability theory not previously used for pool-based AL.

Conclusion: Offers a new theoretical perspective on pool-based AL by providing fundamental limits and a unified information-theoretic view of data selection and learning, potentially guiding the design of AL algorithms and their evaluation under principled bounds.

Abstract: This paper proposes an information-theoretic framework for analyzing the theoretical limits of pool-based active learning (AL), in which a subset of instances is selectively labeled. The proposed framework reformulates pool-based AL as a noisy lossy compression problem by mapping pool observations to noisy symbol observations, data selection to compression, and learning to decoding. This correspondence enables a unified information-theoretic analysis of data selection and learning in pool-based AL. Applying finite blocklength analysis of noisy lossy compression, we derive information-theoretic lower bounds on label complexity and generalization error that serve as theoretical limits for a given learning algorithm under its associated optimal data selection strategy. Specifically, our bounds include terms that reflect overfitting induced by the learning algorithm and the discrepancy between its inductive bias and the target task, and are closely related to established information-theoretic bounds and stability theory, which have not been previously applied to the analysis of pool-based AL. These properties yield a new theoretical perspective on pool-based AL.

</details>


### [166] [Smoothness Errors in Dynamics Models and How to Avoid Them](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05352)
*Edward Berman,Luisa Li,Jung Yeon Park,Robin Walters*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Relaxed unitary convolutions balance smoothing, improving PDE dynamics modeling on graphs/meshes, and outperforming baselines like mesh-aware transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Oversmoothing in graph neural networks (GNNs) can hinder modeling of physical dynamics. Unitary convolutions preserve smoothness but may overconstrain tasks where natural smoothing occurs (e.g., diffusion). A flexible approach is needed.

Method: Introduce relaxed unitary convolutions that interpolate between unitary and standard convolutions; generalize both to meshes. Evaluate on heat and wave PDEs on complex meshes and on weather forecasting; compare with strong baselines including mesh-aware transformers and equivariant networks.

Result: Unitary convolutions harm performance for diffusion-like dynamics; relaxed unitary convolutions yield better accuracy and generalization; the approach outperforms several strong baselines across tasks.

Conclusion: Relaxed unitary convolutions offer a better balance between preserving beneficial smoothness and allowing natural, physics-driven smoothing, and can be extended from graphs to meshes; beneficial for PDEs and weather forecasting.

Abstract: Modern neural networks have shown promise for solving partial differential equations over surfaces, often by discretizing the surface as a mesh and learning with a mesh-aware graph neural network. However, graph neural networks suffer from oversmoothing, where a node's features become increasingly similar to those of its neighbors. Unitary graph convolutions, which are mathematically constrained to preserve smoothness, have been proposed to address this issue. Despite this, in many physical systems, such as diffusion processes, smoothness naturally increases and unitarity may be overconstraining. In this paper, we systematically study the smoothing effects of different GNNs for dynamics modeling and prove that unitary convolutions hurt performance for such tasks. We propose relaxed unitary convolutions that balance smoothness preservation with the natural smoothing required for physical systems. We also generalize unitary and relaxed unitary convolutions from graphs to meshes. In experiments on PDEs such as the heat and wave equations over complex meshes and on weather forecasting, we find that our method outperforms several strong baselines, including mesh-aware transformers and equivariant neural networks.

</details>


### [167] [Stochastic hierarchical data-driven optimization: application to plasma-surface kinetics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04975)
*José Afonso,Vasco Guerra,Pedro Viegas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A stochastic hierarchical optimization framework using a reduced Hessian to identify stiff parameter subspaces, enabling sample-efficient calibration of expensive physical models; validated on plasma-surface interactions with superior performance over baselines; generalizable to complex reaction networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient calibration of high-dimensional, anisotropic parameter spaces with expensive simulations. Inspired by Sloppy Model theory to focus on stiff directions and reduce query counts.

Method: A hierarchical stochastic optimization that employs a reduced Hessian projection to identify stiff subspaces, coupled with a probabilistic objective derived directly from observed data to enable principled inference; minimizes simulation queries while navigating anisotropic landscapes.

Result: Demonstrates superior sample efficiency relative to baseline optimization techniques; validated on plasma-surface interaction problems with uncertainties in surface reactivity parameters and kinetic simulations.

Conclusion: Offers a general, scalable tool for optimizing models of complex reaction systems (plasma chemistry to biochemical networks) under uncertainty, reducing computational cost while maintaining inference rigor.

Abstract: This work introduces a stochastic hierarchical optimization framework inspired by Sloppy Model theory for the efficient calibration of physical models. Central to this method is the use of a reduced Hessian approximation, which identifies and targets the stiff parameter subspace using minimal simulation queries. This strategy enables efficient navigation of highly anisotropic landscapes, avoiding the computational burden of exhaustive sampling. To ensure rigorous inference, we integrate this approach with a probabilistic formulation that derives a principled objective loss function directly from observed data. We validate the framework by applying it to the problem of plasma-surface interactions, where accurate modelling is strictly limited by uncertainties in surface reactivity parameters and the computational cost of kinetic simulations. Comparative analysis demonstrates that our method consistently outperforms baseline optimization techniques in sample efficiency. This approach offers a general and scalable tool for optimizing models of complex reaction systems, ranging from plasma chemistry to biochemical networks.

</details>


### [168] [Bayesian Neighborhood Adaptation for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05358)
*Paribesh Regmi,Rui Li,Kishan K C*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Bayesian framework using a beta process to adaptively infer the neighborhood scope (number of hops) in GNNs, integrating scope inference with GNN optimization to improve expressivity and performance on both homophilic and heterophilic graphs, while delivering well-calibrated node classifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed pre-specified neighborhood scopes (hop counts) in GNNs are inefficient and potentially biased, requiring costly two-stage search and failing to generalize across graph types; adaptive inference of the hop scope is desirable for improving expressivity on diverse graphs.

Method: Model the GNNs' message-passing as a stochastic process where the number of hops is governed by a beta process; develop a Bayesian framework that infers the most plausible neighborhood scope jointly with learning GNN parameters; provide theoretical analysis showing scope inference enhances expressivity; ensure compatibility with state-of-the-art GNN variants.

Result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark homogeneous and heterogeneous graphs for node classification shows competitive or superior performance relative to baselines, with well-calibrated predictive uncertainties; the approach is compatible with modern GNN architectures.

Conclusion: Adaptive, beta-process–based inference of neighborhood scope offers a principled, end-to-end method to improve GNN expressivity and performance across graph types, reducing search cost and yielding calibrated predictions.

Abstract: The neighborhood scope (i.e., number of hops) where graph neural networks (GNNs) aggregate information to characterize a node's statistical property is critical to GNNs' performance. Two-stage approaches, training and validating GNNs for every pre-specified neighborhood scope to search for the best setting, is a time-consuming task and tends to be biased due to the search space design. How to adaptively determine proper neighborhood scopes for the aggregation process for both homophilic and heterophilic graphs remains largely unexplored. We thus propose to model the GNNs' message-passing behavior on a graph as a stochastic process by treating the number of hops as a beta process. This Bayesian framework allows us to infer the most plausible neighborhood scope for message aggregation simultaneously with the optimization of GNN parameters. Our theoretical analysis shows that the scope inference improves the expressivity of a GNN. Experiments on benchmark homophilic and heterophilic datasets show that the proposed method is compatible with state-of-the-art GNN variants, achieving competitive or superior performance on the node classification task, and providing well-calibrated predictions.

</details>


### [169] [Hinge Regression Tree: A Newton Method for Oblique Regression Tree Splitting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05371)
*Hongyi Li,Han Lin,Jun Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Hinge Regression Tree (HRT) for oblique decision trees, formulating splits as nonlinear least-squares with hinge-like envelope, solved by an alternating (Gauss-Newton) procedure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address NP-hardness and inefficiency of learning high-quality oblique splits; enable scalable, stable training with convergence guarantees and regularization.

Method: Split learning is reframed as a two-predictor nonlinear LS problem whose max/min envelope yields ReLU-like nonlinearity; alternating fitting acts like a damped Gauss-Newton method within fixed partitions; backtracking line search ensures monotonic descent; optional ridge regularization included.

Result: Converges rapidly in practice (both fixed/adaptive damping); local objective decreases monotonically with backtracking; HRT achieves universal approximation with O(δ^2) rate; empirical benchmarks show comparable or superior performance to single-tree baselines with more compact trees.

Conclusion: HRT provides a theoretically grounded, efficient oblique-tree method with universal approximation capability and competitive empirical performance, offering a practical alternative to slow or heuristic oblique-split methods.

Abstract: Oblique decision trees combine the transparency of trees with the power of multivariate decision boundaries, but learning high-quality oblique splits is NP-hard, and practical methods still rely on slow search or theory-free heuristics. We present the Hinge Regression Tree (HRT), which reframes each split as a non-linear least-squares problem over two linear predictors whose max/min envelope induces ReLU-like expressive power. The resulting alternating fitting procedure is exactly equivalent to a damped Newton (Gauss-Newton) method within fixed partitions. We analyze this node-level optimization and, for a backtracking line-search variant, prove that the local objective decreases monotonically and converges; in practice, both fixed and adaptive damping yield fast, stable convergence and can be combined with optional ridge regularization. We further prove that HRT's model class is a universal approximator with an explicit $O(δ^2)$ approximation rate, and show on synthetic and real-world benchmarks that it matches or outperforms single-tree baselines with more compact structures.

</details>


### [170] [Learning Rate Matters: Vanilla LoRA May Suffice for LLM Fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04998)
*Yu-Ang Lee,Ching-Yun Ko,Pin-Yu Chen,Mi-Yen Yeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hyperparameter sensitivity dominates observed gains in LoRA variants; with proper learning rate tuning, different LoRA variants perform similarly, and vanilla LoRA remains a strong baseline; a second-order Hessian analysis explains LR-range differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematically re-evaluate four representative LoRA variants and vanilla LoRA with extensive hyperparameter searches to assess robustness of purported gains and the competitiveness of the vanilla baseline across tasks and model scales.

Method: Conduct large-scale hyperparameter sweeps over learning rates and other training configurations for four LoRA variants plus vanilla LoRA, evaluate on mathematical and code-generation tasks across diverse model scales, analyze learning-rate sensitivity and rank effects, and perform a second-order Hessian-based analysis to explain optimal LR ranges.

Result: Once learning rates are properly tuned, all LoRA variants converge to similar peak performance (within 1–2%), with only subtle rank-dependent differences; different methods favor distinct LR ranges; gains reported under fixed configurations may reflect tuning biases rather than intrinsic advantages; vanilla LoRA remains a competitive baseline.

Conclusion: The observed LR-range differences align with the largest Hessian eigenvalue, supporting classic learning theory; robust evaluation requires comprehensive hyperparameter sweeps; claims of superiority for alternative initialization or architectural variants should be tempered by sensitivity to training configurations.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the prevailing approach for efficient large language model (LLM) fine-tuning. Building on this paradigm, recent studies have proposed alternative initialization strategies and architectural modifications, reporting substantial improvements over vanilla LoRA. However, these gains are often demonstrated under fixed or narrowly tuned hyperparameter settings, despite the known sensitivity of neural networks to training configurations. In this work, we systematically re-evaluate four representative LoRA variants alongside vanilla LoRA through extensive hyperparameter searches. Across mathematical and code generation tasks on diverse model scales, we find that different LoRA methods favor distinct learning rate ranges. Crucially, once learning rates are properly tuned, all methods achieve similar peak performance (within 1-2%), with only subtle rank-dependent behaviors. These results suggest that vanilla LoRA remains a competitive baseline and that improvements reported under single training configuration may not reflect consistent methodological advantages. Finally, a second-order analysis attributes the differing optimal learning rate ranges to variations in the largest Hessian eigenvalue, aligning with classical learning theories.

</details>


### [171] [Erase at the Core: Representation Unlearning for Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05375)
*Jaewon Lee,Yongwoo Kim,Donghyun Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Erase at the Core (EC) enforces forgetting across the entire network by multi-layer contrastive unlearning and deeply supervised learning, reducing internal representations’ similarity to the original model while preserving retain-set performance; it works as a plug-in for existing unlearning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address superficial forgetting where logit forgetting does not translate to representational forgetting; existing methods mainly alter the final classifier, leaving intermediate representations largely intact.

Method: Attach auxiliary modules to intermediate layers; apply contrastive unlearning on the forget set and cross-entropy losses at each supervision point; layer-wise weighted losses; jointly optimize forget and retain set preservation; model-agnostic plug-in.

Result: EC achieves effective logit-level forgetting and substantially reduces representational similarity across intermediate layers; improves representation-level forgetting; maintains performance on the retain set.

Conclusion: EC provides a general, plug-in framework to enforce forgetting throughout network hierarchy, addressing superficial forgetting and enhancing unlearning effectiveness.

Abstract: Many approximate machine unlearning methods demonstrate strong logit-level forgetting -- such as near-zero accuracy on the forget set -- yet continue to preserve substantial information within their internal feature representations. We refer to this discrepancy as superficial forgetting. Recent studies indicate that most existing unlearning approaches primarily alter the final classifier, leaving intermediate representations largely unchanged and highly similar to those of the original model. To address this limitation, we introduce the Erase at the Core (EC), a framework designed to enforce forgetting throughout the entire network hierarchy. EC integrates multi-layer contrastive unlearning on the forget set with retain set preservation through deeply supervised learning. Concretely, EC attaches auxiliary modules to intermediate layers and applies both contrastive unlearning and cross-entropy losses at each supervision point, with layer-wise weighted losses. Experimental results show that EC not only achieves effective logit-level forgetting, but also substantially reduces representational similarity to the original model across intermediate layers. Furthermore, EC is model-agnostic and can be incorporated as a plug-in module into existing unlearning methods, improving representation-level forgetting while maintaining performance on the retain set.

</details>


### [172] [Enhanced QKNorm normalization for neural transformers with the Lp norm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05006)
*Ezequiel Lopez-Rubio,Javier Montes-Perez,Esteban Jose Palomo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Generalizes QKNorm with Lp norms for Transformer query/key normalization; preliminary results on a simple task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To stabilize learning in Transformers by exploring non-Euclidean normalization schemes for Q/K vectors.

Method: Introduce an Lp-norm-based generalization of QKNorm normalization; allow non-Euclidean norms; conduct preliminary experiments on a simple problem.

Result: Experimental results indicate the proposed Lp-based normalization is suitable for the tested simple problem.

Conclusion: Lp-based QKNorm generalization is feasible; further comprehensive evaluation on diverse tasks is warranted.

Abstract: The normalization of query and key vectors is an essential part of the Transformer architecture. It ensures that learning is stable regardless of the scale of these vectors. Some normalization approaches are available. In this preliminary work, a generalization of the QKNorm normalization scheme is proposed. The approach is based on the Lp norm, allowing non-Euclidean norms to be employed. Experimental results demonstrate the suitability of the method for a simple problem.

</details>


### [173] [Assessing Electricity Demand Forecasting with Exogenous Data in Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05390)
*Wei Soon Cheong,Lian Lian Jiang,Jamie Ng Suat Ling*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Foundation models for time-series show mixed results in electricity demand forecasting; no universal superiority; performance depends on architecture and climate context; simple baselines can outperform in stable climates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether cross-channel foundation models can leverage exogenous features for electricity demand forecasting across two markets, hourly and daily, and compare against a strong baseline LSTM with reversible instance normalization.

Method: Empirical evaluation of MOIRAI, MOMENT, TinyTimeMixers, ChronosX, and Chronos-2 under three feature configurations (all features, selected features, target-only) across Singapore and Australia at hourly and daily granularities; compare to a baseline LSTM with reversible instance normalization.

Result: Chronos-2 achieves best performance among the foundation models in zero-shot settings, but the simple baseline LSTM frequently outperforms all foundation models in Singapore’s climate, especially for short-term horizons. Architectural factors matter: TTMs channel-mixing and Chronos-2’s grouped attention consistently benefit from exogenous features; other foundation models show inconsistent benefits. Geographic climate context is crucial; foundation models offer advantages primarily in variable climates, challenging the notion of universal superiority of foundation models in energy forecasting.

Conclusion: Foundation-model superiority is not universal in energy forecasting; domain-aware modeling and architecture choices are essential, with exogenous-feature integration and geographic context driving performance. Domain-specific energy models remain valuable.

Abstract: Time-series foundation models have emerged as a new paradigm for forecasting, yet their ability to effectively leverage exogenous features -- critical for electricity demand forecasting -- remains unclear. This paper empirically evaluates foundation models capable of modeling cross-channel correlations against a baseline LSTM with reversible instance normalization across Singaporean and Australian electricity markets at hourly and daily granularities. We systematically assess MOIRAI, MOMENT, TinyTimeMixers, ChronosX, and Chronos-2 under three feature configurations: all features, selected features, and target-only. Our findings reveal highly variable effectiveness: while Chronos-2 achieves the best performance among foundation models (in zero-shot settings), the simple baseline frequently outperforms all foundation models in Singapore's stable climate, particularly for short-term horizons. Model architecture proves critical, with synergistic architectural implementations (TTM's channel-mixing, Chronos-2's grouped attention) consistently leveraging exogenous features, while other approaches show inconsistent benefits. Geographic context emerges as equally important, with foundation models demonstrating advantages primarily in variable climates. These results challenge assumptions about universal foundation model superiority and highlight the need for domain-specific models, specifically in the energy domain.

</details>


### [174] [Laws of Learning Dynamics and the Core of Learners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05026)
*Inkee Jung,Siu Cheong Lau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entropy-based lifelong ensemble learning framework grounded in a conservation-law view of learning dynamics, showing robustness against transfer-based adversarial attacks on CIFAR-10, outperforming naive averaged ensembles, especially under strong perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address robustness and continual learning challenges by formalizing learning dynamics with entropy and conservation principles, and by leveraging an ensemble that adapts over time to defend against distribution shifts and adversarial transfers.

Method: Derive a conservation law for learning dynamics and an entropy-decrease principle; develop an entropy-based lifelong ensemble method (logifold); implement an immunization mechanism to defend against transfer-based adversarial attacks; evaluate against a naive ensemble via averaging models trained on clean and adversarial samples on CIFAR-10 across varying perturbation strengths.

Result: The proposed logifold ensemble achieves higher accuracy than the naive ensemble across most test cases, with particularly large gains under stronger perturbations.

Conclusion: An entropy-based conservation framework effectively guides robust lifelong ensemble learning; the immunization mechanism improves defense against transfer-based adversarial attacks on CIFAR-10, yielding notable gains under strong perturbations.

Abstract: We formulate the fundamental laws governing learning dynamics, namely the conservation law and the decrease of total entropy. Within this framework, we introduce an entropy-based lifelong ensemble learning method. We evaluate its effectiveness by constructing an immunization mechanism to defend against transfer-based adversarial attacks on the CIFAR-10 dataset. Compared with a naive ensemble formed by simply averaging models specialized on clean and adversarial samples, the resulting logifold achieves higher accuracy in most test cases, with particularly large gains under strong perturbations.

</details>


### [175] [Robust Federated Learning via Byzantine Filtering over Encrypted Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05410)
*Adda Akram Bendoukha,Aymen Boudguiga,Nesrine Kaaniche,Renaud Sirdey,Didem Demirag,Sébastien Gambs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A privacy-preserving FL framework that uses CKKS-based secure aggregation combined with meta-classifier–driven Byzantine filtering trained on shadow updates; auto-tunes CKKS hyperparameters; shows 90–94% Byzantine detection accuracy with minor utility loss across multiple benchmarks and modest encryption runtimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To jointly secure aggregation in Federated Learning against privacy attacks and Byzantine faults, addressing the gap where existing work treats secure aggregation and Byzantine resilience separately. The approach leverages property-inference-inspired filtering and automatic CKKS parameter tuning to maintain model utility.

Method: 1) Train an ensemble of SVM-based filtering meta-classifiers on labeled shadow updates to reproduce diverse Byzantine misbehaviors (backdoor, gradient-inversion, label-flipping, shuffling). 2) Use the meta-classifier outputs to reweight and cancel Byzantine encrypted updates during aggregation under CKKS. 3) Auto-select kernel and dimensionality hyperparameters for the CKKS scheme to optimize homomorphic inference, aggregation constraints, and efficiency. 

Result: Extensive experiments on FEMNIST, CIFAR10, GTSRB, and acsincome show that the SVM filtering achieves 90–94% accuracy in identifying Byzantine updates, with marginal losses in model utility. Encrypted inference runtimes range from 6–24 seconds, and per-aggregation runtimes from 9–26 seconds, indicating practical feasibility.

Conclusion: The integrated approach effectively couples privacy-preserving aggregation with Byzantine filtering, enabling efficient and accurate defense in FL. The automated CKKS hyperparameter selection further enhances practicality, though robustness to unseen attacks and scalability to large-scale deployments warrant further study.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) aims to train a collaborative model while preserving data privacy. However, the distributed nature of this approach still raises privacy and security issues, such as the exposure of sensitive data due to inference attacks and the influence of Byzantine behaviors on the trained model. In particular, achieving both secure aggregation and Byzantine resilience remains challenging, as existing solutions often address these aspects independently. In this work, we propose to address these challenges through a novel approach that combines homomorphic encryption for privacy-preserving aggregation with property-inference-inspired meta-classifiers for Byzantine filtering. First, following the property-inference attacks blueprint, we train a set of filtering meta-classifiers on labeled shadow updates, reproducing a diverse ensemble of Byzantine misbehaviors in FL, including backdoor, gradient-inversion, label-flipping and shuffling attacks. The outputs of these meta-classifiers are then used to cancel the Byzantine encrypted updates by reweighting. Second, we propose an automated method for selecting the optimal kernel and the dimensionality hyperparameters with respect to homomorphic inference, aggregation constraints and efficiency over the CKKS cryptosystem. Finally, we demonstrate through extensive experiments the effectiveness of our approach against Byzantine participants on the FEMNIST, CIFAR10, GTSRB, and acsincome benchmarks. More precisely, our SVM filtering achieves accuracies between $90$% and $94$% for identifying Byzantine updates at the cost of marginal losses in model utility and encrypted inference runtimes ranging from $6$ to $24$ seconds and from $9$ to $26$ seconds for an overall aggregation.

</details>


### [176] [BLITZRANK: Principled Zero-shot Ranking Agents with Tournament Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05448)
*Sheshansh Agrawal,Thien Hang Nguyen,Douwe Kiela*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a principled framework for top-m selection from n items using expensive k-wise comparisons via a tournament-graph representation; leverages the transitive closure of a global preference graph to extract more orderings and uses a greedy, information-gain-based query schedule; handles non-transitive preferences by forming equivalence classes for tiered rankings; demonstrates Pareto-dominant performance in LLM reranking benchmarks with substantial token savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently identify top-m items when only costly k-wise comparisons are available, and to exploit the rich information revealed by each comparison to reduce query cost while maintaining high accuracy.

Method: Model comparisons as k-item tournaments; convert each k-wise comparison into a complete pairwise tournament among the k items; accumulate these into a global preference graph and compute its transitive closure to infer additional orderings. Formalize certifiable ranks, and employ a greedy query strategy that maximizes information gain toward identifying the top-m. Handle non-transitive preferences by collapsing cycles into equivalence classes, yielding tiered rankings.

Result: Empirically applied to LLM reranking across 14 benchmarks and 5 models. The proposed method achieves Pareto dominance over baselines: matches or exceeds accuracy while using 25–40% fewer tokens than comparable methods, and about 7 times fewer tokens than pairwise reranking at similar quality.

Conclusion: A principled, information-theoretic framework for k-wise ranking and top-m selection that exploits complete pairwise information from k-item comparisons, handles cycles via tiered rankings, and delivers substantial efficiency gains in practical reranking tasks.

Abstract: Selecting the top $m$ from $n$ items via expensive $k$-wise comparisons is fundamental to settings ranging from LLM-based document reranking to crowdsourced evaluation and tournament design. Existing methods either rely on heuristics that fail to fully exploit the information each comparison reveals, or are inefficient when they do. We introduce a tournament graph framework that provides a principled foundation for $k$-wise ranking. Our key observation is that each $k$-item comparison reveals a complete tournament of $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise preferences; aggregating these into a global preference graph and computing its transitive closure yields many additional orderings without further oracle calls. We formalize when an item's rank is certifiably determined and design a greedy query schedule that maximizes information gain towards identifying the top-$m$ items. The framework also gracefully handles non-transitive preferences (cycles induced by real-world oracles) by collapsing them into equivalence classes that yield principled tiered rankings. Applied to LLM reranking across 14 benchmarks and 5 models, our method achieves Pareto dominance over existing approaches: matching or exceeding accuracy while requiring 25-40% fewer tokens than comparable methods, and $7\times$ fewer than pairwise reranking at near-identical quality.

</details>


### [177] [When Are RL Hyperparameters Benign? A Study in Offline Goal-Conditioned RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05459)
*Jan Malte Töpperwien,Aditya Mohan,Marius Lindauer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline goal-conditioned RL shows robustness to hyperparameters across stationary and non-stationary data; bootstrapping-based HIQL is more hyperparameter-sensitive than QRL, which benefits from small expert data and exhibits broader stable performance. Gradient interference in bootstrapped objectives explains sensitivity; suggests designing objectives to reduce such interference improves robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether hyperparameter sensitivity in RL is intrinsic or amplified by training mechanisms, by controlling data non-stationarity in offline settings and comparing bootstrapped TD-learning vs quasimetric representation learning.

Method: Study offline goal-conditioned RL under controlled data quality shifts (stationary and non-stationary regimes). Compare two algorithms: HIQL (bootstrapped TD-learning) and QRL (quasimetric representation learning). Introduce inter-goal gradient alignment diagnostic to diagnose gradient interference.

Result: Observed substantially greater robustness to hyperparameter changes than online RL. With ~20% expert data, QRL maintains broad near-optimal regions; HIQL shows sharp optima and drift across training phases. Bootstrapped objectives show stronger destructive gradient interference correlating with hyperparameter sensitivity.

Conclusion: Hyperparameter sensitivity in RL is not inevitable; it is amplified by bootstrapping dynamics. Designing objectives to reduce destructive gradient interference can improve robustness.

Abstract: Hyperparameter sensitivity in Deep Reinforcement Learning (RL) is often accepted as unavoidable. However, it remains unclear whether it is intrinsic to the RL problem or exacerbated by specific training mechanisms. We investigate this question in offline goal-conditioned RL, where data distributions are fixed, and non-stationarity can be explicitly controlled via scheduled shifts in data quality. Additionally, we study varying data qualities under both stationary and non-stationary regimes, and cover two representative algorithms: HIQL (bootstrapped TD-learning) and QRL (quasimetric representation learning). Overall, we observe substantially greater robustness to changes in hyperparameter configurations than commonly reported for online RL, even under controlled non-stationarity. Once modest expert data is present ($\approx$ 20\%), QRL maintains broad, stable near-optimal regions, while HIQL exhibits sharp optima that drift significantly across training phases. To explain this divergence, we introduce an inter-goal gradient alignment diagnostic. We find that bootstrapped objectives exhibit stronger destructive gradient interference, which coincides directly with hyperparameter sensitivity. These results suggest that high sensitivity to changes in hyperparameter configurations during training is not inevitable in RL, but is amplified by the dynamics of bootstrapping, offering a pathway toward more robust algorithmic objective design.

</details>


### [178] [ReFORM: Reflected Flows for On-support Offline RL via Noise Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05051)
*Songyuan Zhang,Oswin So,H. M. Sabbir Ahmad,Eric Yang Yu,Matthew Cleaveland,Mitchell Black,Chuchu Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReFORM is an offline RL method using flow policies to enforce a support-preserving constraint, learning a bounded-source BC flow and a reflected flow to generate bounded noise while keeping support, resulting in strong performance across 40 OGBench tasks with fixed hyperparameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL suffers from out-of-distribution (OOD) errors when the learned policy leaves the training data distribution. Existing penalization of a statistical distance to behave policies can restrict improvement and may not fully prevent OOD actions. Additionally, the optimal policy distribution can be multimodal and difficult to represent. There is a need for methods that maintain expressiveness while controlling OOD risk.

Method: ReFORM trains a BC flow policy with a bounded source distribution to capture the support of the action distribution. It then optimizes a reflected flow that produces bounded noise for the BC flow, designed to preserve the support while maximizing policy performance. The method enforces a less restrictive, construction-based support constraint and evaluates on offline data without additional interactions.

Result: Across 40 challenging tasks from the OGBench benchmark with varying data quality, ReFORM dominates all baselines when hyperparameters are fixed, outperforming methods that rely on hand-tuned hyperparameters.

Conclusion: Flow-based offline RL with a support-preserving construction can both mitigate OOD errors and retain expressive policy representations, achieving strong empirical performance on diverse offline datasets.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) aims to learn the optimal policy from a fixed dataset generated by behavior policies without additional environment interactions. One common challenge that arises in this setting is the out-of-distribution (OOD) error, which occurs when the policy leaves the training distribution. Prior methods penalize a statistical distance term to keep the policy close to the behavior policy, but this constrains policy improvement and may not completely prevent OOD actions. Another challenge is that the optimal policy distribution can be multimodal and difficult to represent. Recent works apply diffusion or flow policies to address this problem, but it is unclear how to avoid OOD errors while retaining policy expressiveness. We propose ReFORM, an offline RL method based on flow policies that enforces the less restrictive support constraint by construction. ReFORM learns a behavior cloning (BC) flow policy with a bounded source distribution to capture the support of the action distribution, then optimizes a reflected flow that generates bounded noise for the BC flow while keeping the support, to maximize the performance. Across 40 challenging tasks from the OGBench benchmark with datasets of varying quality and using a constant set of hyperparameters for all tasks, ReFORM dominates all baselines with hand-tuned hyperparameters on the performance profile curves.

</details>


### [179] [Thermodynamic Limits of Physical Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05463)
*Koichi Takahashi,Yusuke Hayashi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two bits-per-joule metrics for physical intelligence: Thermodynamic Epiplexity per Joule (structure information encoded per energy within a boundary) and Empowerment per Joule (sensorimotor channel capacity per energy over a horizon); together they quantify recognition vs control and link to Landauer bounds and MDL surrogates, with a unified reporting framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge AI energy efficiency with physical resource usage by formalizing metrics that quantify information gain and control influence per energy, enabling fair comparisons and practical guidelines.

Method: Theoretical development grounded in stochastic thermodynamics. Derives Landauer-scale closed-cycle benchmarks for epiplexity under explicit boundary/reset assumptions; analyzes decoupling without such assumptions; aligns epiplexity with compute-bounded MDL epiplexity; proposes a unified efficiency framework with reporting checklists and boundary-energy accounting.

Result: Shows conditions under which epiplexity gains are thermodynamically constrained by Landauer costs; demonstrates potential decoupling of information gain and dissipation without boundary assumptions; provides MDL-based surrogates for unavailable latent structure; offers a practical framework and reporting guidelines to enable consistent bits-per-joule comparisons and connects to energy-adjusted scaling analyses.

Conclusion: Presents two complementary, physically-grounded metrics of energy-efficient intelligence, clarifies when information gains incur thermodynamic costs, and delivers practical guidelines and surrogates to enable robust cross-study comparisons.

Abstract: Modern AI systems achieve remarkable capabilities at the cost of substantial energy consumption. To connect intelligence to physical efficiency, we propose two complementary bits-per-joule metrics under explicit accounting conventions: (1) Thermodynamic Epiplexity per Joule -- bits of structural information about a theoretical environment-instance variable newly encoded in an agent's internal state per unit measured energy within a stated boundary -- and (2) Empowerment per Joule -- the embodied sensorimotor channel capacity (control information) per expected energetic cost over a fixed horizon. These provide two axes of physical intelligence: recognition (model-building) vs.control (action influence). Drawing on stochastic thermodynamics, we show how a Landauer-scale closed-cycle benchmark for epiplexity acquisition follows as a corollary of a standard thermodynamic-learning inequality under explicit subsystem assumptions, and we clarify how Landauer-scaled costs act as closed-cycle benchmarks under explicit reset/reuse and boundary-closure assumptions; conversely, we give a simple decoupling construction showing that without such assumptions -- and without charging for externally prepared low-entropy resources (e.g.fresh memory) crossing the boundary -- information gain and in-boundary dissipation need not be tightly linked. For empirical settings where the latent structure variable is unavailable, we align the operational notion of epiplexity with compute-bounded MDL epiplexity and recommend reporting MDL-epiplexity / compression-gain surrogates as companions. Finally, we propose a unified efficiency framework that reports both metrics together with a minimal checklist of boundary/energy accounting, coarse-graining/noise, horizon/reset, and cost conventions to reduce ambiguity and support consistent bits-per-joule comparisons, and we sketch connections to energy-adjusted scaling analyses.

</details>


### [180] [A Unified Framework for Rethinking Policy Divergence Measures in GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05494)
*Qingyuan Wu,Yuhui Wang,Simon Sinong Zhan,Yanning Dai,Shilong Deng,Sarra Habchi,Qi Zhu,Matthias Gallé,Chao Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified clipping framework for RL-based reward optimization in LLMs (RLVR) introducing KL3 as a principled divergence constraint; shows equivalence to asymmetric clipping and improves GRPO-style training on math reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods enforce stable policy updates via clipping likelihood ratios, but a unified framework over general policy divergence is needed to understand trade-offs and guide improvements.

Method: Proposes a unified clipping framework that encompasses various divergences (likelihood ratios, KL, and others). Identifies KL3 as a variance-reduced estimator for KL divergence and proves its equivalence to an asymmetric ratio-based clipping that redirects mass toward high-confidence actions. Integrates KL3 into GRPO-style updates and analyzes exploration vs. stability.

Result: KL3-based constraint yields improved training stability and better final performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks compared to baseline GRPO-style methods.

Conclusion: A principled policy-divergence constraint, instantiated via KL3, enhances RLVR for LLMs by balancing exploration and stability, and the unified framework enables systematic analysis of divergence measures on policy optimization.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verified Reward (RLVR) has emerged as a critical paradigm for advancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Most existing RLVR methods, such as GRPO and its variants, ensure stable updates by constraining policy divergence through clipping likelihood ratios. This paper introduces a unified clipping framework that characterizes existing methods via a general notion of policy divergence, encompassing both likelihood ratios and Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergences and extending to alternative measures. The framework provides a principled foundation for systematically analyzing how different policy divergence measures affect exploration and performance. We further identify the KL3 estimator, a variance-reduced Monte Carlo estimator of the KL divergence, as a key policy divergence constraint. We theoretically demonstrate that the KL3-based constraint is mathematically equivalent to an asymmetric ratio-based clipping that reallocates probability mass toward high-confidence actions, promoting stronger exploration while retaining the simplicity of GRPO-style methods. Empirical results on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that incorporating the KL3 estimator into GRPO improves both training stability and final performance, highlighting the importance of principled policy divergence constraints in policy optimization.

</details>


### [181] [Detecting Misbehaviors of Large Vision-Language Models by Evidential Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05535)
*Tao Huang,Rui Wang,Xiaofei Liu,Yi Qin,Li Duan,Liping Jing*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EUQ is a fine-grained evidential uncertainty quantification method for LVLM misbehavior detection that captures both information conflict and ignorance in a single forward pass; it outperforms strong baselines across hallucinations, jailbreaks, adversarial vulnerabilities, and OOD, and enables layer-wise interpretability. Code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs often misbehave under incompetent or adversarial inputs due to epistemic uncertainty. Existing UQ approaches typically capture overall uncertainty and miss signals like information conflict or ignorance, hindering reliable detection for critical deployment.

Method: Interpret output-head features as supporting (positive) or opposing (negative) evidence. Use Evidence Theory to model and aggregate evidence, yielding measures of internal conflict and ignorance in a single forward pass. Conduct layer-wise analyses to study the evolution of representations.

Result: Extensive evaluation across four misbehavior categories (hallucinations, jailbreaks, adversarial vulnerabilities, OOD). EUQ consistently outperforms strong baselines. Hallucinations linked to high internal conflict; OOD linked to high ignorance. Layer-wise evidential dynamics provide interpretability of representation changes.

Conclusion: EUQ offers effective, efficient detection of LVLM misbehaviors with a single forward pass and provides interpretable signals via conflict and ignorance metrics; source code is available at the listed GitHub repository.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have shown substantial advances in multimodal understanding and generation. However, when presented with incompetent or adversarial inputs, they frequently produce unreliable or even harmful content, such as fact hallucinations or dangerous instructions. This misalignment with human expectations, referred to as \emph{misbehaviors} of LVLMs, raises serious concerns for deployment in critical applications. These misbehaviors are found to stem from epistemic uncertainty, specifically either conflicting internal knowledge or the absence of supporting information. However, existing uncertainty quantification methods, which typically capture only overall epistemic uncertainty, have shown limited effectiveness in identifying such issues. To address this gap, we propose Evidential Uncertainty Quantification (EUQ), a fine-grained method that captures both information conflict and ignorance for effective detection of LVLM misbehaviors. In particular, we interpret features from the model output head as either supporting (positive) or opposing (negative) evidence. Leveraging Evidence Theory, we model and aggregate this evidence to quantify internal conflict and knowledge gaps within a single forward pass. We extensively evaluate our method across four categories of misbehavior, including hallucinations, jailbreaks, adversarial vulnerabilities, and out-of-distribution (OOD) failures, using state-of-the-art LVLMs, and find that EUQ consistently outperforms strong baselines, showing that hallucinations correspond to high internal conflict and OOD failures to high ignorance. Furthermore, layer-wise evidential uncertainty dynamics analysis helps interpret the evolution of internal representations from a new perspective. The source code is available at https://github.com/HT86159/EUQ.

</details>


### [182] [When Shared Knowledge Hurts: Spectral Over-Accumulation in Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05536)
*Yayuan Li,Ze Peng,Jian Zhang,Jintao Guo,Yue Duan,Yinghuan Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SVC is a training- and data-free post-processing method that calibrates singular values to prevent over-counting of shared spectral directions in merged models, boosting performance across vision and language tasks and improving Task Arithmetic by 13.0%. Code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model merging by adding weight updates can inflate singular values when tasks share aligned spectral directions, biasing the merged model toward shared subspaces. Existing methods mostly address task conflict, not this over-counting of shared knowledge.

Method: Compute subspace overlap and adjust singular values by rescaling inflated components without data or further training. This Singular Value Calibration (SVC) acts as a post-processing step after merging.

Result: SVC consistently improves strong merging baselines across vision and language benchmarks and achieves state-of-the-art performance; removing the bias caused by inflated singular values. 13.0% gain on Task Arithmetic when only singular values are modified.

Conclusion: SVC mitigates a key failure mode in model merging by calibrating the spectrum, enabling more balanced transfer of knowledge without additional training or data; it is simple, effective, and broadly applicable.

Abstract: Model merging combines multiple fine-tuned models into a single model by adding their weight updates, providing a lightweight alternative to retraining. Existing methods primarily target resolving conflicts between task updates, leaving the failure mode of over-counting shared knowledge unaddressed. We show that when tasks share aligned spectral directions (i.e., overlapping singular vectors), a simple linear combination repeatedly accumulates these directions, inflating the singular values and biasing the merged model toward shared subspaces. To mitigate this issue, we propose Singular Value Calibration (SVC), a training-free and data-free post-processing method that quantifies subspace overlap and rescales inflated singular values to restore a balanced spectrum. Across vision and language benchmarks, SVC consistently improves strong merging baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance. Furthermore, by modifying only the singular values, SVC improves the performance of Task Arithmetic by 13.0%. Code is available at: https://github.com/lyymuwu/SVC.

</details>


### [183] [Steering Large Reasoning Models towards Concise Reasoning via Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05539)
*Yawei Li,Benjamin Bergner,Yinghan Zhao,Vihang Prakash Patil,Bei Chen,Cheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowSteer learns a nonlinear distributional transport to convert verbose reasoning activations into concise ones using Flow Matching, enabling input-dependent control and improved token efficiency over linear steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The restrictive linear representation hypothesis limits steering of large reasoning models; there is a need for nonlinear, input-sensitive methods that align the model's reasoning activations with concise outputs while preserving performance.

Method: FlowSteer trains a full distributional transport by modeling a velocity field via Flow Matching to transform verbose reasoning distributions into concise ones; the transformation is nonlinear and input-dependent, applied to hidden representations.

Result: FlowSteer achieves stronger task performance and better token efficiency than leading inference-time baselines across diverse reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Modeling full distributional transport with generative techniques provides a principled foundation for controlling LRMs, yielding more compact and efficient reasoning.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) excel at complex reasoning tasks, but their efficiency is often hampered by overly verbose outputs. Prior steering methods attempt to address this issue by applying a single, global vector to hidden representations -- an approach grounded in the restrictive linear representation hypothesis. In this work, we introduce FlowSteer, a nonlinear steering method that goes beyond uniform linear shifts by learning a complete transformation between the distributions associated with verbose and concise reasoning. This transformation is learned via Flow Matching as a velocity field, enabling precise, input-dependent control over the model's reasoning process. By aligning steered representations with the distribution of concise-reasoning activations, FlowSteer yields more compact reasoning than the linear shifts. Across diverse reasoning benchmarks, FlowSteer demonstrates strong task performance and token efficiency compared to leading inference-time baselines. Our work demonstrates that modeling the full distributional transport with generative techniques offers a more effective and principled foundation for controlling LRMs.

</details>


### [184] [Unveiling Implicit Advantage Symmetry: Why GRPO Struggles with Exploration and Difficulty Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05548)
*Zhiqi Yu,Zhangquan Chen,Mengting Liu,Heye Zhang,Liangqiong Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Asymmetric GRAE (A-GRAE) overcomes symmetry-induced exploration and difficulty-focus bottlenecks in Group Relative Advantage Estimation (GRAE) for GRPO, by dynamically modulating advantage signals and adopting a curriculum-like sample prioritization, yielding consistent gains across seven benchmarks and both LLMs and MLLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRAE’s implicit symmetry creates two major limitations: (i) group-level symmetry leaves the correct trajectories’ logits insufficiently explored since wrong-action logits are not sufficiently altered; (ii) sample-level symmetry biases learning toward medium-difficulty samples and ignores non-stationary difficulty demands. These cause suboptimal exploration efficiency and learning progress in GRPO-based RL with verifiable rewards.

Method: Propose Asymmetric GRAE (A-GRAE) that (a) asymmetrically modulates the advantage signals to discourage easy but correct trajectories in some contexts, promoting exploration of alternative correct paths; and (b) implements a curriculum-like transition-prioritization that starts with simpler samples and gradually shifts focus to more complex ones. Validate across seven benchmarks on GRPO and its variants using both LLMs and MLLMs.

Result: A-GRAE consistently improves GRPO and its variants across seven benchmarks, with enhanced exploration and learning efficiency observed for both LLMs and MLLMs.

Conclusion: Dynamic asymmetry in GRAE, coupled with a curriculum-based sample emphasis, yields robust improvements in reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. A-GRAE reduces the exploration bottleneck and adapts to changing difficulty demands, suggesting wide applicability to GRPO-based LLM/MLLM reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), particularly GRPO, has become the standard for eliciting LLM reasoning. However, its efficiency in exploration and difficulty adaptation remains an open challenge. In this work, we argue that these bottlenecks stem from an implicit advantage symmetry inherent in Group Relative Advantage Estimation (GRAE). This symmetry induces two critical limitations: (i) at the group level, strict symmetry in weights between correct and incorrect trajectories leaves unsampled action logits unchanged, thereby hindering exploration of novel correct solution. (ii) at the sample level, the algorithm implicitly prioritizes medium-difficulty samples, remaining agnostic to the non-stationary demands of difficulty focus. Through controlled experiments, we reveal that this symmetric property is sub-optimal, yielding two pivotal insights: (i) asymmetrically suppressing the advantages of correct trajectories encourages essential exploration. (ii) learning efficiency is maximized by a curriculum-like transition-prioritizing simpler samples initially before gradually shifting to complex ones. Motivated by these findings, we propose Asymmetric GRAE (A-GRAE), which dynamically modulates exploration incentives and sample-difficulty focus. Experiments across seven benchmarks demonstrate that A-GRAE consistently improves GRPO and its variants across both LLMs and MLLMs.

</details>


### [185] [Logical Guidance for the Exact Composition of Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05549)
*Francesco Alesiani,Jonathan Warrell,Tanja Bien,Henrik Christiansen,Matheus Ferraz,Mathias Niepert*
