Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FairBoost integrates group fairness constraints into boosting by projecting the exponential-weights distribution onto a convex fairness set, training weak learners on the projected distribution. It yields a KL-divergence–based fairness cost δ_t that reduces the effective edge, establishing an exponential-loss bound; experiments show competitive fairness-accuracy tradeoffs and stable training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To incorporate group fairness into boosting while preserving analytical training dynamics and convergence guarantees.

Method: Project the ensemble-induced exponential-weights distribution onto a convex set of fairness constraints (reweighting surrogate); train weak learners on this fair distribution. The fairness cost is δ_t = sqrt(KL(w^t || q^t)/2), which reduces the effective edge.

Result: An exponential-loss bound is derived where the convergence rate depends on the weak learner edge minus δ_t. Empirical experiments on standard benchmarks validate the tradeoff and demonstrate stable training curves.

Conclusion: FairBoost provides an explicit, quantifiable accuracy-fairness tradeoff in boosting dynamics via KL-based projection, with strong theoretical guarantees and competitive empirical performance.

Abstract: Boosting algorithms enjoy strong theoretical guarantees: when weak learners maintain positive edge, AdaBoost achieves geometric decrease of exponential loss. We study how to incorporate group fairness constraints into boosting while preserving analyzable training dynamics. Our approach, FairBoost, projects the ensemble-induced exponential-weights distribution onto a convex set of distributions satisfying fairness constraints (as a reweighting surrogate), then trains weak learners on this fair distribution. The key theoretical insight is that projecting the training distribution reduces the effective edge of weak learners by a quantity controlled by the KL-divergence of the projection. We prove an exponential-loss bound where the convergence rate depends on weak learner edge minus a "fairness cost" term $δ_t = \sqrt{\mathrm{KL}(w^t \| q^t)/2}$. This directly quantifies the accuracy-fairness tradeoff in boosting dynamics. Experiments on standard benchmarks validate the theoretical predictions and demonstrate competitive fairness-accuracy tradeoffs with stable training curves.

</details>


### [212] [Muon in Associative Memory Learning: Training Dynamics and Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05725)
*Binghui Li,Kaifei Wang,Han Zhong,Pinyan Lu,Liwei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Muon optimizer provides exponential speedup over gradient descent in a linear associative memory with softmax retrieval under a hierarchical frequency spectrum, offering improved scaling in noisy settings and interpretable as an implicit preconditioner; experiments support theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the dynamics and scaling of Muon, particularly its effectiveness in imbalanced learning of frequency components and its relation to preconditioning, across noiseless and noisy regimes.

Method: Theoretical analysis of Muon vs GD in a linear associative memory model with softmax retrieval and a hierarchical query-answer frequency spectrum; derive convergence and scaling laws for noiseless and noisy cases; interpret Muon as an implicit matrix preconditioner via adaptive task alignment and block-symmetric gradient structure; compare to coordinate-wise sign preconditioning; validate with experiments on synthetic long-tail data and LLaMA-style pretraining.

Result: GD learns frequency components at imbalanced rates, causing slow convergence; Muon mitigates the imbalance, enabling faster and more uniform progress. In the noiseless setting Muon achieves exponential speedup over GD. In the noisy setting with a power-decay spectrum, Muon’s scaling law is derived and shown to offer superior scaling over GD. Muon can be interpreted as an implicit preconditioner; a sign-based preconditioner could match Muon given oracle access to task representations, which is infeasible for SignGD in practice. Experiments on synthetic long-tail classification and LLaMA-style pre-training corroborate the theory.

Conclusion: Muon yields faster, more uniform optimization with favorable scaling and a principled preconditioner interpretation, with practical implications for long-tail and large-scale pretraining tasks; further work could explore broader spectrum shapes and practical limitations.

Abstract: Muon updates matrix parameters via the matrix sign of the gradient and has shown strong empirical gains, yet its dynamics and scaling behavior remain unclear in theory. We study Muon in a linear associative memory model with softmax retrieval and a hierarchical frequency spectrum over query-answer pairs, with and without label noise. In this setting, we show that Gradient Descent (GD) learns frequency components at highly imbalanced rates, leading to slow convergence bottlenecked by low-frequency components. In contrast, the Muon optimizer mitigates this imbalance, leading to faster and more uniform progress. Specifically, in the noiseless case, Muon achieves an exponential speedup over GD; in the noisy case with a power-decay frequency spectrum, we derive Muon's optimization scaling law and demonstrate its superior scaling efficiency over GD. Furthermore, we show that Muon can be interpreted as an implicit matrix preconditioner arising from adaptive task alignment and block-symmetric gradient structure. In contrast, the preconditioner with coordinate-wise sign operator could match Muon under oracle access to unknown task representations, which is infeasible for SignGD in practice. Experiments on synthetic long-tail classification and LLaMA-style pre-training corroborate the theory.

</details>


### [213] [CSRv2: Unlocking Ultra-Sparse Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05735)
*Lixuan Guo,Yifei Wang,Tiansheng Wen,Yifan Wang,Aosong Feng,Bo Chen,Stefanie Jegelka,Chenyu You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CSRv2 enables ultra-sparse embeddings (k as low as 2) with full backbone finetuning, using progressive sparsity via k-annealing and supervised contrastive objectives to boost representation quality. It significantly reduces dead neurons, improves accuracy, and yields substantial efficiency gains over dense, CSR, and MRL across text and vision tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense, high-dimensional embeddings impose storage, memory, and latency costs. CSR performs sparsification but collapses in ultra-sparse regimes (>80% dead neurons). There is a need for a training paradigm that stabilizes sparsity and preserves or enhances performance when most neurons are inactive.

Method: Progressive k-annealing to stabilize sparsity; supervised contrastive learning objectives to improve representation quality; end-to-end fine-tuning of the backbone to ensure compatibility with ultra-sparse projections; mapping dense embeddings into high-dimensional but k-sparse vectors.

Result: Dead neurons reduced from 80% to 20%; 14% accuracy gain at k=2; ultra-sparse embeddings with k=2 match CSR at k=8 and MRL at 32 dimensions with only two active features; 7x speedup vs MRL; up to 300x compute/memory efficiency vs dense embeddings in text representation; in text/vision, CSRv2 improves CSR by 7%/4% at k=4 and 14%/6% at k=2; validated across text and vision.

Conclusion: CSRv2 makes ultra-sparse embeddings practical without sacrificing performance, expanding the design space for real-time and edge-deployable AI systems by delivering strong efficiency gains alongside maintained or improved embedding quality.

Abstract: In the era of large foundation models, the quality of embeddings has become a central determinant of downstream task performance and overall system capability. Yet widely used dense embeddings are often extremely high-dimensional, incurring substantial costs in storage, memory, and inference latency. To address these, Contrastive Sparse Representation (CSR) is recently proposed as a promising direction, mapping dense embeddings into high-dimensional but k-sparse vectors, in contrast to compact dense embeddings such as Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL). Despite its promise, CSR suffers severe degradation in the ultra-sparse regime, where over 80% of neurons remain inactive, leaving much of its efficiency potential unrealized. In this paper, we introduce CSRv2, a principled training approach designed to make ultra-sparse embeddings viable. CSRv2 stabilizes sparsity learning through progressive k-annealing, enhances representational quality via supervised contrastive objectives, and ensures end-to-end adaptability with full backbone finetuning. CSRv2 reduces dead neurons from 80% to 20% and delivers a 14% accuracy gain at k=2, bringing ultra-sparse embeddings on par with CSR at k=8 and MRL at 32 dimensions, all with only two active features. While maintaining comparable performance, CSRv2 delivers a 7x speedup over MRL, and yields up to 300x improvements in compute and memory efficiency relative to dense embeddings in text representation. Extensive experiments across text and vision demonstrate that CSRv2 makes ultra-sparse embeddings practical without compromising performance, where CSRv2 achieves 7%/4% improvement over CSR when k=4 and further increases this gap to 14%/6% when k=2 in text/vision representation. By making extreme sparsity viable, CSRv2 broadens the design space for real-time and edge-deployable AI systems where both embedding quality and efficiency are critical.

</details>


### [214] [Learning to Inject: Automated Prompt Injection via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05746)
*Xin Chen,Jie Zhang,Florian Tramer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoInject uses reinforcement learning to automatically generate universal, transferable adversarial suffixes for prompt injection in LLM agents. It optimizes for attack success while preserving benign task utility in a black-box setting, supporting query-based optimization and transfer to unseen models. A 1.5B-parameter adversarial suffix generator attacks frontier models (GPT 5 Nano, Claude Sonnet 3.5, Gemini 2.5 Flash) on AgentDojo, establishing a strong automated baseline for prompt injection research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents are critical but under-explored from an optimization perspective. Current approaches rely on human red-teamers and hand-crafted prompts, limiting scalability and adaptability.

Method: An RL-based framework (AutoInject) that learns a universal adversarial suffix generator. The method jointly optimizes attack success and utility preservation on benign tasks, operates in a black-box setting, and supports query-based optimization and transfer attacks to unseen models and tasks.

Result: Using a 1.5B parameter generator, AutoInject successfully compromises frontier systems (GPT 5 Nano, Claude Sonnet 3.5, Gemini 2.5 Flash) on the AgentDojo benchmark, establishing a stronger baseline for automated prompt injection research.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the viability of automated, transferable prompt-injection generation and highlights the potential risk to frontier LLM systems, while underscoring the need for robust defenses and further evaluation across diverse models and tasks.

Abstract: Prompt injection is one of the most critical vulnerabilities in LLM agents; yet, effective automated attacks remain largely unexplored from an optimization perspective. Existing methods heavily depend on human red-teamers and hand-crafted prompts, limiting their scalability and adaptability. We propose AutoInject, a reinforcement learning framework that generates universal, transferable adversarial suffixes while jointly optimizing for attack success and utility preservation on benign tasks. Our black-box method supports both query-based optimization and transfer attacks to unseen models and tasks. Using only a 1.5B parameter adversarial suffix generator, we successfully compromise frontier systems including GPT 5 Nano, Claude Sonnet 3.5, and Gemini 2.5 Flash on the AgentDojo benchmark, establishing a stronger baseline for automated prompt injection research.

</details>


### [215] [How to Achieve the Intended Aim of Deep Clustering Now, without Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05749)
*Kai Ming Ting,Wei-Jie Xu,Hang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DEC does not inherently overcome k-means' fundamental limitations; a non-deep approach that leverages cluster distribution can effectively address irregular shapes, sizes, and densities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether deep clustering methods (specifically DEC) truly overcome the fundamental limitations of k-means, and to assess the role of data distribution in clustering performance.

Method: Analytical and empirical evaluation of DEC's ability to exploit cluster distribution, comparing it to a non-deep clustering method that utilizes distributional information of clusters in the dataset.

Result: DEC does not fundamentally overcome k-means limitations and deep clustering methods, in general, do not exploit the underlying data distribution; a non-deep approach that leverages distributional information achieves the intended goals of deep clustering by addressing shape, size, and density variations.

Conclusion: The purported advantages of deep clustering may stem from distribution-aware strategies that are not inherently part of DEC; incorporating distributional information—even in non-deep methods—can effectively address the core limitations of k-means, implying a need to rethink the claimed superiority of deep clustering.

Abstract: Deep clustering (DC) is often quoted to have a key advantage over $k$-means clustering. Yet, this advantage is often demonstrated using image datasets only, and it is unclear whether it addresses the fundamental limitations of $k$-means clustering. Deep Embedded Clustering (DEC) learns a latent representation via an autoencoder and performs clustering based on a $k$-means-like procedure, while the optimization is conducted in an end-to-end manner. This paper investigates whether the deep-learned representation has enabled DEC to overcome the known fundamental limitations of $k$-means clustering, i.e., its inability to discover clusters of arbitrary shapes, varied sizes and densities. Our investigations on DEC have a wider implication on deep clustering methods in general. Notably, none of these methods exploit the underlying data distribution. We uncover that a non-deep learning approach achieves the intended aim of deep clustering by making use of distributional information of clusters in a dataset to effectively address these fundamental limitations.

</details>


### [216] [Variational Speculative Decoding: Rethinking Draft Training from Token Likelihood to Sequence Acceptance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05774)
*Xiandong Zou,Jianshu Li,Jing Huang,Pan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Variational Speculative Decoding (VSD) reframes draft training as variational inference over latent draft paths to align training with decoding-time verification, using EM with ARW/CAR to boost proposal quality and reduce variance, yielding measurable decoding speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the training-decoding discrepancy in speculative decoding, where training optimizes single greedy trajectories while decoding requires evaluating and ranking multiple sampled drafts.

Method: Model draft paths z via a variational objective (ELBO) centered on the marginal acceptance probability by the target model. Employ an EM procedure: E-step uses MCMC sampling from an oracle-filtered posterior; M-step maximizes a weighted likelihood with Adaptive Rejection Weighting (ARW) and Confidence-Aware Regularization (CAR), incorporating a path-level utility to reduce variance.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows VSD increases expected acceptance length and speedup. Empirically, VSD achieves up to 9.6% speedup over EAGLE-3 and 7.9% over ViSpec across LLMs/MLLMs, indicating substantial decoding efficiency gains.

Conclusion: VSD effectively closes the training-decoding gap in speculative decoding by optimizing latent draft proposals and leveraging EM with ARW/CAR, yielding practical gains in decoding speed without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates inference for (M)LLMs, yet a training-decoding discrepancy persists: while existing methods optimize single greedy trajectories, decoding involves verifying and ranking multiple sampled draft paths. We propose Variational Speculative Decoding (VSD), formulating draft training as variational inference over latent proposals (draft paths). VSD maximizes the marginal probability of target-model acceptance, yielding an ELBO that promotes high-quality latent proposals while minimizing divergence from the target distribution. To enhance quality and reduce variance, we incorporate a path-level utility and optimize via an Expectation-Maximization procedure. The E-step draws MCMC samples from an oracle-filtered posterior, while the M-step maximizes weighted likelihood using Adaptive Rejection Weighting (ARW) and Confidence-Aware Regularization (CAR). Theoretical analysis confirms that VSD increases expected acceptance length and speedup. Extensive experiments across LLMs and MLLMs show that VSD achieves up to a 9.6% speedup over EAGLE-3 and 7.9% over ViSpec, significantly improving decoding efficiency.

</details>


### [217] [Cross-Domain Offline Policy Adaptation via Selective Transition Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05776)
*Mengbei Yan,Jiafei Lyu,Shengjie Sun,Zhongjian Qiao,Jingwen Yang,Zichuan Lin,Deheng Ye,Xiu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STC introduces Selective Transition Correction for cross-domain offline RL with dynamics mismatch. It corrects source-domain transitions via an inverse policy model and a reward model to align with target dynamics, then uses a forward dynamics model to retain corrected samples that better fit the target. This enables leveraging source data more reliably, achieving superior performance over baselines across dynamic-shift environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The core challenge is adapting policies across domains with mismatched dynamics in offline RL. Simply merging datasets from similar domains can degrade performance due to distributional and dynamic mismatches. Existing methods mitigate this via transition filtering or reward shaping, but may underutilize valuable source data. A method that systematically corrects source data to reflect target dynamics could better exploit cross-domain information.

Method: 1) Learn an inverse policy model to adjust actions in source transitions. 2) Learn a reward model to modify the rewards of source transitions. 3) Use these to transform source data to align with target dynamics. 4) Train a forward dynamics model to assess which corrected samples better match target dynamics and retain them. 5) Integrate these corrected samples into policy learning as the Selective Transition Correction (STC) approach to enable reliable cross-domain offline RL.

Result: Empirical evaluations across multiple environments with dynamics shifts show that STC outperforms existing baselines, demonstrating improved policy performance when leveraging cross-domain offline data.

Conclusion: STC provides a principled framework to leverage source-domain data in cross-domain offline RL by correcting transitions to reflect the target dynamics and selectively retaining samples that better match those dynamics. This leads to more effective policy adaptation compared to prior source-filtering or reward-modification techniques.

Abstract: It remains a critical challenge to adapt policies across domains with mismatched dynamics in reinforcement learning (RL). In this paper, we study cross-domain offline RL, where an offline dataset from another similar source domain can be accessed to enhance policy learning upon a target domain dataset. Directly merging the two datasets may lead to suboptimal performance due to potential dynamics mismatches. Existing approaches typically mitigate this issue through source domain transition filtering or reward modification, which, however, may lead to insufficient exploitation of the valuable source domain data. Instead, we propose to modify the source domain data into the target domain data. To that end, we leverage an inverse policy model and a reward model to correct the actions and rewards of source transitions, explicitly achieving alignment with the target dynamics. Since limited data may result in inaccurate model training, we further employ a forward dynamics model to retain corrected samples that better match the target dynamics than the original transitions. Consequently, we propose the Selective Transition Correction (STC) algorithm, which enables reliable usage of source domain data for policy adaptation. Experiments on various environments with dynamics shifts demonstrate that STC achieves superior performance against existing baselines.

</details>


### [218] [How Controlling the Variance can Improve Training Stability of Sparsely Activated DNNs and CNNs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05779)
*Emily Dent,Jared Tanner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Larger fixed Gaussian process variances at initialization, within Edge-of-Chaos (EoC) regimes, improve expressivity and training stability for networks with high sparsity in activations (e.g., CReLU_{τ,m}); enabling up to ~90% sparsity with near-full accuracy and suggesting potential energy efficiency gains in fully connected layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how the fixed variance of the Gaussian process implied by EoC initialisation affects training dynamics and representational capacity when using sparsity-inducing activations like CReLU. The work seeks to reconcile GP-based theoretical views with practical sparse networks and energy efficiency.

Method: The paper analyzes the limiting Gaussian-process description of intermediate-layer activations under EoC initialisation and empirically evaluates various initial variances in networks using sparsity-inducing activations (CReLU with shift τ and cap m) on DNNs and CNNs, assessing expressivity, stability, and accuracy across different sparsity levels.

Result: Initialisations with larger fixed GP variances yield better expressivity under activation sparsity, improve training stability, and enable near-full accuracy at extremely high sparsity (up to 90%) in hidden layers for both DNNs and CNNs; this points to potential energy efficiency benefits.

Conclusion: Managing the fixed variance of the GP implied by EoC initialisation is a viable strategy to sustain performance at high sparsity, offering a mechanism to reduce energy consumption in models with dense layers; further work could quantify trade-offs and explore broader architectures.

Abstract: The intermediate layers of deep networks can be characterised as a Gaussian process, in particular the Edge-of-Chaos (EoC) initialisation strategy prescribes the limiting covariance matrix of the Gaussian process. Here we show that the under-utilised chosen variance of the Gaussian process is important in the training of deep networks with sparsity inducing activation, such as a shifted and clipped ReLU, $\text{CReLU}_{τ,m}(x)=\min(\max(x-τ,0),m)$. Specifically, initialisations leading to larger fixed Gaussian process variances, allow for improved expressivity with activation sparsity as large as 90% in DNNs and CNNs, and generally improve the stability of the training process. Enabling full, or near full, accuracy at such high levels of sparsity in the hidden layers suggests a promising mechanism to reduce the energy consumption of machine learning models involving fully connected layers.

</details>


### [219] [Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Diffusion Bridge Critics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05783)
*Shutong Ding,Yimiao Zhou,Ke Hu,Mokai Pan,Shan Zhong,Yanwei Fu,Jingya Wang,Ye Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a diffusion-bridge-based distributional critic (DBC) for diffusion RL, modeling the inverse CDF of Q-values to capture the full value distribution and avoid Gaussian collapse; provides an analytic integral for discretization; claims plug-and-play applicability and superior performance on MuJoCo benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate value estimation in RL is crucial, especially under task stochasticity. Distributional modeling of the critic is more informative than point estimates, and diffusion models offer expressive power. The critique has been underexplored for diffusion-based RL compared to diffusion policies.

Method: Introduce Diffusion Bridge Critics (DBC) that directly model the inverse CDF of the Q-value using a diffusion-bridge formulation. Provide an analytic integral to mitigate discretization errors in the diffusion process. Treat DBC as a plug-and-play component compatible with existing RL frameworks.

Result: Empirical evaluation on MuJoCo continuous control tasks shows that DBC outperforms prior distributional critic models.

Conclusion: DBC is the first diffusion-bridge-based critic, enabling accurate distributional value estimation and easy integration into current RL pipelines, leading to improved performance on standard benchmarks.

Abstract: Recent advances in diffusion-based reinforcement learning (RL) methods have demonstrated promising results in a wide range of continuous control tasks. However, existing works in this field focus on the application of diffusion policies while leaving the diffusion critics unexplored. In fact, since policy optimization fundamentally relies on the critic, accurate value estimation is far more important than policy expressiveness. Furthermore, given the stochasticity of most reinforcement learning tasks, it has been confirmed that the critic is more appropriately depicted with a distributional model. Motivated by these points, we propose a novel distributional RL method with Diffusion Bridge Critics (DBC). DBC directly models the inverse cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the Q value. This allows us to accurately capture the value distribution and prevents it from collapsing into a trivial Gaussian distribution owing to the strong distribution-matching capability of the diffusion bridge. Moreover, we further derive an analytic integral formula to address discretization errors in DBC, which is essential in value estimation. To our knowledge, DBC is the first work to employ the diffusion bridge model as the critic. Notably, DBC is also a plug-and-play component and can be integrated into most existing RL frameworks. Experimental results on MuJoCo robot control benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of DBC compared with previous distributional critic models.

</details>


### [220] [Classification Under Local Differential Privacy with Model Reversal and Model Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05797)
*Caihong Qin,Yang Bai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframes private learning under local differential privacy as a transfer learning problem from noisy (source) to clean (target) data, and introduces three LDP-specific techniques—noised binary feedback evaluation, model reversal, and model averaging—to boost classification accuracy. The approach yields theoretical excess risk bounds and shows empirical gains on synthetic and real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LDP protects user privacy at data collection but introduces substantial noise, degrading utility. The work seeks to recover utility without compromising privacy by reinterpreting LDP learning as transfer learning and leveraging noisy source information.

Method: 1) Noised binary feedback-based evaluation to estimate dataset utility under LDP. 2) Model reversal to salvage underperforming classifiers by inverting their decision boundaries. 3) Model averaging to weight multiple reversed classifiers by estimated utility. The study provides theoretical excess risk bounds under LDP and demonstrates utility improvements through experiments.

Result: The authors derive excess risk bounds for learning under LDP and empirically demonstrate substantial gains in classification accuracy on both simulated and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: A principled transfer-learning-inspired framework for LDP learning that injects three tailored techniques to improve utility while maintaining privacy. The work combines theoretical guarantees with empirical validation, suggesting a viable path to higher accuracy in privacy-preserving classification.

Abstract: Local differential privacy (LDP) has become a central topic in data privacy research, offering strong privacy guarantees by perturbing user data at the source and removing the need for a trusted curator. However, the noise introduced by LDP often significantly reduces data utility. To address this issue, we reinterpret private learning under LDP as a transfer learning problem, where the noisy data serve as the source domain and the unobserved clean data as the target. We propose novel techniques specifically designed for LDP to improve classification performance without compromising privacy: (1) a noised binary feedback-based evaluation mechanism for estimating dataset utility; (2) model reversal, which salvages underperforming classifiers by inverting their decision boundaries; and (3) model averaging, which assigns weights to multiple reversed classifiers based on their estimated utility. We provide theoretical excess risk bounds under LDP and demonstrate how our methods reduce this risk. Empirical results on both simulated and real-world datasets show substantial improvements in classification accuracy.

</details>


### [221] [Bifrost: Steering Strategic Trajectories to Bridge Contextual Gaps for Self-Improving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05810)
*Quan M. Tran,Zhuo Huang,Wenbin Zhang,Bo Han,Koji Yatani,Masashi Sugiyama,Tongliang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bifrost introduces a training-free trajectory adaptation method that leverages context-trajectory correlations at the representation level to align past trajectories with new tasks, improving self-improvement performance under context shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Context shifts cause misalignment when reusing successful task trajectories for in-context reasoning; existing approaches discard trajectories or rely on heuristics, incurring costs and uncertain performance.

Method: BrIdge contextual gap FoR imprOv... Bifrost adapts previously solved trajectories to a target task by exploiting context differences, operating in a training-free manner at the representation level using agent hidden states to transform trajectories into a shared context space.

Result: Across diverse benchmarks, Bifrost consistently outperforms existing trajectory reuse and finetuned self-improvement methods, demonstrating robust leverage of past experiences despite substantial context shifts.

Conclusion: Context-trajectory correlation enables effective, training-free trajectory reuse across tasks when adaptation occurs in the representation space; Bifrost achieves improved performance without additional fine-tuning.

Abstract: Autonomous agents excel in self-improvement through reflection and iterative refinement, which reuse successful task trajectories as in-context examples to assist subsequent reasoning. However, shifting across tasks often introduces a context mismatch. Hence, existing approaches either discard the trajectories or manipulate them using heuristics, leading to a non-negligible fine-tuning cost or unguaranteed performance. To bridge this gap, we reveal a context-trajectory correlation, where shifts of context are highly parallel with shifts of trajectory. Based on this finding, we propose BrIdge contextual gap FoR imprOvised trajectory STeering (Bifrost), a training-free method that leverages context differences to precisely guide the adaptation of previously solved trajectories towards the target task, mitigating the misalignment caused by context shifts. Our trajectory adaptation is conducted at the representation level using agent hidden states, ensuring trajectory transformation accurately aligns with the target context in a shared space. Across diverse benchmarks, Bifrost consistently outperforms existing trajectory reuse and finetuned self-improvement methods, demonstrating that agents can effectively leverage past experiences despite substantial context shifts.

</details>


### [222] [Principled Confidence Estimation for Deep Computed Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05812)
*Matteo Gätzner,Johannes Kirschner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled framework for confidence estimation in CT reconstruction using sequential likelihood mixing, yielding coverage-guaranteed confidence regions for classical and deep-learning reconstructions; deep methods provide tighter regions and enable hallucination detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide reliable uncertainty quantification for CT reconstructions, addressing hallucinations and trust in deep learning methods under realistic Poisson-noise forward models (Beer–Lambert law).

Method: Apply the sequential likelihood mixing framework to a log-linear forward model with Poisson noise. Derive confidence regions with coverage guarantees that apply to both classical algorithms and deep-learning reconstructions (e.g., U-Nets, ensembles, diffusion models). Provide interpretable visualizations of confidence regions and a mechanism to detect hallucinations.

Result: Deep reconstruction methods produce substantially tighter confidence regions than classical methods while preserving coverage guarantees; enables detection of hallucinations and supports interpretable uncertainty visualizations.

Conclusion: Deep models can serve as reliable, uncertainty-aware tools for medical imaging, complementing their power as estimators with principled confidence guarantees.

Abstract: We present a principled framework for confidence estimation in computed tomography (CT) reconstruction. Based on the sequential likelihood mixing framework (Kirschner et al., 2025), we establish confidence regions with theoretical coverage guarantees for deep-learning-based CT reconstructions. We consider a realistic forward model following the Beer-Lambert law, i.e., a log-linear forward model with Poisson noise, closely reflecting clinical and scientific imaging conditions. The framework is general and applies to both classical algorithms and deep learning reconstruction methods, including U-Nets, U-Net ensembles, and generative Diffusion models. Empirically, we demonstrate that deep reconstruction methods yield substantially tighter confidence regions than classical reconstructions, without sacrificing theoretical coverage guarantees. Our approach allows the detection of hallucinations in reconstructed images and provides interpretable visualizations of confidence regions. This establishes deep models not only as powerful estimators, but also as reliable tools for uncertainty-aware medical imaging.

</details>


### [223] [Where Does Warm-Up Come From? Adaptive Scheduling for Norm-Constrained Optimizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05813)
*Artem Riabinin,Andrey Veprikov,Arman Bolatov,Martin Takáč,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theory-backed adaptive learning-rate schedule for norm-constrained optimizers (Muon/Lion) yields warm-up-then-decay inherently; a practical auto warm-up scheduler is developed and validated on LLaMA pretraining with competitive results and open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide principled, scalable learning-rate scheduling for norm-constrained optimizers and large-scale language model pretraining, replacing heuristic warm-up rules with theory-driven guarantees.

Method: Introduce a generalized smoothness assumption in which local curvature decreases with the suboptimality gap; prove convergence under an appropriate learning-rate choice, revealing warm-up followed by decay as a natural outcome of the proof. Build a practical LR scheduler using standard hyperparameters that automatically adapts the warm-up duration at training start.

Result: The paper yields convergence guarantees under the proposed LR scheme; empirical evidence shows curvature behavior along optimization trajectories. In large-scale LLM pretraining (LLaMA), the adaptive warm-up method consistently matches or outperforms the best manually tuned warm-ups across setups, without extra hyperparameter search; source code provided at the referenced URL.

Conclusion: A theory-grounded warm-up mechanism enables robust, hyperparameter-light LR scheduling for norm-constrained optimizers, producing effective warm-up dynamics and reducing tuning effort in large-scale language model training; the approach is released with open-source implementation.

Abstract: We study adaptive learning rate scheduling for norm-constrained optimizers (e.g., Muon and Lion). We introduce a generalized smoothness assumption under which local curvature decreases with the suboptimality gap and empirically verify that this behavior holds along optimization trajectories. Under this assumption, we establish convergence guarantees under an appropriate choice of learning rate, for which warm-up followed by decay arises naturally from the proof rather than being imposed heuristically.
  Building on this theory, we develop a practical learning rate scheduler that relies only on standard hyperparameters and adapts the warm-up duration automatically at the beginning of training. We evaluate this method on large language model pretraining with LLaMA architectures and show that our adaptive warm-up selection consistently outperforms or at least matches the best manually tuned warm-up schedules across all considered setups, without additional hyperparameter search. Our source code is available at https://github.com/brain-lab-research/llm-baselines/tree/warmup

</details>


### [224] [Synthesizing Realistic Test Data without Breaking Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05833)
*Laura Plein,Alexi Turcotte,Arina Hallemans,Andreas Zeller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a GAN-inspired privacy-preserving synthetic test data generator using a fuzzer-based test generator and a discriminator to match original distributions indirectly, evaluated on four datasets, showing promising utility under privacy constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for synthetic datasets that preserve statistical properties while maintaining confidentiality; existing GAN-based methods either lack fidelity or leak information via MIA or reconstruction; propose indirect generation approach to reduce risk.

Method: A generation step where a test generator (fuzzer) produces data according to an input specification with constraints from original data; a discriminator assesses proximity to original data; iterative evolution selects 'good samples' to build a synthetic dataset that aligns with original distributions while preserving privacy.

Result: Experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate potential for high utility and privacy preservation; results indicate the synthetic data approximate statistical properties of original data.

Conclusion: This approach is a viable direction for privacy-preserving synthetic data with comparable utility; further work needed to quantify privacy guarantees and compare with baselines.

Abstract: There is a need for synthetic training and test datasets that replicate statistical distributions of original datasets without compromising their confidentiality. A lot of research has been done in leveraging Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) for synthetic data generation. However, the resulting models are either not accurate enough or are still vulnerable to membership inference attacks (MIA) or dataset reconstruction attacks since the original data has been leveraged in the training process. In this paper, we explore the feasibility of producing a synthetic test dataset with the same statistical properties as the original one, with only indirectly leveraging the original data in the generation process. The approach is inspired by GANs, with a generation step and a discrimination step. However, in our approach, we use a test generator (a fuzzer) to produce test data from an input specification, preserving constraints set by the original data; a discriminator model determines how close we are to the original data. By evolving samples and determining "good samples" with the discriminator, we can generate privacy-preserving data that follows the same statistical distributions are the original dataset, leading to a similar utility as the original data. We evaluated our approach on four datasets that have been used to evaluate the state-of-the-art techniques. Our experiments highlight the potential of our approach towards generating synthetic datasets that have high utility while preserving privacy.

</details>


### [225] [Visualizing the loss landscapes of physics-informed neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05849)
*Conor Rowan,Finn Murphy-Blanchard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Survey of loss-landscape literature and empirical analysis of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), showing PINN losses (Deep Ritz and squared residual) share the same landscape properties as data-driven models and are smooth, well-conditioned, and near-convex around solutions; the two PINN losses yield similar landscapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why neural-network optimization reliably finds good minima and extend loss-landscape analysis to physics-informed machine learning, comparing different PINN loss formulations.

Method: Comprehensive literature review of loss landscapes; applying landscape visualization techniques to PINN losses, specifically Deep Ritz and squared residual formulations, and empirically comparing their landscapes.

Result: PINN loss landscapes exhibit properties similar to those seen in data-driven classification tasks. The Deep Ritz and squared residual losses often produce similar landscapes, which tend to be smooth, well-conditioned, and convex near the solution.

Conclusion: Introduce the loss-landscape perspective to the scientific ML community, compare Deep Ritz versus strong-form losses, and challenge the notion that physics-informed PINN losses are inherently more complex in their landscape.

Abstract: Training a neural network requires navigating a high-dimensional, non-convex loss surface to find parameters that minimize this loss. In many ways, it is surprising that optimizers such as stochastic gradient descent and ADAM can reliably locate minima which perform well on both the training and test data. To understand the success of training, a "loss landscape" community has emerged to study the geometry of the loss function and the dynamics of optimization, often using visualization techniques. However, these loss landscape studies have mostly been limited to machine learning for image classification. In the newer field of physics-informed machine learning, little work has been conducted to visualize the landscapes of losses defined not by regression to large data sets, but by differential operators acting on state fields discretized by neural networks. In this work, we provide a comprehensive review of the loss landscape literature, as well as a discussion of the few existing physics-informed works which investigate the loss landscape. We then use a number of the techniques we survey to empirically investigate the landscapes defined by the Deep Ritz and squared residual forms of the physics loss function. We find that the loss landscapes of physics-informed neural networks have many of the same properties as the data-driven classification problems studied in the literature. Unexpectedly, we find that the two formulations of the physics loss often give rise to similar landscapes, which appear smooth, well-conditioned, and convex in the vicinity of the solution. The purpose of this work is to introduce the loss landscape perspective to the scientific machine learning community, compare the Deep Ritz and the strong form losses, and to challenge prevailing intuitions about the complexity of the loss landscapes of physics-informed networks.

</details>


### [226] [Exact Recovery in the Data Block Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05852)
*Amir R. Asadi,Akbar Davoodi,Ramin Javadi,Farzad Parvaresh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DBM extends SBM by incorporating node-associated data; introduces Chernoff–TV divergence to obtain a sharp exact-recovery threshold; provides an efficient algorithm that achieves the threshold and a matching converse proving impossibility below it; simulations validate the benefits of vertex data as side information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by real-world networks where nodes carry attributes or labels, aiming to quantify how side information affects exact recovery thresholds beyond classic connectivity-based results in SBM.

Method: Define a Chernoff–TV divergence for the Data Block Model, derive a sharp exact-recovery threshold, design an efficient algorithm that attains the threshold, and establish a matching converse to show impossibility below it.

Result: Characterization of a sharp exact-recovery threshold for the DBM via Chernoff–TV divergence; an efficient algorithm achieving the threshold; a matching impossibility result below the threshold; simulations demonstrating the benefit of vertex-data as side information.

Conclusion: Vertex data enhances exact recovery in community detection; the Chernoff–TV divergence captures the fundamental threshold; results provide both theoretical limits and practical algorithms for data-augmented SBMs.

Abstract: Community detection in networks is a fundamental problem in machine learning and statistical inference, with applications in social networks, biological systems, and communication networks. The stochastic block model (SBM) serves as a canonical framework for studying community structure, and exact recovery, identifying the true communities with high probability, is a central theoretical question. While classical results characterize the phase transition for exact recovery based solely on graph connectivity, many real-world networks contain additional data, such as node attributes or labels. In this work, we study exact recovery in the Data Block Model (DBM), an SBM augmented with node-associated data, as formalized by Asadi, Abbe, and Verdú (2017). We introduce the Chernoff--TV divergence and use it to characterize a sharp exact recovery threshold for the DBM. We further provide an efficient algorithm that achieves this threshold, along with a matching converse result showing impossibility below the threshold. Finally, simulations validate our findings and demonstrate the benefits of incorporating vertex data as side information in community detection.

</details>


### [227] [DLM-Scope: Mechanistic Interpretability of Diffusion Language Models via Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05859)
*Xu Wang,Bingqing Jiang,Yu Wan,Baosong Yang,Lingpeng Kong,Difan Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DLM-Scope is the first SAE-based interpretability framework for diffusion language models, showing that Top-K sparse autoencoders can faithfully extract interpretable features. Unlike autoregressive LLMs, SAE insertion in DLMs can reduce cross-entropy loss in early layers, enabling stronger diffusion-time interventions. SAE features also reveal decoding-order signals and remain stable post-training, establishing a foundational approach for DLM mechanistic interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the interpretability gap for diffusion language models (DLMs) by adapting sparse autoencoder (SAE) techniques from autoregressive LLMs, enabling interpretable feature extraction, intervention capabilities, and insights into decoding order.

Method: Train Top-K sparse autoencoders on DLM layers (especially early layers); compare SAE insertion effects on cross-entropy with autoregressive LLMs; evaluate interpretability of SAE features; perform diffusion-time interventions and compare to LLM steering; analyze decoding-order signals and post-training stability of SAE features.

Result: SAEs faithfully extract interpretable features in DLMs; SAE insertion reduces loss in early layers (unlike in LLMs); SAE-driven interventions during diffusion steps outperform LLM steering; SAE features provide signals about decoding order and remain stable during post-training; establishes a new baseline and directions for DLM mechanistic interpretability.

Conclusion: SAEs offer a viable and valuable tool for mechanistic interpretability in diffusion language models, with practical benefits for feature extraction, intervention strategies, and decoding dynamics, and pave the way for broader DLM interpretability research.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a standard tool for mechanistic interpretability in autoregressive large language models (LLMs), enabling researchers to extract sparse, human-interpretable features and intervene on model behavior. Recently, as diffusion language models (DLMs) have become an increasingly promising alternative to the autoregressive LLMs, it is essential to develop tailored mechanistic interpretability tools for this emerging class of models. In this work, we present DLM-Scope, the first SAE-based interpretability framework for DLMs, and demonstrate that trained Top-K SAEs can faithfully extract interpretable features. Notably, we find that inserting SAEs affects DLMs differently than autoregressive LLMs: while SAE insertion in LLMs typically incurs a loss penalty, in DLMs it can reduce cross-entropy loss when applied to early layers, a phenomenon absent or markedly weaker in LLMs. Additionally, SAE features in DLMs enable more effective diffusion-time interventions, often outperforming LLM steering. Moreover, we pioneer certain new SAE-based research directions for DLMs: we show that SAEs can provide useful signals for DLM decoding order; and the SAE features are stable during the post-training phase of DLMs. Our work establishes a foundation for mechanistic interpretability in DLMs and shows a great potential of applying SAEs to DLM-related tasks and algorithms.

</details>


### [228] [CFRecs: Counterfactual Recommendations on Real Estate User Listing Interaction Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05861)
*Seyedmasoud Mousavi,Ruomeng Xu,Xiaojing Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CFRecs is a two-stage GNN + Graph-VAE framework that yields minimal, high-impact counterfactual changes in graph structure and node attributes to produce desirable recommendations in graph-based recommender systems; validated on Zillow data, showing meaningful interpretability and actionable insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address interpretability and actionability in graph-based recommendations by turning counterfactual explanations into actionable changes; balance sparsity of edits with validity of predictions.

Method: A two-stage architecture: a GNN captures predictive signals; a Graph-VAE proposes counterfactual changes (edges/attributes) aiming for sparsity and validity; outputs actionable recommendations in real estate context.

Result: Empirical results on Zillow user-listing interaction data demonstrate effectiveness and offer a novel perspective on counterfactual reasoning in graphs; improvements in interpretability and actionable guidance are observed.

Conclusion: CFRecs demonstrates how counterfactual explanations can be operationalized into actionable recommendations in graph-based systems, with potential applicability to other domains requiring interpretable, minimal interventions.

Abstract: Graph-structured data is ubiquitous and powerful in representing complex relationships in many online platforms. While graph neural networks (GNNs) are widely used to learn from such data, counterfactual graph learning has emerged as a promising approach to improve model interpretability. Counterfactual explanation research focuses on identifying a counterfactual graph that is similar to the original but leads to different predictions. These explanations optimize two objectives simultaneously: the sparsity of changes in the counterfactual graph and the validity of its predictions. Building on these qualitative optimization goals, this paper introduces CFRecs, a novel framework that transforms counterfactual explanations into actionable insights. CFRecs employs a two-stage architecture consisting of a graph neural network (GNN) and a graph variational auto-encoder (Graph-VAE) to strategically propose minimal yet high-impact changes in graph structure and node attributes to drive desirable outcomes in recommender systems. We apply CFRecs to Zillow's graph-structured data to deliver actionable recommendations for both home buyers and sellers with the goal of helping them navigate the competitive housing market and achieve their homeownership goals. Experimental results on Zillow's user-listing interaction data demonstrate the effectiveness of CFRecs, which also provides a fresh perspective on recommendations using counterfactual reasoning in graphs.

</details>


### [229] [Constrained Group Relative Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05863)
*Roger Girgis,Rodrigue de Schaetzen,Luke Rowe,Azalée Robitaille,Christopher Pal,Liam Paull*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Constrained GRPO extends Group Relative Policy Optimization with a Lagrangian-based framework for constrained policy optimization using indicator costs; identifies a pathology from naive multi-component advantage estimation and resolves it with a scalarized advantage, validated in a toy gridworld and robotics tasks showing improved constraint satisfaction and task success, suggesting a practical approach for embodied AI. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable scalable, critic-free constrained policy optimization within the GRPO framework and address the failure modes caused by naive multi-term advantage estimation that distorts Lagrangian signals, particularly relevant for embodied AI with safety/constraint requirements.

Method: Introduce a Lagrangian-based extension of GRPO (Constrained GRPO) with indicator cost constraints. Demonstrate that naive multi-component advantage estimation distorts relative importance across objectives; derive a scalarized advantage construction that preserves trade-offs; provide theoretical motivation and empirical validation in a toy gridworld and robotics tasks.

Result: Scalarized advantages restore proper Lagrangian weighting, enabling stable constraint enforcement. Constrained GRPO improves constraint satisfaction and, in robotics tasks, also increases task success.

Conclusion: Constrained GRPO offers a practical, scalable approach to constrained policy optimization within GRPO, with scalarized advantages being essential to maintain the intended reward-constraint trade-off; validated on toy and embodied AI tasks, suggesting broad applicability and a straightforward recipe for constrained policy learning.

Abstract: While Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has emerged as a scalable framework for critic-free policy learning, extending it to settings with explicit behavioral constraints remains underexplored. We introduce Constrained GRPO, a Lagrangian-based extension of GRPO for constrained policy optimization. Constraints are specified via indicator cost functions, enabling direct optimization of violation rates through a Lagrangian relaxation. We show that a naive multi-component treatment in advantage estimation can break constrained learning: mismatched component-wise standard deviations distort the relative importance of the different objective terms, which in turn corrupts the Lagrangian signal and prevents meaningful constraint enforcement. We formally derive this effect to motivate our scalarized advantage construction that preserves the intended trade-off between reward and constraint terms. Experiments in a toy gridworld confirm the predicted optimization pathology and demonstrate that scalarizing advantages restores stable constraint control. In addition, we evaluate Constrained GRPO on robotics tasks, where it improves constraint satisfaction while increasing task success, establishing a simple and effective recipe for constrained policy optimization in embodied AI domains that increasingly rely on large multimodal foundation models.

</details>


### [230] [Large-scale Score-based Variational Posterior Inference for Bayesian Deep Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05873)
*Minyoung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable score-based variational inference (VI) method for Bayesian deep neural networks (BNNs) is proposed, combining score matching with a proximal penalty, avoiding reparameterized sampling, enabling unbiased mini-batch scores, and scalable to large architectures (e.g., Vision Transformers); empirical validation on vision and time-series tasks demonstrates effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ELBO-based VI, while dominant for BNNs, faces scalability and flexibility limitations for large-scale architectures. A scalable, score-based VI with richer variational families is sought to improve uncertainty quantification and robustness while maintaining computational efficiency.

Method: Introduce a scalable VI objective that combines score matching loss with a proximal penalty term per iteration. This avoids reparameterized sampling and allows noisy, unbiased mini-batch scores via stochastic gradients. The framework supports richer variational density families and is applicable to large-scale networks (e.g., Vision Transformers).

Result: Empirical evaluation on visual recognition and time-series forecasting with large-scale networks shows the proposed method is effective and scalable.

Conclusion: A score-based VI with score matching and proximal regularization offers a viable, scalable alternative to ELBO-based VI for BNNs, enabling richer variational densities and applicability to large architectures like Vision Transformers.

Abstract: Bayesian (deep) neural networks (BNN) are often more attractive than the mainstream point-estimate vanilla deep learning in various aspects including uncertainty quantification, robustness to noise, resistance to overfitting, and more. The variational inference (VI) is one of the most widely adopted approximate inference methods. Whereas the ELBO-based variational free energy method is a dominant choice in the literature, in this paper we introduce a score-based alternative for BNN variational inference. Although there have been quite a few score-based variational inference methods proposed in the community, most are not adequate for large-scale BNNs for various computational and technical reasons. We propose a novel scalable VI method where the learning objective combines the score matching loss and the proximal penalty term in iterations, which helps our method avoid the reparametrized sampling, and allows for noisy unbiased mini-batch scores through stochastic gradients. This in turn makes our method scalable to large-scale neural networks including Vision Transformers, and allows for richer variational density families. On several benchmarks including visual recognition and time-series forecasting with large-scale deep networks, we empirically show the effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [231] [Dr. Kernel: Reinforcement Learning Done Right for Triton Kernel Generations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05885)
*Wei Liu,Jiawei Xu,Yingru Li,Longtao Zheng,Tianjian Li,Qian Liu,Junxian He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Systematic RL study for kernel generation with KernelGYM, TRLOO, and profiling-based techniques; Dr Kernel-14B achieves competitive performance and notable speedups on KernelBench, with open-source resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kernel-quality models enable scalable AI; RL-based kernel generation faces reward hacking and lazy optimization; robust environments and unbiased multi-turn RL methods are needed.

Method: Develop KernelGYM—a distributed GPU environment with reward hacking checks and multi-turn data collection for long-horizon RL; identify biased policy gradient from self-inclusion in GRPO; propose Turn-level Reinforce-Leave-One-Out (TRLOO) for unbiased advantage estimation; apply mismatch correction; introduce Profiling-based Rewards (PR) and Profiling-based Rejection Sampling (PRS); train Dr Kernel-14B.

Result: Dr Kernel-14B competes with Claude-4.5-Sonnet on Kernelbench; 31.6% of generated kernels ≥1.2x speedup over Torch baseline on KernelBench Level-2; best-turn selection raises to 47.8%.

Conclusion: Open-source resources (env, training code, models, dataset) in KernelGYM repo; demonstrates viability of robust RL-driven kernel generation and potential performance gains.

Abstract: High-quality kernel is critical for scalable AI systems, and enabling LLMs to generate such code would advance AI development. However, training LLMs for this task requires sufficient data, a robust environment, and the process is often vulnerable to reward hacking and lazy optimization. In these cases, models may hack training rewards and prioritize trivial correctness over meaningful speedup. In this paper, we systematically study reinforcement learning (RL) for kernel generation. We first design KernelGYM, a robust distributed GPU environment that supports reward hacking check, data collection from multi-turn interactions and long-term RL training. Building on KernelGYM, we investigate effective multi-turn RL methods and identify a biased policy gradient issue caused by self-inclusion in GRPO. To solve this, we propose Turn-level Reinforce-Leave-One-Out (TRLOO) to provide unbiased advantage estimation for multi-turn RL. To alleviate lazy optimization, we incorporate mismatch correction for training stability and introduce Profiling-based Rewards (PR) and Profiling-based Rejection Sampling (PRS) to overcome the issue. The trained model, Dr Kernel-14B, reaches performance competitive with Claude-4.5-Sonnet in Kernelbench. Finally, we study sequential test-time scaling for Dr Kernel-14B. On the KernelBench Level-2 subset, 31.6% of the generated kernels achieve at least a 1.2x speedup over the Torch reference, surpassing Claude-4.5-Sonnet (26.7%) and GPT-5 (28.6%). When selecting the best candidate across all turns, this 1.2x speedup rate further increases to 47.8%. All resources, including environment, training code, models, and dataset, are included in https://www.github.com/hkust-nlp/KernelGYM.

</details>


### [232] [Parity, Sensitivity, and Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05896)
*Alexander Kozachinskiy,Tomasz Steifer,Przemysław Wałȩga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new transformer construction can compute PARITY using softmax attention with length-independent, polynomially bounded positional encoding, no layernorm, and works with or without causal masking; a strong height/breadth lower bound shows PARITY cannot be solved by a single layer with a single head.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain the computational limits and minimal architectural requirements of transformers for a fundamental boolean function (PARITY), addressing limitations of prior 2+ layer constructions that rely on impractical features.

Method: Provide a concrete transformer construction that computes PARITY under standard softmax attention, with length-independent, polynomially bounded positional encoding, no layernorm, and compatibility with or without causal masking; also prove a lower bound demonstrating impossibility for a depth-1, single-head transformer to compute PARITY.

Result: The paper demonstrates a new transformer-based construction that can compute PARITY under the stated constraints, and establishes a non-trivial lower bound showing that PARITY cannot be solved by a one-layer, one-head transformer.

Conclusion: The work advances understanding of the minimal architectural capabilities of transformers for a core combinatorial task and tightens the boundary between shallow and deeper transformer models, while offering a practical construction that avoids some previously required features.

Abstract: The transformer architecture is almost a decade old. Despite that, we still have a limited understanding of what this architecture can or cannot compute. For instance, can a 1-layer transformer solve PARITY -- or more generally -- which kinds of transformers can do it? Known constructions for PARITY have at least 2 layers and employ impractical features: either a length-dependent positional encoding, or hardmax, or layernorm without the regularization parameter, or they are not implementable with causal masking.
  We give a new construction of a transformer for PARITY with softmax, length-independent and polynomially bounded positional encoding, no layernorm, working both with and without causal masking. We also give the first lower bound for transformers solving PARITY -- by showing that it cannot be done with only one layer and one head.

</details>


### [233] [Escaping Local Minima Provably in Non-convex Matrix Sensing: A Deterministic Framework via Simulated Lifting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05887)
*Tianqi Shen,Jinji Yang,Junze He,Kunhan Gao,Ziye Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Simulated Oracle Direction (SOD) to deterministically escape spurious local minima in nonconvex low-rank matrix sensing without explicit tensor lifting; provides guarantees and scalable performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenging nonconvex landscape of low-rank matrix sensing by leveraging insights from over-parameterization without incurring the computational cost of tensor lifting; aim for deterministic guarantees against spurious minima.

Method: Introduce how to simulate over-parameterized escape directions and project them onto the original parameter space to guarantee a strict decrease from local minima, avoiding actual lifting and random perturbations; provide a deterministic framework and analyses.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate reliable escape from local minima and convergence to global optima; low computational overhead compared with explicit tensor lifting; claims first deterministic guarantee against spurious minima without randomness.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that simulated over-parameterization can tame challenging nonconvex optimization landscapes and may extend to problems beyond matrix sensing; highlights potential high-level implications for theory and algorithm design.

Abstract: Low-rank matrix sensing is a fundamental yet challenging nonconvex problem whose optimization landscape typically contains numerous spurious local minima, making it difficult for gradient-based optimizers to converge to the global optimum. Recent work has shown that over-parameterization via tensor lifting can convert such local minima into strict saddle points, an insight that also partially explains why massive scaling can improve generalization and performance in modern machine learning. Motivated by this observation, we propose a Simulated Oracle Direction (SOD) escape mechanism that simulates the landscape and escape direction of the over-parametrized space, without resorting to actually lifting the problem, since that would be computationally intractable. In essence, we designed a mathematical framework to project over-parametrized escape directions onto the original parameter space to guarantee a strict decrease of objective value from existing local minima. To the best of the our knowledge, this represents the first deterministic framework that could escape spurious local minima with guarantee, especially without using random perturbations or heuristic estimates. Numerical experiments demonstrate that our framework reliably escapes local minima and facilitates convergence to global optima, while incurring minimal computational cost when compared to explicit tensor over-parameterization. We believe this framework has non-trivial implications for nonconvex optimization beyond matrix sensing, by showcasing how simulated over-parameterization can be leveraged to tame challenging optimization landscapes.

</details>


### [234] [Regularized Calibration with Successive Rounding for Post-Training Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05902)
*Seohyeon Cha,Huancheng Chen,Dongjun Kim,Haoran Zhang,Kevin Chan,Gustavo de Veciana,Haris Vikalo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Regularized asymmetric calibration for post-training quantization (PTQ) improves LLM quantization by interpolating symmetric and asymmetric calibration, enabling a simple successive rounding procedure and a bounded-search extension that trades quantization quality for compute cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PTQ for LLMs is limited by memory/latency and sensitivity to activation distribution; calibration and rounding strategies critically affect quantization quality. A unified objective combining symmetric/asymmetric calibration can regularize PTQ and improve robustness to activation mismatch.

Method: Introduce an interpolation between symmetric and asymmetric calibration as a regularizer. Derive a successive rounding procedure that incorporates asymmetric calibration. Propose a bounded-search extension to explicitly trade quantization quality against compute cost. Evaluate across multiple LLM families, bit-widths, and benchmarks.

Result: The proposed bounded-search method based on regularized asymmetric calibration yields consistent improvements in perplexity and accuracy over PTQ baselines across varied LLMs and bit-widths, with only modest additional computational cost.

Conclusion: Regularized asymmetric calibration with bounded search enhances PTQ for LLMs, providing a tunable mechanism to balance quantization quality and compute cost while improving robustness to activation mismatch.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) deliver robust performance across diverse applications, yet their deployment often faces challenges due to the memory and latency costs of storing and accessing billions of parameters. Post-training quantization (PTQ) enables efficient inference by mapping pretrained weights to low-bit formats without retraining, but its effectiveness depends critically on both the quantization objective and the rounding procedure used to obtain low-bit weight representations. In this work, we show that interpolating between symmetric and asymmetric calibration acts as a form of regularization that preserves the standard quadratic structure used in PTQ while providing robustness to activation mismatch. Building on this perspective, we derive a simple successive rounding procedure that naturally incorporates asymmetric calibration, as well as a bounded-search extension that allows for an explicit trade-off between quantization quality and the compute cost. Experiments across multiple LLM families, quantization bit-widths, and benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed bounded search based on a regularized asymmetric calibration objective consistently improves perplexity and accuracy over PTQ baselines, while incurring only modest and controllable additional computational cost.

</details>


### [235] [DFPO: Scaling Value Modeling via Distributional Flow towards Robust and Generalizable LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05890)
*Dingwei Zhu,Zhiheng Xi,Shihan Dou,Jiahan Li,Chenhao Huang,Junjie Ye,Sixian Li,Mingxu Chai,Yuhui Wang,Yajie Yang,Ming Zhang,Jiazheng Zhang,Shichun Liu,Caishuang Huang,Yunke Zhang,Yuran Wang,Tao Gui,Xipeng Qiu,Qi Zhang,Xuanjing Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DFPO introduces a continuous value-flow representation for distributional RL with conditional risk and consistency constraints, improving robustness and OOD generalization in noisy real-world settings across dialogue, reasoning, and scientific tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges in real-world RL: noisy supervision and poor out-of-domain generalization, exacerbated in LLM post-training. Existing distributional RL models model multiple quantiles independently, yielding coarse value representations and limited conditioning on state information under complex/OOD conditions.

Method: DFPO models values as continuous flows across time steps via a value flow field, enabling richer state-conditioned value representations. It also integrates conditional risk control and consistency constraints along value flow trajectories to stabilize training under noisy feedback.

Result: Empirical results show DFPO outperforms PPO, FlowRL, and other robust baselines under noisy supervision, with improved training stability and generalization across dialogue, math reasoning, and scientific tasks.

Conclusion: A robust distributional RL framework that leverages value flow representations and risk/consistency constraints to achieve better stability and generalization in noisy, complex tasks.

Abstract: Training reinforcement learning (RL) systems in real-world environments remains challenging due to noisy supervision and poor out-of-domain (OOD) generalization, especially in LLM post-training. Recent distributional RL methods improve robustness by modeling values with multiple quantile points, but they still learn each quantile independently as a scalar. This results in rough-grained value representations that lack fine-grained conditioning on state information, struggling under complex and OOD conditions. We propose DFPO (Distributional Value Flow Policy Optimization with Conditional Risk and Consistency Control), a robust distributional RL framework that models values as continuous flows across time steps. By scaling value modeling through learning of a value flow field instead of isolated quantile predictions, DFPO captures richer state information for more accurate advantage estimation. To stabilize training under noisy feedback, DFPO further integrates conditional risk control and consistency constraints along value flow trajectories. Experiments on dialogue, math reasoning, and scientific tasks show that DFPO outperforms PPO, FlowRL, and other robust baselines under noisy supervision, achieving improved training stability and generalization.

</details>


### [236] [Verification of the Implicit World Model in a Generative Model via Adversarial Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05903)
*András Balogh,Márk Jelasity*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adversarial sequence generation is proposed to verify and analyze the soundness of chess sequence models trained on data; none of the models are sound, but training data quality and methods can improve soundness; board-state probes are not causally related to next-move predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether sample-based training of generative sequence models can capture the true rule-based world model of chess, and to develop practical verification tools for soundness.

Method: Develop adversarial sequence generation techniques that produce valid chess sequences to force models to make invalid next-move predictions. Train models on both random and high-quality game datasets with various training recipes, and evaluate soundness. Additionally, explore board-state probes for training and attack analysis.

Result: Across a large set of chess models, none achieved full soundness. Some training choices and dataset selections improved soundness. Board-state probes did not show causal influence on next-move prediction in most models.

Conclusion: Adversarial verification is a practical tool to diagnose and improve soundness in sequence models for rule-based domains like chess. Data quality and training strategies matter for improving soundness, while board-state probes may be largely irrelevant to predicting the next move.

Abstract: Generative sequence models are typically trained on sample sequences from natural or formal languages. It is a crucial question whether -- or to what extent -- sample-based training is able to capture the true structure of these languages, often referred to as the ``world model''. Theoretical results indicate that we can hope for soundness at best, that is, generating valid sequences, but not necessarily all of them. However, it is still important to have practical tools that are able to verify whether a given sequence model is sound. In this study, we focus on chess, as it is a domain that provides enough complexity while having a simple rule-based world model. We propose adversarial sequence generation for verifying the soundness of the sequence model. Our adversaries generate valid sequences so as to force the sequence model to generate an invalid next move prediction. Apart from the falsification of soundness, this method is also suitable for a more fine-grained analysis of the failure modes and the effects of different choices during training. To demonstrate this, we propose a number of methods for adversarial sequence generation and evaluate the approach on a large set of chess models. We train models on random as well as high-quality chess games, using several training recipes. We find that none of the models are sound, but some training techniques and dataset choices are able to improve soundness remarkably. We also investigate the potential application of board state probes in both our training and attack methods. Our findings indicate that the extracted board states have no causal role in next token prediction in most of the models.

</details>


### [237] [ContextBench: A Benchmark for Context Retrieval in Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05892)
*Han Li,Letian Zhu,Bohan Zhang,Rili Feng,Jiaming Wang,Yue Pan,Earl T. Barr,Sarro Federica,Zhaoyang Chu,He Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ContextBench provides a process-oriented evaluation for context retrieval in coding agents, introducing 1,136 issue-resolution tasks with gold contexts, an automated tracker of recall/precision/efficiency, and a cross-LLM/agent study showing limited gains from scaffolding, a bias toward recall, and large gaps between explored and used context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation of end-to-end success metrics by revealing how coding agents retrieve and use code context during problem solving, enabling intermediate signals to diagnose reasoning and guide improvements.

Method: Construct ContextBench with 1,136 tasks from 66 repositories across 8 languages, each with human-annotated gold contexts; develop an automated framework that tracks agent trajectories and computes context recall, precision, and efficiency; evaluate four frontier LLMs and five coding agents.

Result: The evaluation reveals that sophisticated agent scaffolding yields only marginal gains in context retrieval; LLMs prioritize recall over precision; there are substantial gaps between explored and utilized context; ContextBench provides intermediate gold-context metrics alongside end-to-end benchmarks.
