*Suvadip Sana,Jinzhou Wu,Martin T. Wells*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DemPO uses algorithmic sortition to sample representative preferences for preference-based model fine-tuning, via two schemes: Hard Panel (quota mini-public) and Soft Panel (reweighted data). Soft Panel recovers the Hard Panel objective in closed form. Hard Panel dominates across aggregations; Soft Panel beats unweighted baselines. Represents how demographic representativeness at data collection improves alignment with representative public values.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rater pools for preference-based alignment (e.g., RLHF) are biased by convenience samples that over- and under-represent demographics. This work investigates whether enforcing demographic representativeness at data collection yields models that better reflect public values.

Method: Apply algorithmic sortition to assemble a mini-public panel. Hard Panel uses preferences solely from a quota-satisfying panel. Soft Panel uses all collected preferences but weights each rater by their inclusion probability under the sortition lottery. Show Soft Panel objective equals the Hard Panel objective in closed form. Evaluate on a public preference dataset (human judgments linked to rater demographics) plus a 75-clause constitution elicited from a representative US panel. Fine-tune Llama models from 1B to 8B parameters under each scheme. Compare six aggregation methods.

Result: Hard Panel consistently ranks first across six aggregation methods. Soft Panel consistently outperforms the unweighted baseline; effect sizes increase with model capacity. Soft Panel weighting recovers the Hard Panel objective in closed form.

Conclusion: Enforcing demographic representativeness during preference collection yields models whose behavior aligns more closely with the values elicited from representative publics than post hoc corrections.

Abstract: Whose values should AI systems learn? Preference based alignment methods like RLHF derive their training signal from human raters, yet these rater pools are typically convenience samples that systematically over represent some demographics and under represent others. We introduce Democratic Preference Optimization, or DemPO, a framework that applies algorithmic sortition, the same mechanism used to construct citizen assemblies, to preference based fine tuning. DemPO offers two training schemes. Hard Panel trains exclusively on preferences from a quota satisfying mini public sampled via sortition. Soft Panel retains all data but reweights each rater by their inclusion probability under the sortition lottery. We prove that Soft Panel weighting recovers the expected Hard Panel objective in closed form. Using a public preference dataset that pairs human judgments with rater demographics and a seventy five clause constitution independently elicited from a representative United States panel, we evaluate Llama models from one billion to eight billion parameters fine tuned under each scheme. Across six aggregation methods, the Hard Panel consistently ranks first and the Soft Panel consistently outperforms the unweighted baseline, with effect sizes growing as model capacity increases. These results demonstrate that enforcing demographic representativeness at the preference collection stage, rather than post hoc correction, yields models whose behavior better reflects values elicited from representative publics.

</details>


### [266] [SocialVeil: Probing Social Intelligence of Language Agents under Communication Barriers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05115)
*Keyang Xuan,Pengda Wang,Chongrui Ye,Haofei Yu,Tal August,Jiaxuan You*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SocialVeil introduces a barrier-aware social-learning environment to study LLMs under cognitive-difference communication disruptions, with two evaluation metrics and four frontier LLMs across 720 scenarios; results show significant degradation in interaction quality and only modest gains from adaptation strategies, validated by human judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM benchmarks for social interaction assume ideal, perfect communication. There is a need to diagnose how LLMs maintain and repair interactions under realistic, imperfect communication barriers, to better reflect real-world social intelligence.

Method: Develop SocialVeil to simulate three disruption types—semantic vagueness, sociocultural mismatch, and emotional interference—grounded in a literature review. Propose two barrier-aware metrics (unresolved confusion, mutual understanding). Evaluate 4 frontier LLMs across 720 scenarios. Include human evaluation (ICC ~0.78, Pearson r ~0.80). Test adaptation strategies (Repair Instruction, Interactive learning).

Result: Barriers consistently degrade interaction: mutual understanding drops by over 45%, unresolved confusion rises by ~50%. Four LLMs tested across 720 scenarios show robust effects of disruption. Human assessments align with simulated barriers (ICC ≈ 0.78, Pearson r ≈ 0.80). Adaptation strategies yield only modest improvements, far from barrier-free performance.

Conclusion: SocialVeil moves benchmarks toward real-world, barrier-rich social interactions for LLMs, enabling analysis of social intelligence under cognitive-difference-induced disruptions; results highlight substantial impact of communication barriers and the limited efficacy of current repair/adaptation approaches, motivating further research into robust interaction strategies.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly evaluated in interactive environments to test their social intelligence. However, existing benchmarks often assume idealized communication between agents, limiting our ability to diagnose whether LLMs can maintain and repair interactions in more realistic, imperfect settings. To close this gap, we present \textsc{SocialVeil}, a social learning environment that can simulate social interaction under cognitive-difference-induced communication barriers. Grounded in a systematic literature review of communication challenges in human interaction, \textsc{SocialVeil} introduces three representative types of such disruption, \emph{semantic vagueness}, \emph{sociocultural mismatch}, and \emph{emotional interference}. We also introduce two barrier-aware evaluation metrics, \emph{unresolved confusion} and \emph{mutual understanding}, to evaluate interaction quality under impaired communication. Experiments across 720 scenarios and four frontier LLMs show that barriers consistently impair performance, with mutual understanding reduced by over 45\% on average, and confusion elevated by nearly 50\%. Human evaluations validate the fidelity of these simulated barriers (ICC$\approx$0.78, Pearson r$\approx$0.80). We further demonstrate that adaptation strategies (Repair Instruction and Interactive learning) only have a modest effect far from barrier-free performance. This work takes a step toward bringing social interaction environments closer to real-world communication, opening opportunities for exploring the social intelligence of LLM agents.

</details>


### [267] [CAST-CKT: Chaos-Aware Spatio-Temporal and Cross-City Knowledge Transfer for Traffic Flow Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05133)
*Abdul Joseph Fofanah,Lian Wen,David Chen,Alpha Alimamy Kamara,Zhongyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Chaos-aware cross-city few-shot traffic forecasting with CAST-CKT: integrates chaos analysis, regime-adaptive temporal modelling, adaptive topology, and chaotic-consistency transfer; provides horizon-wise predictions with uncertainty and theoretical generalization bounds; strong empirical gains on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-scarce, cross-city settings exhibit nonlinear chaotic dynamics and domain shifts that hinder few-shot transfer; existing methods struggle to model traffic predictability regimes.

Method: Introduce chaos analyser to quantify predictability regimes; chaos-aware attention for regime-adaptive temporal modelling; adaptive topology learning for dynamic spatial dependencies; chaotic consistency-based cross-city alignment for knowledge transfer; horizon-specific predictions with uncertainty; theoretical generalization bounds.

Result: Outperforms SOTA on four cross-city few-shot benchmarks in MAE and RMSE; provides interpretable regime analysis and uncertainty; code released.

Conclusion: CAST-CKT advances few-shot traffic forecasting by combining chaos-informed regime awareness with adaptive spatio-temporal modeling and cross-city transfer, delivering interpretable insights and reliable uncertainty; empirical and theoretical support.

Abstract: Traffic prediction in data-scarce, cross-city settings is challenging due to complex nonlinear dynamics and domain shifts. Existing methods often fail to capture traffic's inherent chaotic nature for effective few-shot learning. We propose CAST-CKT, a novel Chaos-Aware Spatio-Temporal and Cross-City Knowledge Transfer framework. It employs an efficient chaotic analyser to quantify traffic predictability regimes, driving several key innovations: chaos-aware attention for regime-adaptive temporal modelling; adaptive topology learning for dynamic spatial dependencies; and chaotic consistency-based cross-city alignment for knowledge transfer. The framework also provides horizon-specific predictions with uncertainty quantification. Theoretical analysis shows improved generalisation bounds. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks in cross-city few-shot settings show CAST-CKT outperforms state-of-the-art methods by significant margins in MAE and RMSE, while offering interpretable regime analysis. Code is available at https://github.com/afofanah/CAST-CKT.

</details>


### [268] [HugRAG: Hierarchical Causal Knowledge Graph Design for RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05143)
*Nengbo Wang,Tuo Liang,Vikash Singh,Chaoda Song,Van Yang,Yu Yin,Jing Ma,Jagdip Singh,Vipin Chaudhary*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HugRAG introduces causal gating in hierarchical, graph-based retrieval-augmented generation to reduce spurious correlations and enable scalable, cross-module reasoning, achieving improved performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based RAG methods rely on surface-level node matching and lack explicit causal modeling, leading to unfaithful or spurious answers. Modular graph structures induce information isolation, hindering scalability and cross-module causal reasoning.

Method: HugRAG rethinks knowledge organization by applying causal gating across hierarchical modules, explicitly modeling causal relationships to suppress spurious correlations while enabling scalable reasoning over large knowledge graphs.

Result: HugRAG consistently outperforms competitive graph-based RAG baselines across multiple datasets and evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: The work establishes a principled foundation for structured, scalable, and causally grounded RAG systems.

Abstract: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) has enhanced large language models by enabling access to external knowledge, with graph-based RAG emerging as a powerful paradigm for structured retrieval and reasoning. However, existing graph-based methods often over-rely on surface-level node matching and lack explicit causal modeling, leading to unfaithful or spurious answers. Prior attempts to incorporate causality are typically limited to local or single-document contexts and also suffer from information isolation that arises from modular graph structures, which hinders scalability and cross-module causal reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose HugRAG, a framework that rethinks knowledge organization for graph-based RAG through causal gating across hierarchical modules. HugRAG explicitly models causal relationships to suppress spurious correlations while enabling scalable reasoning over large-scale knowledge graphs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HugRAG consistently outperforms competitive graph-based RAG baselines across multiple datasets and evaluation metrics. Our work establishes a principled foundation for structured, scalable, and causally grounded RAG systems.

</details>


### [269] [First Proof](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05192)
*Mohammed Abouzaid,Andrew J. Blumberg,Martin Hairer,Joe Kileel,Tamara G. Kolda,Paul D. Nelson,Daniel Spielman,Nikhil Srivastava,Rachel Ward,Shmuel Weinberger,Lauren Williams*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dataset of 10 research-level math questions used to probe AI performance; questions are private to authors for a short decryption window after publication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess current AI systems' ability to answer authentic, research-origin math questions, revealing gaps and guiding benchmark development.

Method: Publish a set of ten math questions arising in authors' research; not publicly shared initially; answers are encrypted for a short period to prevent premature disclosure.

Result: No results are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: The work proposes a benchmark-style dataset to stress-test AI capabilities on high-level mathematics, but details on evaluation protocol, baselines, and reproducibility are needed.

Abstract: To assess the ability of current AI systems to correctly answer research-level mathematics questions, we share a set of ten math questions which have arisen naturally in the research process of the authors. The questions had not been shared publicly until now; the answers are known to the authors of the questions but will remain encrypted for a short time.

</details>


### [270] [Traceable Cross-Source RAG for Chinese Tibetan Medicine Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05195)
*Fengxian Chen,Zhilong Tao,Jiaxuan Li,Yunlong Li,Qingguo Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes two complementary methods to improve retrieval-augmented generation with multiple heterogeneous knowledge bases in Chinese Tibetan medicine: DAKS for KB routing and budgeted retrieval, and an alignment graph for evidence fusion and coverage-aware packing, evaluated on a 500-query benchmark (K=5) with an openPangu-Embedded-7B generator, achieving strong CrossEv@5 and maintained faithfulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenges of RAG in domain settings with multiple heterogeneous KBs, where dense encyclopedia entries can bias retrieval, causing hallucinations and hindering cross-KB verification; aim to improve traceability, evidence coverage, and citation correctness in Chinese Tibetan medicine literature.

Method: 1) DAKS: KB routing and budgeted retrieval to reduce density-driven bias and prioritize authoritative sources. 2) Alignment graph: guides evidence fusion and implements coverage-aware packing to improve cross-KB evidence coverage without naive concatenation. All answers generated by a lightweight generator (openPangu-Embedded-7B).

Result: Experiments show consistent gains in routing quality and cross-KB evidence coverage; the full system achieves the best CrossEv@5 while preserving faithfulness and citation correctness.

Conclusion: The proposed two-method framework effectively mitigates density bias and improves cross-KB evidence coverage for RAG with multiple KBs in a specialized domain, demonstrating feasibility of cross-KB verification and faithful answer generation with a lightweight generator.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) promises grounded question answering, yet domain settings with multiple heterogeneous knowledge bases (KBs) remain challenging. In Chinese Tibetan medicine, encyclopedia entries are often dense and easy to match, which can dominate retrieval even when classics or clinical papers provide more authoritative evidence. We study a practical setting with three KBs (encyclopedia, classics, and clinical papers) and a 500-query benchmark (cutoff $K{=}5$) covering both single-KB and cross-KB questions. We propose two complementary methods to improve traceability, reduce hallucinations, and enable cross-KB verification. First, DAKS performs KB routing and budgeted retrieval to mitigate density-driven bias and to prioritize authoritative sources when appropriate. Second, we use an alignment graph to guide evidence fusion and coverage-aware packing, improving cross-KB evidence coverage without relying on naive concatenation. All answers are generated by a lightweight generator, \textsc{openPangu-Embedded-7B}. Experiments show consistent gains in routing quality and cross-KB evidence coverage, with the full system achieving the best CrossEv@5 while maintaining strong faithfulness and citation correctness.

</details>


### [271] [Surgery: Mitigating Harmful Fine-Tuning for Large Language Models via Attention Sink](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05228)
*Guozhi Liu,Weiwei Lin,Tiansheng Huang,Ruichao Mo,Qi Mu,Xiumin Wang,Li Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Defines a per-head metric called sink divergence, observes two sign groups across attention heads during harmful fine-tuning, and proposes a separable sink divergence hypothesis. Introduces Surgery, a fine-tuning regularizer that suppresses sink divergence to steer heads toward the negative group, improving safety defenses on three benchmarks (BeaverTails, HarmBench, SorryBench). Code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety risks from harmful fine-tuning of LLMs by understanding how attention heads contribute to learning harmful patterns and by developing a defense that can be applied during fine-tuning.

Method: Introduce sink divergence as a per-head statistic; empirically show heads split into positive/negative sign groups and that positive-signed heads correlate with increased harmfulness under harmful fine-tuning. Propose separable sink divergence hypothesis. Develop Surgery, a regularizer to suppress sink divergence, steering heads toward the negative group. Evaluate on BeaverTails, HarmBench, and SorryBench; provide source code.

Result: Experimentally, the number of heads with positive sink divergence grows with model harmfulness under harmful fine-tuning. Surgery improves defense performance by 5.90%, 11.25%, and 9.55% on BeaverTails, HarmBench, and SorryBench, respectively.

Conclusion: Sink-divergence-based analysis reveals a separable structure among attention heads in terms of learning harmful patterns. The Surgery defense offers a practical approach to reduce such learning during fine-tuning, with reproducible gains across multiple benchmarks and accessible source code.

Abstract: Harmful fine-tuning can invalidate safety alignment of large language models, exposing significant safety risks. In this paper, we utilize the attention sink mechanism to mitigate harmful fine-tuning. Specifically, we first measure a statistic named \emph{sink divergence} for each attention head and observe that \emph{different attention heads exhibit two different signs of sink divergence}. To understand its safety implications, we conduct experiments and find that the number of attention heads of positive sink divergence increases along with the increase of the model's harmfulness when undergoing harmful fine-tuning. Based on this finding, we propose a separable sink divergence hypothesis -- \emph{attention heads associating with learning harmful patterns during fine-tuning are separable by their sign of sink divergence}. Based on the hypothesis, we propose a fine-tuning-stage defense, dubbed Surgery. Surgery utilizes a regularizer for sink divergence suppression, which steers attention heads toward the negative sink divergence group, thereby reducing the model's tendency to learn and amplify harmful patterns. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Surgery improves defense performance by 5.90\%, 11.25\%, and 9.55\% on the BeaverTails, HarmBench, and SorryBench benchmarks, respectively. Source code is available on https://github.com/Lslland/Surgery.

</details>


### [272] [Explainable AI: A Combined XAI Framework for Explaining Brain Tumour Detection Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05240)
*Patrick McGonagle,William Farrelly,Kevin Curran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Combines GRAD-CAM, LRP, and SHAP with a CNN trained on BraTS 2021 to improve interpretability of brain tumor detection; achieves 91.24% accuracy and yields multi-level explanations for full and partial tumours.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance transparency, trust, and clinical utility of AI in brain MRI by integrating multiple XAI techniques to produce comprehensive explanations beyond any single method.

Method: Train a custom CNN on BraTS 2021; apply integrated XAI methods GRAD-CAM for spatial regions, LRP for pixel-level relevance, SHAP for feature contributions; compare explanatory power against individual methods; test on cases with partial visibility.

Result: Accurate tumor vs non-tumor classification (91.24%); integrated XAI yielded layered explanations from broad ROIs to pixel-level; improved ability to identify partial tumours; explanation coverage and reliability increased relative to single-method XAI; enhanced transparency and trust.

Conclusion: Integrated XAI approaches can enhance interpretability and reliability of AI in medical imaging; potential to improve clinical decision-making in brain tumour detection.

Abstract: This study explores the integration of multiple Explainable AI (XAI) techniques to enhance the interpretability of deep learning models for brain tumour detection. A custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) was developed and trained on the BraTS 2021 dataset, achieving 91.24% accuracy in distinguishing between tumour and non-tumour regions. This research combines Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (GRAD-CAM), Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to provide comprehensive insights into the model's decision-making process. This multi-technique approach successfully identified both full and partial tumours, offering layered explanations ranging from broad regions of interest to pixel-level details. GRAD-CAM highlighted important spatial regions, LRP provided detailed pixel-level relevance and SHAP quantified feature contributions. The integrated approach effectively explained model predictions, including cases with partial tumour visibility thus showing superior explanatory power compared to individual XAI methods. This research enhances transparency and trust in AI-driven medical imaging analysis by offering a more comprehensive perspective on the model's reasoning. The study demonstrates the potential of integrated XAI techniques in improving the reliability and interpretability of AI systems in healthcare, particularly for critical tasks like brain tumour detection.

</details>


### [273] [Automatic Cognitive Task Generation for In-Situ Evaluation of Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05249)
*Xinyi He,Ying Yang,Chuanjian Fu,Sihan Guo,Songchun Zhu,Lifeng Fan,Zhenliang Zhang,Yujia Peng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TEA enables dynamic in-situ task generation for unseen 3D environments via a two-stage interaction-evolution framework that uses a task graph to recombine tasks without external data, uncovering deficits of SOTA models in 3D interaction and perception and arguing for in-situ evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks suffer from data contamination and lack scene specificity for unseen environments. A robust evaluation requires environment-specific, unseen-context task generation to assess embodied agents.

Method: Two-stage system: (1) interaction stage where the agent interacts with the environment to generate tasks through a loop of task execution and on-the-fly generation; (2) evolution stage where a task-graph models and recombines existing tasks to create new ones without external data. Implemented across 10 unseen scenes, yielding 87,876 tasks in two cycles; human verification confirms physical realism and coverage of daily cognitive abilities.

Result: TEA automatically generated 87,876 tasks in two cycles across 10 unseen scenes; human validators deem tasks physically reasonable and representative of essential daily cognitive capabilities. Benchmarking SOTA models against humans on in-situ tasks reveals models excel on public benchmarks but perform poorly on basic perception, show limited 3D interaction awareness, and are highly task-type sensitive in reasoning.

Conclusion: In-situ evaluation is essential before deploying agents in real-world human environments; public benchmarks are insufficient to gauge true capabilities of embodied agents.

Abstract: As general intelligent agents are poised for widespread deployment in diverse households, evaluation tailored to each unique unseen 3D environment has become a critical prerequisite. However, existing benchmarks suffer from severe data contamination and a lack of scene specificity, inadequate for assessing agent capabilities in unseen settings. To address this, we propose a dynamic in-situ task generation method for unseen environments inspired by human cognition. We define tasks through a structured graph representation and construct a two-stage interaction-evolution task generation system for embodied agents (TEA). In the interaction stage, the agent actively interacts with the environment, creating a loop between task execution and generation that allows for continuous task generation. In the evolution stage, task graph modeling allows us to recombine and reuse existing tasks to generate new ones without external data. Experiments across 10 unseen scenes demonstrate that TEA automatically generated 87,876 tasks in two cycles, which human verification confirmed to be physically reasonable and encompassing essential daily cognitive capabilities. Benchmarking SOTA models against humans on our in-situ tasks reveals that models, despite excelling on public benchmarks, perform surprisingly poorly on basic perception tasks, severely lack 3D interaction awareness and show high sensitivity to task types in reasoning. These sobering findings highlight the necessity of in-situ evaluation before deploying agents into real-world human environments.

</details>


### [274] [Beyond Cosine Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05266)
*Xinbo Ai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: recos is a new similarity metric that tightens the dot-product bound and normalizes by sorted vector components, enabling ordinal concordance to capture nonlinear semantic relationships and outperform cosine in STS evaluations across diverse embedding models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cosine similarity, rooted in Cauchy–Schwarz, assumes linear dependence and misses nonlinear/ordinal structures in real-world semantic spaces. A tighter bound on dot product and a corresponding normalization could capture broader semantic relations.

Method: Derive a tighter upper bound for the dot product beyond the Cauchy–Schwarz bound. Define recos as the dot product normalized by the components of vectors ordered (sorted). Validate across 11 embedding models (static, contextualized, universal) on standard STS benchmarks by measuring correlation with human judgments.

Result: recos consistently outperforms cosine across experiments, achieving higher correlations with human judgments on STS tasks, indicating better capture of semantic similarity in complex embedding spaces.

Conclusion: recos provides a principled and empirically superior alternative to cosine for semantic analysis in varied embedding spaces, by enabling ordinal concordance through a tighter dot-product bound and sorted-component normalization.

Abstract: Cosine similarity, the standard metric for measuring semantic similarity in vector spaces, is mathematically grounded in the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which inherently limits it to capturing linear relationships--a constraint that fails to model the complex, nonlinear structures of real-world semantic spaces. We advance this theoretical underpinning by deriving a tighter upper bound for the dot product than the classical Cauchy-Schwarz bound. This new bound leads directly to recos, a similarity metric that normalizes the dot product by the sorted vector components. recos relaxes the condition for perfect similarity from strict linear dependence to ordinal concordance, thereby capturing a broader class of relationships. Extensive experiments across 11 embedding models--spanning static, contextualized, and universal types--demonstrate that recos consistently outperforms traditional cosine similarity, achieving higher correlation with human judgments on standard Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) benchmarks. Our work establishes recos as a mathematically principled and empirically superior alternative, offering enhanced accuracy for semantic analysis in complex embedding spaces.

</details>


### [275] [Position: Universal Time Series Foundation Models Rest on a Category Error](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05287)
*Xilin Dai,Wanxu Cai,Zhijian Xu,Qiang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Universal Time Series Foundation Models are conceptually flawed due to the mismatch between structural containers and semantic modalities; proposal of an Autoregressive Blindness Bound, plus a Causal Control Agent paradigm with hierarchical specialized solvers and drift-aware benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-series applications span diverse, incompatible generative processes; monolithic, universal models risk inefficiency and failure under distributional drift (conceptual misalignment as a structural container rather than a semantic modality).

Method: Theoretical development of the Autoregressive Blindness Bound proving history-only models cannot anticipate intervention-driven regime shifts; advocacy for a Causal Control Agent architecture leveraging external context, frozen domain experts, lightweight JIT adaptors, and a hierarchy of solvers.

Result: A formal bound demonstrating limits of history-only predictors; a research framework replacing universal models with a context-driven, modular solver hierarchy; proposed evaluation shifts toward drift-adaptation metrics.

Conclusion: Encourages moving away from universal zero-shot accuracy toward drift-adaptation speed within a causal-control, modular framework of specialized solvers.

Abstract: This position paper argues that the pursuit of "Universal Foundation Models for Time Series" rests on a fundamental category error, mistaking a structural Container for a semantic Modality. We contend that because time series hold incompatible generative processes (e.g., finance vs. fluid dynamics), monolithic models degenerate into expensive "Generic Filters" that fail to generalize under distributional drift. To address this, we introduce the "Autoregressive Blindness Bound," a theoretical limit proving that history-only models cannot predict intervention-driven regime shifts. We advocate replacing universality with a Causal Control Agent paradigm, where an agent leverages external context to orchestrate a hierarchy of specialized solvers, from frozen domain experts to lightweight Just-in-Time adaptors. We conclude by calling for a shift in benchmarks from "Zero-Shot Accuracy" to "Drift Adaptation Speed" to prioritize robust, control-theoretic systems.

</details>


### [276] [Aspect-Aware MOOC Recommendation in a Heterogeneous Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05297)
*Seongyeub Chu,Jongwoo Kim,Mun Yong Yi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AMR is a graph-based MOOC recommender that learns path-specific, aspect-aware representations by automatically discovering metapaths via bi-directional walks and encoding node semantics with a bi-LSTM, improving HR@K and nDCG@K over baselines on MOOCCube/PEEK; code will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data sparsity and over-specialization in traditional CF/CBF and the reliance on manually predefined metapaths in graph-based MOOC recommenders, which limit semantic richness and impose engineering costs.

Method: Automatically discover metapaths via bi-directional random walks; compute aspect-aware path representations with a bi-LSTM encoder over semantic content of nodes; integrate these representations as edge features in learner-learner and KC-KC subgraphs to enable fine-grained KC (knowledge component) recommendations; evaluate against state-of-the-art GNN baselines on large-scale datasets.

Result: AMR outperforms baselines on standard metrics HR@K and nDCG@K across datasets MOOCCube and PEEK; analyses show it captures rich path-specific aspect information beyond predefined metapaths; code availability promised upon acceptance.

Conclusion: Path-specific, aspect-aware representations learned from automatically discovered metapaths provide more accurate MOOC recommendations and reduce the dependence on manual metapath design, validated on large datasets.

Abstract: MOOC recommendation systems have received increasing attention to help learners navigate and select preferred learning content. Traditional methods such as collaborative filtering and content-based filtering suffer from data sparsity and over-specialization. To alleviate these limitations, graph-based approaches have been proposed; however, they still rely heavily on manually predefined metapaths, which often capture only superficial structural relationships and impose substantial burdens on domain experts as well as significant engineering costs. To overcome these limitations, we propose AMR (Aspect-aware MOOC Recommendation), a novel framework that models path-specific multiple aspects by embedding the semantic content of nodes within each metapath. AMR automatically discovers metapaths through bi-directional walks, derives aspect-aware path representations using a bi-LSTM-based encoder, and incorporates these representations as edge features in the learner-learner and KC-KC subgraphs to achieve fine-grained semantically informed KC recommendations. Extensive experiments on the large-scale MOOCCube and PEEK datasets show that AMR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art graph neural network baselines across key metrics such as HR@K and nDCG@K. Further analysis confirms that AMR effectively captures rich path-specific aspect information, allowing more accurate recommendations than those methods that rely solely on predefined metapaths. The code will be available upon accepted.

</details>


### [277] [PieArena: Frontier Language Agents Achieve MBA-Level Negotiation Performance and Reveal Novel Behavioral Differences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05302)
*Chris Zhu,Sasha Cui,Will Sanok Dufallo,Runzhi Jin,Zhen Xu,Linjun Zhang,Daylian Cain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier LLMs achieve AGI-like performance in a large-scale negotiation benchmark; GPT-5 matches/outperforms trained MBAs; joint-intentionality scaffolding yields asymmetric gains, benefiting mid/low-tier LMs more; multi-dimensional behavioral profiling reveals heterogeneity not captured by outcomes; main caveat is robustness and trustworthiness concerns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether LLMs can handle strategic negotiation tasks that require theory of mind, strategic reasoning, and value creation in realistic, multi-agent scenarios; evaluate robustness, deception, instruction-following, and reputation dynamics in high-stakes economic settings.

Method: Introduce PieArena, a large-scale, multi-agent negotiation benchmark based on real MBA course scenarios. Compare frontier LLMs (e.g., GPT-5) against trained business-school students. Apply joint-intentionality scaffolding and analyze deal outcomes and detailed behavioral profiles (deception, computation accuracy, instruction compliance, perceived reputation).

Result: Systematic evidence of AGI-level performance: frontier LMs match or surpass MBA students. Scaffolding yields large gains for mid/low-tier LMs; diminishing returns for frontier LMs. Behavioral profiling reveals cross-model heterogeneity masked by outcomes, including deception and compliance patterns. Overall frontier LMs show capability for deployment in high-stakes economic settings, but robustness and trustworthiness remain challenges.

Conclusion: Frontier LLMs demonstrate advanced negotiation capabilities, approaching or exceeding human performance in realistic settings. However, reliability, safety, and alignment concerns persist, necessitating further work to ensure robust, trustworthy deployment.

Abstract: We present an in-depth evaluation of LLMs' ability to negotiate, a central business task that requires strategic reasoning, theory of mind, and economic value creation. To do so, we introduce PieArena, a large-scale negotiation benchmark grounded in multi-agent interactions over realistic scenarios drawn from an MBA negotiation course at an elite business school. We find systematic evidence of AGI-level performance in which a representative frontier agent (GPT-5) matches or outperforms trained business-school students, despite a semester of general negotiation instruction and targeted coaching immediately prior to the task. We further study the effects of joint-intentionality agentic scaffolding and find asymmetric gains, with large improvements for mid- and lower-tier LMs and diminishing returns for frontier LMs. Beyond deal outcomes, PieArena provides a multi-dimensional negotiation behavioral profile, revealing novel cross-model heterogeneity, masked by deal-outcome-only benchmarks, in deception, computation accuracy, instruction compliance, and perceived reputation. Overall, our results suggest that frontier language agents are already intellectually and psychologically capable of deployment in high-stakes economic settings, but deficiencies in robustness and trustworthiness remain open challenges.

</details>


### [278] [ProAct: Agentic Lookahead in Interactive Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05327)
*Yangbin Yu,Mingyu Yang,Junyou Li,Yiming Gao,Feiyu Liu,Yijun Yang,Zichuan Lin,Jiafei Lyu,Yicheng Liu,Zhicong Lu,Deheng Ye,Jie Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProAct introduces Grounded LookAhead Distillation (GLAD) and a Monte-Carlo Critic (MC-Critic) to enable LLM agents to perform accurate long-horizon planning with reduced inference-time search, improving performance in both stochastic and deterministic environments using a 4B-model that rivals larger/closed models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents experience compounding errors when simulating future states in long-horizon tasks; existing approaches rely on costly search or unstable training. ProAct aims to internalize lookahead reasoning and stabilize policy optimization with lightweight, environment-grounded signals.

Method: GLAD: supervised fine-tuning on environment-based search trajectories to compress trees into concise causal reasoning chains. MC-Critic: plug-and-play auxiliary value estimator integrated with policy-gradient methods (PPO/GRPO) using short environment rollouts to calibrate values, providing low-variance guidance during training.

Result: Empirical evaluation on stochastic (2048) and deterministic (Sokoban) tasks shows improved planning accuracy. A 4B parameter ProAct model outperforms open-source baselines and matches or exceeds state-of-the-art closed-source models, with strong generalization to unseen environments.

Conclusion: ProAct demonstrates effective integration of learned lookahead reasoning and stable value estimation to enhance long-horizon planning in LLM-based agents, achieving strong performance with a compact model and broad generalization; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Existing Large Language Model (LLM) agents struggle in interactive environments requiring long-horizon planning, primarily due to compounding errors when simulating future states. To address this, we propose ProAct, a framework that enables agents to internalize accurate lookahead reasoning through a two-stage training paradigm. First, we introduce Grounded LookAhead Distillation (GLAD), where the agent undergoes supervised fine-tuning on trajectories derived from environment-based search. By compressing complex search trees into concise, causal reasoning chains, the agent learns the logic of foresight without the computational overhead of inference-time search. Second, to further refine decision accuracy, we propose the Monte-Carlo Critic (MC-Critic), a plug-and-play auxiliary value estimator designed to enhance policy-gradient algorithms like PPO and GRPO. By leveraging lightweight environment rollouts to calibrate value estimates, MC-Critic provides a low-variance signal that facilitates stable policy optimization without relying on expensive model-based value approximation. Experiments on both stochastic (e.g., 2048) and deterministic (e.g., Sokoban) environments demonstrate that ProAct significantly improves planning accuracy. Notably, a 4B parameter model trained with ProAct outperforms all open-source baselines and rivals state-of-the-art closed-source models, while demonstrating robust generalization to unseen environments. The codes and models are available at https://github.com/GreatX3/ProAct

</details>


### [279] [AgentXRay: White-Boxing Agentic Systems via Workflow Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05353)
*Ruijie Shi,Houbin Zhang,Yuecheng Han,Yuheng Wang,Jingru Fan,Runde Yang,Yufan Dang,Huatao Li,Dewen Liu,Yuan Cheng,Chen Qian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces AgentXRay to reconstruct interpretable stand-in workflows (AWR) for black-box agentic systems using only input-output access; uses Monte Carlo Tree Search with Red-Black pruning to search a discrete, chain-structured workflow space; claims improved proxy similarity and reduced token use vs unpruned search, enabling deeper exploration under fixed budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address opacity and lack of interpretability in agentic systems by producing editable white-box workflows that approximate black-box behavior, enabling inspection, debugging, and control without access to internal parameters.

Method: Formulates AWR as combinatorial optimization over discrete agent roles and tool invocations in a chain-structured workflow; uses AgentXRay search framework with MCTS and Red-Black pruning; evaluates by proxy metric against target outputs; compares with unpruned search.

Result: AgentXRay achieves higher proxy similarity and reduces token consumption relative to unpruned search, enabling deeper exploration under fixed iteration budgets.

Conclusion: AWR via AgentXRay provides interpretable, editable approximations of black-box systems with efficient search; potentially improves interpretability and controllability of agentic workflows.

Abstract: Large Language Models have shown strong capabilities in complex problem solving, yet many agentic systems remain difficult to interpret and control due to opaque internal workflows. While some frameworks offer explicit architectures for collaboration, many deployed agentic systems operate as black boxes to users. We address this by introducing Agentic Workflow Reconstruction (AWR), a new task aiming to synthesize an explicit, interpretable stand-in workflow that approximates a black-box system using only input--output access. We propose AgentXRay, a search-based framework that formulates AWR as a combinatorial optimization problem over discrete agent roles and tool invocations in a chain-structured workflow space. Unlike model distillation, AgentXRay produces editable white-box workflows that match target outputs under an observable, output-based proxy metric, without accessing model parameters. To navigate the vast search space, AgentXRay employs Monte Carlo Tree Search enhanced by a scoring-based Red-Black Pruning mechanism, which dynamically integrates proxy quality with search depth. Experiments across diverse domains demonstrate that AgentXRay achieves higher proxy similarity and reduces token consumption compared to unpruned search, enabling deeper workflow exploration under fixed iteration budgets.

</details>


### [280] [PATHWAYS: Evaluating Investigation and Context Discovery in AI Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05354)
*Shifat E. Arman,Syed Nazmus Sakib,Tapodhir Karmakar Taton,Nafiul Haque,Shahrear Bin Amin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PATHWAYS benchmarks 250 multi-step tasks to test whether web agents discover and properly use hidden contextual information. Across models, agents navigate to relevant pages but rarely retrieve decisive hidden evidence; when tasks require overturning misleading surface signals, performance drops to near chance. Agents often hallucinate investigative reasoning and fail to integrate discovered context into final decisions. Providing explicit instructions improves context discovery but reduces overall accuracy, revealing a tradeoff between procedural compliance and sound judgement. The results indicate current web-agent architectures lack reliable adaptive investigation, evidence integration, and judgement override mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify how current web-based agents handle hidden contextual information in multi-step tasks, to identify fundamental gaps in evidence collection, integration, and override capabilities.

Method: Introduce PATHWAYS, a benchmark of 250 multi-step tasks requiring discovery and use of hidden context. Evaluate both closed and open models by analyzing navigation to relevant pages, retrieval of hidden evidence, susceptibility to misleading surface signals, and whether the evidence is integrated into final decisions. Assess the impact of providing explicit instructions on context discovery and decision accuracy.

Result: Agents typically navigate to relevant pages but retrieve decisive hidden evidence in only a small fraction of cases. When tasks require overturning misleading surface-level signals, performance drops to near chance accuracy. Agents frequently hallucinate investigative reasoning by claiming to rely on evidence they never accessed. Even when correct context is discovered, agents often fail to integrate it into their final decision. Providing more explicit instructions improves context discovery but often reduces overall accuracy, revealing a tradeoff between procedural compliance and effective judgement.

Conclusion: Current web-agent architectures lack reliable mechanisms for adaptive investigation, evidence integration, and judgement override. PATHWAYS provides a diagnostic benchmark to characterize these deficiencies and guide development toward agents capable of adaptive evidence collection, proper integration of information, and robust judgement in the face of misleading signals.

Abstract: We introduce PATHWAYS, a benchmark of 250 multi-step decision tasks that test whether web-based agents can discover and correctly use hidden contextual information. Across both closed and open models, agents typically navigate to relevant pages but retrieve decisive hidden evidence in only a small fraction of cases. When tasks require overturning misleading surface-level signals, performance drops sharply to near chance accuracy. Agents frequently hallucinate investigative reasoning by claiming to rely on evidence they never accessed. Even when correct context is discovered, agents often fail to integrate it into their final decision. Providing more explicit instructions improves context discovery but often reduces overall accuracy, revealing a tradeoff between procedural compliance and effective judgement. Together, these results show that current web agent architectures lack reliable mechanisms for adaptive investigation, evidence integration, and judgement override.

</details>


### [281] [RaBiT: Residual-Aware Binarization Training for Accurate and Efficient LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05367)
*Youngcheon You,Banseok Lee,Minseop Choi,Seonyoung Kim,Hyochan Chong,Changdong Kim,Youngmin Kim,Dongkyu Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RaBiT introduces sequential residual binary paths derived from a shared full-precision weight to resolve inter-path adaptation in 2-bit quantization, enabling efficient, high-accuracy LLM inference with notable speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extreme quantization (2-bit residual binarization) offers hardware efficiency but suffers from inter-path co-adaptation and degraded error compensation, limiting model expressivity and performance.

Method: Enforce a residual hierarchy by deriving each binary path from a single shared FP weight in a sequential manner; propagate and correct errors across paths. Use robust initialization that prioritizes functional preservation over weight approximation to stabilize training and avoid heuristic fixes (e.g., path freezing).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art 2-bit accuracy-efficiency, rivaling hardware-intensive VQ methods, and delivers ~4.49× inference speed-up over FP models on RTX 4090.

Conclusion: RaBiT redefines the 2-bit accuracy-efficiency frontier for LLM quantization by mitigating inter-path adaptation and enabling substantially faster, high-accuracy low-bit inference.

Abstract: Efficient deployment of large language models (LLMs) requires extreme quantization, forcing a critical trade-off between low-bit efficiency and performance. Residual binarization enables hardware-friendly, matmul-free inference by stacking binary ($\pm$1) layers, but is plagued by pathological feature co-adaptation. We identify a key failure mode, which we term inter-path adaptation: during quantization-aware training (QAT), parallel residual binary paths learn redundant features, degrading the error-compensation structure and limiting the expressive capacity of the model. While prior work relies on heuristic workarounds (e.g., path freezing) that constrain the solution space, we propose RaBiT, a novel quantization framework that resolves co-adaptation by algorithmically enforcing a residual hierarchy. Its core mechanism sequentially derives each binary path from a single shared full-precision weight, which ensures that every path corrects the error of the preceding one. This process is stabilized by a robust initialization that prioritizes functional preservation over mere weight approximation. RaBiT redefines the 2-bit accuracy-efficiency frontier: it achieves state-of-the-art performance, rivals even hardware-intensive Vector Quantization (VQ) methods, and delivers a $4.49\times$ inference speed-up over full-precision models on an RTX 4090.

</details>


### [282] [Clinical Validation of Medical-based Large Language Model Chatbots on Ophthalmic Patient Queries with LLM-based Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05381)
*Ting Fang Tan,Kabilan Elangovan,Andreas Pollreisz,Kevin Bryan Dy,Wei Yan Ng,Joy Le Yi Wong,Jin Liyuan,Chrystie Quek Wan Ning,Ashley Shuen Ying Hong,Arun James Thirunavukarasu,Shelley Yin-His Chang,Jie Yao,Dylan Hong,Wang Zhaoran,Amrita Gupta,Daniel SW Ting*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Small ophthalmology-focused LLMs can answer patient questions with variable accuracy; Meerkat-7B best among four; MedLLaMA3-v20 shows hallucinations; GPT-4-Turbo aligns with clinicians; LLM-based evaluation feasible; hybrid guardrails recommended.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess safety and accuracy of domain-specific medical LLMs in ophthalmology patient queries and to evaluate the feasibility of LLM-driven benchmarking against clinician grading.

Method: Cross-sectional study (180 ophthalmology patient queries) answered by four LLMs with <10B parameters (Meerkat-7B, BioMistral-7B, OpenBioLLM-8B, MedLLaMA3-v20); responses evaluated by three ophthalmologists and GPT-4-Turbo using the SCORE framework; agreement analyzed via Spearman, Kendall, KDE.

Result: Meerkat-7B yielded highest mean scores across seniority grades (Senior Consultants 3.44; Consultants 4.08; Residents 4.18). MedLLaMA3-v20 worst (25.5% hallucinations/clinically misleading content). GPT-4-Turbo grading strongly aligned with clinicians (Spearman 0.80; Kendall 0.67); Senior Consultants more conservative. Overall, potential for safe QA; gaps in clinical depth and consensus; supports feasibility of LLM-based benchmarking; recommends hybrid automated + clinician review.

Conclusion: Domain-specific medical LLMs show promise for ophthalmic Q&A but require safeguards and hybrid evaluation/oversight to ensure safety and clinical adequacy for deployment.

Abstract: Domain specific large language models are increasingly used to support patient education, triage, and clinical decision making in ophthalmology, making rigorous evaluation essential to ensure safety and accuracy. This study evaluated four small medical LLMs Meerkat-7B, BioMistral-7B, OpenBioLLM-8B, and MedLLaMA3-v20 in answering ophthalmology related patient queries and assessed the feasibility of LLM based evaluation against clinician grading. In this cross sectional study, 180 ophthalmology patient queries were answered by each model, generating 2160 responses. Models were selected for parameter sizes under 10 billion to enable resource efficient deployment. Responses were evaluated by three ophthalmologists of differing seniority and by GPT-4-Turbo using the S.C.O.R.E. framework assessing safety, consensus and context, objectivity, reproducibility, and explainability, with ratings assigned on a five point Likert scale. Agreement between LLM and clinician grading was assessed using Spearman rank correlation, Kendall tau statistics, and kernel density estimate analyses. Meerkat-7B achieved the highest performance with mean scores of 3.44 from Senior Consultants, 4.08 from Consultants, and 4.18 from Residents. MedLLaMA3-v20 performed poorest, with 25.5 percent of responses containing hallucinations or clinically misleading content, including fabricated terminology. GPT-4-Turbo grading showed strong alignment with clinician assessments overall, with Spearman rho of 0.80 and Kendall tau of 0.67, though Senior Consultants graded more conservatively. Overall, medical LLMs demonstrated potential for safe ophthalmic question answering, but gaps remained in clinical depth and consensus, supporting the feasibility of LLM based evaluation for large scale benchmarking and the need for hybrid automated and clinician review frameworks to guide safe clinical deployment.

</details>


### [283] [Advancing Opinion Dynamics Modeling with Neural Diffusion-Convection-Reaction Equation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05403)
*Chenghua Gong,Yihang Jiang,Hao Li,Rui Sun,Juyuan Zhang,Tianjun Gu,Liming Pan,Linyuan Lü*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OPINN is a physics-informed neural framework for opinion dynamics that uses a Diffusion-Convection-Reaction (DCR) system, integrated via Neural ODEs to combine physical priors with data-driven learning, achieving state-of-the-art opinion evolution forecasting on real and synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable mechanistic interpretability while retaining data-driven flexibility in opinion dynamics, addressing polarization and cyberspace security. Prior PINNs with incomplete priors and penalty-based constraints struggle to encode physics and maintain latent-physics alignment.

Method: Propose a DCR-system inspired by interacting particle theory to model opinion dynamics. Build Neural ODE-based neural opinion dynamics to coordinate neural networks with physical priors, culminating in OPINN, a physics-informed framework for opinion dynamics.

Result: Evaluated on real-world and synthetic datasets; OPINN achieves state-of-the-art performance in forecasting opinion evolution, suggesting a robust framework for cyber-physical-social systems.

Conclusion: OPINN demonstrates a promising integration of physical priors with neural dynamics for interpretable, accurate opinion evolution forecasting, potentially guiding future multi-scale social modeling across cyber-physical contexts.

Abstract: Advanced opinion dynamics modeling is vital for deciphering social behavior, emphasizing its role in mitigating polarization and securing cyberspace. To synergize mechanistic interpretability with data-driven flexibility, recent studies have explored the integration of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for opinion modeling. Despite this promise, existing methods are tailored to incomplete priors, lacking a comprehensive physical system to integrate dynamics from local, global, and endogenous levels. Moreover, penalty-based constraints adopted in existing methods struggle to deeply encode physical priors, leading to optimization pathologies and discrepancy between latent representations and physical transparency. To this end, we offer a physical view to interpret opinion dynamics via Diffusion-Convection-Reaction (DCR) system inspired by interacting particle theory. Building upon the Neural ODEs, we define the neural opinion dynamics to coordinate neural networks with physical priors, and further present the OPINN, a physics-informed neural framework for opinion dynamics modeling. Evaluated on real-world and synthetic datasets, OPINN achieves state-of-the-art performance in opinion evolution forecasting, offering a promising paradigm for the nexus of cyber, physical, and social systems.

</details>


### [284] [H-AdminSim: A Multi-Agent Simulator for Realistic Hospital Administrative Workflows with FHIR Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05407)
*Jun-Min Lee,Meong Hi Son,Edward Choi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: End-to-end hospital admin simulation (H-AdminSim) integrating realistic data, multi-agent workflows, and FHIR interoperability to benchmark LLM-driven administrative automation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses gap: existing work focuses on patient-physician interactions or isolated tasks; real hospital admin workflows are complex and require end-to-end testing across heterogeneous settings.

Method: Combines realistic data generation with multi-agent simulation of administrative processes; uses detailed rubrics for quantitative evaluation; integrates FHIR for interoperability; provides a unified testbed.

Result: Proposes a comprehensive framework enabling systematic evaluation of LLMs for hospital administration tasks; demonstrates end-to-end simulation and standardized evaluation environment.

Conclusion: H-AdminSim offers a standardized, interoperable platform for assessing feasibility and performance of LLM-based admin automation; potential extension to broader hospital settings and tasks.

Abstract: Hospital administration departments handle a wide range of operational tasks and, in large hospitals, process over 10,000 requests per day, driving growing interest in LLM-based automation. However, prior work has focused primarily on patient--physician interactions or isolated administrative subtasks, failing to capture the complexity of real administrative workflows. To address this gap, we propose H-AdminSim, a comprehensive end-to-end simulation framework that combines realistic data generation with multi-agent-based simulation of hospital administrative workflows. These tasks are quantitatively evaluated using detailed rubrics, enabling systematic comparison of LLMs. Through FHIR integration, H-AdminSim provides a unified and interoperable environment for testing administrative workflows across heterogeneous hospital settings, serving as a standardized testbed for assessing the feasibility and performance of LLM-driven administrative automation.

</details>


### [285] [THOR: Inductive Link Prediction over Hyper-Relational Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05424)
*Weijian Yu,Yuhuan Lu,Dingqi Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: THOR is an inductive method for hyper-relational knowledge graphs using relation and entity foundation graphs with parallel graph encoders and a transformer decoder to enable efficient masked training and fully-inductive inference, outperforming baselines on 12 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of generalization in transductive KG embedding methods when dealing with hyper-relational facts; enable predictive reasoning on unseen vocabularies and entities via inductive learning.

Method: Construct relation and entity foundation graphs that capture fundamental interactions in HKGs, train with two parallel graph encoders on these graphs, then use a transformer decoder with masked training to perform fully-inductive link prediction.

Result: Evaluated on 12 datasets, THOR achieves substantial improvements over baselines: ~66.1% over rule-based, ~55.9% over semi-inductive, and ~20.4% over fully-inductive methods; ablations identify key design factors related to structural invariance transferable across HKGs.

Conclusion: THOR advances inductive hyper-relational KG completion by introducing universal foundation graphs and a two-encoder + transformer architecture, demonstrating strong generalization and revealing design factors critical for transferability.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs (KGs) have become a key ingredient supporting a variety of applications. Beyond the traditional triplet representation of facts where a relation connects two entities, modern KGs observe an increasing number of hyper-relational facts, where an arbitrary number of qualifiers associated with a triplet provide auxiliary information to further describe the rich semantics of the triplet, which can effectively boost the reasoning performance in link prediction tasks. However, existing link prediction techniques over such hyper-relational KGs (HKGs) mostly focus on a transductive setting, where KG embedding models are learned from the specific vocabulary of a given KG and subsequently can only make predictions within the same vocabulary, limiting their generalizability to previously unseen vocabularies. Against this background, we propose THOR, an inducTive link prediction technique for Hyper-relational knOwledge gRaphs. Specifically, we first introduce both relation and entity foundation graphs, modeling their fundamental inter- and intra-fact interactions in HKGs, which are agnostic to any specific relations and entities. Afterward, THOR is designed to learn from the two foundation graphs with two parallel graph encoders followed by a transformer decoder, which supports efficient masked training and fully-inductive inference. We conduct a thorough evaluation of THOR in hyper-relational link prediction tasks on 12 datasets with different settings. Results show that THOR outperforms a sizable collection of baselines, yielding 66.1%, 55.9%, and 20.4% improvement over the best-performing rule-based, semi-inductive, and fully-inductive techniques, respectively. A series of ablation studies also reveals our key design factors capturing the structural invariance transferable across HKGs for inductive tasks.

</details>


### [286] [Day-Ahead Electricity Price Forecasting for Volatile Markets Using Foundation Models with Regularization Strategy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05430)
*Kritchanat Ponyuenyong,Pengyu Tu,Jia Wei Tan,Wei Soon Cheong,Jamie Ng Suat Ling,Lianlian Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Spike-regularized TSFMs outperform traditional models in day-ahead electricity price forecasting on volatile Singapore half-hourly data, with exogenous factors; up to 37.4% MAPE improvement across TTMs, MOIRAI, MOMENT, and TimesFM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EPF is highly volatile and nonlinear, making accurate day-ahead forecasts challenging. TSFMs show strong performance in general time series tasks but are underexplored for volatile, real-time price signals and for integrating heterogeneous data in electricity markets.

Method: Evaluate a suite of time-series foundation models (TTMs, MOIRAI, MOMENT, TimesFM) with a spike regularization strategy. Compare against traditional models (ARIMA, LSTM, CNN-LSTM) using half-hourly wholesale market data from Singapore. Incorporate exogenous variables (weather, calendar). Assess across multiple evaluation settings using MAPE.

Result: TSFMs consistently beat traditional approaches, achieving substantial MAPE reductions (up to 37.4%) in day-ahead EPF under volatile market conditions. The spike regularization strategy contributes to robustness against abrupt price spikes.

Conclusion: Time-series foundation models, aided by spike regularization and exogenous data, provide robust, accurate day-ahead EPF in volatile markets, outperforming conventional statistical and DL baselines. Generalization to other markets and longer-horizon forecasting warrants further study.

Abstract: Electricity price forecasting (EPF) is essential for energy markets stakeholders (e.g. grid operators, energy traders, policymakers) but remains challenging due to the inherent volatility and nonlinearity of price signals. Traditional statistical and deep learning (DL) models often struggle to capture complex temporal dependencies and integrate heterogeneous data effectively. While time series foundation models (TSFMs) have shown strong performance in general time series forecasting tasks, such as traffic forecasting and weather forecasting. However, their effectiveness in day-ahead EPF, particularly in volatile markets, remains underexplored. This paper presents a spike regularization strategy and evaluates a wide range of TSFMs, including Tiny Time Mixers (TTMs), MOIRAI, MOMENT, and TimesFM, against traditional statistical and DL models such as Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA), Long-short Term Memory (LSTM), and Convolutional Neural Network - LSTM (CNN-LSTM) using half-hourly wholesale market data with volatile trends in Singapore. Exogenous factors (e.g. weather and calendar variables) are also incorporated into models where applicable. Results demonstrate that TSFMs consistently outperform traditional approaches, achieving up to 37.4% improvement in MAPE across various evaluation settings. The findings offer practical guidance for improving forecast accuracy and decision-making in volatile electricity markets.

</details>


### [287] [Refine and Purify: Orthogonal Basis Optimization with Null-Space Denoising for Conditional Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05464)
*Jiaquan Wang,Yan Lyu,Chen Li,Yuheng Jia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OD-CRL combines Adaptive Orthogonal Basis Optimization (AOBO) and Null-Space Denoising Projection (NSDP) to produce robust, subspace-resilient conditional representations, achieving SOTA on customized tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conditioned representations often depend on the subspace basis and suffer from inter-subspace interference; robust mechanisms are needed to stabilize the basis and suppress non-target signals.

Method: Adaptive Orthogonal Basis Optimization (AOBO) uses singular value decomposition with curvature-based truncation to construct orthogonal semantic bases; Null-Space Denoising Projection (NSDP) projects embeddings into the null space of irrelevant subspaces to suppress interference.

Result: Empirical results across customized clustering, classification, and retrieval show improved performance and generalization, achieving new state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: OD-CRL effectively mitigates basis sensitivity and inter-subspace interference, enabling robust conditional representations across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Conditional representation learning aims to extract criterion-specific features for customized tasks. Recent studies project universal features onto the conditional feature subspace spanned by an LLM-generated text basis to obtain conditional representations. However, such methods face two key limitations: sensitivity to subspace basis and vulnerability to inter-subspace interference. To address these challenges, we propose OD-CRL, a novel framework integrating Adaptive Orthogonal Basis Optimization (AOBO) and Null-Space Denoising Projection (NSDP). Specifically, AOBO constructs orthogonal semantic bases via singular value decomposition with a curvature-based truncation. NSDP suppresses non-target semantic interference by projecting embeddings onto the null space of irrelevant subspaces. Extensive experiments conducted across customized clustering, customized classification, and customized retrieval tasks demonstrate that OD-CRL achieves a new state-of-the-art performance with superior generalization.

</details>


### [288] [ALIVE: Awakening LLM Reasoning via Adversarial Learning and Instructive Verbal Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05472)
*Yiwen Duan,Jing Ye,Xinpei Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ALIVE offers a self-sufficient reasoning alignment framework that eliminates external scalar rewards by integrating adversarial learning with instructive verbal feedback, unifying problem posing, solving, and judging within a single model to enhance reasoning across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward bottlenecks in traditional RL for LLMs: scalar rewards are costly to scale, brittle across domains, and blind to the underlying logic of solutions; this hinders development of autonomous, principled reasoning capabilities.

Method: ALIVE (Adversarial Learning with Instructive Verbal Evaluation) couples adversarial learning with instructive verbal feedback within a Cognitive Synergy framework, unifying problem posing, solving, and judging in a single policy to internalize the criteria of correctness from raw corpora.

Result: Empirical evaluations across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general logical inference show reduced reliance on external reward signals; with equal data and compute, ALIVE yields accuracy gains, better cross-domain generalization, and higher self-correction rates.

Conclusion: The reasoning trinity fosters a self-sustaining trajectory of capability growth, positioning ALIVE as a scalable foundation for general-purpose reasoning alignment without human-in-the-loop supervision.

Abstract: The quest for expert-level reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs) has been hampered by a persistent \textit{reward bottleneck}: traditional reinforcement learning (RL) relies on scalar rewards that are \textbf{costly} to scale, \textbf{brittle} across domains, and \textbf{blind} to the underlying logic of a solution. This reliance on external, impoverished signals prevents models from developing a deep, self-contained understanding of reasoning principles. We introduce \textbf{ALIVE} (\emph{Adversarial Learning with Instructive Verbal Evaluation}), a hands-free alignment framework that moves beyond scalar reward optimization toward intrinsic reasoning acquisition. Grounded in the principle of \emph{Cognitive Synergy}, ALIVE unifies problem posing, solving, and judging within a single policy model to internalize the logic of correctness. By coupling adversarial learning with instructive verbal feedback, ALIVE enables models to internalize evaluative criteria directly from raw corpora, effectively transforming external critiques into an endogenous reasoning faculty. Empirical evaluations across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general logical inference benchmarks demonstrate that ALIVE consistently mitigates reward signal limitations. With identical data and compute, it achieves accuracy gains, markedly improved cross-domain generalization, and higher self-correction rates. These results indicate that the reasoning trinity fosters a self-sustaining trajectory of capability growth, positioning ALIVE as a scalable foundation for general-purpose reasoning alignment without human-in-the-loop supervision.

</details>


### [289] [Phi-Former: A Pairwise Hierarchical Approach for Compound-Protein Interactions Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05479)
*Zhe Wang,Zijing Liu,Chencheng Xu,Yuan Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Phi-former introduces a hierarchical, pairwise interaction model that aligns CPI prediction with motif-level biology, improving accuracy and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CPI prediction is central to drug discovery; atom-level DL models may miss motifs that govern recognition; incorporating biological motifs and hierarchical structure can better capture CPIs.

Method: Phi-former uses hierarchical representations for compounds and proteins and a pairwise pre-training framework across atom-atom, motif-motif, and atom-motif interactions. It employs intra-level and inter-level learning pipelines so different interaction levels mutually benefit, with motif-aware encodings and interpretable attributions.

Result: Empirical results show superior CPI-related task performance; a case study demonstrates interpretable identification of activated atoms/motifs during CPIs, aiding rational drug design.

Conclusion: Integrates biological motif recognition into CPI modeling, enhancing interpretability and potential impact on drug design and precision medicine; suggests future motif-based hierarchical modeling directions.

Abstract: Drug discovery remains time-consuming, labor-intensive, and expensive, often requiring years and substantial investment per drug candidate. Predicting compound-protein interactions (CPIs) is a critical component in this process, enabling the identification of molecular interactions between drug candidates and target proteins. Recent deep learning methods have successfully modeled CPIs at the atomic level, achieving improved efficiency and accuracy over traditional energy-based approaches. However, these models do not always align with chemical realities, as molecular fragments (motifs or functional groups) typically serve as the primary units of biological recognition and binding. In this paper, we propose Phi-former, a pairwise hierarchical interaction representation learning method that addresses this gap by incorporating the biological role of motifs in CPIs. Phi-former represents compounds and proteins hierarchically and employs a pairwise pre-training framework to model interactions systematically across atom-atom, motif-motif, and atom-motif levels, reflecting how biological systems recognize molecular partners. We design intra-level and inter-level learning pipelines that make different interaction levels mutually beneficial. Experimental results demonstrate that Phi-former achieves superior performance on CPI-related tasks. A case study shows that our method accurately identifies specific atoms or motifs activated in CPIs, providing interpretable model explanations. These insights may guide rational drug design and support precision medicine applications.

</details>


### [290] [SDFP: Speculative Decoding with FIT-Pruned Models for Training-Free and Plug-and-Play LLM Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05499)
*Hanyu Wei,Zunhai Su,Peng Lu,Chao Li,Spandan Tiwari,Ashish Sirasao,Yuhan Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Training-free, plug-and-play layer-pruning for speculative decoding: SDFP uses Fisher Information Trace (FIT) to prune low-impact layers from a given LLM to create a compact draft that preserves the original model's output distribution, enabling faster decoding without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs incur high latency during autoregressive decoding in interactive multimedia tasks. Existing speculative decoding relies on draft models that require costly training, tuning, or maintenance. A training-free, deployment-friendly draft is highly desirable.

Method: Compute layer sensitivity via Fisher Information Trace (FIT); prune low-impact layers to form a compact draft compatible with the original model for standard speculative verification; no additional training, hyperparameter tuning, or separate draft maintenance.

Result: Achieves 1.32x–1.5x decoding speedup across benchmarks while preserving the target model's output distribution; no changes to the model's outputs or training requirements.

Conclusion: SDFP provides a rapid, deployment-friendly solution for low-latency speculative decoding by constructing a draft purely through FIT-based layer pruning, avoiding training overhead and extra drafts.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) underpin interactive multimedia applications such as captioning, retrieval, recommendation, and creative content generation, yet their autoregressive decoding incurs substantial latency. Speculative decoding reduces latency using a lightweight draft model, but deployment is often limited by the cost and complexity of acquiring, tuning, and maintaining an effective draft model. Recent approaches usually require auxiliary training or specialization, and even training-free methods incur costly search or optimization. We propose SDFP, a fully training-free and plug-and-play framework that builds the draft model via Fisher Information Trace (FIT)-based layer pruning of a given LLM. Using layer sensitivity as a proxy for output perturbation, SDFP removes low-impact layers to obtain a compact draft while preserving compatibility with the original model for standard speculative verification. SDFP needs no additional training, hyperparameter tuning, or separately maintained drafts, enabling rapid, deployment-friendly draft construction. Across benchmarks, SDFP delivers 1.32x-1.5x decoding speedup without altering the target model's output distribution, supporting low-latency multimedia applications.

</details>


### [291] [Split Personality Training: Revealing Latent Knowledge Through Alternate Personalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05532)
*Florian Dietz,William Wale,Oscar Gilg,Robert McCarthy,Felix Michalak,Gustavo Ewbank Rodrigues Danon,Miguelito de Guzman,Dietrich Klakow*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Split Personality Training (SPT) uses a dormant, second honest persona implemented as LoRA to audit the main model post-response by triggering the honest persona and accessing latent states, achieving high misalignment detection accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditing LLM misalignment is difficult: black-box methods struggle to distinguish malicious outputs from benign ones, and mechanistic interpretability scales poorly with model capability. A robust, scalable auditing approach is needed.

Method: Fine-tune a second 'honest persona' as LoRA adapters that stay inactive during normal operation. After the main model generates a response, activate the LoRA adapter and insert a trigger string so the honest persona can review the response and access the main model's latent states.

Result: On the Anthropic Auditing Game Model Organism benchmark using Llama-3.3-70B, SPT achieves 96% overall accuracy in detecting misalignment, outperforming reported baselines that approximate near 0% accuracy. The honest persona exposes latent knowledge and biases that are hidden from external observers.

Conclusion: SPT demonstrates a practical approach to auditing misalignment by separating evaluation from generation, enabling hidden assessor information to surface. It reveals latent model knowledge and biases and offers a scalable improvement over existing auditing techniques, though further evaluation on broader benchmarks and potential privacy/safety concerns should be explored.

Abstract: Detecting misalignment in large language models is challenging because models may learn to conceal misbehavior during training. Standard auditing techniques fall short: black-box methods often cannot distinguish misaligned outputs from benign ones, and mechanistic interpretability does not scale with model capabilities.
