We introduce Split Personality Training (SPT), which fine-tunes a second ``honest persona'' into LoRA parameters that remain inactive during normal operation. After the main model responds, we activate the LoRA adapter and insert a trigger string, enabling the honest persona to review the response while accessing the main model's latent states. We test our method on the Anthropic Auditing Game Model Organism, a benchmark where Llama-3.3-70B is trained to exploit reward hacks while concealing this behavior. SPT achieves 96% overall accuracy, whereas Anthropic reports near 0% accuracy. The honest persona reveals latent knowledge inaccessible to external observers, such as the fictional biases the compromised model was trained on.

</details>


### [292] [Reasoning-guided Collaborative Filtering with Language Models for Explainable Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05544)
*Fahad Anwaar,Adil Mehmood Khan,Muhammad Khalid,Usman Zia,Kezhi Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RGCF-XRec integrates reasoning-guided CF knowledge into a language model to deliver explainable sequential recommendations in one step, achieving improved recommendation quality and explanations with a lightweight backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the underutilization of collaborative signals in LLM-based explainable recommender systems and to avoid treating recommendation and explanation as separate tasks that increase memory footprint.

Method: A unified framework that (1) augments CF knowledge through contextual prompting to uncover latent user preferences and interpretable reasoning paths; (2) employs a four-dimension scoring mechanism (coherence, completeness, relevance, consistency) to filter noisy CF reasoning traces; (3) learns a joint representation encoding collaborative and semantic signals to form a structured prompt for an LLM (LLaMA 3.2-3B) for explainable sequential recommendation.

Result: Empirical results show consistent gains: HR@10 improvements in Sports (7.38%) and Toys (4.59%); ROUGE-L gains in Sports (8.02%) and Toys (3.49%); reduced cold/warm gaps (14.5% in cold-start, 11.9% in warm-start); zero-shot HR@5 gains in Beauty (18.54%) and Toys (23.16%); training with a lightweight LLaMA 3.2-3B backbone indicating scalability.

Conclusion: RGCF-XRec delivers a unified, efficient, and robust explainable sequential recommender by tightly integrating CF signals into LLM prompting, achieving superior performance and generalization while maintaining scalability.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit potential for explainable recommendation systems but overlook collaborative signals, while prevailing methods treat recommendation and explanation as separate tasks, resulting in a memory footprint. We present RGCF-XRec, a hybrid framework that introduces reasoning-guided collaborative filtering (CF) knowledge into a language model to deliver explainable sequential recommendations in a single step. Theoretical grounding and empirical findings reveal that RGCF-XRec offers three key merits over leading CF-aware LLM-based methods: (1) reasoning-guided augmentation of CF knowledge through contextual prompting to discover latent preferences and interpretable reasoning paths; (2) an efficient scoring mechanism based on four dimensions: coherence, completeness, relevance, and consistency to mitigate noisy CF reasoning traces and retain high-quality explanations; (3) a unified representation learning network that encodes collaborative and semantic signals, enabling a structured prompt to condition the LLM for explainable sequential recommendation. RGCF-XRec demonstrates consistent improvements across Amazon datasets, Sports, Toys, and Beauty, comprising 642,503 user-item interactions. It improves HR@10 by 7.38\% in Sports and 4.59\% in Toys, along with ROUGE-L by 8.02\% and 3.49\%, respectively. It reduces the cold warm performance gap, achieving overall gains of 14.5\% in cold-start and 11.9\% in warm start scenarios, and enhances zero-shot HR@5 by 18.54\% in Beauty and 23.16\% in Toys, highlighting effective generalization and robustness. Moreover, RGCF-XRec achieves training efficiency with a lightweight LLaMA 3.2-3B backbone, ensuring scalability for real-world applications.

</details>


### [293] [Learning Event-Based Shooter Models from Virtual Reality Experiments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06023)
*Christopher A. McClurg,Alan R. Wagner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A data-driven discrete-event simulator (DES) models shooter behavior learned from VR studies to enable scalable evaluation and learning of autonomous, robot-based interventions in school-security scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VR studies offer experimental control and behavioral fidelity for evaluating school-security measures but are limited by the need for new participant cohorts per condition and the high number of training episodes required to learn effective interventions.

Method: Build a data-driven DES that treats shooter movement and in-region actions as stochastic processes learned from participant behavior in VR; validate that it reproduces key empirical patterns; use DES to evaluate and learn a robot-based intervention strategy; demonstrate a high-to-mid fidelity simulation workflow as a scalable surrogate.

Result: The DES reproduces key empirical patterns from VR data and enables scalable evaluation and learning of intervention strategies that are impractical to train directly with human subjects; shows feasibility of using a high-to-mid fidelity workflow for autonomous school-security interventions.

Conclusion: A scalable surrogate simulation workflow can accelerate development and evaluation of autonomous security interventions in schools by leveraging VR-derived behavioral models, enabling iterative learning and large-scale testing without extensive human subject recruitment.

Abstract: Virtual reality (VR) has emerged as a powerful tool for evaluating school security measures in high-risk scenarios such as school shootings, offering experimental control and high behavioral fidelity. However, assessing new interventions in VR requires recruiting new participant cohorts for each condition, making large-scale or iterative evaluation difficult. These limitations are especially restrictive when attempting to learn effective intervention strategies, which typically require many training episodes. To address this challenge, we develop a data-driven discrete-event simulator (DES) that models shooter movement and in-region actions as stochastic processes learned from participant behavior in VR studies. We use the simulator to examine the impact of a robot-based shooter intervention strategy. Once shown to reproduce key empirical patterns, the DES enables scalable evaluation and learning of intervention strategies that are infeasible to train directly with human subjects. Overall, this work demonstrates a high-to-mid fidelity simulation workflow that provides a scalable surrogate for developing and evaluating autonomous school-security interventions.

</details>


### [294] [BhashaSetu: Cross-Lingual Knowledge Transfer from High-Resource to Extreme Low-Resource Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05599)
*Subhadip Maji,Arnab Bhattacharya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes GETR, a graph-enhanced token representation framework for cross-lingual knowledge transfer to low-resource languages; demonstrates significant gains in POS, sentiment, and NER using GNNs and compares to two baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity and limited linguistic resources in low-resource languages; leverage high-resource languages to transfer linguistic knowledge; applicable to sentence-level and word-level tasks.

Method: Graph-Enhanced Token Representation (GETR) using a Graph Neural Network to transfer knowledge; baselines include (a) hidden-layer augmentation and (b) token embedding transfer via token translation.

Result: Outperforms existing multilingual/cross-lingual baselines; 13-percentage-point gains on POS tagging for Mizo and Khasi; 20- and 27-percentage-point improvements in macro-F1 on Marathi, Bangla, Malayalam across sentiment classification and NER; provides transfer mechanism analysis and identifies key factors for successful transfer.

Conclusion: GNN-based GETR effectively facilitates cross-lingual transfer for low-resource languages; insights into factors driving successful transfer in this linguistic setting.

Abstract: Despite remarkable advances in natural language processing, developing effective systems for low-resource languages remains a formidable challenge, with performances typically lagging far behind high-resource counterparts due to data scarcity and insufficient linguistic resources. Cross-lingual knowledge transfer has emerged as a promising approach to address this challenge by leveraging resources from high-resource languages. In this paper, we investigate methods for transferring linguistic knowledge from high-resource languages to low-resource languages, where the number of labeled training instances is in hundreds. We focus on sentence-level and word-level tasks. We introduce a novel method, GETR (Graph-Enhanced Token Representation) for cross-lingual knowledge transfer along with two adopted baselines (a) augmentation in hidden layers and (b) token embedding transfer through token translation. Experimental results demonstrate that our GNN-based approach significantly outperforms existing multilingual and cross-lingual baseline methods, achieving 13 percentage point improvements on truly low-resource languages (Mizo, Khasi) for POS tagging, and 20 and 27 percentage point improvements in macro-F1 on simulated low-resource languages (Marathi, Bangla, Malayalam) across sentiment classification and NER tasks respectively. We also present a detailed analysis of the transfer mechanisms and identify key factors that contribute to successful knowledge transfer in this linguistic context.

</details>


### [295] [Determining Energy Efficiency Sweet Spots in Production LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05695)
*Hiari Pizzini Cavagna,Andrea Proia,Giacomo Madella,Giovanni B. Esposito,Francesco Antici,Daniele Cesarini,Zeynep Kiziltan,Andrea Bartolini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Analytical non-linear model for LLM energy efficiency as a function of input/output lengths, validated on H100 GPUs across 1B–9B parameter models; reveals energy 'sweet spots' and potential energy savings by length alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing energy estimates are linear in input/output lengths; observed non-linear energy efficiency regimes necessitate a more accurate model to guide energy-aware inference and deployment.

Method: Derives an analytical model from Transformer compute and memory-access complexity, capturing non-linear energy dependence on input and output lengths. Validated with TensorRT-LLM on NVIDIA H100 across multiple models (OPT, LLaMA, Gemma, Falcon, Qwen2, Granite) with input/output lengths from 64 to 4096 tokens.

Result: Mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 1.79% between model predictions and measured energy consumption, and identification of energy efficiency 'sweet spots' where short-to-moderate inputs and medium outputs maximize efficiency; energy efficiency deteriorates for very long inputs or very short outputs.

Conclusion: Align sequence lengths with identified sweet spots to substantially reduce energy usage, enabling truncation, summarization, and adaptive generation strategies in production systems.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) inference is central in modern AI applications, making it critical to understand their energy footprint. Existing approaches typically estimate energy consumption through simple linear functions of input and output sequence lengths, yet our observations reveal clear Energy Efficiency regimes: peak efficiency occurs with short-to-moderate inputs and medium-length outputs, while efficiency drops sharply for long inputs or very short outputs, indicating a non-linear dependency. In this work, we propose an analytical model derived from the computational and memory-access complexity of the Transformer architecture, capable of accurately characterizing the efficiency curve as a function of input and output lengths. To assess its accuracy, we evaluate energy consumption using TensorRT-LLM on NVIDIA H100 GPUs across a diverse set of LLMs ranging from 1B to 9B parameters, including OPT, LLaMA, Gemma, Falcon, Qwen2, and Granite, tested over input and output lengths from 64 to 4096 tokens, achieving a mean MAPE of 1.79%. Our results show that aligning sequence lengths with these efficiency "Sweet Spots" can substantially reduce energy usage, supporting informed truncation, summarization, and adaptive generation strategies in production systems.

</details>


### [296] [Nonlinearity as Rank: Generative Low-Rank Adapter with Radial Basis Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05709)
*Yihao Ouyang,Shiwei Li,Haozhao Wang,Xiandi Luo,Zhuoqi Hu,Yuetong Song,Qiyu Qin,Yichen Li,Ruixuan Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GenLoRA replaces explicit low-rank basis vectors with a lightweight nonlinear generator using per-matrix latent vectors and radial basis functions, enabling higher effective ranks at smaller parameter costs and improved fine-tuning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA''s explicit-rank paradigm causes parameter growth as capacity increases; observed redundancy in basis vectors suggests nonlinear synthesis can compress the basis without harming expressiveness.

Method: For each low-rank update, maintain a latent vector; synthesize basis vectors via a set of lightweight radial basis functions (RBFs) to generate the columns/rows; fewer parameters than explicit basis; integrate into LoRA update; experiments across datasets/architectures.

Result: GenLoRA achieves higher effective LoRA ranks under tight parameter budgets and yields superior fine-tuning performance across multiple setups; code available at provided URL.

Conclusion: Nonlinear basis generation with GenLoRA improves parameter efficiency and performance, suggesting a general approach to compress low-rank adapters via learned nonlinear synthesis.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) approximates the update of a pretrained weight matrix using the product of two low-rank matrices. However, standard LoRA follows an explicit-rank paradigm, where increasing model capacity requires adding more rows or columns (i.e., basis vectors) to the low-rank matrices, leading to substantial parameter growth. In this paper, we find that these basis vectors exhibit significant parameter redundancy and can be compactly represented by lightweight nonlinear functions. Therefore, we propose Generative Low-Rank Adapter (GenLoRA), which replaces explicit basis vector storage with nonlinear basis vector generation. Specifically, GenLoRA maintains a latent vector for each low-rank matrix and employs a set of lightweight radial basis functions (RBFs) to synthesize the basis vectors. Each RBF requires far fewer parameters than an explicit basis vector, enabling higher parameter efficiency in GenLoRA. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and architectures show that GenLoRA attains higher effective LoRA ranks under smaller parameter budgets, resulting in superior fine-tuning performance. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GenLoRA-1519.

</details>


### [297] [Anchored Policy Optimization: Mitigating Exploration Collapse Via Support-Constrained Rectification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05717)
*Tianyi Wang,Long Li,Hongcan Guo,Yibiao Chen,Yixia Li,Yong Wang,Yun Chen,Guanhua Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Anchored Policy Optimization (APO) mitigates Recursive Space Contraction (RSC) in RL with verifiable rewards by replacing global KL Shape Matching with a Safe Manifold–based Support Coverage objective, enabling gradient-aligned sharpening, Elastic Recovery, and improved Pass@1 with restored Pass@K diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RSC arises from the combination of positive sharpening and negative squeezing in RFVR leading to collapse of valid alternative space; KL regularization enforces Shape Matching, creating gradient conflicts and hindering efficient exploration. A method that preserves high-confidence support while permitting sharpening is needed.

Method: Define a Safe Manifold based on the reference model's high-confidence support. Optimize for maximal support coverage (instead of matching the full density). Allow aggressive sharpening for efficiency; introduce a selective restorative force during error correction to avoid collapse. Provide a theoretical gradient-alignment analysis showing APO maximizes support coverage; enable Elastic Recovery to re-inflate valid branches.

Result: The paper provides a gradient-aligned objective for support coverage and a mechanism (Elastic Recovery) to re-inflate valid branches. Empirically, on mathematical benchmarks, APO improves Pass@1 and restores Pass@K diversity, breaking the typical accuracy–diversity trade-off seen with standard policy gradient methods.

Conclusion: APO offers a robust alternative to KL-based Shape Matching in RLVR by focusing on safe support coverage, enabling efficient sharpening without collapse and preserving diversity, with strong theoretical and empirical support.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is increasingly viewed as a tree pruning mechanism. However, we identify a systemic pathology termed Recursive Space Contraction (RSC), an irreversible collapse driven by the combined dynamics of positive sharpening and negative squeezing, where the sampling probability of valid alternatives vanishes. While Kullback-Leibler (KL) regularization aims to mitigate this, it imposes a rigid Shape Matching constraint that forces the policy to mimic the reference model's full density, creating a gradient conflict with the sharpening required for correctness. We propose Anchored Policy Optimization (APO), shifting the paradigm from global Shape Matching to Support Coverage. By defining a Safe Manifold based on the reference model's high-confidence support, APO permits aggressive sharpening for efficiency while selectively invoking a restorative force during error correction to prevent collapse. We theoretically derive that APO serves as a gradient-aligned mechanism to maximize support coverage, enabling an Elastic Recovery that re-inflates valid branches. Empirical evaluations on mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that APO breaks the accuracy-diversity trade-off, significantly improving Pass@1 while restoring the Pass@K diversity typically lost by standard policy gradient methods.

</details>


### [298] [Mitigating Hallucination in Financial Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Fine-Grained Knowledge Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05723)
*Taoye Yin,Haoyuan Hu,Yaxin Fan,Xinhao Chen,Xinya Wu,Kai Deng,Kezun Zhang,Feng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes RL with Fine-grained Knowledge Verification (RLFKV) to align finance RAG outputs with retrieved docs by decomposing outputs into atomic knowledge units and using a fine-grained faithful reward plus an informativeness reward; shows improvements on FDD tasks including a new FDD-ANT dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address hallucinations and misalignment in finance RAG by precise knowledge verification and incentive design.

Method: Decompose outputs into atomic knowledge units; assess correctness per unit; compute a fine-grained faithful reward; add an informativeness reward to prevent reward hacking; apply an RL framework to optimize generation.

Result: Consistent improvements on the public FDD task and the newly proposed FDD-ANT dataset, indicating improved fidelity and alignment with retrieved documents.

Conclusion: Fine-grained verification with RL improves fidelity and preserves informativeness, with positive results on finance-specific datasets; supports domain-specific RAG alignment and suggests potential generalization to other knowledge-intensive domains.

Abstract: In financial Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, models frequently rely on retrieved documents to generate accurate responses due to the time-sensitive nature of the financial domain. While retrieved documents help address knowledge gaps, model-generated responses still suffer from hallucinations that contradict the retrieved information. To mitigate this inconsistency, we propose a Reinforcement Learning framework enhanced with Fine-grained Knowledge Verification (RLFKV). Our method decomposes financial responses into atomic knowledge units and assesses the correctness of each unit to compute the fine-grained faithful reward. This reward offers more precise optimization signals, thereby improving alignment with the retrieved documents. Additionally, to prevent reward hacking (e.g., overly concise replies), we incorporate an informativeness reward that encourages the policy model to retain at least as many knowledge units as the base model. Experiments conducted on the public Financial Data Description (FDD) task and our newly proposed FDD-ANT dataset demonstrate consistent improvements, confirming the effectiveness of our approach.

</details>


### [299] [LeakBoost: Perceptual-Loss-Based Membership Inference Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05748)
*Amit Kravchik Taub,Fred M. Grabovski,Guy Amit,Yisroel Mirsky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LeakBoost uses a perceptual-loss objective to synthesize an interrogation image that amplifies activation-space differences between training members and non-members, enabling a fixed detector to uncover hidden membership signals; yields large gains in AUC and TPR@1%FPR across datasets, and highlights deeper layers and gradient-based detectors as the main leakage sources; modular and efficient for white-box privacy risk assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MIAs rely on static indicators and under-exploit the dynamic behavior of models under probing. There is a need to actively interrogate internal representations to reveal hidden membership signals and better quantify privacy risks in practical settings.

Method: For a candidate input, synthesize an interrogation image by optimizing a perceptual (activation-space) objective to maximize representational differences between members and non-members. The synthesized image is evaluated by an off-the-shelf membership detector without modifying the detector. The framework can augment existing MIAs.

Result: Compared with strong baselines, LeakBoost raises AUC from ~0.53–0.62 to ~0.81–0.88 and increases TPR at 1% FPR by over an order of magnitude. Deeper layers and short, low-learning-rate optimization yield the strongest leakage; improvements concentrate in gradient-based detectors. Validated across multiple image datasets and architectures.

Conclusion: LeakBoost offers a modular, computationally efficient way to assess privacy risks in white-box settings and advances the study of dynamic membership inference by exploiting activation-space interrogation.

Abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) aim to determine whether a sample was part of a model's training set, posing serious privacy risks for modern machine-learning systems. Existing MIAs primarily rely on static indicators, such as loss or confidence, and do not fully leverage the dynamic behavior of models when actively probed. We propose LeakBoost, a perceptual-loss-based interrogation framework that actively probes a model's internal representations to expose hidden membership signals. Given a candidate input, LeakBoost synthesizes an interrogation image by optimizing a perceptual (activation-space) objective, amplifying representational differences between members and non-members. This image is then analyzed by an off-the-shelf membership detector, without modifying the detector itself. When combined with existing membership inference methods, LeakBoost achieves substantial improvements at low false-positive rates across multiple image classification datasets and diverse neural network architectures. In particular, it raises AUC from near-chance levels (0.53-0.62) to 0.81-0.88, and increases TPR at 1 percent FPR by over an order of magnitude compared to strong baseline attacks. A detailed sensitivity analysis reveals that deeper layers and short, low-learning-rate optimization produce the strongest leakage, and that improvements concentrate in gradient-based detectors. LeakBoost thus offers a modular and computationally efficient way to assess privacy risks in white-box settings, advancing the study of dynamic membership inference.

</details>


### [300] [RocqSmith: Can Automatic Optimization Forge Better Proof Agents?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05762)
*Andrei Kozyrev,Nikita Khramov,Denis Lochmelis,Valerio Morelli,Gleb Solovev,Anton Podkopaev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Few-shot bootstrapping is the most reliable automatic optimizer for tuning an automated Rocq proof-generation agent, but all tested optimizers fall short of a carefully engineered state-of-the-art agent; simple optimization helps but cannot replace expert engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether automatic AI agent optimization can meaningfully tune formal verification agents and automate aspects like prompt design and control strategies.

Method: Apply several automatic agent optimizers to tune a Rocq proof-generation agent; compare against baselines and a manually engineered state-of-the-art agent.

Result: Several optimizers yield measurable improvements; few-shot bootstrapping is the most consistently effective; none match the performance of the carefully engineered state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: Automated optimizers provide gains but cannot match expert-tuned agents; further work needed to close the gap, possibly by integrating human knowledge into automated tuning.

Abstract: This work studies the applicability of automatic AI agent optimization methods to real-world agents in formal verification settings, focusing on automated theorem proving in Rocq as a representative and challenging domain. We evaluate how different automatic agent optimizers perform when applied to the task of optimizing a Rocq proof-generation agent, and assess whether parts of the fine-grained tuning of agentic systems, such as prompt design, contextual knowledge, and control strategies, can be automated. Our results show that while several optimizers yield measurable improvements, simple few-shot bootstrapping is the most consistently effective; however, none of the studied methods matches the performance of a carefully engineered state-of-the-art proof agent.

</details>


### [301] [RL-VLA$^3$: Reinforcement Learning VLA Accelerating via Full Asynchronism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05765)
*Zhong Guan,Haoran Sun,Yongjian Guo,Shuai Di,Xiaodong Bai,Jing Long,Tianyun Zhao,Mingxi Luo,Chen Zhou,Yucheng Guo,Qiming Yang,Wanting Xu,Wen Huang,Yunxuan Ma,Hongke Zhao,Likang Wu,Xiaotie Deng,Xi Xiao,Sheng Wen,Yicheng Gong,Junwu Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A fully asynchronous, multi-level decoupled training framework for Vision-Language-Action models that replaces synchronous RL pipelines, yielding substantial throughput gains and scalable performance on large GPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synchronous RL pipelines underutilize resources and limit throughput during environment interaction, rollout generation, and model updates; existing RLinf frameworks improve generalization but remain bottlenecked by synchronization.

Method: Design a three-layer asynchronous pipeline with: 1) asynchronous environment interaction and trajectory collection, 2) streaming policy generation (policy rollout) with streaming execution, 3) decoupled scheduling for training updates—organized in a multi-level architecture inspired by asynchronous optimization in large-model RL. Implemented and evaluated across LIBERO and various VLA models/environments; ablation experiments; scaling tests from 8 to 256 GPUs.

Result: Throughput improvements up to 59.25% over synchronous strategies; up to 126.67% with optimized separation strategies; ablations confirm contribution of each asynchronous component; scaling laws validated across 8-256 GPUs demonstrating strong scalability under most conditions.

Conclusion: The fully-asynchronous framework effectively alleviates pipeline bottlenecks in VLA training, enabling higher throughput and scalable performance, with potential for broader adoption and further investigation into stability and generalization under asynchronous updates.

Abstract: In recent years, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a crucial pathway towards general embodied intelligence, yet their training efficiency has become a key bottleneck. Although existing reinforcement learning (RL)-based training frameworks like RLinf can enhance model generalization, they still rely on synchronous execution, leading to severe resource underutilization and throughput limitations during environment interaction, policy generation (rollout), and model update phases (actor). To overcome this challenge, this paper, for the first time, proposes and implements a fully-asynchronous policy training framework encompassing the entire pipeline from environment interaction, rollout generation, to actor policy updates. Systematically drawing inspiration from asynchronous optimization ideas in large model RL, our framework designs a multi-level decoupled architecture. This includes asynchronous parallelization of environment interaction and trajectory collection, streaming execution for policy generation, and decoupled scheduling for training updates. We validated the effectiveness of our method across diverse VLA models and environments. On the LIBERO benchmark, the framework achieves throughput improvements of up to 59.25\% compared to existing synchronous strategies. When deeply optimizing separation strategies, throughput can be increased by as much as 126.67\%. We verified the effectiveness of each asynchronous component via ablation studies. Scaling law validation across 8 to 256 GPUs demonstrates our method's excellent scalability under most conditions.

</details>


### [302] [FiMI: A Domain-Specific Language Model for Indian Finance Ecosystem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05794)
*Aboli Kathar,Aman Kumar,Anusha Kamath,Araveeti Srujan,Ashish Sharma,Chandra Bhushan,Dilip Asbe,Divya Sorate,Duddu Prasanth Kumar,Evan Acharya,Harsh Sharma,Hrithik Kadam,Kanishk Singla,Keyur Doshi,Kiran Praveen,Kolisetty Krishna SK,Krishanu Adhikary,Lokesh MPT,Mayurdeep Sonowal,Nadeem Shaikh,Navya Prakash,Nimit Kothari,Nitin Kukreja,Prashant Devadiga,Rakesh Paul,Ratanjeet Pratap Chauhan,Raunak Kalani,Raviraj Joshi,Shamanth MH,Shantanu Pandey,Shubham Soni,Siddharth Dixit,Smriti Jopat,Sunil Patel,Suraj Singh,Suvradip Paul,Tulasi Pilla,Utkarsh Vaidya,Vineeth Nambiar,Vishal Kanvaty,Yatharth Dedhia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FiMI is a domain-specific Indian finance LLM built on Mistral Small 24B with 68B-token multilingual pretraining, yielding substantial gains in finance reasoning and tool-calling while preserving general performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Create a domain-specialized financial LLM tailored for Indian digital payment systems, enabling multilingual support and robust tool-driven workflows.

Method: Multi-stage training: (1) continuous pre-training on 68B tokens of curated financial, multilingual (English, Hindi, Hinglish), and synthetic data on Mistral Small 24B; (2) instruction fine-tuning; (3) domain-specific supervised fine-tuning targeting multi-turn, tool-driven conversations (e.g., transaction disputes, mandate lifecycle).

Result: FiMI Base improves finance reasoning by 20% over Mistral Small 24B Base on a finance reasoning benchmark. FiMI Instruct improves domain-specific tool-calling by 87% over Mistral Small 24B Instruct. Both variants retain comparable general benchmark performance to similarly-sized models.

Conclusion: FiMI delivers strong domain specialization for Indian finance tasks with marked gains in finance reasoning and tool usage, without sacrificing general performance, highlighting the effectiveness of multilingual, curated data and tool-focused fine-tuning.

Abstract: We present FiMI (Finance Model for India), a domain-specialized financial language model developed for Indian digital payment systems. We develop two model variants: FiMI Base and FiMI Instruct. FiMI adapts the Mistral Small 24B architecture through a multi-stage training pipeline, beginning with continuous pre-training on 68 Billion tokens of curated financial, multilingual (English, Hindi, Hinglish), and synthetic data. This is followed by instruction fine-tuning and domain-specific supervised fine-tuning focused on multi-turn, tool-driven conversations that model real-world workflows, such as transaction disputes and mandate lifecycle management. Evaluations reveal that FiMI Base achieves a 20% improvement over the Mistral Small 24B Base model on finance reasoning benchmark, while FiMI Instruct outperforms the Mistral Small 24B Instruct model by 87% on domain-specific tool-calling. Moreover, FiMI achieves these significant domain gains while maintaining comparable performance to models of similar size on general benchmarks.

</details>


### [303] [NEX: Neuron Explore-Exploit Scoring for Label-Free Chain-of-Thought Selection and Model Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05805)
*Kang Chen,Zhuoka Feng,Sihan Zhao,Kai Xiong,Junjie Nian,Yaoning Wang,Changyi Xiao,Yixin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NEX is a white-box, unsupervised scoring framework for LLM reasoning that detects exploration-exploitation phases in neuron activations, ranks responses with a single Good-Mass Fraction score, and predicts downstream accuracy without task labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the shift of bottlenecks from generation to selection in LLMs, particularly the lack of supervision on the target distribution, and the need for interpretable, label-free guidance for ranking and variant selection.

Method: Monitor sparse MLP activations per token to detect E-phase spikes, apply a sticky two-state HMM to infer E vs X phases, credit neurons activated during E by their reuse in X, derive interpretable neuron weights, and compute a single Good-Mass Fraction score to rank candidate responses and merged variants without labeled data.

Result: NEX, evaluated on reasoning benchmarks and Qwen3 merge families, predicts downstream accuracy from a small unlabeled activation set and identifies superior variants. Human annotations validate the E-X signals, and causal evidence is provided via Effective-vs-Redundant neuron transfer.

Conclusion: NEX provides an interpretable, label-free scoring mechanism that can predict model performance and guide variant selection by separating exploration from exploitation in neural reasoning, with empirical validation and causal grounding.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly spend inference compute sampling multiple chain-of-thought traces or searching over merged checkpoints. This shifts the bottleneck from generation to selection, often without supervision on the target distribution. We show entropy-based exploration proxies follow an inverted-U with accuracy, suggesting extra exploration can become redundant and induce overthinking. We propose NEX, a white-box label-free unsupervised scoring framework that views reasoning as alternating E-phase (exploration) and X-phase (exploitation). NEX detects E-phase as spikes in newly activated MLP neurons per token from sparse activation caches, then uses a sticky two-state HMM to infer E-X phases and credits E-introduced neurons by whether they are reused in the following X span. These signals yield interpretable neuron weights and a single Good-Mass Fraction score to rank candidate responses and merged variants without task answers. Across reasoning benchmarks and Qwen3 merge families, NEX computed on a small unlabeled activation set predicts downstream accuracy and identifies better variants; we further validate the E-X signal with human annotations and provide causal evidence via "Effective-vs-Redundant" neuron transfer.

</details>


### [304] [STProtein: predicting spatial protein expression from multi-omics data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05811)
*Zhaorui Jiang,Yingfang Yuan,Lei Hu,Wei Pang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STProtein uses graph neural networks and multi-task learning to predict spatial protein expression from spatial transcriptomics, addressing scarcity of spatial proteomics and enabling integrated spatial multi-omics analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The major bottleneck is the imbalance between abundant spatial transcriptomics data and scarce spatial proteomics data due to technical and cost constraints; predicting proteins from transcripts can accelerate multi-omics integration.

Method: A graph neural network–based framework (STProtein) with a multi-task learning strategy that learns to infer unknown spatial protein expressions from available spatial multi-omics data, leveraging spatial and cross-modality relationships across tissue spots.

Result: The abstract presents a conceptual framework with no reported empirical results; it outlines the approach and potential benefits but does not provide validation or performance metrics.

Conclusion: STProtein is proposed to accelerate discovery by revealing spatial protein patterns, uncovering relationships between marker genes, and enabling exploration of biological 'Dark Matter' in tissues.

Abstract: The integration of spatial multi-omics data from single tissues is crucial for advancing biological research. However, a significant data imbalance impedes progress: while spatial transcriptomics data is relatively abundant, spatial proteomics data remains scarce due to technical limitations and high costs. To overcome this challenge we propose STProtein, a novel framework leveraging graph neural networks with multi-task learning strategy. STProtein is designed to accurately predict unknown spatial protein expression using more accessible spatial multi-omics data, such as spatial transcriptomics. We believe that STProtein can effectively addresses the scarcity of spatial proteomics, accelerating the integration of spatial multi-omics and potentially catalyzing transformative breakthroughs in life sciences. This tool enables scientists to accelerate discovery by identifying complex and previously hidden spatial patterns of proteins within tissues, uncovering novel relationships between different marker genes, and exploring the biological "Dark Matter".

</details>


### [305] [TKG-Thinker: Towards Dynamic Reasoning over Temporal Knowledge Graphs via Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05818)
*Zihao Jiang,Miao Peng,Zhenyan Shan,Wenjie Xu,Ben Liu,Gong Chen,Ziqi Gao,Min Peng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TKG-Thinker introduces an autonomous planning agent with SFT and RL to enable dynamic, multi-turn reasoning over temporal knowledge graphs for QA, achieving state-of-the-art results and better generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation: address reasoning hallucinations and lack of adaptability in static LLM prompting for temporal KGQA; need dynamic interaction with TKG environments and optimized planning.

Method: Method: build TKG-Thinker with autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval; dual-training: supervised fine-tuning with chain-of-thought data to seed planning, then reinforcement learning with multi-dimensional rewards to refine policies under temporal constraints; perform dynamic multi-turn interactions with TKGs; evaluate across datasets with 3 open-source LLMs.

Result: Results: achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets; strong generalization across complex TKGQA settings; demonstrated across three open-source LLMs.

Conclusion: Conclusion: integrating autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval via SFT+RL enables robust temporal reasoning over KGs, surpassing static prompting baselines and improving generalization; suggests broader applicability to KGQA tasks and potential for further RL-based optimization.

Abstract: Temporal knowledge graph question answering (TKGQA) aims to answer time-sensitive questions by leveraging temporal knowledge bases. While Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate significant potential in TKGQA, current prompting strategies constrain their efficacy in two primary ways. First, they are prone to reasoning hallucinations under complex temporal constraints. Second, static prompting limits model autonomy and generalization, as it lack optimization through dynamic interaction with temporal knowledge graphs (TKGs) environments. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{TKG-Thinker}, a novel agent equipped with autonomous planning and adaptive retrieval capabilities for reasoning over TKGs. Specifically, TKG-Thinker performs in-depth temporal reasoning through dynamic multi-turn interactions with TKGs via a dual-training strategy. We first apply Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with chain-of thought data to instill core planning capabilities, followed by a Reinforcement Learning (RL) stage that leverages multi-dimensional rewards to refine reasoning policies under intricate temporal constraints. Experimental results on benchmark datasets with three open-source LLMs show that TKG-Thinker achieves state-of-the-art performance and exhibits strong generalization across complex TKGQA settings.

</details>


### [306] [Learning Compact Boolean Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05830)
*Shengpu Wang,Yuhao Mao,Yani Zhang,Martin Vechev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes three-pronged approach to learning compact, accurate Boolean networks for vision tasks: parameter-free connection learning, locality-aware Boolean convolutions, and adaptive discretization for binarization, yielding up to 37x fewer Boolean ops while improving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for energy-efficient, fast inference; floating-point nets are costly; Boolean networks offer gains but are hard to train due to combinatorial search; aim to improve accuracy and efficiency on standard vision benchmarks.

Method: 1) learn efficient connections without extra parameters; 2) redesign Boolean convolution to exploit locality and reduce operations; 3) adaptive discretization to minimize accuracy loss during conversion from continuous to Boolean; evaluation on vision benchmarks.

Result: Pareto frontier of accuracy vs. computation surpasses prior SOTA; better accuracy with up to 37x fewer Boolean operations.

Conclusion: Integrating parameter-free connections, locality-aware Boolean convolutions, and adaptive discretization yields substantial efficiency gains and accuracy, enabling resource-constrained deployment of Boolean networks in vision tasks.

Abstract: Floating-point neural networks dominate modern machine learning but incur substantial inference cost, motivating interest in Boolean networks for resource-constrained settings. However, learning compact and accurate Boolean networks is challenging due to their combinatorial nature. In this work, we address this challenge from three different angles: learned connections, compact convolutions and adaptive discretization. First, we propose a novel strategy to learn efficient connections with no additional parameters and negligible computational overhead. Second, we introduce a novel convolutional Boolean architecture that exploits the locality with reduced number of Boolean operations than existing methods. Third, we propose an adaptive discretization strategy to reduce the accuracy drop when converting a continuous-valued network into a Boolean one. Extensive results on standard vision benchmarks demonstrate that the Pareto front of accuracy vs. computation of our method significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art, achieving better accuracy with up to 37x fewer Boolean operations.

</details>


### [307] [OmniVideo-R1: Reinforcing Audio-visual Reasoning with Query Intention and Modality Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05847)
*Zhangquan Chen,Jiale Tao,Ruihuang Li,Yihao Hu,Ruitao Chen,Zhantao Yang,Xinlei Yu,Haodong Jing,Manyuan Zhang,Shuai Shao,Biao Wang,Qinglin Lu,Ruqi Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OmniVideo-R1 introduces a reinforced omnivideo model with query-intensive grounding and modality-attentive fusion to improve cross-modal reasoning, achieving strong gains on audio-visual benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing omnivideo models struggle with robust audio-visual understanding and mixed-modality reasoning; a unified framework that leverages omnimodal cues is needed.

Method: Propose OmniVideo-R1, a reinforced framework employing two strategies: (1) query-intensive grounding based on self-supervised learning to align queries with omnimodal cues, and (2) modality-attentive fusion using contrastive learning to fuse modalities effectively.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks show consistent outperforming of strong baselines, with robust generalization capabilities across tasks.

Conclusion: OmniVideo-R1 demonstrates that leveraging self-supervised grounding and contrastive modality fusion improves mixed-modality reasoning in omnivideo models, offering a scalable path to better audio-visual understanding.

Abstract: While humans perceive the world through diverse modalities that operate synergistically to support a holistic understanding of their surroundings, existing omnivideo models still face substantial challenges on audio-visual understanding tasks. In this paper, we propose OmniVideo-R1, a novel reinforced framework that improves mixed-modality reasoning. OmniVideo-R1 empowers models to "think with omnimodal cues" by two key strategies: (1) query-intensive grounding based on self-supervised learning paradigms; and (2) modality-attentive fusion built upon contrastive learning paradigms. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that OmniVideo-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines, highlighting its effectiveness and robust generalization capabilities.

</details>


### [308] [BABE: Biology Arena BEnchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05857)
*Junting Zhou,Jin Chen,Linfeng Hao,Denghui Cao,Zheyu Wang,Qiguang Chen,Chaoyou Fu,Jiaze Chen,Yuchen Wu,Ge Zhang,Mingxuan Wang,Wenhao Huang,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BABE is a new benchmark for biological experimental reasoning, built from peer‑reviewed papers and real‑world studies to test causal and cross‑scale reasoning in AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing biology benchmarks inadequately assess the integration of experimental results with contextual scientific knowledge; there is a need to evaluate AI systems on authentic experimental reasoning akin to practicing biologists.

Method: Constructed from peer‑reviewed research papers and real‑world biological studies; tasks are designed to require causal reasoning and cross‑scale inference, reflecting the interdisciplinary nature of actual scientific inquiry.

Result: Introduces the BABE benchmark and its framework for evaluating experimental reasoning in biology; the abstract presents the benchmark design and intended use but does not report empirical results.

Conclusion: BABE offers an authentic metric to gauge AI systems’ ability to reason like practicing scientists in biology, by challenging them with cross‑scale, causal inference tasks derived from real biology literature.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of large language models (LLMs) has expanded their capabilities from basic dialogue to advanced scientific reasoning. However, existing benchmarks in biology often fail to assess a critical skill required of researchers: the ability to integrate experimental results with contextual knowledge to derive meaningful conclusions. To address this gap, we introduce BABE(Biology Arena BEnchmark), a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the experimental reasoning capabilities of biological AI systems. BABE is uniquely constructed from peer-reviewed research papers and real-world biological studies, ensuring that tasks reflect the complexity and interdisciplinary nature of actual scientific inquiry. BABE challenges models to perform causal reasoning and cross-scale inference. Our benchmark provides a robust framework for assessing how well AI systems can reason like practicing scientists, offering a more authentic measure of their potential to contribute to biological research.

</details>


### [309] [Beyond Manual Planning: Seating Allocation for Large Organizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05875)
*Anton Ipsen,Michael Cashmore,Kirsty Fielding,Nicolas Marchesotti,Parisa Zehtabi,Daniele Magazzeni,Manuela Veloso*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes HSAP and an end-to-end framework using PRM/RRT to compute seat distances; solves HSAP via heuristic search, DP, and integer programming; evaluated on varying instance sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large organizations with hierarchies need closeness of related teams; manual seating planning is inefficient and hard to replan; automation can improve proximity and scalability.

Method: Compute seat-to-seat distances with probabilistic road map (PRM) and rapidly-exploring random trees (RRT); combine with heuristic search and dynamic programming; formulate HSAP as an integer programming problem; evaluate on different instance sizes.

Result: Demonstrates scalability and feasibility of the PRM framework and allocations; results evaluated both quantitatively and qualitatively across instance sizes.

Conclusion: End-to-end framework enabling scalable, automated HSAP solutions; reduces manual replanning and adapts to various floor plans; future work could extend to dynamic updates and multi-floor planning.

Abstract: We introduce the Hierarchical Seating Allocation Problem (HSAP) which addresses the optimal assignment of hierarchically structured organizational teams to physical seating arrangements on a floor plan. This problem is driven by the necessity for large organizations with large hierarchies to ensure that teams with close hierarchical relationships are seated in proximity to one another, such as ensuring a research group occupies a contiguous area. Currently, this problem is managed manually leading to infrequent and suboptimal replanning efforts. To alleviate this manual process, we propose an end-to-end framework to solve the HSAP. A scalable approach to calculate the distance between any pair of seats using a probabilistic road map (PRM) and rapidly-exploring random trees (RRT) which is combined with heuristic search and dynamic programming approach to solve the HSAP using integer programming. We demonstrate our approach under different sized instances by evaluating the PRM framework and subsequent allocations both quantitatively and qualitatively.

</details>


### [310] [Agent2Agent Threats in Safety-Critical LLM Assistants: A Human-Centric Taxonomy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05877)
*Lukas Stappen,Ahmet Erkan Turan,Johann Hagerer,Georg Groh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces AgentHeLLM threat modeling framework for LLM-based vehicle agents, separating asset identification from attack-path analysis, with a victim-model-driven asset taxonomy and a graph model distinguishing poison vs trigger paths; backed by an open-source Attack Path Generator tool using bi-level search to automate threat discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the methodological gap in safety-critical system security where assets and attack paths are entangled, applying rigorous threat modeling to LLM-enabled automotive agents communicating with external services (e.g., via A2A), to mitigate risks from data poisoning and inappropriate activations.

Method: Develop Asset taxonomy via harm-oriented victim modeling inspired by the Universal Declaration of Human Rights; introduce a formal graph-based model distinguishing poison paths (malicious data propagation) from trigger paths (activation actions); implement AgentHeLLM Attack Path Generator that uses a bi-level search to automate multi-stage threat discovery; demonstrate practical applicability with an open-source tool.

Result: Framework and tool are presented; demonstration of practical applicability through multi-stage threat discovery; open-source release.

Conclusion: Provides a separation-of-concerns threat modeling approach for LLM-enabled automotive agents, enabling automated threat discovery and informing mitigations.

Abstract: The integration of Large Language Model (LLM)-based conversational agents into vehicles creates novel security challenges at the intersection of agentic AI, automotive safety, and inter-agent communication. As these intelligent assistants coordinate with external services via protocols such as Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A), they establish attack surfaces where manipulations can propagate through natural language payloads, potentially causing severe consequences ranging from driver distraction to unauthorized vehicle control. Existing AI security frameworks, while foundational, lack the rigorous "separation of concerns" standard in safety-critical systems engineering by co-mingling the concepts of what is being protected (assets) with how it is attacked (attack paths). This paper addresses this methodological gap by proposing a threat modeling framework called AgentHeLLM (Agent Hazard Exploration for LLM Assistants) that formally separates asset identification from attack path analysis. We introduce a human-centric asset taxonomy derived from harm-oriented "victim modeling" and inspired by the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, and a formal graph-based model that distinguishes poison paths (malicious data propagation) from trigger paths (activation actions). We demonstrate the framework's practical applicability through an open-source attack path suggestion tool AgentHeLLM Attack Path Generator that automates multi-stage threat discovery using a bi-level search strategy.

</details>


### [311] [A Guide to Large Language Models in Modeling and Simulation: From Core Techniques to Critical Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05883)
*Philippe J. Giabbanelli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Practical guidelines for using large language models in Modeling & Simulation (M&S), addressing common pitfalls and offering a framework for principled design, diagnostics, and empirical evaluation to decide when, how, and whether to rely on LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid adoption of LLMs in M&S introduces subtle issues (e.g., backfire from more data, unnecessary fine-tuning, non-determinism, information loss from naive data simplification). There is a need for structured guidance to help modelers manage knowledge augmentation, data decomposition, and hyper-parameter settings.

Method: A synthesis/perspective that identifies sources of confusion and provides guidance through principled design choices, diagnostic strategies, and empirical evaluation. Topics include non-determinism, knowledge augmentation (including RAG and LoRA), decomposition of M&S data, and hyper-parameter settings, delivered as a framework or checklist for practitioners.

Result: Delivers actionable guidance and a diagnostic/decision framework to improve robustness and efficiency of LLM use in M&S. It clarifies trade-offs, outlines evaluation approaches, and offers concrete guidelines for when and how to employ LLMs.

Conclusion: With principled design, rigorous diagnostics, and empirical evaluation, practitioners can make informed decisions about LLM use in M&S, mitigating pitfalls and leveraging benefits safely and effectively.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have rapidly become familiar tools to researchers and practitioners. Concepts such as prompting, temperature, or few-shot examples are now widely recognized, and LLMs are increasingly used in Modeling & Simulation (M&S) workflows. However, practices that appear straightforward may introduce subtle issues, unnecessary complexity, or may even lead to inferior results. Adding more data can backfire (e.g., deteriorating performance through model collapse or inadvertently wiping out existing guardrails), spending time on fine-tuning a model can be unnecessary without a prior assessment of what it already knows, setting the temperature to 0 is not sufficient to make LLMs deterministic, providing a large volume of M&S data as input can be excessive (LLMs cannot attend to everything) but naive simplifications can lose information. We aim to provide comprehensive and practical guidance on how to use LLMs, with an emphasis on M&S applications. We discuss common sources of confusion, including non-determinism, knowledge augmentation (including RAG and LoRA), decomposition of M&S data, and hyper-parameter settings. We emphasize principled design choices, diagnostic strategies, and empirical evaluation, with the goal of helping modelers make informed decisions about when, how, and whether to rely on LLMs.

</details>


### [312] [Quantum Reinforcement Learning with Transformers for the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05920)
*Eva Andrés*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Quantum-enhanced and hybrid quantum-classical RL (A2C with transformers) applied to CVRP; hybrid model achieves the best overall performance, outperforming classical baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CVRP is a hard combinatorial optimization problem. Investigating quantum RL with attention-based architectures may yield more structured routing policies and robustness in multi-vehicle, capacity-constrained settings.

Method: Three A2C variants (classical, full quantum, hybrid) using transformer-based encoders with self- and cross-attention to model depot, vehicles, and clients. Experiments on CVRP with 20 clients and 4 vehicles, across 10 runs. Evaluation via routing distance, route compactness, and route overlap; qualitative visualizations included.

Result: All three variants learn effective routing policies. Quantum-enhanced models outperform the classical baseline, with the hybrid quantum-classical architecture delivering the best performance across distance, compactness, and route overlap. Qualitative analyses indicate more structured and coherent routing under quantum methods.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the potential of hybrid quantum-classical reinforcement learning for CVRP and related combinatorial optimization problems. Further work should explore scalability to larger instances, hardware noise, and broader VRP variants, as well as robustness of learned policies.

Abstract: This paper addresses the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) by comparing classical and quantum Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches. An Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) agent is implemented in classical, full quantum, and hybrid variants, integrating transformer architectures to capture the relationships between vehicles, clients, and the depot through self- and cross-attention mechanisms. The experiments focus on multi-vehicle scenarios with capacity constraints, considering 20 clients and 4 vehicles, and are conducted over ten independent runs. Performance is assessed using routing distance, route compactness, and route overlap. The results show that all three approaches are capable of learning effective routing policies. However, quantum-enhanced models outperform the classical baseline and produce more robust route organization, with the hybrid architecture achieving the best overall performance across distance, compactness, and route overlap. In addition to quantitative improvements, qualitative visualizations reveal that quantum-based models generate more structured and coherent routing solutions. These findings highlight the potential of hybrid quantum-classical reinforcement learning models for addressing complex combinatorial optimization problems such as the CVRP.

</details>


### [313] [Geographically-aware Transformer-based Traffic Forecasting for Urban Motorway Digital Twins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05983)
*Krešimir Kušić,Vinny Cahill,Ivana Dusparic*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Geographically-aware Transformer-based Traffic Forecasting (GATTF) uses mutual information to leverage sensor geography for motorway traffic forecasting, improving accuracy over a standard Transformer without added model complexity, demonstrated on the Geneva motorway network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic systems are spatio-temporal, nonlinear, and require real-time, high-resolution predictions for proactive decision-making in digital twins. Existing sequence models struggle with forecasting accuracy and efficiently utilizing geographical sensor relationships.

Method: Proposes GATTF, a Transformer-based forecasting model augmented with geographical awareness derived from mutual information (MI) between distributed sensors to define relationships and weighting in the model, evaluated on real-time Geneva motorway data.

Result: Empirical results show that incorporating geographical awareness through MI improves forecasting accuracy compared to a standard Transformer, without increasing model complexity.

Conclusion: MI-informed geographical relationships enhance Transformer-based motorway traffic forecasting, supporting more effective digital-twin traffic management and suggesting potential applicability to other spatio-temporal forecasting tasks.

Abstract: The operational effectiveness of digital-twin technology in motorway traffic management depends on the availability of a continuous flow of high-resolution real-time traffic data. To function as a proactive decision-making support layer within traffic management, a digital twin must also incorporate predicted traffic conditions in addition to real-time observations. Due to the spatio-temporal complexity and the time-variant, non-linear nature of traffic dynamics, predicting motorway traffic remains a difficult problem. Sequence-based deep-learning models offer clear advantages over classical machine learning and statistical models in capturing long-range, temporal dependencies in time-series traffic data, yet limitations in forecasting accuracy and model complexity point to the need for further improvements. To improve motorway traffic forecasting, this paper introduces a Geographically-aware Transformer-based Traffic Forecasting GATTF model, which exploits the geographical relationships between distributed sensors using their mutual information (MI). The model has been evaluated using real-time data from the Geneva motorway network in Switzerland and results confirm that incorporating geographical awareness through MI enhances the accuracy of GATTF forecasting compared to a standard Transformer, without increasing model complexity.

</details>


### [314] [Speech Emotion Recognition Leveraging OpenAI's Whisper Representations and Attentive Pooling Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06000)
*Ali Shendabadi,Parnia Izadirad,Mostafa Salehi,Mahmoud Bijankhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two attention-based pooling modules (Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling) are designed to compress Whisper representations for Speech Emotion Recognition (SER). They yield state-of-the-art results on ShEMO Persian and competitive results on IEMOCAP English, using Whisper Tiny/Small as feature extractors. Intermediate Whisper layers may be more effective for Persian, offering a lightweight alternative to larger models like HuBERT X-Large.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SER suffers from limited large-scale, labeled datasets. Leveraging pre-trained models (Whisper as an ASR backbone) as fixed feature extractors, together with efficient pooling, can improve emotion recognition performance while preserving efficiency across languages.

Method: Replace raw Whisper features with two attention-based pooling schemes: (1) Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling (MH-AAP) and (2) QKV Pooling (Query-Key-Value attention). Extract Whisper representations from Tiny and Small encoders; evaluate across English (IEMOCAP) and Persian (ShEMO); compare different Whisper encoder layers to identify where emotional content is best preserved; use a lightweight classifier on pooled features.

Result: The proposed QKV architecture achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ShEMO Persian dataset with a 2.47 percentage point improvement in unweighted accuracy. Intermediate Whisper encoder layers often provide better SER performance for Persian, highlighting the viability of smaller Whisper configurations as efficient SER backbones compared to larger models like HuBERT X-Large.

Conclusion: Attention-based pooling effectively preserves emotional cues in Whisper features for SER. Whisper, even in compact variants, can serve as a competent representation extractor for cross-linguistic SER, with layer selection and pooling strategy playing crucial roles in performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) research has faced limitations due to the lack of standard and sufficiently large datasets. Recent studies have leveraged pre-trained models to extract features for downstream tasks such as SER. This work explores the capabilities of Whisper, a pre-trained ASR system, in speech emotion recognition by proposing two attention-based pooling methods, Multi-head Attentive Average Pooling and QKV Pooling, designed to efficiently reduce the dimensionality of Whisper representations while preserving emotional features. We experiment on English and Persian, using the IEMOCAP and ShEMO datasets respectively, with Whisper Tiny and Small. Our multi-head QKV architecture achieves state-of-the-art results on the ShEMO dataset, with a 2.47% improvement in unweighted accuracy. We further compare the performance of different Whisper encoder layers and find that intermediate layers often perform better for SER on the Persian dataset, providing a lightweight and efficient alternative to much larger models such as HuBERT X-Large. Our findings highlight the potential of Whisper as a representation extractor for SER and demonstrate the effectiveness of attention-based pooling for dimension reduction.

</details>


### [315] [AgenticPay: A Multi-Agent LLM Negotiation System for Buyer-Seller Transactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06008)
*Xianyang Liu,Shangding Gu,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgenticPay provides a language-driven benchmark and simulation framework for multi-agent buyer-seller negotiations, featuring 110+ tasks, private constraints, and product-dependent valuations with evaluation metrics for feasibility, efficiency, and welfare.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of principled benchmarks for evaluating language-mediated economic interactions among multiple agents and long-horizon strategic negotiation in LLM-based systems.

Method: AgenticPay builds a framework for multi-agent bargaining driven by natural language, including bilateral and many-to-many markets, private constraints and valuations, multi-round negotiation with structured action extraction, and metrics for feasibility, efficiency, and welfare; it supports over 110 tasks and benchmarks various LLMs.

Result: Benchmarking shows substantial gaps in negotiation performance for state-of-the-art LLMs and highlights challenges in long-horizon strategic reasoning, establishing a foundation for language-based market interaction research.

Conclusion: AgenticPay establishes a foundation for studying agentic commerce and language-based market interaction; code and dataset are publicly available for further research.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents are increasingly expected to negotiate, coordinate, and transact autonomously, yet existing benchmarks lack principled settings for evaluating language-mediated economic interaction among multiple agents. We introduce AgenticPay, a benchmark and simulation framework for multi-agent buyer-seller negotiation driven by natural language. AgenticPay models markets in which buyers and sellers possess private constraints and product-dependent valuations, and must reach agreements through multi-round linguistic negotiation rather than numeric bidding alone. The framework supports a diverse suite of over 110 tasks ranging from bilateral bargaining to many-to-many markets, with structured action extraction and metrics for feasibility, efficiency, and welfare. Benchmarking state-of-the-art proprietary and open-weight LLMs reveals substantial gaps in negotiation performance and highlights challenges in long-horizon strategic reasoning, establishing AgenticPay as a foundation for studying agentic commerce and language-based market interaction. Code and dataset are available at the link: https://github.com/SafeRL-Lab/AgenticPay.

</details>


### [316] [DyTopo: Dynamic Topology Routing for Multi-Agent Reasoning via Semantic Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06039)
*Yuxing Lu,Yucheng Hu,Xukai Zhao,Jiuxin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A manager-guided dynamic sparse communication framework (DyTopo) for prompt-driven multi-agent LLMs, constructing per-round directed graphs to route lightweight need/offer messages, yielding improved accuracy and interpretable coordination traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed, trajectory-wide communication patterns are ill-suited for stage-dependent iterative reasoning; adaptive, goal-conditioned routing can enhance performance and transparency in multi-agent LLM setups.

Method: Introduce DyTopo: at each round, a manager defines the round goal; agents emit concise 'need' (query) and 'offer' (key) descriptors; embed and semantically match these descriptors to induce a sparse directed graph; route private messages along the induced edges; reconstruct the graph every round; evaluate across code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks with four LLM backbones.

Result: Empirical gains of avg +6.2 accuracy over the strongest baseline, consistent across code generation and math benchmarks and multiple backbones; provides an interpretable coordination trace showing how communication pathways reconfigure over rounds.

Conclusion: Dynamic, manager-guided sparse communication graphs improve multi-agent performance and offer transparent insight into communication dynamics, with potential applicability across diverse reasoning tasks and backbones.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems built from prompted large language models can improve multi-round reasoning, yet most existing pipelines rely on fixed, trajectory-wide communication patterns that are poorly matched to the stage-dependent needs of iterative problem solving. We introduce DyTopo, a manager-guided multi-agent framework that reconstructs a sparse directed communication graph at each round. Conditioned on the manager's round goal, each agent outputs lightweight natural-language query (need) and \key (offer) descriptors; DyTopo embeds these descriptors and performs semantic matching, routing private messages only along the induced edges. Across code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks and four LLM backbones, DyTopo consistently outperforms over the strongest baseline (avg. +6.2). Beyond accuracy, DyTopo yields an interpretable coordination trace via the evolving graphs, enabling qualitative inspection of how communication pathways reconfigure across rounds.

</details>


### [317] [M$^2$-Miner: Multi-Agent Enhanced MCTS for Mobile GUI Agent Data Mining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05429)
*Rui Lv,Juncheng Mo,Tianyi Chu,Chen Rao,Hongyi Jing,Jiajie Teng,Jiafu Chen,Shiqi Zhang,Liangzi Ding,Shuo Fang,Huaizhong Lin,Ziqiang Dang,Chenguang Ma,Lei Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: M^2-Miner: a low-cost, automated mobile GUI data-mining framework using Monte Carlo Tree Search with a collaborative multi-agent setup (InferAgent, OrchestraAgent, JudgeAgent), including an intent recycling strategy and progressive model-in-the-loop training; achieves state-of-the-art results on mobile GUI benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual annotation of GUI interaction data is costly and yields low data quality and limited diversity. An automated, scalable data-mining framework is needed to produce rich, high-quality intent-trajectory data for training GUI agents.

Method: Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)-based data mining (M^2-Miner) with a collaborative multi-agent system (InferAgent, OrchestraAgent, JudgeAgent) for guidance, acceleration, and evaluation; an intent recycling strategy to harvest additional valuable trajectories; and a progressive model-in-the-loop training regime to improve mining success.

Result: Mining-produced data enable the GUI agent to achieve state-of-the-art performance on several standard mobile GUI benchmarks after fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a cost-efficient, scalable path to generate high-quality, diverse GUI interaction data and improves GUI agent performance, with a community-release planned.

Abstract: Graphical User Interface (GUI) agent is pivotal to advancing intelligent human-computer interaction paradigms. Constructing powerful GUI agents necessitates the large-scale annotation of high-quality user-behavior trajectory data (i.e., intent-trajectory pairs) for training. However, manual annotation methods and current GUI agent data mining approaches typically face three critical challenges: high construction cost, poor data quality, and low data richness. To address these issues, we propose M$^2$-Miner, the first low-cost and automated mobile GUI agent data-mining framework based on Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). For better data mining efficiency and quality, we present a collaborative multi-agent framework, comprising InferAgent, OrchestraAgent, and JudgeAgent for guidance, acceleration, and evaluation. To further enhance the efficiency of mining and enrich intent diversity, we design an intent recycling strategy to extract extra valuable interaction trajectories. Additionally, a progressive model-in-the-loop training strategy is introduced to improve the success rate of data mining. Extensive experiments have demonstrated that the GUI agent fine-tuned using our mined data achieves state-of-the-art performance on several commonly used mobile GUI benchmarks. Our work will be released to facilitate the community research.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [318] [PLATO Hand: Shaping Contact Behavior with Fingernails for Precise Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05156)
*Dong Ho Kang,Aaron Kim,Mingyo Seo,Kazuto Yokoyama,Tetsuya Narita,Luis Sentis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A dexterous robotic hand (PLATO Hand) uses a hybrid fingertip (rigid fingernail in compliant pulp) to tailor contact behavior, enabling robust manipulation across varied shapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address diverse interaction modes and precise manipulation across object geometries by shaping contact, while preserving local indentation and suppressing undesirable bending.

Method: Introduce a strain-energy-based bending-indentation model to guide fingertip design and explain contact mechanics; fabricate a fingertip embedding a rigid fingernail in compliant pulp and validate via experiments.

Result: Improved pinching stability, enhanced force observability, and successful execution of edge-sensitive tasks (paper singulation, card picking, orange peeling).

Conclusion: Coupling structured contact geometry with a force-motion transparent mechanism yields a principled, physically embodied approach for precise manipulation.

Abstract: We present the PLATO Hand, a dexterous robotic hand with a hybrid fingertip that embeds a rigid fingernail within a compliant pulp. This design shapes contact behavior to enable diverse interaction modes across a range of object geometries. We develop a strain-energy-based bending-indentation model to guide the fingertip design and to explain how guided contact preserves local indentation while suppressing global bending. Experimental results show that the proposed robotic hand design demonstrates improved pinching stability, enhanced force observability, and successful execution of edge-sensitive manipulation tasks, including paper singulation, card picking, and orange peeling. Together, these results show that coupling structured contact geometry with a force-motion transparent mechanism provides a principled, physically embodied approach to precise manipulation.

</details>


### [319] [Informative Path Planning with Guaranteed Estimation Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05198)
*Kalvik Jakkala,Saurav Agarwal,Jason O'Kane,Srinivas Akella*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework that guarantees estimation uncertainty in environmental monitoring by combining Gaussian-process-based informative path planning with a mapping from kernel structure to binary coverage, planning near-optimal routes under travel constraints while handling nonstationary processes and obstacles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between traditional geometric coverage (boustrophedon) and informative path planning by ensuring a user-specified uncertainty bound on the GP posterior variance, thereby reducing wasted samples and improving efficiency.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: (i) learn a GP model from prior data; (ii) convert the GP kernel into binary coverage maps indicating where uncertainty can be reduced below the target; (iii) plan a near-shortest route whose combined coverage satisfies the global uncertainty constraint. Handles nonstationary kernels and obstacles; provides approximation guarantees for sensing-location selection and joint selection-routing under a travel budget.

Result: Demonstrates that the planners meet the uncertainty target using fewer sensing locations and shorter travel distances than a baseline on real-world topographic data; field experiments with bathymetry-mapping vehicles show practical feasibility.

Conclusion: The proposed approach yields guaranteed estimation uncertainty with improved efficiency, scalable to nonstationary spatial processes and complex environments, and is validated both in simulation/real data and field experiments.

Abstract: Environmental monitoring robots often need to reconstruct spatial fields (e.g., salinity, temperature, bathymetry) under tight distance and energy constraints. Classical boustrophedon lawnmower surveys provide geometric coverage guarantees but can waste effort by oversampling predictable regions. In contrast, informative path planning (IPP) methods leverage spatial correlations to reduce oversampling, yet typically offer no guarantees on reconstruction quality.
