This paper bridges these approaches by addressing informative path planning with guaranteed estimation uncertainty: computing the shortest path whose measurements ensure that the Gaussian-process (GP) posterior variance -- an intrinsic uncertainty measure that lower-bounds the mean-squared prediction error under the GP model -- falls below a user-specified threshold over the monitoring region.
  We propose a three-stage approach: (i) learn a GP model from available prior information; (ii) transform the learned GP kernel into binary coverage maps for each candidate sensing location, indicating which locations' uncertainty can be reduced below a specified target; and (iii) plan a near-shortest route whose combined coverage satisfies the global uncertainty constraint. To address heterogeneous phenomena, we incorporate a nonstationary kernel that captures spatially varying correlation structure, and we accommodate non-convex environments with obstacles. Algorithmically, we present methods with provable approximation guarantees for sensing-location selection and for the joint selection-and-routing problem under a travel budget. Experiments on real-world topographic data show that our planners meet the uncertainty target using fewer sensing locations and shorter travel distances than a recent baseline, and field experiments with bathymetry-mapping autonomous surface and underwater vehicles demonstrate real-world feasibility.

</details>


### [320] [MobileManiBench: Simplifying Model Verification for Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05233)
*Wenbo Wang,Fangyun Wei,QiXiu Li,Xi Chen,Yaobo Liang,Chang Xu,Jiaolong Yang,Baining Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Sim-first benchmark MobileManiBench for mobile manipulation in Isaac Sim; RL-generated, richly annotated trajectories to benchmark Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models across embodiments and sensing modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome reliance on large teleoperation-collected, static tabletop datasets and validate VLA architectures in a controllable, scalable simulated environment before real-world deployment.

Method: A simulation-first pipeline that autonomously generates diverse manipulation trajectories (300K) using RL in NVIDIA Isaac Sim, with two mobile platforms (parallel-gripper and dexterous-hand), two synchronized cameras, 630 objects across 20 categories, and 5 skills (open, close, pull, push, pick); provides rich annotations (language instructions, multi-view RGB-depth-segmentation, synchronized states and actions).

Result: Benchmarks representative VLA models; yields insights into perception, reasoning, and control in complex simulated settings; establishes a scalable data-generating framework to study data efficiency and generalization.

Conclusion: MobileManiBench enables controlled, scalable studies of robot embodiments and sensing modalities, accelerating VLA research and providing a verification-before-deployment pathway for robotic manipulation

Abstract: Vision-language-action models have advanced robotic manipulation but remain constrained by reliance on the large, teleoperation-collected datasets dominated by the static, tabletop scenes. We propose a simulation-first framework to verify VLA architectures before real-world deployment and introduce MobileManiBench, a large-scale benchmark for mobile-based robotic manipulation. Built on NVIDIA Isaac Sim and powered by reinforcement learning, our pipeline autonomously generates diverse manipulation trajectories with rich annotations (language instructions, multi-view RGB-depth-segmentation images, synchronized object/robot states and actions). MobileManiBench features 2 mobile platforms (parallel-gripper and dexterous-hand robots), 2 synchronized cameras (head and right wrist), 630 objects in 20 categories, 5 skills (open, close, pull, push, pick) with over 100 tasks performed in 100 realistic scenes, yielding 300K trajectories. This design enables controlled, scalable studies of robot embodiments, sensing modalities, and policy architectures, accelerating research on data efficiency and generalization. We benchmark representative VLA models and report insights into perception, reasoning, and control in complex simulated environments.

</details>


### [321] [Low-Cost Underwater In-Pipe Centering and Inspection Using a Minimal-Sensing Robot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05265)
*Kalvik Jakkala,Jason O'Kane*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A minimal-sensing autonomous underwater navigation approach centers and traverses flooded pipes using IMU, pressure, a downward single-beam sonar, and a rotating 360° sonar; it derives wall ranges from sonar intensity, estimates pipe center via a closed-form geometry, and uses an adaptive PD controller for alignment, demonstrated in a 46 cm pipe with a BlueROV2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous in-pipe inspection underwater is challenging due to confined geometry, turbidity, and scarce reliable localization cues. A lightweight, robust sensing scheme is needed to enable reliable centering and traversal without complex multi-sensor suites or external tracking.

Method: Develop a computationally efficient method to extract range estimates from single-beam sonar intensity data. Use two sonar ranges in a closed-form geometric model to estimate the pipe center. Implement an adaptive, confidence-weighted PD controller to maintain alignment during traversal. The system relies on IMU and pressure data, plus two sonar modalities, with no DVL or external tracking.

Result: Validated in experiments inside a submerged 46 cm-diameter pipe using a BlueROV2 heavy ROV. The approach achieved stable centering and full-pipe traversal despite ambient flow and pipe deformations, demonstrating reliable in-pipe navigation with a lightweight sensing stack.

Conclusion: A lightweight, computationally efficient sensing and processing architecture enables practical autonomous in-pipe navigation and inspection in confined underwater environments, reducing reliance on heavy sensor suites and external localization.

Abstract: Autonomous underwater inspection of submerged pipelines is challenging due to confined geometries, turbidity, and the scarcity of reliable localization cues. This paper presents a minimal-sensing strategy that enables a free-swimming underwater robot to center itself and traverse a flooded pipe of known radius using only an IMU, a pressure sensor, and two sonars: a downward-facing single-beam sonar and a rotating 360 degree sonar. We introduce a computationally efficient method for extracting range estimates from single-beam sonar intensity data, enabling reliable wall detection in noisy and reverberant conditions. A closed-form geometric model leverages the two sonar ranges to estimate the pipe center, and an adaptive, confidence-weighted proportional-derivative (PD) controller maintains alignment during traversal. The system requires no Doppler velocity log, external tracking, or complex multi-sensor arrays. Experiments in a submerged 46 cm-diameter pipe using a Blue Robotics BlueROV2 heavy remotely operated vehicle demonstrate stable centering and successful full-pipe traversal despite ambient flow and structural deformations. These results show that reliable in-pipe navigation and inspection can be achieved with a lightweight, computationally efficient sensing and processing architecture, advancing the practicality of autonomous underwater inspection in confined environments.

</details>


### [322] [Affordance-Aware Interactive Decision-Making and Execution for Ambiguous Instructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05273)
*Hengxuan Xu,Fengbo Lan,Zhixin Zhao,Shengjie Wang,Mengqiao Liu,Jieqian Sun,Yu Cheng,Tao Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AIDE is a dual-stream framework that couples interactive exploration with vision-language reasoning to enable zero-shot affordance analysis and robust interpretation of ambiguous instructions. It achieves high planning success (>80%) and closed-loop execution accuracy (>95% at 10 Hz), outperforming VLM-based baselines in open-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots operating in unfamiliar environments under ambiguous human directives face inefficient reasoning and limited environmental interaction, which hampers real-time planning and execution. There is a need for a system that can actively explore and interpret affordances to resolve ambiguity.

Method: AIDE introduces a dual-stream architecture: Multi-Stage Inference (MSI) for decision-making and Accelerated Decision-Making (ADM) for execution. It integrates interactive exploration with vision-language reasoning, enabling zero-shot affordance analysis and interpretation of ambiguous instructions relevant to task-relevant objects (e.g., identifying cups/beverages for 'I\'m thirsty').

Result: Empirical evaluation in both simulation and real-world settings shows task planning success >80% and closed-loop continuous execution accuracy >95% at 10 Hz, with clear performance advantages over existing VLM-based methods across diverse open-world scenarios.

Conclusion: By uniting interactive exploration with a two-stream decision-execution pipeline and incorporating zero-shot affordance reasoning, AIDE advances robust interpretation of ambiguous instructions and real-time task execution in open-world environments.

Abstract: Enabling robots to explore and act in unfamiliar environments under ambiguous human instructions by interactively identifying task-relevant objects (e.g., identifying cups or beverages for "I'm thirsty") remains challenging for existing vision-language model (VLM)-based methods. This challenge stems from inefficient reasoning and the lack of environmental interaction, which hinder real-time task planning and execution. To address this, We propose Affordance-Aware Interactive Decision-Making and Execution for Ambiguous Instructions (AIDE), a dual-stream framework that integrates interactive exploration with vision-language reasoning, where Multi-Stage Inference (MSI) serves as the decision-making stream and Accelerated Decision-Making (ADM) as the execution stream, enabling zero-shot affordance analysis and interpretation of ambiguous instructions. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world environments show that AIDE achieves the task planning success rate of over 80\% and more than 95\% accuracy in closed-loop continuous execution at 10 Hz, outperforming existing VLM-based methods in diverse open-world scenarios.

</details>


### [323] [Learning Soccer Skills for Humanoid Robots: A Progressive Perception-Action Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05310)
*Jipeng Kong,Xinzhe Liu,Yuhang Lin,Jinrui Han,Sören Schwertfeger,Chenjia Bai,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PAiD is a staged perception-action integrated architecture for humanoid soccer, decoupling learning into motion-skill, perception-action integration, and sim-to-real transfer, enabling stable, generalizable kicking and balance on Unitree G1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modular soccer robots suffer inter-module instability; end-to-end learning can conflict objectives and hinder skill transfer. A scalable, staged framework is needed to acquire embodied soccer skills with stable foundations and robust real-world transfer.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) motion-skill acquisition via human motion tracking; (2) lightweight perception-action integration for positional generalization; (3) physics-aware sim-to-real transfer. Progressive training stabilizes foundational skills, avoids reward conflicts during perception integration, and reduces sim-to-real gaps. Demonstrated on Unitree G1 under diverse conditions (static/rolling balls, various positions, disturbances; indoor/outdoor).

Result: Demonstrated high-fidelity, human-like kicking with robustness to ball state and disturbances; consistent execution across indoor and outdoor scenarios, indicating stable progression from perception to action and effective sim-to-real transfer.

Conclusion: PAiD presents a divide-and-conquer approach that yields robust, scalable embodied soccer skills and offers a reusable framework for complex embodied skill acquisition.

Abstract: Soccer presents a significant challenge for humanoid robots, demanding tightly integrated perception-action capabilities for tasks like perception-guided kicking and whole-body balance control. Existing approaches suffer from inter-module instability in modular pipelines or conflicting training objectives in end-to-end frameworks. We propose Perception-Action integrated Decision-making (PAiD), a progressive architecture that decomposes soccer skill acquisition into three stages: motion-skill acquisition via human motion tracking, lightweight perception-action integration for positional generalization, and physics-aware sim-to-real transfer. This staged decomposition establishes stable foundational skills, avoids reward conflicts during perception integration, and minimizes sim-to-real gaps. Experiments on the Unitree G1 demonstrate high-fidelity human-like kicking with robust performance under diverse conditions-including static or rolling balls, various positions, and disturbances-while maintaining consistent execution across indoor and outdoor scenarios. Our divide-and-conquer strategy advances robust humanoid soccer capabilities and offers a scalable framework for complex embodied skill acquisition. The project page is available at https://soccer-humanoid.github.io/.

</details>


### [324] [RoboPaint: From Human Demonstration to Any Robot and Any View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05325)
*Jiacheng Fan,Zhiyue Zhao,Yiqian Zhang,Chao Chen,Peide Wang,Hengdi Zhang,Zhengxue Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Presents a Real-Sim-Real data pipeline for dexterous manipulation that converts human demonstrations into robot-executable training data using standardized multimodal capture, tactile-aware retargeting, and photorealistic simulation; achieves high success rates in real robots and VLA policies trained solely on the generated data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the bottleneck of acquiring large-scale, high-fidelity robot demonstration data for scaling Vision-Language-Action models in dexterous manipulation.

Method: 1) Standardized data collection rooms capture multimodal human demonstrations (synchronized 3 RGB-D videos, 11 RGB videos, 29-DoF glove joints, 14-channel tactile signals). 2) Tactile-aware retargeting maps human hand states to robot dexter-hand states via geometry- and force-guided optimization. 3) Retargeted trajectories are rendered in a photorealistic Isaac Sim environment to produce robot training data.

Result: Retargeted dex-hand trajectories achieved 84% success across 10 object manipulation tasks. Vision-Language-Action policies trained only on the generated data achieved 80% average success on pick-and-place, pushing, and pouring tasks.

Conclusion: Robot training data can be efficiently painted from human demonstrations using the Real-Sim-Real pipeline, offering a scalable, cost-effective alternative to teleoperation with minimal performance loss for complex dexterous manipulation.

Abstract: Acquiring large-scale, high-fidelity robot demonstration data remains a critical bottleneck for scaling Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in dexterous manipulation. We propose a Real-Sim-Real data collection and data editing pipeline that transforms human demonstrations into robot-executable, environment-specific training data without direct robot teleoperation. Standardized data collection rooms are built to capture multimodal human demonstrations (synchronized 3 RGB-D videos, 11 RGB videos, 29-DoF glove joint angles, and 14-channel tactile signals). Based on these human demonstrations, we introduce a tactile-aware retargeting method that maps human hand states to robot dex-hand states via geometry and force-guided optimization. Then the retargeted robot trajectories are rendered in a photorealistic Isaac Sim environment to build robot training data. Real world experiments have demonstrated: (1) The retargeted dex-hand trajectories achieve an 84\% success rate across 10 diverse object manipulation tasks. (2) VLA policies (Pi0.5) trained exclusively on our generated data achieve 80\% average success rate on three representative tasks, i.e., pick-and-place, pushing and pouring. To conclude, robot training data can be efficiently "painted" from human demonstrations using our real-sim-real data pipeline. We offer a scalable, cost-effective alternative to teleoperation with minimal performance loss for complex dexterous manipulation.

</details>


### [325] [Benchmarking Affordance Generalization with BusyBox](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05441)
*Dean Fortier,Timothy Adamson,Tess Hellebrekers,Teresa LaScala,Kofi Ennin,Michael Murray,Andrey Kolobov,Galen Mullins*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BusyBox is a modular physical benchmark for evaluating affordance generalization in vision-language-action models; results show nontrivial generalization gaps even for strong open-weight models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify affordance generalization in VLAs—i.e., manipulating new objects with familiar physical affordances—and provide a practical, shareable benchmark to spur robust evaluation.

Method: A six-module BusyBox with interchangeable components (switches, sliders, wires, buttons, display, dial) that can be rearranged to create varied appearances but identical affordances; semi-automatic evaluation of VLAs; dataset of language-annotated demonstrations collected with a bimanual Mobile Aloha robot on canonical configuration; CAD files, BOM, and instructions released for easy lab replication.

Result: Across BusyBox variants, generalization is challenging; strong open-weight VLAs (π_{0.5} and GR00T-N1.6) struggle to transfer to new appearances.

Conclusion: BusyBox serves as a practical, reproducible benchmark to study and improve affordance generalization in VLAs, with open materials to encourage community-wide evaluation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have been attracting the attention of researchers and practitioners thanks to their promise of generalization. Although single-task policies still offer competitive performance, VLAs are increasingly able to handle commands and environments unseen in their training set. While generalization in vision and language space is undoubtedly important for robust versatile behaviors, a key meta-skill VLAs need to possess is affordance generalization -- the ability to manipulate new objects with familiar physical features.
  In this work, we present BusyBox, a physical benchmark for systematic semi-automatic evaluation of VLAs' affordance generalization. BusyBox consists of 6 modules with switches, sliders, wires, buttons, a display, and a dial. The modules can be swapped and rotated to create a multitude of BusyBox variations with different visual appearances but the same set of affordances. We empirically demonstrate that generalization across BusyBox variants is highly challenging even for strong open-weights VLAs such as $π_{0.5}$ and GR00T-N1.6. To encourage the research community to evaluate their own VLAs on BusyBox and to propose new affordance generalization experiments, we have designed BusyBox to be easy to build in most robotics labs. We release the full set of CAD files for 3D-printing its parts as well as a bill of materials for (optionally) assembling its electronics. We also publish a dataset of language-annotated demonstrations that we collected using the common bimanual Mobile Aloha robot on the canonical BusyBox configuration. All of the released materials are available at https://microsoft.github.io/BusyBox.

</details>


### [326] [Ontology-Driven Robotic Specification Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05456)
*Maksym Figat,Ryan M. Mackey,Michel D. Ingham*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Ontology-driven hierarchical transformation from high-level robotic objectives to executable stochastic timed Petri-net models with resources (RSTM2), enabling Monte Carlo simulations across mission, system, and subsystem levels; supports explainable AI-assisted autonomous specification synthesis; applicable to complex multi-robot systems like NASA CADRE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between high-level goals and formal, executable specifications for safety- and mission-critical robotics; enable architectural trades, resource allocation, and performance analysis under uncertainty.

Method: Ontology-driven, hierarchical approach using stochastic timed Petri nets with resources; Monte Carlo simulations across multiple levels (mission, system, subsystem); hypothetical case study; ontological foundations enable explainable AI assistants for autonomous specification synthesis.

Result: Demonstrates capability to support architectural trades, resource allocation, and performance analysis under uncertainty; shows potential for autonomous specification synthesis; applicable to decentralized, resource-aware, adaptive multi-robot systems (e.g., NASA CADRE).

Conclusion: RSTM2 offers benefits for complex multi-robot systems and future autonomous, decentralized, resource-aware, adaptive robotics; ontology-based explainable AI enables autonomous specification synthesis; further work needed on validation, scalability, and integration.

Abstract: This paper addresses robotic system engineering for safety- and mission-critical applications by bridging the gap between high-level objectives and formal, executable specifications. The proposed method, Robotic System Task to Model Transformation Methodology (RSTM2) is an ontology-driven, hierarchical approach using stochastic timed Petri nets with resources, enabling Monte Carlo simulations at mission, system, and subsystem levels. A hypothetical case study demonstrates how the RSTM2 method supports architectural trades, resource allocation, and performance analysis under uncertainty. Ontological concepts further enable explainable AI-based assistants, facilitating fully autonomous specification synthesis. The methodology offers particular benefits to complex multi-robot systems, such as the NASA CADRE mission, representing decentralized, resource-aware, and adaptive autonomous systems of the future.

</details>


### [327] [TaSA: Two-Phased Deep Predictive Learning of Tactile Sensory Attenuation for Improving In-Grasp Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05468)
*Pranav Ponnivalavan,Satoshi Funabashi,Alexander Schmitz,Tetsuya Ogata,Shigeki Sugano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TaSA is a two-phase deep predictive framework that learns self-touch dynamics to distinguish self-contact from object contact, improving dexterous manipulation; demonstrated higher success on insertion tasks compared to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots often struggle with self-contact, which contaminates tactile data and risks damage. Humans use sensory attenuation to predict self-touch; translating this to robotics could improve robustness and generalization in contact-rich tasks.

Method: Phase 1: learn a predictive model of self-touch dynamics (how the robot's actions generate tactile feedback). Phase 2: integrate this model into the motion-learning stage to emphasize object-contact signals and attenuate predictable self-touch during manipulation. Evaluation on insertion tasks with varied orientations, positions, and sizes (pencil lead in mechanical pencil, coins in a slot, paper-clip on paper).

Result: Policies trained with TaSA show significantly higher success rates than baselines across all tasks, evidencing that integrating self-touch predictions improves tactile discrimination and dexterous manipulation.

Conclusion: Sensory-attenuation-inspired self-touch modeling enables robust, generalizable in-hand manipulation by explicitly predicting self-generated tactile signals and biasing learning toward object-contact cues.

Abstract: Humans can achieve diverse in-hand manipulations, such as object pinching and tool use, which often involve simultaneous contact between the object and multiple fingers. This is still an open issue for robotic hands because such dexterous manipulation requires distinguishing between tactile sensations generated by their self-contact and those arising from external contact. Otherwise, object/robot breakage happens due to contacts/collisions. Indeed, most approaches ignore self-contact altogether, by constraining motion to avoid/ignore self-tactile information during contact. While this reduces complexity, it also limits generalization to real-world scenarios where self-contact is inevitable. Humans overcome this challenge through self-touch perception, using predictive mechanisms that anticipate the tactile consequences of their own motion, through a principle called sensory attenuation, where the nervous system differentiates predictable self-touch signals, allowing novel object stimuli to stand out as relevant. Deriving from this, we introduce TaSA, a two-phased deep predictive learning framework. In the first phase, TaSA explicitly learns self-touch dynamics, modeling how a robot's own actions generate tactile feedback. In the second phase, this learned model is incorporated into the motion learning phase, to emphasize object contact signals during manipulation. We evaluate TaSA on a set of insertion tasks, which demand fine tactile discrimination: inserting a pencil lead into a mechanical pencil, inserting coins into a slot, and fixing a paper clip onto a sheet of paper, with various orientations, positions, and sizes. Across all tasks, policies trained with TaSA achieve significantly higher success rates than baseline methods, demonstrating that structured tactile perception with self-touch based on sensory attenuation is critical for dexterous robotic manipulation.

</details>


### [328] [DECO: Decoupled Multimodal Diffusion Transformer for Bimanual Dexterous Manipulation with a Plugin Tactile Adapter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05513)
*Xukun Li,Yu Sun,Lei Zhang,Bosheng Huang,Yibo Peng,Yuan Meng,Haojun Jiang,Shaoxuan Xie,Guacai Yao,Alois Knoll,Zhenshan Bing,Xinlong Wang,Zhenguo Sun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DECO is a DiT-based policy that decouples multimodal conditioning for bimanual dexterous manipulation, integrating visual, proprioceptive, and tactile inputs using joint self-attention, adaptive layer norm, cross-attention, and LoRA adapters; accompanied by DECO-50, a large tactile-sensing manipulation dataset (4 scenarios, 28 sub-tasks, ~50 hours, ~5M frames, 8000 successes).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable efficient, multimodal conditioning and fine-tuning for dexterous manipulation by decoupling modalities and leveraging a large tactile-sensing dataset.

Method: A DiT-based policy where image and action tokens interact via joint self-attention; proprioceptive states and optional conditions injected through adaptive layer normalization; tactile signals injected via cross-attention; LoRA-based adapter for efficient fine-tuning; accompanies DECO-50 dataset.

Result: Proposes a multimodal manipulation framework (DECO) and a large-scale tactile dataset (DECO-50) enabling effective conditioning and fine-tuning for dexterous tasks.

Conclusion: DECO provides a scalable, efficient multimodal policy for dexterous manipulation with tactile sensing and contributes a rich dataset (DECO-50) to support future research.

Abstract: Overview of the Proposed DECO Framework.} DECO is a DiT-based policy that decouples multimodal conditioning. Image and action tokens interact via joint self attention, while proprioceptive states and optional conditions are injected through adaptive layer normalization. Tactile signals are injected via cross attention, while a lightweight LoRA-based adapter is used to efficiently fine-tune the pretrained policy. DECO is also accompanied by DECO-50, a bimanual dexterous manipulation dataset with tactile sensing, consisting of 4 scenarios and 28 sub-tasks, covering more than 50 hours of data, approximately 5 million frames, and 8,000 successful trajectories.

</details>


### [329] [Virtual-Tube-Based Cooperative Transport Control for Multi-UAV Systems in Constrained Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05516)
*Runxiao Liu,Pengda Mao,Xiangli Le,Shuang Gu,Yapeng Chen,Quan Quan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel, scalable control framework for cooperative transport of cable-suspended loads by multiple UAVs in cluttered environments, combining virtual tube theory with dissipative systems to achieve low-computation, stable, robust multi-UAV coordination validated by simulations and outdoor experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe, efficient, and scalable multi-UAV cable-load transportation in obstacle-rich/constraint environments, addressing tension distribution, obstacle avoidance, and computational efficiency while ensuring stability.

Method: Integrates virtual tube theory (for safe corridor/obstacle-aware path planning) with dissipative systems theory (to guarantee stability/robustness). The framework dynamically adapts UAV configurations around obstacle layouts to maintain efficient navigation and distribute cable tension with low computational overhead.

Result: Demonstrated scalability through extensive simulations for large-scale multi-UAV systems and validated experimentally in outdoor scenarios, confirming practical feasibility and robustness under real-world conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a stable, efficient, and scalable solution for cooperative UAV-cable-load transport in constrained environments, balancing low computation with robust performance and empirical validation.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel control framework for cooperative transportation of cable-suspended loads by multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) operating in constrained environments. Leveraging virtual tube theory and principles from dissipative systems theory, the framework facilitates efficient multi-UAV collaboration for navigating obstacle-rich areas. The proposed framework offers several key advantages. (1) It achieves tension distribution and coordinated transportation within the UAV-cable-load system with low computational overhead, dynamically adapting UAV configurations based on obstacle layouts to facilitate efficient navigation. (2) By integrating dissipative systems theory, the framework ensures high stability and robustness, essential for complex multi-UAV operations. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated through extensive simulations, demonstrating its scalability for large-scale multi-UAV systems. Furthermore, the method is experimentally validated in outdoor scenarios, showcasing its practical feasibility and robustness under real-world conditions.

</details>


### [330] [VLN-Pilot: Large Vision-Language Model as an Autonomous Indoor Drone Operator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05552)
*Bessie Dominguez-Dager,Sergio Suescun-Ferrandiz,Felix Escalona,Francisco Gomez-Donoso,Miguel Cazorla*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A vision-and-language model serves as an autonomous indoor drone pilot by grounding natural language instructions in visual input to plan and execute trajectories, demonstrated in a photorealistic indoor simulation with high success on long-horizon, semantically-guided tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address GPS-denied indoor UAVs and reduce human operator workload by leveraging multimodal reasoning to combine semantic understanding with perception; move beyond rule-based/path planning to language-guided autonomy; enable scalable indoor UAVs for inspection, search-and-rescue, facility monitoring.

Method: Propose VLN-Pilot framework where a large VLLM interprets free-form instructions and grounds them in observed imagery to produce planning actions and control signals for drone trajectories; integrate semantic language grounding with visual perception to support context-aware high-level flight behaviors; minimal task-specific engineering; validated in a custom photorealistic indoor simulator; supports autonomous instruction following and obstacle/dynamic event handling.

Result: Demonstrates high success rates on complex instruction-following tasks and long-horizon navigation with multiple semantic targets; shows potential of VLLM-driven pilots to replace remote drone pilots; improved safety and mission flexibility; supports scalable human-friendly control for indoor UAVs.

Conclusion: VLLM-based pilots offer a promising direction for autonomous, instruction-driven indoor drone operation, achieving context-aware navigational behaviors with reduced operator workload and increased safety; future work may focus on real-world deployment, robustness, and broader task domains (inspection, search-and-rescue, facility monitoring).

Abstract: This paper introduces VLN-Pilot, a novel framework in which a large Vision-and-Language Model (VLLM) assumes the role of a human pilot for indoor drone navigation. By leveraging the multimodal reasoning abilities of VLLMs, VLN-Pilot interprets free-form natural language instructions and grounds them in visual observations to plan and execute drone trajectories in GPS-denied indoor environments. Unlike traditional rule-based or geometric path-planning approaches, our framework integrates language-driven semantic understanding with visual perception, enabling context-aware, high-level flight behaviors with minimal task-specific engineering. VLN-Pilot supports fully autonomous instruction-following for drones by reasoning about spatial relationships, obstacle avoidance, and dynamic reactivity to unforeseen events. We validate our framework on a custom photorealistic indoor simulation benchmark and demonstrate the ability of the VLLM-driven agent to achieve high success rates on complex instruction-following tasks, including long-horizon navigation with multiple semantic targets. Experimental results highlight the promise of replacing remote drone pilots with a language-guided autonomous agent, opening avenues for scalable, human-friendly control of indoor UAVs in tasks such as inspection, search-and-rescue, and facility monitoring. Our results suggest that VLLM-based pilots may dramatically reduce operator workload while improving safety and mission flexibility in constrained indoor environments.

</details>


### [331] [TOLEBI: Learning Fault-Tolerant Bipedal Locomotion via Online Status Estimation and Fallibility Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05596)
*Hokyun Lee,Woo-Jeong Baek,Junhyeok Cha,Jaeheung Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TOLEBI is a learning-based fault-tolerant framework for bipedal locomotion that trains with fault injections (joint locking, power loss, disturbances), uses sim-to-real transfer, and includes an online joint-status module for runtime fault classification; validated on the humanoid TOCABI in both simulation and real-world experiments, claimed as the first such framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL-driven bipedal locomotion shows high task success but robustness to hardware faults and disturbances is underexplored; real-world operations require mechanisms to tolerate faults and adapt during execution to avoid severe consequences.

Method: Train a fault-tolerant policy under simulated faults (joint locking, power loss, external disturbances). Enable sim-to-real transfer of the learned policy and integrate an online joint-status module that classifies joint conditions from real-time observations during operation. Validation performed on the TOCABI humanoid in both simulation and real-world experiments.

Result: The approach demonstrates feasibility and transferability of fault-tolerant locomotion strategies; real-world and simulated experiments validate the applicability of TOLEBI, highlighting its potential as a foundational framework in learning-based fault tolerance for bipedal locomotion.

Conclusion: TOLEBI is proposed as the first learning-based fault-tolerant framework for bipedal locomotion, expected to spur further research and development of robust learning methods for humanoid robotics.

Abstract: With the growing employment of learning algorithms in robotic applications, research on reinforcement learning for bipedal locomotion has become a central topic for humanoid robotics. While recently published contributions achieve high success rates in locomotion tasks, scarce attention has been devoted to the development of methods that enable to handle hardware faults that may occur during the locomotion process. However, in real-world settings, environmental disturbances or sudden occurrences of hardware faults might yield severe consequences. To address these issues, this paper presents TOLEBI (A faulT-tOlerant Learning framEwork for Bipedal locomotIon) that handles faults on the robot during operation. Specifically, joint locking, power loss and external disturbances are injected in simulation to learn fault-tolerant locomotion strategies. In addition to transferring the learned policy to the real robot via sim-to-real transfer, an online joint status module incorporated. This module enables to classify joint conditions by referring to the actual observations at runtime under real-world conditions. The validation experiments conducted both in real-world and simulation with the humanoid robot TOCABI highlight the applicability of the proposed approach. To our knowledge, this manuscript provides the first learning-based fault-tolerant framework for bipedal locomotion, thereby fostering the development of efficient learning methods in this field.

</details>


### [332] [HiCrowd: Hierarchical Crowd Flow Alignment for Dense Human Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05608)
*Yufei Zhu,Shih-Min Yang,Martin Magnusson,Allan Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HiCrowd combines hierarchical reinforcement learning (RL) with model predictive control (MPC) to navigate dense crowds by using surrounding pedestrian motion as guidance. A high-level RL policy picks a follow point aligned with a pedestrian group, while a low-level MPC tracks this guidance with short-horizon planning, resulting in safer, more efficient navigation with reduced freezing behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the freezing robot problem in crowded environments and improve safety and efficiency by treating humans as motion-guidance sources rather than mere dynamic obstacles.

Method: A two-level approach where a high-level RL policy outputs a follow point to align the robot with a suitable pedestrian group, and a low-level MPC tracks this guidance over a short horizon. The surrounding pedestrian motion serves as guidance for both levels. The system is evaluated offline (replaying recorded trajectories) and online (robot reacts to updated trajectories in simulation).

Result: HiCrowd outperforms reactive and learning-based baselines in navigation efficiency and safety, with reduced freezing behaviors, on both real-world and synthetic crowd datasets in offline and online settings.

Conclusion: Leveraging human motion as guidance is a powerful principle for safe and efficient robot navigation in crowds, enabling long-term crowd-aware decisions with safe short-term execution.

Abstract: Navigating through dense human crowds remains a significant challenge for mobile robots. A key issue is the freezing robot problem, where the robot struggles to find safe motions and becomes stuck within the crowd. To address this, we propose HiCrowd, a hierarchical framework that integrates reinforcement learning (RL) with model predictive control (MPC). HiCrowd leverages surrounding pedestrian motion as guidance, enabling the robot to align with compatible crowd flows. A high-level RL policy generates a follow point to align the robot with a suitable pedestrian group, while a low-level MPC safely tracks this guidance with short horizon planning. The method combines long-term crowd aware decision making with safe short-term execution. We evaluate HiCrowd against reactive and learning-based baselines in offline setting (replaying recorded human trajectories) and online setting (human trajectories are updated to react to the robot in simulation). Experiments on a real-world dataset and a synthetic crowd dataset show that our method outperforms in navigation efficiency and safety, while reducing freezing behaviors. Our results suggest that leveraging human motion as guidance, rather than treating humans solely as dynamic obstacles, provides a powerful principle for safe and efficient robot navigation in crowds.

</details>


### [333] [From Vision to Decision: Neuromorphic Control for Autonomous Navigation and Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05683)
*Chuwei Wang,Eduardo Sebastián,Amanda Prorok,Anastasia Bizyaeva*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A neuromorphic, parsimonious control framework for vision-guided navigation that maps image targets to egocentric commands via dynamic neural populations, uses a bifurcation-based indecision mechanism, and validates in simulation and on a quadrotor with low computation and interpretable parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between reactive sensor-based control and model-based planners; address indecision when multiple goals exist and computational load for planning becomes prohibitive.

Method: Encode image pixels as inputs to dynamic neuronal populations that translate target excitation into motion commands. Implement a dynamic bifurcation mechanism to delay commitment until environmental geometry triggers a decision. Draw inspiration from mechanistic models of cognition and opinion dynamics; ensure real-time autonomy with few parameters and easy integration with image pipelines.

Result: Demonstrated real-time autonomous navigation in both simulation and an experimental quadrotor platform, with a minimal computational burden and a small set of interpretable parameters.

Conclusion: The neuromorphic controller provides a viable bridge between reactive and planning-based approaches for vision-guided navigation and tracking, enabling real-time autonomy on resource-constrained platforms and easy integration into application-specific pipelines.

Abstract: Robotic navigation has historically struggled to reconcile reactive, sensor-based control with the decisive capabilities of model-based planners. This duality becomes critical when the absence of a predominant option among goals leads to indecision, challenging reactive systems to break symmetries without computationally-intense planners. We propose a parsimonious neuromorphic control framework that bridges this gap for vision-guided navigation and tracking. Image pixels from an onboard camera are encoded as inputs to dynamic neuronal populations that directly transform visual target excitation into egocentric motion commands. A dynamic bifurcation mechanism resolves indecision by delaying commitment until a critical point induced by the environmental geometry. Inspired by recently proposed mechanistic models of animal cognition and opinion dynamics, the neuromorphic controller provides real-time autonomy with a minimal computational burden, a small number of interpretable parameters, and can be seamlessly integrated with application-specific image processing pipelines. We validate our approach in simulation environments as well as on an experimental quadrotor platform.

</details>


### [334] [Task-Oriented Robot-Human Handovers on Legged Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05760)
*Andreea Tulbure,Carmen Scheidemann,Elias Steiner,Marco Hutter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AFT-Handover combines LLM-driven affordance reasoning with texture-based affordance transfer to achieve zero-shot, generalizable task-oriented handovers (TOH).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TOH requires adaptable affordances beyond object/task-specific cues; current methods struggle to generalize to novel scenarios.

Method: Retrieve a proxy exemplar for a novel object-task pair; use LLM reasoning to establish part-level correspondences; texture affordances for feature-based point cloud transfer to enable zero-shot handovers.

Result: Improved handover success rates and generalization vs baselines; user study shows clear preference and reduced regrasping; demonstrated on legged manipulators, indicating real-world potential.

Conclusion: The framework shows strong potential for generalizable TOH by integrating LLM-based reasoning with texture-based affordance transfer, enabling zero-shot adaptation to new object-task combinations.

Abstract: Task-oriented handovers (TOH) are fundamental to effective human-robot collaboration, requiring robots to present objects in a way that supports the human's intended post-handover use. Existing approaches are typically based on object- or task-specific affordances, but their ability to generalize to novel scenarios is limited. To address this gap, we present AFT-Handover, a framework that integrates large language model (LLM)-driven affordance reasoning with efficient texture-based affordance transfer to achieve zero-shot, generalizable TOH. Given a novel object-task pair, the method retrieves a proxy exemplar from a database, establishes part-level correspondences via LLM reasoning, and texturizes affordances for feature-based point cloud transfer. We evaluate AFT-Handover across diverse task-object pairs, showing improved handover success rates and stronger generalization compared to baselines. In a comparative user study, our framework is significantly preferred over the current state-of-the-art, effectively reducing human regrasping before tool use. Finally, we demonstrate TOH on legged manipulators, highlighting the potential of our framework for real-world robot-human handovers.

</details>


### [335] [Scalable and General Whole-Body Control for Cross-Humanoid Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05791)
*Yufei Xue,YunFeng Lin,Wentao Dong,Yang Tang,Jingbo Wang,Jiangmiao Pang,Ming Zhou,Minghuan Liu,Weinan Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A single universal humanoid controller learned via cross-embodiment training generalizes to unseen morphologies, enabling zero-shot transfer across diverse robots using one-time training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current humanoid controllers often require robot-specific training. A generalist policy that works across many morphologies reduces development time and enables rapid deployment.

Method: Introduce XHugWBC: (1) physics-consistent morphological randomization, (2) semantically aligned observation/action spaces across diverse humanoids, (3) policy architectures that model morphological/dynamical properties. The policy learns motion priors from a broad distribution of randomized embodiments to acquire a structural bias for zero-shot transfer.

Result: Experiments on twelve simulated humanoids and seven real-world robots demonstrate strong generalization and robustness of the universal controller.

Conclusion: A single cross-embodiment policy can robustly generalize across a wide range of humanoid designs with one-time training and supports zero-shot transfer to unseen robots.

Abstract: Learning-based whole-body controllers have become a key driver for humanoid robots, yet most existing approaches require robot-specific training. In this paper, we study the problem of cross-embodiment humanoid control and show that a single policy can robustly generalize across a wide range of humanoid robot designs with one-time training. We introduce XHugWBC, a novel cross-embodiment training framework that enables generalist humanoid control through: (1) physics-consistent morphological randomization, (2) semantically aligned observation and action spaces across diverse humanoid robots, and (3) effective policy architectures modeling morphological and dynamical properties. XHugWBC is not tied to any specific robot. Instead, it internalizes a broad distribution of morphological and dynamical characteristics during training. By learning motion priors from diverse randomized embodiments, the policy acquires a strong structural bias that supports zero-shot transfer to previously unseen robots. Experiments on twelve simulated humanoids and seven real-world robots demonstrate the strong generalization and robustness of the resulting universal controller.

</details>


### [336] [A Hybrid Autoencoder for Robust Heightmap Generation from Fused Lidar and Depth Data for Humanoid Robot Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05855)
*Dennis Bank,Joost Cordes,Thomas Seel,Simon F. G. Ehlers*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A learning-based, robot-centric heightmap framework using a CNN-GRU encoder-decoder and multimodal fusion (RealSense, LiDAR with spherical projection, IMU) to robustly map terrain; 3.2 s temporal context reduces drift and improves reconstruction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional single-sensor pipelines struggle in unstructured, human-centric environments; a multimodal, temporally-consistent representation is needed for reliable terrain perception by humanoid robots.

Method: Hybrid Encoder-Decoder Structure (EDS) with CNN-based spatial feature extraction and a GRU core for temporal integration. Multimodal fusion of Intel RealSense depth, LIVOX MID-360 LiDAR projected in spherical coordinates, and onboard IMU to produce a robot-centric heightmap. Temporal window of 3.2 seconds used to enhance mapping stability.

Result: Fusion of modalities yields 7.2% higher reconstruction accuracy over depth-only and 9.9% over LiDAR-only baselines. Temporal context reduces mapping drift.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that integrating multimodal sensors with a temporal horizon improves terrain mapping accuracy and drift resistance, supporting reliable humanoid navigation in unstructured settings.

Abstract: Reliable terrain perception is a critical prerequisite for the deployment of humanoid robots in unstructured, human-centric environments. While traditional systems often rely on manually engineered, single-sensor pipelines, this paper presents a learning-based framework that uses an intermediate, robot-centric heightmap representation. A hybrid Encoder-Decoder Structure (EDS) is introduced, utilizing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for spatial feature extraction fused with a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) core for temporal consistency. The architecture integrates multimodal data from an Intel RealSense depth camera, a LIVOX MID-360 LiDAR processed via efficient spherical projection, and an onboard IMU. Quantitative results demonstrate that multimodal fusion improves reconstruction accuracy by 7.2% over depth-only and 9.9% over LiDAR-only configurations. Furthermore, the integration of a 3.2 s temporal context reduces mapping drift.

</details>


### [337] [Residual Reinforcement Learning for Waste-Container Lifting Using Large-Scale Cranes with Underactuated Tools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05895)
*Qi Li,Karsten Berns*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A residual reinforcement learning (RRL) framework augments a physics-based Cartesian controller with a PPO-trained residual policy to improve precision and robustness in a simulated waste-container lifting task using a hydraulic loader crane. The approach yields better tracking, reduced swing, and higher lifting success than the nominal controller alone, aided by domain randomization and randomized initialization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tight geometric tolerances between discharge-unit hooks and container rings demand precise trajectory tracking and swing suppression in cluttered urban environments. End-to-end learning from scratch is data-intensive; a hybrid approach can leverage a reliable nominal controller while learning to compensate for unmodeled dynamics and variations.

Method: 1) Nominal Cartesian controller: admittance control for trajectory tracking, pendulum-aware swing damping; damped least-squares IK with a nullspace posture term for joint velocity commands. 2) PPO-trained residual policy (Isaac Lab) adds a learned residual to the nominal output to compensate for unmodeled dynamics and parameter variations. 3) Training with domain randomization over payload properties, actuator gains, and passive joint parameters; randomized episode initialization to improve generalization. 4) Evaluation in simulation comparing RRL vs nominal controller alone on a waste-container lifting task with underactuated discharge unit.

Result: The residual-augmented controller demonstrates improved tracking accuracy, reduced oscillations, and higher lifting success rates compared to the nominal controller alone. Domain randomization and randomized initialization enhance robustness and generalization across simulated variations.

Conclusion: A residual reinforcement learning augmentation to a physics-based Cartesian controller can substantially improve precision and robustness in complex robotic manipulation tasks with underactuated end-effectors, enabling better performance in simulation and suggesting potential for real-world deployment with further transfer strategies.

Abstract: This paper studies the container lifting phase of a waste-container recycling task in urban environments, performed by a hydraulic loader crane equipped with an underactuated discharge unit, and proposes a residual reinforcement learning (RRL) approach that combines a nominal Cartesian controller with a learned residual policy. All experiments are conducted in simulation, where the task is characterized by tight geometric tolerances between the discharge-unit hooks and the container rings relative to the overall crane scale, making precise trajectory tracking and swing suppression essential. The nominal controller uses admittance control for trajectory tracking and pendulum-aware swing damping, followed by damped least-squares inverse kinematics with a nullspace posture term to generate joint velocity commands. A PPO-trained residual policy in Isaac Lab compensates for unmodeled dynamics and parameter variations, improving precision and robustness without requiring end-to-end learning from scratch. We further employ randomized episode initialization and domain randomization over payload properties, actuator gains, and passive joint parameters to enhance generalization. Simulation results demonstrate improved tracking accuracy, reduced oscillations, and higher lifting success rates compared to the nominal controller alone.

</details>


### [338] [From Bench to Flight: Translating Drone Impact Tests into Operational Safety Limits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05922)
*Aziz Mohamed Mili,Louis Catar,Paul Gérard,Ilyass Tabiai,David St-Onge*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end data-driven safety governor for indoor MAVs that converts benchtop impact tests into runtime speed bounds using a compact rig, models, and a ROS2 enforcement node.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor MAVs operating near humans require actionable, repeatable limits on motion to ensure safety; current methods lack practical tunable bounds and deployable tooling.

Method: 1) Build a compact, replicable impact rig and protocol to capture force–time profiles across drone classes and contact surfaces; 2) Develop data-driven mappings from pre-impact speed to impulse and contact duration; 3) Release scripts and a ROS2 node to enforce computed bounds online and log compliance, with support for facility-specific policies.

Result: Validated workflow on multiple off-the-shelf quadrotors and indoor assets; derived governors preserve task throughput while meeting force constraints specified by safety stakeholders; shareable datasets, code, and a repeatable process for certification of indoor MAV operations near humans.

Conclusion: Provides a practical bridge from measured impacts to runtime safety limits, enabling teams to certify indoor MAV operations with open tools and datasets.

Abstract: Indoor micro-aerial vehicles (MAVs) are increasingly used for tasks that require close proximity to people, yet practitioners lack practical methods to tune motion limits based on measured impact risk. We present an end-to-end, open toolchain that converts benchtop impact tests into deployable safety governors for drones. First, we describe a compact and replicable impact rig and protocol for capturing force-time profiles across drone classes and contact surfaces. Second, we provide data-driven models that map pre-impact speed to impulse and contact duration, enabling direct computation of speed bounds for a target force limit. Third, we release scripts and a ROS2 node that enforce these bounds online and log compliance, with support for facility-specific policies. We validate the workflow on multiple commercial off-the-shelf quadrotors and representative indoor assets, demonstrating that the derived governors preserve task throughput while meeting force constraints specified by safety stakeholders. Our contribution is a practical bridge from measured impacts to runtime limits, with shareable datasets, code, and a repeatable process that teams can adopt to certify indoor MAV operations near humans.

</details>


### [339] [Visuo-Tactile World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06001)
*Carolina Higuera,Sergio Arnaud,Byron Boots,Mustafa Mukadam,Francois Robert Hogan,Franziska Meier*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-task Visuo-Tactile World Models (VT-WM) integrate vision and touch to model contact dynamics, improving object permanence and physics fidelity in contact-rich manipulation; enables transferable planning and strong zero-shot real-robot gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-only world models struggle under occlusion and ambiguous contact states in manipulation tasks. Incorporating tactile sensing aims to capture the physics of contact, improving interaction understanding and planning robustness.

Method: Train VT-WM on multiple contact-rich manipulation tasks using aligned visuo-tactile inputs. Evaluate physics fidelity via autoregressive rollouts, test zero-shot real-robot performance, and assess transfer to a novel task with limited demonstrations.

Result: VT-WM achieves 33% better object permanence and 29% better adherence to the laws of motion in autoregressive rollouts. In zero-shot real-robot experiments, it yields up to 35% higher success rates, especially in multi-step contact-rich tasks. Demonstrates adaptability to novel tasks with limited demonstrations.

Conclusion: Grounding contact dynamics in tactile sensing enhances physical fidelity and planning, enabling better performance in contact-rich scenarios and versatile transfer to new tasks.

Abstract: We introduce multi-task Visuo-Tactile World Models (VT-WM), which capture the physics of contact through touch reasoning. By complementing vision with tactile sensing, VT-WM better understands robot-object interactions in contact-rich tasks, avoiding common failure modes of vision-only models under occlusion or ambiguous contact states, such as objects disappearing, teleporting, or moving in ways that violate basic physics. Trained across a set of contact-rich manipulation tasks, VT-WM improves physical fidelity in imagination, achieving 33% better performance at maintaining object permanence and 29% better compliance with the laws of motion in autoregressive rollouts. Moreover, experiments show that grounding in contact dynamics also translates to planning. In zero-shot real-robot experiments, VT-WM achieves up to 35% higher success rates, with the largest gains in multi-step, contact-rich tasks. Finally, VT-WM demonstrates significant downstream versatility, effectively adapting its learned contact dynamics to a novel task and achieving reliable planning success with only a limited set of demonstrations.

</details>


### [340] [CommCP: Efficient Multi-Agent Coordination via LLM-Based Communication with Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.06038)
*Xiaopan Zhang,Zejin Wang,Zhixu Li,Jianpeng Yao,Jiachen Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A multi-agent, multi-task Embodied Question Answering (MM-EQA) framework, CommCP, uses LLM-based decentralized communication with conformal prediction to coordinate heterogeneous robots in photo-realistic household environments, improving task success and exploration efficiency over baselines on a new MM-EQA benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for cooperative information gathering among heterogeneous robots to complete natural language assignments in embodied settings, where non-redundant communication and efficient exploration are critical.

Method: Introduce CommCP, a decentralized, LLM-driven communication framework for MM-EQA. It employs conformal prediction to calibrate messages and reduce receiver distraction, and is evaluated on a new MM-EQA benchmark with photo-realistic household scenes.

Result: CommCP significantly improves task success rate and exploration efficiency compared to baselines on the MM-EQA benchmark; experimental videos, code, and dataset are provided.

Conclusion: Decentralized LLM-based communication with conformal prediction boosts coordination and reliability in MM-EQA tasks, enabling more effective multi-robot information gathering and operation in embodied environments.

Abstract: To complete assignments provided by humans in natural language, robots must interpret commands, generate and answer relevant questions for scene understanding, and manipulate target objects. Real-world deployments often require multiple heterogeneous robots with different manipulation capabilities to handle different assignments cooperatively. Beyond the need for specialized manipulation skills, effective information gathering is important in completing these assignments. To address this component of the problem, we formalize the information-gathering process in a fully cooperative setting as an underexplored multi-agent multi-task Embodied Question Answering (MM-EQA) problem, which is a novel extension of canonical Embodied Question Answering (EQA), where effective communication is crucial for coordinating efforts without redundancy. To address this problem, we propose CommCP, a novel LLM-based decentralized communication framework designed for MM-EQA. Our framework employs conformal prediction to calibrate the generated messages, thereby minimizing receiver distractions and enhancing communication reliability. To evaluate our framework, we introduce an MM-EQA benchmark featuring diverse, photo-realistic household scenarios with embodied questions. Experimental results demonstrate that CommCP significantly enhances the task success rate and exploration efficiency over baselines. The experiment videos, code, and dataset are available on our project website: https://comm-cp.github.io.

</details>


### [341] [Differentiable Inverse Graphics for Zero-shot Scene Reconstruction and Robot Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05029)
*Octavio Arriaga,Proneet Sharma,Jichen Guo,Marc Otto,Siddhant Kadwe,Rebecca Adam*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A differentiable neuro-graphics approach integrates neural foundation models with physics-based differentiable rendering to enable zero-shot, physically-consistent scene reconstruction and grasping from a single RGB-D image without extra 3D data or test-time samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data inefficiency and lack of interpretability in current black-box scene representations for robotics operating in novel environments; push towards zero-shot, physically grounded scene understanding.

Method: Constrained optimization to jointly estimate scene geometry (meshes), lighting, materials, and 6D object poses from RGB-D and bounding boxes; leverages neural foundation models and differentiable rendering; requires no additional 3D data or test-time sampling.

Result: Outperforms model-free few-shot pose estimation benchmarks and demonstrates zero-shot grasping, validating the accuracy of the reconstructed scene and the practicality of the approach.

Conclusion: Zero-shot, physically-consistent scene reconstruction and grasping enable data-efficient, interpretable, and generalizable robot autonomy in novel environments.

Abstract: Operating effectively in novel real-world environments requires robotic systems to estimate and interact with previously unseen objects. Current state-of-the-art models address this challenge by using large amounts of training data and test-time samples to build black-box scene representations. In this work, we introduce a differentiable neuro-graphics model that combines neural foundation models with physics-based differentiable rendering to perform zero-shot scene reconstruction and robot grasping without relying on any additional 3D data or test-time samples. Our model solves a series of constrained optimization problems to estimate physically consistent scene parameters, such as meshes, lighting conditions, material properties, and 6D poses of previously unseen objects from a single RGBD image and bounding boxes. We evaluated our approach on standard model-free few-shot benchmarks and demonstrated that it outperforms existing algorithms for model-free few-shot pose estimation. Furthermore, we validated the accuracy of our scene reconstructions by applying our algorithm to a zero-shot grasping task. By enabling zero-shot, physically-consistent scene reconstruction and grasping without reliance on extensive datasets or test-time sampling, our approach offers a pathway towards more data efficient, interpretable and generalizable robot autonomy in novel environments.

</details>


### [342] [Reinforcement Learning Enhancement Using Vector Semantic Representation and Symbolic Reasoning for Human-Centered Autonomous Emergency Braking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05079)
*Vinal Asodia,Iman Sharifi,Saber Fallah*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic feature representation with SFOL rewards improves safety and robustness in autonomous driving in CARLA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two limitations of camera-based DRL: lack of high-level scene context in feature representations and rigid fixed reward functions; propose context-aware, value-aligned decision-making.

Method: Propose a neuro-symbolic pipeline that builds semantic, spatial, and shape features with spatially boosted representations of dynamic entities (emphasizing safety-critical road users). Introduce a Soft First-Order Logic (SFOL) reward function that balances human values via a symbolic reasoning module. Semantic/spatial predicates are extracted from segmentation maps and converted into linguistic rules to compute reward weights.

Result: In CARLA, the proposed neuro-symbolic representation and SFOL reward function improve policy robustness and safety-related metrics compared to baselines across varying traffic densities and occlusion levels.

Conclusion: Integrating holistic representations and soft reasoning enables context-aware and value-aligned decision-making for autonomous driving.

Abstract: The problem with existing camera-based Deep Reinforcement Learning approaches is twofold: they rarely integrate high-level scene context into the feature representation, and they rely on rigid, fixed reward functions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel pipeline that produces a neuro-symbolic feature representation that encompasses semantic, spatial, and shape information, as well as spatially boosted features of dynamic entities in the scene, with an emphasis on safety-critical road users. It also proposes a Soft First-Order Logic (SFOL) reward function that balances human values via a symbolic reasoning module. Here, semantic and spatial predicates are extracted from segmentation maps and applied to linguistic rules to obtain reward weights. Quantitative experiments in the CARLA simulation environment show that the proposed neuro-symbolic representation and SFOL reward function improved policy robustness and safety-related performance metrics compared to baseline representations and reward formulations across varying traffic densities and occlusion levels. The findings demonstrate that integrating holistic representations and soft reasoning into Reinforcement Learning can support more context-aware and value-aligned decision-making for autonomous driving.

</details>
